- Step 1: Evaluate `A && B` → `true && false` = `false`
- Step 2: Evaluate `!(false)` = `true`
- Step 3: Evaluate `C || A` → `true || true` = `true`
- Step 4: Evaluate `true && true` = **`true`**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Operator Precedence Misapplication:** Forgetting that `!` > `&&` > `||`. In expression `!A || B && C`, the student must first compute `!A` and `B && C` separately, then OR them. Many students evaluate left-to-right blindly.
> 2. **Case-sensitivity of Literals:** Writing `True` or `FALSE` in code/markup loses full marks in viva/code-tracing questions.
> 3. **Confusing `=` with `==`:** In conditional checks, `if (flag = true)` is an *assignment* in C but a *compile error* in Java. Knowing this distinction scores bonus marks.
> 4. **Default Value Misconception:** Students often claim the default of `boolean` is "undefined". The correct answer is **`false`** for class/static fields; local variables have no default (compile error on read).
> 5. **Truth-Table Incompleteness:** Skipping even one row of a 2-input truth table costs **0.5 to 1 mark** per missing row — always draw all 4 rows.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Boolean literals** are the **two literal constants** `true` and `false` (lowercase, keyword, primitive type `boolean`).
- **Exactly two values exist** — no third option, no numeric equivalent in Java.
- **Case-sensitive:** `True`, `TRUE`, `False` are **errors**, not literals.
- **Default value** of a boolean class field is `false`; local variables have no default.
- **No numeric interconversion** — `boolean` cannot be cast to/from `int`, `char`, etc.
- **Operators on booleans:** `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT), with strict precedence `!` > `&&` > `||`.
- **Short-circuit evaluation** skips the right operand when the result is already determined.
- **Wrapper class** is `java.lang.Boolean` (capital B) for OOP and collection contexts.
- **De Morgan's Laws** are the most-tested identity: $\lnot(A \land B) \equiv \lnot A \lor \lnot B$ and $\lnot(A \lor B) \equiv \lnot A \land \lnot B$.
- **Truth tables** must list **all $2^n$ rows** for $n$ input variables — partial tables lose marks.
- **Common exam traps:** confusing `=` with `==`; using `True`/`FALSE`; assuming `0`/`1` work in Java.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type Conversion and Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Type Conversion and Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)** using Java, **Type Conversion** refers to the automatic or manual process of changing a value from one data type to another. It is broadly classified into two distinct operations governed by the Java Language Specification (JLS):

1. **Type Conversion (Implicit / Widening):** A safe, lossless conversion performed *automatically by the Java compiler* when a smaller data type is assigned to a larger, compatible data type.
2. **Type Casting (Explicit / Narrowing):** A *programmer-directed* conversion that forces a value from a larger data type into a smaller one, where potential data loss must be explicitly acknowledged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The terms **"Conversion"** and **"Casting"** are *not* interchangeable in Java. Conversion = Automatic + Safe + Widening. Casting = Manual + Risky + Narrowing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are pouring water from a **small cup (250 mL)** into a **large bucket (10 L)**. The water fits perfectly without spilling. This is **Type Conversion (Widening)** — the smaller value moves into a larger container automatically and safely.

Now imagine the reverse: you must pour the bucket of water back into the **small cup**. Some water *will* spill out. You must do this **manually**, knowing that loss is possible. This is **Type Casting (Narrowing)**.

| Real-World Analogy | Java Operation | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|
| Small cup → Large bucket | Implicit Conversion (Widening) | **None** (Safe) |
| Large bucket → Small cup | Explicit Casting (Narrowing) | **High** (Data Loss) |
| Pouring apple juice into a coffee mug | Incompatible types | **Compile-time ERROR** |

## 1.3 The Java Type Spectrum

Java has **8 primitive data types**, ranked by size (widening hierarchy):

> **[byte → short → int → long → float → double]**

The single quote `char` type is an *unsigned* 16-bit integer that can be converted *to* the integer family but cannot be directly assigned an `int` without casting.

```java
// Safe implicit conversion chain
byte  ->  short  ->  int  ->  long  ->  float  ->  double
   1B        2B        4B      8B        4B*        8B
```
> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Fact:** `float` is a 32-bit IEEE-754 floating-point type but is *wider* than `long` (64-bit integer) because its range of representable values is far larger, even if precision differs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Major Branches of Type Transformation

### A. Primitive Type Conversions

**(i) Widening Primitive Conversion (Implicit)**
- Performed **automatically by the compiler** (no syntax needed).
- Rule: Destination type must be **wider** (larger range) than source type.
- **No data loss** ever occurs.
- Example: `int x = 100; long y = x;` — valid.

**(ii) Narrowing Primitive Conversion (Explicit Cast)**
- Performed using **cast operator `(targetType)`**.
- May cause **truncation, overflow, or precision loss**.
- Example: `double d = 99.99; int i = (int) d;` — `i` becomes `99` (fractional part truncated).

### B. Reference Type Conversions (Object Casting)

**(i) Upcasting (Implicit)** — Subclass → Superclass
- Always **safe**; done automatically.
- Example: `Dog d = new Dog(); Animal a = d;` — valid.

**(ii) Downcasting (Explicit)** — Superclass → Subclass
- Requires `(SubclassName)` cast.
- Risky: Throws `ClassCastException` at runtime if the object is not actually an instance of the target type.

## 2.2 Promotion Rules in Expressions (Binary Numeric Promotion)

When an operator (like `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`) is applied to two operands of different types, Java applies a strict set of promotion rules defined in **JLS §5.6.2**:

| Rule # | Condition | Promoted Operand Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | If either operand is `double` | Both become `double` |
| 2 | Else if either operand is `float` | Both become `float` |
| 3 | Else if either operand is `long` | Both become `long` |
| 4 | Else (neither double, float, long) | Both become `int` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Even `byte + byte` results in an **`int`**! This causes compile errors if assigned back to a `byte` without casting.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax | Automatic? | Data Loss Possible? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Widening Conversion | `large = small;` | Yes | **No** |
| Narrowing Casting | `small = (smallType) large;` | No | **Yes** |
| Upcasting (Object) | `Super s = subObj;` | Yes | **No** |
| Downcasting (Object) | `Sub s = (Sub) superRef;` | No | Runtime `ClassCastException` |
| Expression Promotion | `int + double` → `double` | Yes | Possible (precision) |
| `final` Constant Compile-Time Check | `byte b = 10;` | Yes | No (if value fits) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Type conversion is critical in:
- **Sensor Data Acquisition:** Reading ADC values (`int`) and converting to physical units (`double` temperature in °C).
- **Network Protocols:** Converting between `byte` streams and high-level `String` data.
- **Database Systems:** Mapping SQL `INTEGER` to Java `long` automatically.
- **Game Engines:** Casting `float` (GPU coordinates) to `int` (pixel screen positions) — explicit loss of sub-pixel precision.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Tracing a Numeric Expression

Let us trace the evaluation of the following Java expression step by step to demonstrate **Binary Numeric Promotion**:

```java
byte b = 10;
short s = 20;
double result = b + s * 3.0;
```

### Step 1 — Operator Precedence Analysis

In Java, the multiplicative operator `*` has **higher precedence** than the additive operator `+`. Hence:

$$
\text{Expression Tree} = b + (s \times 3.0)
$$

### Step 2 — Resolve `s * 3.0`

The operands are `short s` (16-bit integer) and the literal `3.0` (default type `double`, 64-bit).

Applying **Rule 1** of Binary Numeric Promotion:

$$
\text{Since one operand is } \texttt{double}, \text{ both are promoted to } \texttt{double}
$$

$$
s_{\text{promoted}} = 20.0 \quad (\texttt{double})
$$

$$
s \times 3.0 = 20.0 \times 3.0 = 60.0 \quad (\texttt{double})
$$

### Step 3 — Resolve `b + 60.0`

The operands are now `byte b` (8-bit integer) and `60.0` (`double`). Applying **Rule 1** again:

$$
b_{\text{promoted}} = 10.0 \quad (\texttt{double})
$$

$$
10.0 + 60.0 = 70.0 \quad (\texttt{double})
$$

### Step 4 — Final Assignment

The left-hand side `double result` can safely receive the `double` value `70.0` via **implicit widening**. No cast needed.

```java
double result = 70.0;  // System.out.println(result) -> 70.0
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Narrowing Casting with Truncation

```java
double price = 149.99;
int rupees = (int) price;
```

### Step 1 — Identify Cast Operator

`(int)` is the **explicit narrowing cast** operator. It instructs the JVM to truncate the fractional bits.

### Step 2 — Bit-Level Truncation (IEEE-754)

The IEEE-754 double `149.99` is stored in 64 bits as sign + exponent + mantissa. The cast `(int)` drops everything beyond the integer boundary:

$$
149.99 \xrightarrow{\text{(int) cast}} \lfloor 149.99 \rfloor = 149
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Java's narrowing cast for floating-to-integer always performs **truncation toward zero**, *not* rounding. So `-149.99` becomes `-149`, **not** `-150`.

### Step 3 — Overflow Example

```java
int big = 130;
byte small = (byte) big;
```

Binary of `130` is `10000010` (8 bits). Casting to `byte` keeps only the lower 8 bits:

$$
130_{10} = 1000\,0010_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{(byte)}}\; \text{Two's complement} = -126
$$

```java
System.out.println(small);  // Output: -126
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Allowed vs Forbidden Conversions

| Source → Destination | Implicit Allowed? | Explicit Cast Allowed? |
|---|---|---|
| `byte → int` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| `int → byte` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| `char → int` | ❌ No (requires cast for variable, OK for literal math) | ✅ Yes |
| `boolean → int` | ❌ **Never** | ❌ **Never** |
| `double → int` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| `String → int` | ❌ No | ❌ No (use `Integer.parseInt()`) |
| `Object → String` | ❌ No | ❌ No (use `.toString()`) |

> [!WARNING]
> **`boolean` cannot be cast to any numeric type** and vice-versa in Java — this is a famous KTU question trap!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Widening Conversion Hierarchy (Primitive Types)

```mermaid
graph LR
    BYTE[BYTE 8-bit]
    SHORT[SHORT 16-bit]
    CHAR[CHAR 16-bit]
    INT[INT 32-bit]
    LONG[LONG 64-bit]
    FLOAT[FLOAT 32-bit IEEE]
    DOUBLE[DOUBLE 64-bit IEEE]
    
    BYTE --> SHORT
    SHORT --> INT
    CHAR --> INT
    INT --> LONG
    LONG --> FLOAT
    FLOAT --> DOUBLE
    
    style BYTE fill:#a8e6cf
    style SHORT fill:#a8e6cf
    style CHAR fill:#ffd3b6
    style INT fill:#ffd3b6
    style LONG fill:#ffaaa5
    style FLOAT fill:#ffaaa5
    style DOUBLE fill:#ff8b94
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Green nodes → orange → red indicates the **widening risk-free path**. An arrow means "can be implicitly converted to".

## 4.2 Casting Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source value of Type S] --> B{Is Target T wider than S?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Implicit Widening Conversion]
    B -- No --> D{Is Target T narrower than S?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Explicit Cast Required]
    D -- No --> F{Is T the SAME type as S?}
    F -- Yes --> G[No conversion needed]
    F -- No --> H{Incompatible types?}
    H -- Yes, primitives --> I[COMPILE ERROR]
    H -- Yes, unrelated objects --> J[COMPILE ERROR]
    E --> K[Data loss may occur]
    K --> L{Is object actual instance?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Downcast succeeds]
    L -- No --> N[ClassCastException at runtime]
    
    style C fill:#a8e6cf
    style E fill:#ffd3b6
    style K fill:#ffd3b6
    style I fill:#ff8b94
    style J fill:#ff8b94
    style N fill:#ff8b94
```

## 4.3 Object Reference Casting (Upcasting vs Downcasting)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
        +eat() void
    }
    class Dog {
        +bark() void
        +eat() void
    }
    class Puppy {
        +weep() void
    }
    
    Animal <|-- Dog : extends
    Dog <|-- Puppy : extends
    
    note for Animal "UPCAST: Dog -> Animal (implicit, safe)"
    note for Dog "DOWNCAST: Animal -> Dog (explicit, risky)"
    note for Puppy "DOWNCAST: Animal -> Puppy (must verify with instanceof)"
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Flow: Compilation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Java Source Code] --> LEX[Lexer Tokenizes Literals]
    LEX --> PARSE[Parser Builds AST]
    PARSE --> CTX{Type Check by Compiler}
    CTX -- Compatible widening --> OK1[Insert implicit conversion]
    CTX -- Narrowing needed --> ERR1[Compile Error: Possible lossy conversion]
    CTX -- Explicit cast found --> OK2[Insert narrowing instruction]
    CTX -- Incompatible --> ERR2[Compile Error: Inconvertible types]
    OK1 --> BYTE[Generate Bytecode]
    OK2 --> BYTE
    BYTE --> JVM[JVM Executes]
    JVM -- Downcast object --> RTCHK{instanceof check by verifier}
    RTCHK -- Pass --> OK3[Reference assigned]
    RTCHK -- Fail --> THROW[Throw ClassCastException]
    
    style OK1 fill:#a8e6cf
    style OK2 fill:#a8e6cf
    style OK3 fill:#a8e6cf
    style ERR1 fill:#ff8b94
    style ERR2 fill:#ff8b94
    style THROW fill:#ff8b94
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between implicit type conversion and explicit type casting in Java with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Implicit Type Conversion (Widening):** Performed *automatically* by the compiler when a smaller type is assigned to a larger compatible type. No data loss occurs.  
  *Example:* `int a = 10; long b = a;` (int → long automatically)
- **Explicit Type Casting (Narrowing):** Performed *manually* by the programmer using `(targetType)` syntax to convert a larger type into a smaller one. Data loss may occur.  
  *Example:* `double d = 9.78; int i = (int) d;` (i becomes 9, fractional part lost)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Why does `byte a = 10; byte b = 20; byte c = a + b;` produce a compile-time error? How do you fix it?**

**Model Answer:**
According to Java's **Binary Numeric Promotion Rules** (JLS §5.6.2), whenever a binary arithmetic operator is applied to operands smaller than `int`, both operands are *promoted to `int`* **before** the operation. Therefore, `a + b` evaluates to an `int`. Assigning an `int` back into a `byte` requires an explicit narrowing cast.

**Fix:**
```java
byte c = (byte)(a + b);   // Explicit cast — accepts truncation if value > 127
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly write "Java doesn't allow `byte` arithmetic". **WRONG.** Java *allows* it — it just *promotes* to `int`. Full marks require mentioning the **promotion rule**.
> - Another common error: writing `byte c = a + b;` without parentheses around `(byte)(a + b)`. Casting has *higher precedence* than `+`, so `byte c = (byte)a + b;` still evaluates `a + b` as `int`. **Always wrap the whole expression**: `(byte)(a + b)`.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — Module 1, Internal Choice Option A

#### **(a)** Explain the concept of **type conversion** in Java. List the rules of **automatic type promotion** in expressions with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Type conversion in Java is the process of transforming a value of one data type into another. It is of two kinds: **implicit (widening)** and **explicit (narrowing)**. Implicit conversion is performed automatically by the compiler whenever a smaller primitive type is used in a context that expects a wider type.

**Rules of Automatic Type Promotion in Expressions:**
When an operator is applied to two operands of different types, Java applies the following promotion rules:

1. **If either operand is `double`**, the other is converted to `double`.
2. **Otherwise, if either operand is `float`**, the other is converted to `float`.
3. **Otherwise, if either operand is `long`**, the other is converted to `long`.
4. **Otherwise**, both operands are converted to `int`.

**Examples:**

```java
// Rule 1
int i = 5;
double d = 2.0;
double result = i + d;     // i promoted to 5.0, result = 7.0

// Rule 4 (NOT preserved in byte)
byte a = 10, b = 20;
int sum = a + b;           // a, b both promoted to int; sum = 30

// Rule 3
long l = 1000L;
int  j = 5;
long r = l + j;            // j promoted to long; r = 1005L
```

**Key Point:** Note that `byte`, `short`, and `char` are *always* promoted to `int` before any binary operation — even when both operands are of the same smaller type.

---

#### **(b)** What is **type casting**? Write a Java program demonstrating narrowing cast from `double` to `int`, widening conversion from `int` to `double`, and a `char` to `int` cast. Show the output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Type Casting:** Type casting is the explicit conversion of a value from one data type to another using the cast operator `(targetType)`. It is required when converting from a *wider* type to a *narrower* type (narrowing cast). It is also called **type narrowing**.

**Java Program:**
```java
public class TypeCastingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // (1) Narrowing cast: double -> int (data loss)
        double price = 149.99;
        int rupees = (int) price;
        System.out.println("Narrowing double->int: " + rupees);  // 149

        // (2) Widening conversion: int -> double (safe, automatic)
        int count = 42;
        double preciseCount = count;        // implicit
        System.out.println("Widening int->double: " + preciseCount); // 42.0

        // (3) char -> int cast (ASCII / Unicode value)
        char letter = 'A';
        int ascii = (int) letter;
        System.out.println("char 'A' as int: " + ascii);         // 65

        // (4) int -> char cast (must be explicit, even if value is 65)
        int code = 66;
        char symbol = (char) code;
        System.out.println("int 66 as char: " + symbol);         // B
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Narrowing double->int: 149
Widening int->double: 42.0
char 'A' as int: 65
int 66 as char: B
```

**Explanation:**
- Line (1): `149.99` is truncated to `149` — the fractional `.99` is discarded.
- Line (2): `42` (int) is automatically promoted to `42.0` (double).
- Line (3): `'A'` has Unicode value `65`; casting exposes it as integer.
- Line (4): Conversely, `66` cast back to `char` gives `'B'`.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` — Module 1, Internal Choice Option B

#### **(a)** Discuss **upcasting and downcasting** in Java with reference to class hierarchy. Why is downcasting risky? How does `instanceof` help? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Upcasting (Implicit, Safe):**
Upcasting is the conversion of a reference from a *subclass* type to a *superclass* type. It is performed automatically because a subclass object *is-a* superclass object (via inheritance).

```java
class Animal { void eat() { System.out.println("Animal eats"); } }
class Dog extends Animal { void bark() { System.out.println("Dog barks"); } }

Dog d = new Dog();
Animal a = d;       // implicit upcasting — safe
a.eat();            // OK — inherited method
// a.bark();        // Compile error — bark() not in Animal reference
```

**Downcasting (Explicit, Risky):**
Downcasting is the conversion of a reference from a *superclass* type back to a *subclass* type. It must be done explicitly and only succeeds if the *actual runtime object* is indeed an instance of the target subclass.

```java
Animal a = new Dog();         // upcast implicitly
Dog d = (Dog) a;              // explicit downcast — OK because actual object is Dog
d.bark();                     // Now accessible

Animal a2 = new Animal();
Dog d2 = (Dog) a2;            // RUNTIME FAILURE — ClassCastException
```

**Why Downcasting is Risky:**
The compiler only checks that the cast is between classes in the same inheritance hierarchy — it cannot verify the *actual* runtime type. If the object is not a true instance of the target subclass, the JVM throws `ClassCastException` at runtime, crashing the program.

**Role of `instanceof` Operator:**
The `instanceof` operator checks the runtime type of an object before performing a downcast, preventing exceptions:

```java
Animal a = getAnimal();   // method returning Animal
if (a instanceof Dog) {
    Dog d = (Dog) a;
    d.bark();
} else {
    System.out.println("Not a Dog, skipping downcast.");
}
```

---

#### **(b)** Explain with a Java program how expression **type promotion** can lead to an unexpected compile error and how `final` variables differ. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Compile-Error Trap:**
When a `byte` (or `short`, `char`) participates in a binary arithmetic expression, it is *promoted* to `int`. Assigning the result back to `byte` fails compilation.

**Demonstration Program:**
```java
public class PromotionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        byte x = 10;
        byte y = 20;

        // (A) Compile error — x + y is promoted to int
        // byte sum = x + y;                 // ERROR: possible lossy conversion

        // (B) Correct fix using cast
        byte sum = (byte)(x + y);
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);   // 30

        // (C) Final constant exception
        final byte FB = 50;
        byte total = FB + 100;                // OK! Because FB is a compile-time constant
        System.out.println("Total = " + total); // 150

        // (D) final variable must NOT overflow
        final byte BIG = 200;
        // byte danger = BIG;                 // ERROR: 200 does not fit in byte range

        // (E) Mixed promotion with double
        int n = 5;
        double d = n / 2.0;                   // 2.5 (promoted to double)
        int trap = n / 2;                     // 2 (integer division, no promotion here)
        System.out.println(d + " vs " + trap);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Sum = 30
Total = 150
2.5 vs 2
```

**Explanation of `final` Exception (Case C):**
Although `FB + 100` is technically an `int + int` expression producing an `int`, the Java compiler performs a **constant-folding optimization**. Since `FB` is `final` and its value `50` is known at compile time, the expression is folded into the constant `150`, which fits in `byte`. The compiler then allows the assignment *without* an explicit cast.

**However**, in case (D), the constant `200` exceeds `Byte.MAX_VALUE` (127), so the compiler still rejects it — the *value-range check* is performed even with `final`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (for both Q A and Q B):**
> - **Do NOT** write answers in generic "OOP terms" — KTU expects **Java-specific terminology** like *JLS §5.6.2*, *Binary Numeric Promotion*, `ClassCastException`, and `instanceof`.
> - Always show **complete compilable programs**, not just snippets, in 7-mark coding questions.
> - When asked about *risks*, mention **both** compile-time AND runtime consequences.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **Widening Conversion** = automatic, lossless, smaller → larger.
- 🔹 **Narrowing Casting** = manual `(type)`, may lose data, larger → smaller.
- 🔹 **Widening hierarchy:** `byte → short → int → long → float → double`.
- 🔹 `char` is **unsigned 16-bit**, convertible *to* integers but *not directly assignable from* `int`.
- 🔹 `boolean` **cannot** be cast to/from any numeric type.
- 🔹 `byte + byte` ⇒ **`int`** (due to binary numeric promotion).
- 🔹 **Upcasting** = Subclass → Superclass = implicit + safe.
- 🔹 **Downcasting** = Superclass → Subclass = explicit + risky (use `instanceof`).
- 🔹 **`final` constant exception:** compile-time constants of smaller types *can* be assigned to those smaller types without cast (if value fits).
- 🔹 Floating-to-integer cast **truncates toward zero** (not rounds).
- 🔹 Casting does **not change the underlying object** — only the *reference type*.
- 🔹 **Two keywords to remember for object safety:** `instanceof` + `(SubclassName)`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)**, a **Variable** is a named, typed storage location in a program's memory that holds a value which can be modified during the execution of the program. Every variable in OOP languages (such as **Java** and **C++**) is characterized by four fundamental attributes:

1. **Name (Identifier)** – A symbolic label used to reference the variable.
2. **Data Type** – Defines the kind of data stored and the operations permitted.
3. **Value** – The actual data stored in the memory location.
4. **Memory Address** – The physical/logical location in RAM where the value resides.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEEIT741**, variables are the foundational building blocks of any class definition, and their proper understanding is a prerequisite for mastering **classes, objects, methods, encapsulation, and data abstraction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> A variable in OOP is a *combination of an identifier and a data type bound to a memory location*, where the binding of type to identifier determines how the underlying bits are interpreted by the compiler/JVM.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Think of a **variable as a labelled storage box** in a warehouse.

| Real-World Analogy | Programming Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A labelled cardboard box | A variable |
| The size of the box (small, medium, large) | Data type (int, float, double) |
| The item placed inside the box | The value stored in the variable |
| The barcode/label on the box | The variable name (identifier) |
| The shelf number where the box is placed | The memory address |

When you write:
```java
int age = 21;
```
You are essentially telling the computer: *"Allocate a box large enough to hold an integer, label it 'age', and put the number 21 inside it."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Metaphor Clarification:**
> Unlike a real box, the contents of a variable *can change* during program execution. The box (memory) remains, but what is stored inside (the value) is replaceable.

---

### 1.3 Why Variables Are Critical in OOP

In OOP, variables are classified not just by data type, but also by **their role within a class**:

- **Member Variables** hold the **state** of an object (attributes).
- **Local Variables** hold transient data used during method execution.
- **Reference Variables** point to objects in memory, enabling polymorphism and dynamic binding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> In Java, all variables must be declared with a data type *before* use (statically typed). In C++, the language also enforces static typing but allows low-level pointer manipulation.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Conventions in KTU

| Metric | Standard Value (Java) | Standard Value (C++) |
|---|---|---|
| `int` size | **4 bytes (32 bits)** | **4 bytes (typical)** |
| `double` size | **8 bytes (64 bits)** | **8 bytes** |
| `char` size | **2 bytes (Unicode)** | **1 byte (ASCII)** |
| `boolean` size | **1 bit (logical)** | **1 byte** |
| `float` size | **4 bytes** | **4 bytes** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Endianness Convention:**
> Java is platform-independent and uses **big-endian** (network byte order) for internal storage. C++ behaviour depends on the underlying architecture (little-endian on x86/x64).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Variables in OOP

Variables in OOP languages are categorized based on **scope, lifetime, and storage location**.

#### 2.1.1 Based on Data Type

1. **Primitive Variables** – Store simple values directly.
   - Java: `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`
   - C++: `int`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `bool`, plus `long`, `short`, `unsigned` variants

2. **Reference Variables** – Store the address of an object (Java) or pointer (C++).

#### 2.1.2 Based on Scope & Lifetime

| Variable Type | Declared Inside | Lifetime | Default Value | Visibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Local Variable** | Method/Block | Until method/block ends | None (must be initialized) | Within block only |
| **Instance Variable** | Class (outside methods) | Until object is GC'd | `0`, `false`, `null` | Throughout class (object-bound) |
| **Static (Class) Variable** | Class with `static` | Until class unloaded | `0`, `false`, `null` | Shared across all objects |
| **Parameter Variable** | Method signature | During method call | Passed by caller | Within method only |
| **Reference Variable** | Class or method | Until reference lost | `null` | Points to object on heap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> *Instance variables* belong to objects; *static variables* belong to the class. Changing a static variable through one object affects all objects of that class.

---

### 2.2 Variable Declaration Syntax

#### Java Syntax
```java
// Declaration only
int count;

// Declaration with initialization
int count = 10;

// Multiple declaration
int a, b, c;

// Reference variable
String name = "KTU";
```

#### C++ Syntax
```cpp
// Primitive declaration
int count = 10;

// Reference variable
int& ref = count;   // C++ reference (alias)
int* ptr = &count;  // C++ pointer (address-of)

// Constant variable
const float PI = 3.14f;
```

---

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Variable Mechanics

#### Why Data Types Exist
- **Memory Efficiency**: Allocate exact bytes required (no waste).
- **Type Safety**: Prevent nonsensical operations (e.g., multiplying a `String`).
- **Compiler Optimization**: Allow compile-time instruction selection.

#### How Memory is Allocated
1. **Compile Time**: Compiler reserves space for `static` variables in the **Method Area / Data Segment**.
2. **Object Creation (`new`)**: Memory for instance variables is allocated on the **Heap**.
3. **Method Invocation**: Local variables and parameters are placed on the **Stack**.
4. **Garbage Collection**: When an object has no live references, JVM reclaims heap memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:**
> In embedded systems (a KTU-recommended focus area), variable memory footprint directly affects MCU selection. Choosing `int` (4 bytes) over `short` (2 bytes) in a sensor array can mean the difference between fitting in 8 KB or 16 KB of RAM.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Memory size of an array | $\text{Total Size} = N \times \text{sizeof}(\text{type})$ | bytes |
| Address of nth element | $\text{Addr}(a[n]) = \text{Base} + n \times \text{sizeof}(\text{type})$ | bytes offset |
| Offset between two pointers | $\text{Offset} = \frac{\text{Addr}_2 - \text{Addr}_1}{\text{sizeof}(\text{type})}$ | elements |
| Range of signed integer | $-2^{(b-1)}$ to $2^{(b-1)} - 1$ | where $b = \text{bits}$ |
| Range of unsigned integer | $0$ to $2^{b} - 1$ | where $b = \text{bits}$ |
| IEEE 754 float precision | $\approx 7$ decimal digits | single precision |
| IEEE 754 double precision | $\approx 15$ to $17$ decimal digits | double precision |
| Java int range | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | i.e., $-2147483648$ to $2147483647$ |
| Address of pointer `p+i` | $p + i \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{type})$ | pointer arithmetic |
| Reference variable storage | $\text{sizeof}(\text{reference}) = \text{constant}$ (Java: depends on JVM, typically **4 to 8 bytes**) | bytes |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Variables underpin nearly every engineering software system:

- **IoT Firmware (C++)**: Sensor readings stored as `float temperature;`.
- **Banking Applications (Java)**: `double balance;` representing account state.
- **Game Development**: `int playerHealth;` defining mutable game state.
- **Database ORM Mapping**: Each class field becomes a column — instance variables map to schema attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 OOP Connection:**
> Understanding variables is the gateway to **Encapsulation** — variables are typically declared `private` and accessed via `public` getters/setters, preserving the OOP principle of **data hiding**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Memory Size Calculation for Primitive Arrays

**Problem (KTU Style):**
Given the declaration `int[] marks = new int[50];` in Java, compute the total memory consumed on the heap, assuming a 32-bit JVM where each `int` occupies **4 bytes** and the array header consumes **12 bytes**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the components of array memory.**

An array in Java allocates:
1. **Object Header** (12 bytes on 32-bit JVM): contains class pointer, lock info, length.
2. **Element Storage**: contiguous block of $N$ elements.

**Step 2 — Define the formula.**

$$
\text{Total Memory} = \text{Header Size} + (N \times \text{sizeof}(\text{element}))
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{Total Memory} = 12 + (50 \times 4)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the multiplication.**

$$
50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total.**

$$
\text{Total Memory} = 12 + 200 = 212 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Memory} = 212 \text{ bytes}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Address of an Array Element (Pointer Arithmetic)

**Problem:**
Given `int arr[] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};` in C++, where the base address of `arr` is **1000** and `sizeof(int) = 4`, find the address of `arr[3]`.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the address formula.**

$$
\text{Addr}(a[i]) = \text{Base Address} + (i \times \text{sizeof}(\text{type}))
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the inputs.**

- Base Address $= 1000$
- Index $i = 3$
- `sizeof(int) = 4` bytes

**Step 3 — Compute the offset.**

$$
\text{Offset} = 3 \times 4 = 12
$$

**Step 4 — Add offset to base address.**

$$
\text{Addr}(arr[3]) = 1000 + 12 = 1012
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Address of } arr[3] = 1012}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Check:** `arr[0]` would be at 1000, `arr[1]` at 1004, `arr[2]` at 1008, `arr[3]` at 1012 ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Integer Range for Signed Type

**Problem:**
Determine the range of values that can be stored in a **signed 16-bit integer (`short` in Java)**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the formula for signed integers.**

$$
\text{Range} = -2^{(b-1)} \text{ to } 2^{(b-1)} - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $b = 16$.**

$$
\text{Range} = -2^{(16-1)} \text{ to } 2^{(16-1)} - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent.**

$$
2^{15} = 32768
$$

**Step 4 — Express the range.**

$$
\text{Range} = -32768 \text{ to } 32767
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{-32768 \leq \text{value} \leq 32767}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Variable Behaviour in Memory

| Operation | Java Behaviour | C++ Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Declare uninitialized local | **Compile-time error** | **Garbage value** (undefined) |
| `int x = 5; int y = x;` | Both have value 5 (independent copies) | Both have value 5 (independent copies) |
| Reference copy `obj1 = obj2` | Both point to **same object** (shallow) | Pointer copy: both point to same object |
| Modify primitive in method | **No effect** on caller (pass-by-value) | **No effect** unless passed by reference |
| Modify object via method | **Affects caller** (same reference) | **Affects caller** (same pointer) |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Total Memory for a Class Object

**Problem:**
A Java class is defined as:
```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;        // 4 bytes
    String name;       // 4 bytes (reference)
    double marks;      // 8 bytes
    boolean active;    // 1 byte
}
```
Assuming a 32-bit JVM with **no padding**, calculate the size of one `Student` object on the heap.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Sum primitive fields.**

$$
\text{Primitive Size} = 4 + 8 + 1 = 13 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Add reference field.**

$$
\text{Reference Size} = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total instance data.**

$$
\text{Instance Data} = 13 + 4 = 17 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Add object header (typical 12 bytes on 32-bit JVM).**

$$
\text{Total Object Size} = 12 + 17 = 29 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Object Size} = 29 \text{ bytes (no padding)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, JVMs use **8-byte alignment**, so the actual size would be padded to the nearest multiple of 8: $\lceil 29 / 8 \rceil \times 8 = 32 \text{ bytes}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Java Runtime Memory Layout (Variables Perspective)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[JVM Runtime Memory] --> B[Heap]
    A --> C[Stack]
    A --> D[Method Area]

    B --> B1[Object1: Student]
    B --> B2[Object2: Student]
    B --> B3[String Pool]

    B1 --> B1a[rollNo: int]
    B1 --> B1b[name: ref to String]
    B1 --> B1c[marks: double]
    B1 --> B1d[active: boolean]

    C --> C1[main Stack Frame]
    C1 --> C1a[Local: int x]
    C1 --> C1b[Local: Student s1 ref]

    D --> D1[Class Metadata]
    D --> D2[Static Variables]
    D2 --> D2a[static int count]
    D2 --> D2b[static String school]

    C1b -. points to .-> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - The **Heap** stores objects and their instance variables.
> - The **Stack** stores local variables and reference variables.
> - The **Method Area** stores class-level static variables.
> - Dotted arrows show that **reference variables in the stack point to objects in the heap**.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Variable Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    V[Variables in OOP] --> V1[Primitive Variables]
    V --> V2[Reference Variables]

    V1 --> V1a[Integer Types]
    V1 --> V1b[Floating Types]
    V1 --> V1c[Character]
    V1 --> V1d[Boolean]

    V1a --> V1a1[byte]
    V1a --> V1a2[short]
    V1a --> V1a3[int]
    V1a --> V1a4[long]

    V1b --> V1b1[float]
    V1b --> V1b2[double]

    V2 --> V2a[Class References]
    V2 --> V2b[Array References]
    V2 --> V2c[Interface References]

    V2a --> V2a1[String]
    V2a --> V2a2[Custom Objects]

    V --> V3[By Scope]
    V3 --> V3a[Local Variable]
    V3 --> V3b[Instance Variable]
    V3 --> V3c[Static Variable]
    V3 --> V3d[Parameter Variable]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Variable Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code Written] --> B[Compiler Phase]
    B --> C{Variable Type?}
    C -->|Primitive| D[Allocate on Stack or Heap-Embedded]
    C -->|Reference| E[Allocate Reference on Stack]
    D --> F[Initialize with Value]
    E --> G[Reference Points to Heap Object]
    G --> H[Object Constructed via new]
    F --> I[Program Execution]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Reference Lost?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Garbage Collector Reclaims Heap]
    J -->|No| I
    I --> L[Method Ends]
    L --> M[Stack Frame Popped]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> This lifecycle explains why **memory leaks** occur in OOP — when references are unintentionally retained (e.g., static collections), garbage collection is prevented.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Scope Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Variable Accessed Inside Method] --> Q1{Is it Local?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| UseLocal[Use Local Variable]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a Parameter?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| UseParam[Use Parameter]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it Instance Variable?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| UseInst[Use this.variable]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it Static Variable?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| UseStatic[Use ClassName.variable]
    Q4 -->|No| Error[Compilation Error]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a variable in Java. Explain the different types of variables with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

A **variable** in Java is a named memory location that holds a value of a declared data type during program execution.

Java supports the following variable types:

1. **Local Variables** (1 Mark)
   - Declared inside methods, constructors, or blocks.
   - Must be initialized before use; no default value.
   - Example: `void display() { int x = 5; }`

2. **Instance Variables** (1 Mark)
   - Declared inside a class but outside methods.
   - Each object has its own copy; default value `0`, `false`, or `null`.
   - Example: `class Student { int rollNo; }`

3. **Static (Class) Variables** (1 Mark)
   - Declared with the `static` keyword inside a class.
   - Shared across all objects of the class.
   - Example: `static String college = "KTU";`

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between primitive and reference variables in Java. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Stores | Actual value | Address (reference) of an object |
| Memory Location | Stack (if local) | Stack holds reference; object on heap |
| Default Value | Type-specific (`0`, `false`) | `null` |
| Size | Fixed (e.g., 4 bytes for int) | JVM-dependent (typically 4 to 8 bytes) |
| Example | `int age = 25;` | `String name = "Kerala";` |

**Examples (for full marks):**

```java
int marks = 95;                // Primitive: stores 95 directly
String student = "Anu";        // Reference: points to a String object on heap
```

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different categories of variables in Java with examples. Discuss their scope and lifetime. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of instance, static, and local variables. Explain the output. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Categories of Variables in Java:**

1. **Local Variables** (2 Marks)
   - Declared inside methods, blocks, or constructors.
   - **Scope:** Within the block they are declared.
   - **Lifetime:** Created when block is entered, destroyed when block exits.
   - **Example:**
     ```java
     void calc() {
         int sum = 10;  // local
     }
     ```

2. **Instance Variables** (2 Marks)
   - Declared inside class, outside methods, without `static`.
   - **Scope:** Throughout the class (accessible to non-static methods).
   - **Lifetime:** As long as the object exists in memory.
   - **Example:**
     ```java
     class Employee {
         int id;        // instance variable
         String name;   // instance variable
     }
     ```

3. **Static (Class) Variables** (2 Marks)
   - Declared with `static` keyword inside class.
   - **Scope:** Entire class (accessible to all methods, even `main`).
   - **Lifetime:** Loaded when class is loaded, destroyed when class is unloaded.
   - **Example:**
     ```java
     class Counter {
         static int count = 0;  // shared across all Counter objects
     }
     ```

4. **Parameter Variables** (1 Mark)
   - Declared in method signatures.
   - Act as local variables within the method.

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Java Program:**

```java
class Demo {
    static int staticVar = 100;     // static variable
    int instanceVar = 50;           // instance variable

    void display() {
        int localVar = 10;          // local variable
        System.out.println("Static: " + staticVar);
        System.out.println("Instance: " + instanceVar);
        System.out.println("Local: " + localVar);
        staticVar++;                // modifies static
        instanceVar++;              // modifies instance for this object
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Demo obj1 = new Demo();
        Demo obj2 = new Demo();
        obj1.display();
        obj2.display();
    }
}
```

**Output Explanation (Full Marks):**

```
Static: 100
Instance: 50
Local: 10
Static: 101
Instance: 50
Local: 10
```

**Explanation:**

- `staticVar` is shared: increments from 100 → 101 between calls. **(2 Marks)**
- `instanceVar` is per-object: each object starts fresh at 50. **(2 Marks)**
- `localVar` is recreated every method call, always 10. **(2 Marks)**
- `staticVar` and `instanceVar` are accessed without `this` from within instance methods. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** What is a reference variable in Java? Explain how it differs from a primitive variable in terms of memory allocation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a Java class `Box` with two `int` instance variables (length, breadth). Write a program to create two `Box` objects, assign values, and demonstrate that modifying one object does not affect the other (since instance variables are per-object). **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A **reference variable** in Java is a variable that stores the **memory address (reference)** of an object rather than the actual data. It is declared using a class or interface type.

**Memory Allocation Difference:**

| Aspect | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Stores | Actual value | Object reference (address) |
| Memory Location | Stack | Reference on stack; object on heap |
| Allocation | At declaration | Reference created on stack; object allocated on `new` |
| Default Value | Type-specific (`0`, `false`) | `null` |
| Deallocation | Automatic on scope exit | Reference popped; object garbage-collected |

**Diagram (2 Marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Stack] --> B[refVar]
    B -. points to .-> C[Heap: Box Object]
    C --> D[length: 10]
    C --> E[breadth: 20]

    F[Stack] --> G[intVar]
    G --> H[Value 42 directly on Stack]
```

**Explanation (3 Marks):**

- Primitive `int x = 42;` — the value 42 is stored directly on the stack. **(1 Mark)**
- Reference `Box b = new Box();` — `b` on stack holds the address of the `Box` object on heap. **(1 Mark)**
- Multiple reference variables can point to the same object; assignment of primitives creates independent copies. **(1 Mark)**

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Java Program:**

```java
class Box {
    int length;
    int breadth;

    Box(int l, int b) {
        length = l;
        breadth = b;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Length: " + length + ", Breadth: " + breadth);
    }

    void modify(int l, int b) {
        length = l;
        breadth = b;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box box1 = new Box(10, 20);
        Box box2 = new Box(30, 40);

        System.out.println("Before modification:");
        box1.display();   // Length: 10, Breadth: 20
        box2.display();   // Length: 30, Breadth: 40

        box1.modify(99, 99);

        System.out.println("After modifying box1:");
        box1.display();   // Length: 99, Breadth: 99
        box2.display();   // Length: 30, Breadth: 40 (unchanged!)
    }
}
```

**Explanation (Full Marks Breakdown):**

- Class definition with two `int` instance variables. **(1 Mark)**
- Constructor to initialize. **(1 Mark)**
- Creation of two separate `Box` objects in `main`. **(1 Mark)**
- Calling `modify` on `box1` only changes `box1`'s instance variables. **(2 Marks)**
- `box2` retains original values because each object has its own memory on heap. **(2 Marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Variables Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *static* with *instance* variables**: Students often claim `static` variables are per-object. They are NOT — they belong to the **class** and are shared. Expect to lose **2 to 3 marks** if confused.
>
> 2. **Forgetting initialization of local variables**: Java compiler throws an error for uninitialized local variables. C++ allows garbage values. Examiners often set trick questions testing this — be explicit about the language.
>
> 3. **Mixing pass-by-value semantics**: In Java, even reference variables are passed by value (the reference is copied). Modifying the reference inside a method does not affect the caller's reference.
>
> 4. **Ignoring memory padding**: In size-calculation questions, students compute raw sizes without alignment. Examiners expect awareness of JVM padding (multiples of 8 bytes).
>
> 5. **Not labeling the diagram**: In 7-mark questions involving memory layouts, a clear diagram (Stack vs Heap) typically earns the full marks allocated for visualization.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **variable** = identifier + data type + memory location + value.
- ✅ **Primitive variables** store actual values; **reference variables** store object addresses.
- ✅ **Local variables** must be initialized before use (Java enforces this strictly).
- ✅ **Instance variables** have default values (`0`, `false`, `null`) and belong to objects.
- ✅ **Static variables** belong to the class — shared across all instances.
- ✅ **Scope** = where a variable is accessible; **Lifetime** = how long it exists in memory.
- ✅ Java memory: **Stack** (locals, references) vs **Heap** (objects, instance data) vs **Method Area** (static, class metadata).
- ✅ Array address formula: $\text{Addr}(a[i]) = \text{Base} + i \times \text{sizeof}(\text{type})$
- ✅ Integer range formula: $-2^{(b-1)}$ to $2^{(b-1)} - 1$ for signed types.
- ✅ Every variable in Java is **pass-by-value** — even references are copied.
- ✅ Variables are the **state** of objects — foundation for **encapsulation** (next logical OOP concept).
- ✅ For KTU exams: always mention **scope AND lifetime** together when categorizing variables.
- ✅ Use **UML-style or Mermaid diagrams** in 7-mark answers to earn visualization marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Array (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** An array is a **linear, homogeneous, contiguous data structure** that stores a **fixed-size sequential collection** of elements of the **same data type** under a **single, common identifier (name)**, where each element is accessed using an **integer index (subscript)** that represents its position relative to the base address.

In the context of Object Oriented Programming (OOP) using languages like **C++** and **Java**, an array is the most fundamental **derived/user-defined data structure** that allows the programmer to handle multiple data items using a single variable name, instead of declaring separate variables for each value.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Breakdown

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Element** | A single data value stored in the array. |
| **Index / Subscript** | A non-negative integer that identifies the position of an element. |
| **Base Address** | The memory location of the **first element** (index 0) of the array. |
| **Size / Length** | The total number of elements the array can hold (fixed at compile-time in C++; dynamic via `ArrayList` in Java). |
| **Dimension** | The number of indices required to uniquely access an element (1D, 2D, …, nD). |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Locker Room**
> Imagine a **row of identical lockers** in a school corridor, each numbered sequentially from **0, 1, 2, 3, …, n-1**. Every locker is the **same size** and **same type** (homogeneous), they are placed **side-by-side without gaps** (contiguous), and you refer to a locker by its **number** (index). The entire row has **one name** ("Class 10-A Lockers"), but you access any specific locker using `lockers[7]`.

This perfectly captures the essence of an array: a single name, a fixed count, a starting position (base), and integer-based random access.

## 1.4 Why Arrays? — The Engineering Motivation

In engineering applications, we almost never deal with a **single sensor reading, a single transaction, or a single image pixel**. We deal with **thousands or millions of such values simultaneously**. Arrays provide:

- **Random Access in O(1) Time:** Any element can be fetched instantly using its index.
- **Cache Locality:** Contiguous memory storage exploits CPU caching, yielding superior performance in DSP, image processing, and matrix computations.
- **Foundation for Advanced DS:** Stacks, queues, hash tables, heaps, and matrices are all built *on top of* arrays.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Under the **NEP 2020 / OBE framework**, the CO (Course Outcome) mapped to arrays typically falls under **CO1 — Apply** (Bloom's Level 3: *Apply*) of Module 1, focusing on problem-solving using linear data structures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Arrays

Arrays are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### A. By Dimension
- **One-Dimensional (1D) Array:** A simple linear list. Syntax (C++): `int marks[5];`
- **Two-Dimensional (2D) Array:** A mathematical matrix with rows and columns. Syntax: `int matrix[3][4];`
- **Multi-Dimensional (nD) Array:** Extension to 3 or more indices, used in volumetric data, tensors, and simulations.

### B. By Storage Duration (C++ specific)
- **Static / Global Arrays:** Lifetime = entire program, allocated in the **data segment**.
- **Automatic / Local Arrays:** Lifetime = function/block scope, allocated on the **stack**.
- **Dynamic Arrays:** Allocated on the **heap** using `new[]` (C++) or `malloc()` (C-style).

### C. By Mutability of Size
- **Fixed-size (C/C++):** Size must be a compile-time constant.
- **Resizable / Dynamic (Java `ArrayList`, C++ `std::vector`):** Size can grow at runtime.

## 2.2 Internal Memory Representation

The compiler allocates a **single contiguous block** of memory whose size equals:

$$\text{Total Memory (Bytes)} = \text{Number of Elements} \times \text{sizeof(data type)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All elements are stored **back-to-back** with **no padding** (in most practical implementations of primitive arrays), enabling a **pure arithmetic addressing scheme** that is the basis of the famous "Address Calculation Formula."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the following table — these are tested repeatedly in KTU ESE questions.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Valid Index Range | $0 \le i \le (n-1)$ | Where $n$ = array size |
| Last Index | $n - 1$ | Out-of-bounds access → **Undefined Behavior** |
| 1D Address of `A[i]` | $B + (i \times w)$ | Bytes, base indexing |
| 1D Address of `A[i]` (1-indexed) | $B + ((i - 1) \times w)$ | FORTRAN/Pascal style |
| 2D Address of `A[i][j]` (Row-Major) | $B + ((i \times n) + j) \times w$ | C, C++, Java, Python |
| 2D Address of `A[i][j]` (Column-Major) | $B + ((j \times m) + i) \times w$ | FORTRAN, MATLAB |
| Number of Elements in `A[m][n]` | $m \times n$ | Total memory cells |
| Memory Footprint | $\text{rows} \times \text{cols} \times \text{sizeof(type)}$ | Bytes |

**Legend:**
- $B$ = Base address of the array
- $w$ = Size of one element in bytes (e.g., 4 for `int`, 8 for `double`)
- $n$ = number of columns
- $m$ = number of rows
- $i$ = row index, $j$ = column index

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** C/C++/Java store 2D arrays in **Row-Major Order**. If a question asks "column-major", they are testing whether you know the storage convention — read the question carefully!

## 2.4 Operations Supported on Arrays

| Operation | Time Complexity (1D) | Description |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Traversal** | $O(n)$ | Visit each element once. |
| **Insertion** (at end) | $O(1)$ | Append — only if size not full. |
| **Insertion** (at position $k$) | $O(n - k)$ | Requires shifting. |
| **Deletion** (at position $k$) | $O(n - k)$ | Requires shifting left. |
| **Search — Linear** | $O(n)$ | Sequential scan. |
| **Search — Binary** | $O(\log n)$ | Requires **sorted** array. |
| **Update** | $O(1)$ | Direct index access. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Arrays underpin virtually every quantitative engineering discipline:

- **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** Audio samples stored in 1D arrays for FFT filtering.
- **Computer Graphics:** 2D image pixel grids (RGB matrices).
- **Machine Learning:** Feature vectors, weight matrices, tensors in neural networks.
- **Embedded Systems:** Lookup tables (LUTs) for trigonometric functions on microcontrollers.
- **Structural Engineering:** Stiffness matrices for Finite Element Analysis (FEA).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Address of a 1D Array Element

**Problem Statement:** Given a 1D array `A` of `n` elements, each of size `w` bytes, with base address `B`, derive the address of the element at index `i` (0-indexed).

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the starting point.**
The first element of the array, `A[0]`, is stored at the base address `B`. Thus, the address of `A[0]` is simply $B$.

**Step 2 — Identify the offset of the $i$-th element.**
The $i$-th element is `i` positions *after* the first element. Since each element occupies exactly `w` bytes contiguously, the total byte-offset for the $i$-th element is:

$$\text{offset} = i \times w$$

**Step 3 — Combine base and offset.**
The physical memory address where `A[i]` resides is the base address plus the byte offset:

$$\text{Address}(A[i]) = B + (i \times w)$$

**Step 4 — Validate with boundary cases.**
- For $i = 0$: Address $= B + 0 = B$ ✓ (matches the first element)
- For $i = n - 1$: Address $= B + (n - 1)w$ ✓ (matches the last valid element)
- For $i = n$: Address $= B + n \cdot w$ → **Out of bounds!** Compiler does not check, leading to undefined behavior.

**Final Generalized Formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{Address}(A[i]) = B + (i \times w)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Address of a 2D Array Element (Row-Major Order)

**Problem Statement:** Given a 2D array `A` of dimensions `m × n` (m rows, n columns), each element of size `w` bytes, with base address `B`, derive the address of `A[i][j]` stored in **row-major order**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:

**Step 1 — Understand Row-Major Storage Layout.**
In row-major order (used by C, C++, Java), the elements are stored row-by-row. The first row is laid out completely in memory, *then* the second row, and so on:

$$A[0][0],\; A[0][1],\; \ldots,\; A[0][n-1],\; A[1][0],\; A[1][1],\; \ldots$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of elements before row $i$.**
To reach row $i$, we must skip the entirety of rows $0, 1, 2, \ldots, i-1$. Each row has $n$ elements, so the number of elements skipped *before* row $i$ is:

$$\text{elements\_before\_row\_i} = i \times n$$

**Step 3 — Add the column offset within row $i$.**
Within row $i$, we further advance by $j$ elements to reach column $j$. The number of elements skipped *within* the row is simply $j$.

**Step 4 — Sum the element offsets and convert to byte offset.**
The total number of elements between the base and `A[i][j]` is:

$$\text{total\_element\_offset} = (i \times n) + j$$

Multiplying by the size of each element, $w$, gives the byte offset:

$$\text{byte\_offset} = ((i \times n) + j) \times w$$

**Step 5 — Add to the base address.**

$$\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + ((i \times n) + j) \times w$$

**Step 6 — Validate boundary cases.**
- $i=0, j=0$: Address $= B + 0 = B$ ✓
- $i=0, j=n-1$: Address $= B + (n-1)w$ ✓ (end of first row)
- $i=m-1, j=n-1$: Address $= B + (m \cdot n - 1)w$ ✓ (final element of the matrix)

**Final Generalized Formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + ((i \times n) + j) \times w}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** Consider the C++ declaration `int A[10][20];` stored in row-major order starting at base address `B = 1000`. Find the address of `A[5][7]`, given `sizeof(int) = 2` bytes.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $B = 1000$
- Number of columns $n = 20$
- Element size $w = 2$ bytes
- Target indices: $i = 5,\; j = 7$

**Step 2 — Apply the row-major formula.**

$$\text{Address}(A[5][7]) = B + ((i \times n) + j) \times w$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\text{Address}(A[5][7]) = 1000 + ((5 \times 20) + 7) \times 2$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the inner parenthesis.**

$$= 1000 + (100 + 7) \times 2$$

**Step 5 — Multiply.**

$$= 1000 + 107 \times 2$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$= 1000 + 214 = 1214$$

$$\boxed{\text{Address}(A[5][7]) = 1214 \text{ bytes}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — 1D vs 2D vs Dynamic Arrays

| Feature | 1D Array | 2D Array | Dynamic (`std::vector` / `ArrayList`) |
|---------|----------|----------|----------------------------------------|
| Declaration | `int a[5];` | `int a[3][4];` | `vector<int> a;` |
| Memory Layout | Contiguous | Contiguous (row-major) | Contiguous (heap-allocated) |
| Size known at compile-time? | Yes | Yes | No (runtime) |
| Indexing Complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Resizable? | No (in C/C++) | No | Yes |
| Bound Checking | No (C/C++) | No | Yes (Java `ArrayList`) |
| Typical KTU Use | Marks, list of readings | Matrices, images | Unknown-length data streams |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Continued)

## 3.5 Derivation 3 — Column-Major Address Formula

**Step 1 — Reverse the traversal logic.**
In **column-major order** (used by FORTRAN, MATLAB, and certain numerical libraries), the array is stored column-by-column. To reach column $j$, we must first skip the entirety of columns $0, 1, \ldots, j-1$.

**Step 2 — Compute elements before column $j$.**
Each column has $m$ elements, so:

$$\text{elements\_before\_column\_j} = j \times m$$

**Step 3 — Add the row offset within column $j$.**
Within column $j$, the $i$-th element is $i$ positions down. So the offset inside the column is $i$.

**Step 4 — Sum total offset and convert to bytes.**

$$\text{byte\_offset} = ((j \times m) + i) \times w$$

**Step 5 — Final formula:**

$$\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + ((j \times m) + i) \times w$$

**Step 6 — Verify with boundary case.**
- $i=0, j=0$: Address $= B + 0 = B$ ✓
- $i=m-1, j=n-1$: Address $= B + (m \cdot n - 1)w$ ✓

$$\boxed{\text{Address}_{\text{col-major}}(A[i][j]) = B + ((j \times m) + i) \times w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Insight:** Many students *memorize* only the row-major formula and lose marks when the question subtly specifies column-major. **Always re-read the question** to identify the storage convention.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 4 — Total Number of Elements in an n-Dimensional Array

**Step 1 — Generalize the 1D and 2D cases.**
- For 1D array of size $d_1$: total elements $= d_1$
- For 2D array of size $d_1 \times d_2$: total elements $= d_1 \times d_2$

**Step 2 — Identify the pattern.**
Each new dimension multiplies the total element count by its size.

**Step 3 — Inductive generalization to n-dimensions.**
For an n-dimensional array with dimensions $d_1, d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n$:

$$\text{Total Elements} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} d_k = d_1 \times d_2 \times d_3 \times \ldots \times d_n$$

**Step 4 — Generalize the address formula.**
For an n-dimensional array stored in row-major order, the address of element at indices $(i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_n)$ is given by the **horner's rule** form:

$$\text{Address} = B + w \cdot \left( i_1 \cdot d_2 \cdot d_3 \cdots d_n \;+\; i_2 \cdot d_3 \cdots d_n \;+\; \cdots \;+\; i_{n-1} \cdot d_n \;+\; i_n \right)$$

**Step 5 — Compact symbolic form.**

$$\text{Address} = B + w \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left( i_k \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{n} d_j \right)$$

where the innermost empty product is defined as **1**.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Searching Algorithms on Arrays

> [!TIP]
> **KTU often tests the difference between linear and binary search in Module 1 / 2. Memorize the following comparison.**

| Parameter | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Prerequisite | None | Array **must be sorted** |
| Best Case | $O(1)$ (element at index 0) | $O(1)$ (middle element) |
| Average Case | $O(n)$ | $O(\log_2 n)$ |
| Worst Case | $O(n)$ | $O(\log_2 n)$ |
| Space Complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ iterative / $O(\log n)$ recursive |
| Works on unsorted data? | Yes | No |
| Indexing access | Sequential | Random (jump to mid) |
| KTU Tag | Module 1 (Arrays) | Module 2 (Searching/Sorting) |

**Step-by-step verification of binary search complexity:**
- Each iteration halves the search space.
- After $k$ iterations, the search space is $n / 2^k$.
- Search terminates when $n / 2^k \le 1$, i.e., $2^k \ge n$, i.e., $k \ge \log_2 n$.
- Hence, worst-case comparisons $= \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$.

---

## 3.8 Worked Example — Array Traversal Logic (Pseudocode)

**Problem:** Given a 1D integer array `A` of size $n$, find the **sum of all elements** and the **largest element**.

**Step 1 — Initialize accumulators.**
- $\text{sum} = 0$
- $\text{max} = A[0]$ (initialize to first element to avoid $-\infty$ assumption)

**Step 2 — Iterate from index $0$ to $n-1$.**
For each $i$ in $[0, n-1]$:
- $\text{sum} \mathrel{+}= A[i]$
- If $A[i] > \text{max}$, then $\text{max} = A[i]$.

**Step 3 — Loop termination condition.**
Loop exits when $i = n$ (all $n$ elements processed).

**Step 4 — Return the result.**

**Algorithmic Complexity:**
- **Time:** $O(n)$ — single pass.
- **Space:** $O(1)$ — only two auxiliary variables.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — One-Dimensional Array Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0["A 0 : 1000"] --- A1["A 1 : 1004"] --- A2["A 2 : 1008"] --- A3["A 3 : 1012"] --- A4["A 4 : 1016"] --- A5["A 5 : 1020"]
    BASE["Base Address B = 1000"] -.-> A0
    W["Element Size w = 4 bytes"] -.-> A0
```

**Interpretation:** Each block represents one memory cell of width $w = 4$ bytes (e.g., `int`). The addresses grow linearly: $1000, 1004, 1008, \ldots$ — a textbook illustration of **contiguous allocation**.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Two-Dimensional Array (Row-Major Storage)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph R0["Row 0 — A 0 to A 0 n-1"]
        R0C0["A 0 0 : 1000"]
        R0C1["A 0 1 : 1004"]
        R0C2["A 0 2 : 1008"]
        R0C3["A 0 3 : 1012"]
    end
    subgraph R1["Row 1 — A 1 to A 1 n-1"]
        R1C0["A 1 0 : 1016"]
        R1C1["A 1 1 : 1020"]
        R1C2["A 1 2 : 1024"]
        R1C3["A 1 3 : 1028"]
    end
    subgraph R2["Row 2 — A 2 to A 2 n-1"]
        R2C0["A 2 0 : 1032"]
        R2C1["A 2 1 : 1036"]
        R2C2["A 2 2 : 1040"]
        R2C3["A 2 3 : 1044"]
    end
    R0C0 --- R0C1 --- R0C2 --- R0C3 --- R1C0 --- R1C1 --- R1C2 --- R1C3 --- R2C0 --- R2C1 --- R2C2 --- R2C3
```

**Interpretation:** Read the chain **left-to-right** to see the *actual physical memory order*. Rows are stored contiguously; the linear index of `A[i][j]` in the chain is exactly $(i \times n + j)$.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Array Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Program]) --> DECL["Declare Array : int A n and B Base Address"]
    DECL --> ALLOC["Allocate Contiguous Memory : n times w bytes"]
    ALLOC --> INPUT["Input Phase : read n elements into A 0 to A n-1"]
    INPUT --> PROC["Processing Phase : traverse / search / sort / update"]
    PROC --> OUT["Output Phase : display results"]
    OUT --> FREE["Deallocate Memory : C++ delete, Java GC"]
    FREE --> STOP([End Program])
```

**Interpretation:** A high-level flow showing the **lifecycle of an array** from declaration → allocation → I/O → processing → cleanup. This is the framework examiners expect in 7-mark descriptive answers.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Indexing Boundary Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    VALID["Valid Indices : 0 to n-1"]:::valid
    NEG["Negative Index : Undefined Behavior in C++"]:::invalid
    OVER["Index = n : Buffer Overflow"]:::invalid
    FAR["Index greater than n : Segfault / Crash"]:::invalid
    classDef valid fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef invalid fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** Visual reinforcement of the **safe vs unsafe index ranges** — a frequently tested concept in KTU viva and MCQ sections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Buffer overflow vulnerabilities (e.g., the infamous *Heartbleed* bug of 2014) exploit exactly this boundary mismatch. Secure coding practices mandate **explicit bounds checking** before every array access.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Module 1 typically contributes **20–25%** of the theory paper. Within that, *Arrays* is a **high-weightage** topic — expect either a **3-mark short answer** OR a **sub-part of a 14-mark question** in every exam cycle.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define an array. List any four advantages of using arrays in a C++ program.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
An array is a collection of a fixed number of elements of the **same data type** stored in **contiguous memory locations** and accessed using a **common name** with an integer index. The valid index range is $0$ to $n-1$, where $n$ is the size of the array.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — any four):**
1. **Random Access:** Any element can be accessed directly in $O(1)$ time using its index.
2. **Cache Locality:** Contiguous memory layout improves CPU cache performance.
3. **Code Simplicity:** Replaces multiple variable declarations with a single structure.
4. **Easy Traversal:** A `for` loop can iterate through all elements cleanly.
5. **Foundation for Advanced DS:** Stacks, queues, and matrices are built upon arrays.
6. **Memory Efficiency:** No per-element overhead (no pointers, no headers like linked lists).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a one-dimensional and a two-dimensional array. Give one example declaration for each in C++.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 1D Array | 2D Array |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Number of indices | 1 (column) | 2 (row, column) |
| Visual model | Linear list | Matrix/Table |
| Declaration | `int marks[5];` | `int matrix[3][4];` |
| Address formula | $B + i \cdot w$ | $B + ((i \cdot n) + j) \cdot w$ |
| Use case | Marks list, sensor readings | Image pixels, tables |

**Example:**
- 1D: `float temperatures[7];` (one week of readings)
- 2D: `int sudoku[9][9];` (a sudoku puzzle grid)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a **14-mark question with internal choice** (i.e., either Q(A) or Q(B) to be answered). Each 14-mark question is split into two **7-mark sub-parts**.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the memory representation of a 2D array in row-major order. Derive the address calculation formula for `A[i][j]` in a row-major-stored 2D array of size `m × n` with base address `B` and element size `w`. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition and Concept (2 marks):**
A 2D array is a collection of $m \times n$ elements arranged in a grid of $m$ rows and $n$ columns, stored in **contiguous memory locations** with a single common name. In **row-major order** (used by C, C++, Java), the elements of the first row are stored first, followed by the second row, and so on. If $m = 2, n = 3$, the storage order is:

$$A[0][0],\; A[0][1],\; A[0][2],\; A[1][0],\; A[1][1],\; A[1][2]$$

**Step 2 — Skipping Rows (2 marks):**
To reach row $i$, we must skip rows $0, 1, 2, \ldots, i-1$. Each row contains $n$ elements, so the number of elements skipped before reaching row $i$ is $i \times n$.

**Step 3 — Skipping Columns Within the Row (1 mark):**
Within row $i$, to reach column $j$, we advance by $j$ positions.

**Step 4 — Total Element Offset (1 mark):**
The total number of elements between the base address and `A[i][j]` is $(i \times n) + j$.

**Step 5 — Final Formula (1 mark):**
Multiplying by the element size $w$ and adding to the base address $B$:

$$\boxed{\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + ((i \times n) + j) \times w}$$

---

#### (b) Given a C++ declaration `double score[10][15];` stored in row-major order with base address `B = 2000` and `sizeof(double) = 8` bytes, calculate the address of `score[4][9]`. Also, find the total memory consumed by the array. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Base address $B = 2000$
- Number of rows $m = 10$, Number of columns $n = 15$
- Element size $w = 8$ bytes (double)
- Target indices: $i = 4, j = 9$

**Step 1 — Apply the row-major address formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{Address}(score[4][9]) = B + ((i \times n) + j) \times w$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (1 mark):**

$$= 2000 + ((4 \times 15) + 9) \times 8$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inner product (1 mark):**

$$= 2000 + (60 + 9) \times 8 = 2000 + 69 \times 8$$

**Step 4 — Final address (1 mark):**

$$= 2000 + 552 = 2552$$

$$\boxed{\text{Address of score[4][9]} = 2552 \text{ bytes}}$$

**Step 5 — Total memory consumed (1 mark):**

$$\text{Total Memory} = m \times n \times w = 10 \times 15 \times 8 = 1200 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Memory Consumed} = 1200 \text{ bytes}}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the concept of a 1D array in C++ with a suitable example. Discuss how elements are stored in memory, and derive the address formula for `A[i]`. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of 1D Array (2 marks):**
A 1D array is a linear collection of elements of the same data type, accessed using a single index. **Example:** `int age[6] = {21, 25, 30, 18, 22, 27};` stores six integer ages.

**Step 2 — Memory Representation (2 marks):**
The compiler allocates a contiguous block of $6 \times 4 = 24$ bytes (assuming `int` is 4 bytes). The elements are stored sequentially:

| Index | `age[0]` | `age[1]` | `age[2]` | `age[3]` | `age[4]` | `age[5]` |
|-------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Value | 21 | 25 | 30 | 18 | 22 | 27 |
| Address | 1000 | 1004 | 1008 | 1012 | 1016 | 1020 |

**Step 3 — Address Derivation (2 marks):**
- Base address $B$ is the location of `age[0]`.
- Each element occupies $w$ bytes.
- The $i$-th element is $i$ positions past the base, contributing an offset of $i \times w$.

**Step 4 — Final Formula (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{Address}(A[i]) = B + i \times w}$$

---

#### (b) Compare linear search and binary search algorithms on a 1D array. Under what condition is binary search applicable? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear Search Explanation (1.5 marks):**
Linear search sequentially examines each element of the array from index $0$ to $n-1$ until the desired key is found or the array ends. It works on **both sorted and unsorted** arrays.

**Step 2 — Binary Search Explanation (1.5 marks):**
Binary search works on **sorted arrays only**. It compares the key with the **middle element** of the search range; if equal, search ends; if less, search continues in the **left half**; if greater, in the **right half**. Each iteration halves the search space.

**Step 3 — Time Complexity Comparison (2 marks):**
- Linear Search: $O(n)$ worst case.
- Binary Search: $O(\log_2 n)$ worst case.

**Step 4 — Worked Example (1 mark):**
Searching for 25 in `[10, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50]`:
- Linear: 3 comparisons (at index 2).
- Binary: 2 comparisons (mid = 30 → 25 < 30 → left half [10,20,25] → mid = 20 → 25 > 20 → right = 25 ✓).

**Step 5 — Precondition for Binary Search (1 mark):**
**The array must be sorted in ascending or descending order.** If unsorted, binary search gives incorrect results.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing row-major with column-major** — Read the question twice. C/C++/Java are row-major by default; FORTRAN/MATLAB are column-major.
> 2. **Off-by-one error in indexing** — Forgetting that the valid range is $0$ to $n-1$, not $1$ to $n$. (Deducts 0.5 to 1 mark.)
> 3. **Wrong `w` value** — Using `sizeof(int) = 2` when the system uses 4 bytes, or vice versa. Always check what the question states.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps** — The 7-mark sub-parts demand the *derivation* shown step-by-step, not just the final formula. A bare formula with no derivation will fetch **at most 2 of 7 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — Always append "bytes" after a numerical address.
> 6. **Out-of-bounds discussion** — Examiners love it when you mention that **C/C++ does NOT perform runtime bounds checking**, while Java does.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Arrays (Module 1, PEEIT741)**

- **Definition:** A fixed-size, homogeneous, contiguous collection accessed by integer index.
- **Index Range:** Valid range is $0$ to $n - 1$; accessing outside this is **undefined behavior** in C/C++.
- **Base Address (`B`):** Memory location of the first element, `A[0]`.
- **Element Size (`w`):** Size in bytes of one element (`int = 4`, `char = 1`, `double = 8`, etc.).
- **1D Address Formula:** $B + i \cdot w$
- **2D Row-Major Formula:** $B + ((i \cdot n) + j) \cdot w$
- **2D Column-Major Formula:** $B + ((j \cdot m) + i) \cdot w$
- **Total Memory:** $m \times n \times w$ (bytes)
- **Storage Convention:** C, C++, Java, Python → **Row-Major**; FORTRAN, MATLAB → **Column-Major**.
- **Array Traversal:** Use a `for` loop from $i = 0$ to $i < n$.
