- **Time Complexities:** Access = $O(1)$; Traversal = $O(n)$; Linear Search = $O(n)$; Binary Search = $O(\log n)$ (sorted only).
- **Types of Arrays:** 1D, 2D, Multi-dimensional; Static vs Dynamic (`std::vector`, `ArrayList`).
- **Initialization:** `int a[5] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};` — partial init fills the rest with `0`.
- **Bounds Checking:** C/C++ = NO; Java = YES (`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`).
- **Engineering Use Cases:** DSP sample buffers, image pixel grids, ML feature vectors, lookup tables, finite-element matrices.
- **Foundation For:** Strings, Matrices, Stacks, Queues, Hash Tables, Heaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always carry a clear derivation of the **row-major address formula** in your answer sheet. Pair the derivation with a small **diagram showing contiguous memory** — examiners reward visual clarity with 1–2 grace marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)**, a **String** is defined as an **immutable, sequential collection of Unicode characters** represented as an **instance of the `java.lang.String` class** (in Java) or the `std::string` class (in C++). In KTU's OOP curriculum (typically Java-based), a String is fundamentally a **user-friendly wrapper over a `char[]` array** that is **deliberately made immutable** to ensure security, thread-safety, caching (via the **String Constant Pool**), and reliable behavior when used as a key in hash-based data structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Verbatim-Ready):**
> *"A String is a built-in, non-primitive, reference-type object in Java that represents an immutable sequence of characters stored internally as a `char[]` (or `byte[]` in compact strings, Java 9+), allocated on the heap and managed via the String Constant Pool for literal-based memory optimization."*

### Key Properties of Strings (as per KTU OOP Syllabus)
| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Package** | `java.lang` (auto-imported) |
| **Mutability** | **Immutable** (cannot be modified after creation) |
| **Underlying Storage** | `char[]` / `byte[]` (JDK 9+ Compact Strings) |
| **Special Status** | Can be created **without `new`** (using literals) |
| **Implements** | `Serializable`, `Comparable<String>`, `CharSequence` |
| **Default Value** | `null` (since it is a reference type) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Necklace of Beads"

Imagine a **String** as a **sealed necklace of alphabet-beads** 🔤.

- Each **bead** = a single character (`'a'`, `'b'`, `'1'`, `'@'`).
- The **necklace string is sealed** = once tied, you **cannot add, remove, or rearrange beads**. This is **immutability**.
- If you want a *different* arrangement, you must build a **brand-new necklace**. The old one is discarded (or garbage-collected).
- The **String Pool** is like a **public showcase rack** in a jewellery shop: when you ask for a necklace spelling `"HELLO"`, the shopkeeper first checks the rack. If it exists, he hands you the **same reference** (no new necklace made). If not, he creates a new one and places it on the rack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why immutability matters (Intuition):**
> 1. **Security** — Passwords, file paths, and URLs are Strings. You don't want them silently changed.
> 2. **Hashing** — Strings are used as keys in `HashMap` / `HashSet`. A changing key would corrupt the hash table.
> 3. **Thread-Safety** — Immutable objects are automatically safe across multiple threads.
> 4. **Caching** — The String Pool can safely share one `"hello"` among thousands of variables.

---

## 1.3 Primitive vs. Object Nature of Strings

Although Strings *look* like primitives (you can write `String s = "Hello";` without `new`), they are **full-fledged objects**. Every operation on a String is a **method call**, and every String literal creates an object on the heap.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Trick Question:**
> *"Is String a primitive data type in Java?"*
> **Answer:** **No.** Java has exactly **8 primitives** (`byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean`). String is a **reference type** declared in `java.lang`, but Java provides **special syntax sugar** (literals, `+` operator overloading) to make it *feel* like a primitive.

---

## 1.4 The Two Ways to Create a String

```java
// Method 1: String Literal (uses String Pool)
String s1 = "Hello KTU";

// Method 2: Using 'new' keyword (forces new object in heap)
String s2 = new String("Hello KTU");
```

**Critical Distinction:**
- `s1` may point to an **existing pooled object** (memory efficient).
- `s2` **always creates a brand-new object on the heap**, even if `"Hello KTU"` already exists in the pool.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a String — Internal Memory Layout

A Java `String` object internally contains three fields (simplified pre-Java 9 view):

| Internal Field | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `char[] value` | The actual character array storing the data |
| `int hash` | Cached hash code (lazy-computed, defaults to `0`) |
| `int hash32` | Alternative 32-bit hash (Java 11+) for faster hashtables |

From **Java 9 onwards**, strings use a **Compact Strings** representation: a `byte[]` array plus a `byte coder` field (`LATIN1` or `UTF16`) to save memory when characters are all ASCII.

---

## 2.2 The String Constant Pool (SCP) — Deep Dive

The **String Constant Pool** is a special region inside the **Method Area / Metaspace** (historically part of the heap) where **unique String literals are stored**.

### Logic Steps — How a String Literal is Resolved

1. **Step 1 — Literal Encountered:** The compiler encounters `"Hello"` in your source code.
2. **Step 2 — Pool Lookup:** At class loading, the JVM checks the SCP for an identical literal.
3. **Step 3 — Reuse or Insert:**
   - If a `"Hello"` already exists → **reuse the same reference** (no new object).
   - If not → **create a new object**, intern it, and store the reference in the SCP.
4. **Step 4 — Return Reference:** The variable is assigned the SCP reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`intern()` Method:** You can manually force a heap-allocated String into the SCP using `s2.intern();`. After interning, `s1 == s2` becomes `true` (reference equality holds).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — String Methods Reference Table

> ⚠️ **Markdown Safety Note:** Vertical pipes inside tables are escaped as `\vert` to prevent syntax breakage.

| # | Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose / Formula Equivalent | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `length()` | `int` | Returns number of characters: $L = \vert s \vert$ | `"KTU".length()` → `3` |
| 2 | `charAt(int i)` | `char` | Returns character at index $i$ (0-based) | `"Java".charAt(2)` → `'v'` |
| 3 | `substring(int begin)` | `String` | Returns substring from $i$ to end | `"Hello".substring(2)` → `"llo"` |
| 4 | `substring(int begin, int end)` | `String` | Returns substring from $i$ to $end-1$ | `"Hello".substring(1,4)` → `"ell"` |
| 5 | `concat(String s)` | `String` | Concatenates: $r = s_1 \oplus s_2$ | `"Hi".concat(" KTU")` → `"Hi KTU"` |
| 6 | `equals(Object o)` | `boolean` | Content comparison (overrides `Object.equals`) | `"a".equals("a")` → `true` |
| 7 | `equalsIgnoreCase(String s)` | `boolean` | Case-insensitive content compare | `"JAVA".equalsIgnoreCase("java")` → `true` |
| 8 | `compareTo(String s)` | `int` | Lexicographic difference: $r = s_1 - s_2$ | `"a".compareTo("b")` → `-1` |
| 9 | `indexOf(String s)` | `int` | First index of substring, $-1$ if absent | `"hello".indexOf("ll")` → `2` |
| 10 | `lastIndexOf(String s)` | `int` | Last index of substring | `"hello".lastIndexOf("l")` → `3` |
| 11 | `toUpperCase()` | `String` | Returns uppercase copy | `"ktu".toUpperCase()` → `"KTU"` |
| 12 | `toLowerCase()` | `String` | Returns lowercase copy | `"KTU".toLowerCase()` → `"ktu"` |
| 13 | `trim()` | `String` | Removes leading/trailing whitespace | `"  hi  ".trim()` → `"hi"` |
| 14 | `replace(char o, char n)` | `String` | Replaces all occurrences | `"aba".replace('a','x')` → `"xbx"` |
| 15 | `split(String regex)` | `String[]` | Splits via regex | `"a,b,c".split(",")` → `["a","b","c"]` |
| 16 | `startsWith(String p)` | `boolean` | Tests prefix | `"Java".startsWith("Ja")` → `true` |
| 17 | `endsWith(String s)` | `boolean` | Tests suffix | `"file.txt".endsWith(".txt")` → `true` |
| 18 | `isEmpty()` | `boolean` | True if $L = 0$ | `"".isEmpty()` → `true` |
| 19 | `intern()` | `String` | Returns canonical pool reference | `s2.intern()` |
| 20 | `valueOf(int/long/etc.)` | `String` | Static converter: $s = \text{String}(n)$ | `String.valueOf(42)` → `"42"` |

---

## 2.4 String Immutability — The "Why" Behind It

When you write `s = s.concat(" KTU");`, you are **NOT modifying** the original `"Hello"`. You are:
1. Creating a **new String object** `"Hello KTU"`.
2. Pointing the reference `s` to the new object.
3. The old `"Hello"` becomes eligible for **garbage collection** (if not pooled/referenced elsewhere).

**Mathematical Analogy of Immutability:**
Let $s_0$ be the initial string. Every "modification" produces a *new* string $s_{n+1} = f(s_n)$, but $s_n$ itself never changes:
$$s_0 \xrightarrow{f_1} s_1 \xrightarrow{f_2} s_2 \xrightarrow{f_3} \cdots \xrightarrow{f_n} s_n$$

The original $s_0$ remains untouched in memory until garbage collected.

---

## 2.5 StringBuffer vs. StringBuilder vs. String

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU board exams. Master the comparison:

| Feature | `String` | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mutability** | Immutable | **Mutable** | **Mutable** |
| **Thread-Safety** | Yes (immutable ⇒ inherently safe) | **Yes** (synchronized methods) | **No** (not synchronized) |
| **Performance** | Slow for repeated edits (creates new objects) | Slower (sync overhead) | **Fastest** (no sync overhead) |
| **Storage** | String Pool / Heap | Heap only | Heap only |
| **Introduced In** | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.0 | **JDK 1.5** |
| **Use Case** | Fixed text (names, keys) | Multi-threaded string editing | Single-threaded string editing (loops, builders) |
| **Capacity Behavior** | Always fits exact length | Grows: $\text{newCap} = (oldCap \times 2) + 2$ | Same growth formula as StringBuffer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - **String** → Storing configuration values, user input, JSON keys.
> - **StringBuilder** → Building log messages, SQL queries, JSON responses in high-performance web servers.
> - **StringBuffer** → Shared mutable buffers in legacy multi-threaded parsers (rarely used today).

---

## 2.6 String Concatenation Internals — The `+` Operator

Although `+` looks like an operator, the compiler **translates it** into a `StringBuilder` (or `invokedynamic` with `StringConcatFactory` in Java 9+).

**Source:**
```java
String result = "Total: " + 42 + " items";
```

**Compiler-Translated Equivalent (Java 8 and earlier):**
```java
String result = new StringBuilder()
                     .append("Total: ")
                     .append(42)
                     .append(" items")
                     .toString();
```

**Performance Implication:** Using `+` inside a **loop** creates a new `StringBuilder` *per iteration* — **O(n²)** complexity. Fix:
```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) sb.append(i);
String result = sb.toString();   // O(n) total
```

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since this is an OOP topic, "derivations" translate to **exhaustive program execution traces** showing step-by-step memory behavior, object creation, and output prediction — exactly the kind of problems KTU examiners set.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Trace #1: String Pool vs. `new` Keyword

### Problem Code
```java
public class StringPoolDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s1 = "Hello KTU";
        String s2 = "Hello KTU";
        String s3 = new String("Hello KTU");
        String s4 = s3.intern();

        System.out.println(s1 == s2);     // Line A
        System.out.println(s1 == s3);     // Line B
        System.out.println(s1 == s4);     // Line C
        System.out.println(s1.equals(s3));// Line D
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Memory Trace

**Step 1 — `String s1 = "Hello KTU";`**
- Compiler sees literal `"Hello KTU"`. SCP is empty. JVM creates a new String object `Obj_Hello_KTU` in the **SCP** (inside heap). `s1` → `Obj_Hello_KTU`.

**Step 2 — `String s2 = "Hello KTU";`**
- Compiler sees literal `"Hello KTU"`. JVM checks SCP → already present. `s2` → **same** `Obj_Hello_KTU`. No new object created.

**Step 3 — `String s3 = new String("Hello KTU");`**
- The `new` keyword **forces** creation of a new object in the **general heap** (outside SCP). Call it `Obj_New`. `s3` → `Obj_New`.
- The literal `"Hello KTU"` argument still causes SCP lookup, but the new object itself is in regular heap.

**Step 4 — `String s4 = s3.intern();`**
- `s3.intern()` returns the SCP reference. `s4` → `Obj_Hello_KTU` (the pooled one).

**Step 5 — Evaluate `Line A`: `s1 == s2`**
- Both reference `Obj_Hello_KTU` (same memory address).
- `$s_1 == s_2 \Rightarrow \text{true}$

**Step 6 — Evaluate `Line B`: `s1 == s3`**
- `s1` → SCP object. `s3` → distinct heap object. Different addresses.
- $s_1 == s_3 \Rightarrow \text{false}$

**Step 7 — Evaluate `Line C`: `s1 == s4`**
- `s4` was interned to the SCP, so both point to `Obj_Hello_KTU`.
- $s_1 == s_4 \Rightarrow \text{true}$

**Step 8 — Evaluate `Line D`: `s1.equals(s3)`**
- `.equals()` compares **content** character-by-character, not reference. Both contain `"Hello KTU"`.
- $\text{equals}(s_1, s_3) \Rightarrow \text{true}$

### Final Output
```
true
false
true
true
```

### Memory Diagram (ASCII Representation)
```
    STRING CONSTANT POOL              GENERAL HEAP
    ┌────────────────────┐           ┌────────────────────┐
    │  Obj_Hello_KTU     │◄────s1    │  Obj_New           │◄── s3
    │  "Hello KTU"       │◄────s2    │  "Hello KTU"       │
    │                    │◄────s4    │                    │
    └────────────────────┘           └────────────────────┘
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Trace #2: Immutability Proof

### Problem Code
```java
public class ImmutabilityDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s = "Java";
        s.concat(" KTU");
        System.out.println(s);          // Line X

        s = s.concat(" 2024");
        System.out.println(s);          // Line Y
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Trace

**Step 1 — `String s = "Java";`**
- SCP gets `Obj_Java`. `s` → `Obj_Java`.

**Step 2 — `s.concat(" KTU");`**
- `concat()` creates a **new** object `Obj_Java_KTU` in the heap, but the return value is **discarded** (not assigned).
- `s` **still points to** `Obj_Java` (immutability preserved).
- Garbage collector will eventually reclaim `Obj_Java_KTU`.

**Step 3 — `Line X`: `System.out.println(s);`**
- Prints value at `Obj_Java` → `"Java"`.

**Step 4 — `s = s.concat(" 2024");`**
- Creates new object `Obj_Java_2024`. **Reassigns** `s` → `Obj_Java_2024`. Original `Obj_Java` untouched in SCP.

**Step 5 — `Line Y`: `System.out.println(s);`**
- Prints value at `Obj_Java_2024` → `"Java 2024"`.

### Final Output
```
Java
Java 2024
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The line `s.concat(" KTU");` is the **#1 KTU exam trap**. Students assume `s` becomes `"Java KTU"`. It does not — the result is **discarded** because `concat()` returns a *new* String but does not modify the original.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Trace #3: `==` vs. `equals()` Deep Dive

### Problem Code
```java
public class EqualityDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String a = "KTU";
        String b = "KTU";
        String c = new String("KTU");
        String d = c;

        System.out.println(a == b);         // (1)
        System.out.println(a == c);         // (2)
        System.out.println(c == d);         // (3)
        System.out.println(a.equals(c));    // (4)
        System.out.println(a.compareTo(c)); // (5)
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

| Line | Expression | Memory Logic | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (1) | `a == b` | Both → SCP `"KTU"` (same reference) | `true` |
| (2) | `a == c` | `a` → SCP, `c` → new heap obj (different reference) | `false` |
| (3) | `c == d` | `d = c` copies reference; both → same heap obj | `true` |
| (4) | `a.equals(c)` | Content compare: both = `"KTU"` | `true` |
| (5) | `a.compareTo(c)` | Lexicographic: `"KTU"` vs `"KTU"` → $0$ | `0` |

### Mathematical Justification of `compareTo`
$$\text{compareTo}(s_1, s_2) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } s_1 = s_2 \text{ (lexicographically equal)} \\ \text{negative} & \text{if } s_1 < s_2 \\ \text{positive} & \text{if } s_1 > s_2 \end{cases}$$

Where comparison is **character-by-character** using Unicode code points:
$$\text{diff} = s_1.\text{charAt}(i) - s_2.\text{charAt}(i) \text{ for first } i \text{ where characters differ}$$

For `"KTU"` vs `"KTU"`, no differing index exists → return $0$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Trace #4: StringBuilder Concatenation in a Loop

### Problem Code
```java
public class BuilderDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Hello");
        sb.append(' ').append("KTU").append(' ').append(2024);
        System.out.println(sb.toString());
        System.out.println(sb.length());
        System.out.println(sb.reverse().toString());
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Trace

**Step 1 — `new StringBuilder("Hello")`**
- Internal buffer capacity: $\text{cap} = (\text{old} \times 2) + 2 = (5 \times 2) + 2 = 12$
- Buffer state: `['H','e','l','l','o', _, _, _, _, _, _, _]` (underscore = unused)
- `count` (length) = $5$

**Step 2 — `sb.append(' ')`**
- Buffer: `['H','e','l','l','o',' ', _, _, _, _, _, _]`, count = $6$

**Step 3 — `sb.append("KTU")`**
- Buffer: `['H','e','l','l','o',' ','K','T','U', _, _, _]`, count = $9$

**Step 4 — `sb.append(' ')`**
- count = $10$

**Step 5 — `sb.append(2024)`**
- `append(int)` calls `Integer.toString(2024)` → `"2024"`
- Buffer: `['H','e','l','l','o',' ','K','T','U',' ','2','0']` — capacity exceeded!
- **Auto-grow:** newCap = $(12 \times 2) + 2 = 26$
- Copy buffer → new array of size 26, then append `'2','2','4'`
- Final buffer: `['H','e','l','l','o',' ','K','T','U',' ','2','0','2','4', _, _, ...]`
- count = $14$

**Step 6 — `sb.toString()`**
- Returns new String `"Hello KTU 2024"`.

**Step 7 — `sb.length()`** → returns `count` = $14$.

**Step 8 — `sb.reverse()`**
- Mutates buffer in place: `['4','2','0','2',' ','U','T','K',' ','o','l','l','e','H', ...]`
- Returns same `sb` reference. `.toString()` → `"4202 UTK olleH"`

### Final Output
```
Hello KTU 2024
14
4202 UTK olleH
```

---

## 3.5 Analytical Trace #5: StringTokenizer / `split()` Operation

### Problem Code
```java
public class TokenizeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String csv = "Apple,Banana,Cherry,Date";
        String[] tokens = csv.split(",");

        for (String t : tokens) {
            System.out.println("[" + t + "]");
        }

        // Count tokens
        System.out.println("Count: " + tokens.length);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Iteration | `t` value | Printed Output |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `"Apple"` | `[Apple]` |
| 2 | `"Banana"` | `[Banana]` |
| 3 | `"Cherry"` | `[Cherry]` |
| 4 | `"Date"` | `[Date]` |

**Token Count:** $n = 4$ (delimiter `,` is removed, producing 4 segments).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical View:** For a string of length $L$ with $k$ delimiter occurrences, `split()` produces at most $k+1$ tokens:
> $$N_{\text{tokens}} \leq k + 1$$
> Adjacent delimiters produce empty strings (e.g., `"a,,b".split(",")` → `["a", "", "b"]`).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — String Internal Class Hierarchy


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Q: Explain the concept of String immutability in Java. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** String immutability means that once a `String` object is created, its content **cannot be modified**. Any operation that appears to "change" a String (e.g., `concat()`, `replace()`, `toUpperCase()`) actually returns a **new String object**, leaving the original untouched.

**Importance (2 marks):**
1. **Security:** Strings store sensitive data (passwords, file paths, URLs). Immutability prevents malicious modification.
2. **Thread-Safety:** Immutable objects are inherently safe to share across multiple threads without synchronization.
3. **Hashing Reliability:** Strings are widely used as keys in `HashMap`/`HashSet`. A changing key would corrupt the hash table — immutability guarantees stable `hashCode()`.
4. **String Pool Optimization:** The JVM can safely share one pooled instance among multiple references because the content can never change.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Q: Differentiate between `String`, `StringBuffer`, and `StringBuilder` in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `String` | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mutability | Immutable | Mutable | Mutable |
| Thread-Safety | Inherently safe | Synchronized (thread-safe) | Not synchronized |
| Speed | Slow for edits | Moderate | Fastest |
| Storage | SCP / Heap | Heap only | Heap only |
| Introduced | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.5 |

(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for stating when to use each, 1 mark for example code)

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module questions have **internal choice**. You must answer **either Option A or Option B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Module 1]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the String Constant Pool in Java with a suitable diagram. How does the `intern()` method work? Discuss the difference between `==` and `.equals()` for String comparison. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Write a Java program to perform the following String operations without using built-in functions: (i) Find the length of a String, (ii) Reverse a String, (iii) Check whether a String is a palindrome. Use `StringBuffer` to demonstrate reverse. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**1. String Constant Pool (SCP) — 2 marks**

The **String Constant Pool** is a special memory region inside the JVM (in the heap, near the metaspace) that stores **unique String literals**. When a String literal is encountered:
- The JVM checks the SCP for an identical value.
- If present, the **same reference** is reused (memory optimization).
- If absent, a new String object is created and added to the SCP.

**2. The `intern()` Method — 1 mark**

`intern()` is an instance method that returns the **canonical (pooled) representation** of a String.
```java
String s1 = new String("Hello");  // heap object
String s2 = s1.intern();           // pool reference
String s3 = "Hello";               // pool reference
System.out.println(s2 == s3);      // true — both point to SCP
```

**3. `==` vs. `.equals()` — 2 marks**

| Operator | Compares | Example | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `==` | **Reference** (memory address) | `new String("a") == new String("a")` | `false` |
| `.equals()` | **Content** (character sequence) | `new String("a").equals(new String("a"))` | `true` |

**4. Diagram — 2 marks** (See SECTION 4.1 for the full mermaid SCP diagram)

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A(b) [7 marks]

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class StringOps {
    // (i) Length without using length() — 2 marks
    static int findLength(String s) {
        int count = 0;
        try {
            while (true) {
                s.charAt(count);  // throws exception when out of bounds
                count++;
            }
        } catch (StringIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            return count;
        }
    }

    // (ii) Reverse using StringBuffer — 3 marks
    static String reverseString(String s) {
        StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer(s);
        return sb.reverse().toString();
    }

    // (iii) Palindrome check — 2 marks
    static boolean isPalindrome(String s) {
        String reversed = reverseString(s);
        return s.equals(reversed);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a string: ");
        String input = sc.nextLine();

        System.out.println("Length: " + findLength(input));
        System.out.println("Reversed: " + reverseString(input));
        System.out.println("Is Palindrome? " + isPalindrome(input));
    }
}
```

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter a string: malayalam
Length: 9
Reversed: malayalam
Is Palindrome? true
```

**Explanation Notes:**
- `findLength()` uses `charAt()` in a loop and catches the boundary exception (2 marks).
- `reverseString()` delegates to `StringBuffer.reverse()` (3 marks).
- `isPalindrome()` compares the original with the reversed using `.equals()` (2 marks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Module 1]`

**Q: (a)** What is the difference between `String`, `StringBuilder`, and `StringBuffer`? Write a Java program using `StringBuilder` to count the number of vowels, consonants, digits, and special characters in a given String. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain method overloading with respect to the `String` class methods. Write a program to demonstrate `substring()`, `concat()`, `equals()`, `compareTo()`, and `valueOf()` methods with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Comparison — 3 marks** (Same table as Question A2 above, with example use-cases.)

**Java Program — 4 marks:**
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class CharCategory {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a string: ");
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(sc.nextLine());

        int vowels = 0, consonants = 0, digits = 0, specials = 0;

        for (int i = 0; i < sb.length(); i++) {
            char c = sb.charAt(i);
            if (Character.isLetter(c)) {
                char lower = Character.toLowerCase(c);
                if (lower == 'a' || lower == 'e' || lower == 'i' ||
                    lower == 'o' || lower == 'u') {
                    vowels++;
                } else {
                    consonants++;
                }
            } else if (Character.isDigit(c)) {
                digits++;
            } else {
                specials++;
            }
        }

        System.out.println("Vowels: " + vowels);
        System.out.println("Consonants: " + consonants);
        System.out.println("Digits: " + digits);
        System.out.println("Special Characters: " + specials);
    }
}
```

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter a string: KTU@2024#Exam
Vowels: 3
Consonants: 6
Digits: 4
Special Characters: 2
```

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Method Overloading Concept (1 mark):**
The `String` class demonstrates **method overloading** by providing multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists**. Examples include `substring(int)`, `substring(int, int)`, `valueOf(int)`, `valueOf(double)`, `valueOf(char[])`, etc.

**Java Program (6 marks):**
```java
public class StringMethodsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s1 = "Hello";
        String s2 = "World";

        // 1. substring() - overloaded: (int) and (int, int)
        System.out.println("substring(2): " + s1.substring(2));        // "llo"
        System.out.println("substring(1,4): " + s1.substring(1, 4));  // "ell"

        // 2. concat()
        String joined = s1.concat(" ").concat(s2);
        System.out.println("concat: " + joined);                        // "Hello World"

        // 3. equals() vs ==
        String s3 = new String("Hello");
        System.out.println("s1 == s3: " + (s1 == s3));                  // false
        System.out.println("s1.equals(s3): " + s1.equals(s3));          // true

        // 4. compareTo()
        System.out.println("compareTo: " + s1.compareTo(s2));           // negative
        System.out.println("compareTo same: " + s1.compareTo("Hello")); // 0

        // 5. valueOf() - overloaded for all primitive types
        System.out.println("valueOf(int): " + String.valueOf(42));
        System.out.println("valueOf(double): " + String.valueOf(3.14));
        System.out.println("valueOf(boolean): " + String.valueOf(true));
        System.out.println("valueOf(char[]): " + String.valueOf(new char[]{'A','B','C'}));
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
substring(2): llo
substring(1,4): ell
concat: Hello World
s1 == s3: false
s1.equals(s3): true
compareTo: -15
compareTo same: 0
valueOf(int): 42
valueOf(double): 3.14
valueOf(boolean): true
valueOf(char[]): ABC
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `concat()` returns a new String** — writing `s.concat(" KTU")` without reassigning and assuming `s` changed. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 2. **Using `==` instead of `.equals()` for content comparison** — KTU examiners are *very strict* about this. Always use `.equals()` for String content. **−1 mark per instance.**
> 3. **Confusing `length()` (String method) with `length` (array property)** — String is an object, so it uses the method `length()`. Arrays use the property `arr.length`. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Ignoring String Pool in tracing problems** — When asked "what is the output of `s1 == s2`?", always state whether the literals are pooled or `new` was used. **−2 marks for incomplete reasoning.**
> 5. **Not knowing `compareTo()` returns 0 for equal strings** — Writing `"a".compareTo("a") == 1` will cost you. It returns **`0`**, not `1` or `true`. **−1 mark.**
> 6. **Mutating a String directly in a loop** — Using `s += "x"` inside a 1000-iteration loop instead of `StringBuilder` shows lack of performance awareness. **−1 mark (under "best practices").**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **String is immutable** — every "modification" returns a new object.
- [x] **String is in `java.lang`** — auto-imported, no need for `import`.
- [x] **String is NOT a primitive** — it is a reference type with **8 primitive siblings only**.
- [x] **Two creation methods:** `"literal"` (uses SCP) vs `new String("...")` (heap).
- [x] **String Pool (SCP)** stores unique literals — same literal → same reference.
- [x] **`==` compares references** (memory addresses); **`.equals()` compares content** (characters).
- [x] **`.equalsIgnoreCase()`** is the case-insensitive version of `.equals()`.
- [x] **`compareTo()`** returns `int`: negative / zero / positive (not boolean).
- [x] **`intern()`** moves a heap String into the SCP and returns the canonical reference.
- [x] **`charAt(i)`** is 0-indexed; `s.charAt(s.length())` throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
- [x] **`substring(begin, end)`** — `end` index is **exclusive** (`end - 1` is included).
- [x] **`split(regex)`** returns `String[]`; adjacent delimiters produce empty tokens.
- [x] **`StringBuilder` is fastest** for single-threaded mutable string work.
- [x] **`StringBuffer` is synchronized** — thread-safe but slower.
- [x] **`+` operator is sugar** — compiler converts to `StringBuilder` (or `StringConcatFactory` in Java 9+).
- [x] **Avoid `+` in loops** — use `StringBuilder` explicitly to avoid O(n²) complexity.
- [x] **`StringTokenizer`** is a legacy class; modern code prefers `String.split()`.
- [x] **Java 9+ Compact Strings** use `byte[]` + `coder` field instead of `char[]` for memory efficiency.
- [x] **Empty String `""` is NOT the same as `null`** — `null` means "no object"; `""` is an object of length 0.
- [x] **`length()`** is a method (`s.length()`), not a property — unlike arrays (`arr.length`).
- [x] **String implements `CharSequence`** — also implemented by `StringBuffer` and `StringBuilder`.

---

**End of Module 1 — Strings Notes**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Operators in Java

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
An **operator** in Java is a special symbol consisting of one or more characters that performs a specific operation on one, two, or three operands, and returns a result. The combination of an operator and its operands forms an **expression**. Operators are the fundamental building blocks that allow the programmer to manipulate data and produce new values from existing ones.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** *"Operators are special symbols that perform operations on operands and produce a result. Java provides a rich set of operators classified based on the number of operands they accept (unary, binary, ternary) and based on the functionality they provide."*

### Classification of Operators by Operand Count

| Category | Operand Count | Example Operators | Notation |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|----------|
| **Unary Operators** | 1 | `+`, `-`, `++`, `--`, `!`, `~` | `op x` or `x op` |
| **Binary Operators** | 2 | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`, `==`, `&&` | `x op y` |
| **Ternary Operator** | 3 | `? :` | `x ? y : z` |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Operator as a Machine"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an operator as a **machine in a factory**.
> - The **operands** are the **raw materials** fed into the machine.
> - The **operator symbol** is the **machine itself** that transforms the materials.
> - The **result** is the **finished product** that comes out.
>
> For example, the addition operator `+` is like a **mixing machine** that takes two ingredients (operands) and produces a blended product (sum). Just as a factory has different machines for cutting, polishing, and assembling, Java has different operators for arithmetic, comparison, and logic.

---

## 1.2 Control Statements

**Formal Definition:**
**Control statements** in Java are statements that alter the normal sequential flow of program execution. By default, a Java program executes statements from top to bottom, one after another. Control statements allow the programmer to introduce **decision-making**, **repetition (looping)**, and **branching** into the program, thereby enabling the construction of complex, real-world algorithmic logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's View:** Control statements are the **"traffic signals"** of a program. Without them, the program is a one-way street that always moves forward. With them, the program can take detours, stop, or loop back.

### Three Major Categories of Control Statements

1. **Selection (Decision-Making) Statements** — `if`, `if-else`, `if-else-if`, `switch`, ternary
2. **Iteration (Looping) Statements** — `for`, `while`, `do-while`, enhanced `for`
3. **Jump (Transfer-of-Control) Statements** — `break`, `continue`, `return`, `goto` (deprecated)

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Control Flow as a Train Journey"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a train traveling from Station A to Station B.
> - **Sequential flow** is a train moving straight on a single track with no switches.
> - **Selection statements** are **railway junctions** where the track splits, and a signalman decides which branch the train takes based on conditions (e.g., if passenger count > 50, go express; else, go local).
> - **Iteration statements** are **circular tracks** or **shuttle loops** where the train keeps going around a section until a condition is met.
> - **Jump statements** are **emergency exits** or **shortcuts** that allow the train to abandon its current track and move to another.

---

## 1.3 Selection Statements

**Formal Definition:**
**Selection statements** (also called **decision-making** or **branching statements**) are control statements that evaluate a Boolean condition and execute one of two or more possible code blocks based on whether the condition evaluates to `true` or `false`. They form the cognitive core of any non-trivial algorithm.

### The Five Primary Selection Constructs in Java

1. **`if` statement** — Single-path conditional execution
2. **`if-else` statement** — Two-path conditional execution
3. **`if-else-if` ladder** — Multi-path conditional execution
4. **Nested `if`** — Conditional execution within conditional execution
5. **`switch` statement** — Multi-way branching based on discrete values

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Selection as a Restaurant Menu"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Walking into a restaurant and looking at the menu is a perfect analogy for selection statements.
> - **`if`**: "If I am very hungry, I will order the large meal." (One option, no alternative)
> - **`if-else`**: "If I have cash, I will pay cash; else, I will pay by card." (Two exclusive options)
> - **`if-else-if` ladder**: "If I am vegetarian, I order pasta; else if I want spicy food, I order curry; else if I am on a diet, I order salad; else, I order the chef's special." (Multiple cascading options)
> - **`switch`**: "I will choose from items numbered 1 through 10 on the menu, and the kitchen will prepare item #3." (Discrete case selection)

### Why Selection Statements Matter in OOP

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** In Object-Oriented Programming, selection statements are used to:
> - Implement **business logic** (e.g., discount eligibility, loan approval)
> - Handle **exception-like conditions** (e.g., input validation)
> - Enable **polymorphism decisions** (e.g., choosing the correct subclass method at runtime)
> - Control **menu-driven programs** (a common KTU lab exam pattern)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Operator Classification in Java

Java provides **~44 distinct operators** organized into **8 functional categories** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. Below is the exhaustive academic analysis.

### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Operators

Arithmetic operators perform mathematical operations on numeric operands.

$$\text{Operands must be numeric types: } byte, short, int, long, float, double, char$$

| Operator | Name | Operation | Example | Result |
|----------|------|-----------|---------|--------|
| `+` | Addition | Adds two operands | `7 + 3` | `10` |
| `-` | Subtraction | Subtracts right from left | `7 - 3` | `4` |
| `*` | Multiplication | Multiplies two operands | `7 * 3` | `21` |
| `/` | Division | Quotient of division | `7 / 3` | `2` (integer) |
| `%` | Modulus | Remainder of division | `7 % 3` | `1` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integer Division Truncation Rule:** When both operands of `/` or `%` are integers, the result is an **integer** — the fractional part is **truncated (rounded toward zero)**, NOT rounded to the nearest integer. This is a common KTU exam pitfall.

### 2.1.2 Unary Operators

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|----------|---------|---------|--------|
| `+` | Unary plus (positive value) | `+a` | Positive `a` |
| `-` | Unary minus (negation) | `-a` | Negated `a` |
| `++` | Pre-increment / Post-increment | `++a` or `a++` | `a` + 1 |
| `--` | Pre-decrement / Post-decrement | `--a` or `a--` | `a` - 1 |
| `!` | Logical NOT | `!true` | `false` |
| `~` | Bitwise NOT (one's complement) | `~5` | `-6` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre vs. Post Increment (KTU Favorite Question):**
> - `++a` (Pre-increment): Increment `a` first, **then use the new value** in the expression.
> - `a++` (Post-increment): Use the **current value** of `a` in the expression, **then increment** it.

### 2.1.3 Relational (Comparison) Operators

Relational operators always return a **boolean** result (`true` or `false`).

$$\text{Result type: } boolean \quad \text{(Java does NOT return 0/1 like C/C++)}$$

| Operator | Name | Example | Returns `true` when |
|----------|------|---------|---------------------|
| `==` | Equal to | `a == b` | `a` equals `b` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `a != b` | `a` differs from `b` |
| `<` | Less than | `a < b` | `a` is smaller |
| `>` | Greater than | `a > b` | `a` is larger |
| `<=` | Less than or equal to | `a <= b` | `a` is smaller or equal |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal to | `a >= b` | `a` is larger or equal |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Frequency Trap:** Students often confuse `=` (assignment) with `==` (comparison). Writing `if (a = 5)` instead of `if (a == 5)` is a **compilation error in Java** because `5` is not a boolean, unlike C/C++.

### 2.1.4 Logical Operators

Logical operators work with **boolean operands** and return **boolean** results.

| Operator | Name | Short-Circuit? | Truth Table Summary |
|----------|------|----------------|---------------------|
| `&&` | Logical AND | ✅ Yes | `true` only if **both** operands are `true` |
| `\vert\vert` | Logical OR | ✅ Yes | `true` if **at least one** operand is `true` |
| `!` | Logical NOT | ❌ No | Inverts the boolean value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short-Circuit Evaluation (KTU Board-Favorite):**
> - In `A && B`: If `A` is `false`, `B` is **NOT evaluated** (short-circuited).
> - In `A || B`: If `A` is `true`, `B` is **NOT evaluated** (short-circuited).
> - This is critical for avoiding exceptions like `NullPointerException` (e.g., `obj != null && obj.method()`).

### 2.1.5 Bitwise Operators

| Operator | Name | Example (a=5, b=3) | Binary Operation |
|----------|------|--------------------|--------------------|
| `&` | Bitwise AND | `a & b` = `1` | `0101 & 0011 = 0001` |
| $\vert$ | Bitwise OR | `a \vert b` = `7` | `0101 \vert 0011 = 0111` |
| `^` | Bitwise XOR | `a ^ b` = `6` | `0101 ^ 0011 = 0110` |
| `~` | Bitwise NOT | `~a` = `-6` | `~00000101 = 11111010` |
| `<<` | Left shift | `a << 1` = `10` | `0101 → 1010` |
| `>>` | Right shift (signed) | `a >> 1` = `2` | `0101 → 0010` |
| `>>>` | Unsigned right shift | `-5 >>> 1` = large positive | Fills with 0s |

### 2.1.6 Assignment and Compound Assignment Operators

$$x \text{ op}= y \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad x = x \text{ op } y$$

| Operator | Expansion |
|----------|-----------|
| `+=` | `a = a + b` |
| `-=` | `a = a - b` |
| `*=` | `a = a * b` |
| `/=` | `a = a / b` |
| `%=` | `a = a % b` |
| `&=` | `a = a & b` |
| $\vert=$ | `a = a \vert b` |
| `^=` | `a = a ^ b` |
| `<<=` | `a = a << b` |
| `>>=` | `a = a >> b` |
| `>>>=` | `a = a >>> b` |

### 2.1.7 Ternary (Conditional) Operator

$$\text{result} = \text{condition} \,?\, \text{valueIfTrue} \,:\, \text{valueIfFalse}$$

**Example:** `int max = (a > b) ? a : b;`

> [!NOTE]
> The ternary operator is the **only ternary operator in Java** and is a compact replacement for simple `if-else` constructs.

### 2.1.8 `instanceof` Operator

$$\text{result} = \text{objectReference} \text{ instanceof} \text{ ClassName}$$

Returns `true` if the object is an instance of the specified class (or its subclass). Used extensively in OOP for type checking before downcasting.

---

## 2.2 Operator Precedence and Associativity (The "Golden Table")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** This precedence table is tested almost every semester. Memorize the **order of evaluation**, NOT just the operators.

| Precedence Level | Operator Category | Operators | Associativity |
|:----------------:|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 (Highest) | Postfix | `++`, `--` (post), function call, array index | Left-to-Right |
| 2 | Unary | `++`, `--` (pre), `+`, `-`, `~`, `!` | Right-to-Left |
| 3 | Multiplicative | `*`, `/`, `%` | Left-to-Right |
| 4 | Additive | `+`, `-` | Left-to-Right |
| 5 | Shift | `<<`, `>>`, `>>>` | Left-to-Right |
| 6 | Relational | `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `instanceof` | Left-to-Right |
| 7 | Equality | `==`, `!=` | Left-to-Right |
| 8 | Bitwise AND | `&` | Left-to-Right |
| 9 | Bitwise XOR | `^` | Left-to-Right |
| 10 | Bitwise OR | $\vert$ | Left-to-Right |
| 11 | Logical AND | `&&` | Left-to-Right |
| 12 | Logical OR | $\vert\vert$ | Left-to-Right |
| 13 | Ternary | `? :` | Right-to-Left |
| 14 | Assignment | `=`, `+=`, `-=`, etc. | Right-to-Left |
| 15 (Lowest) | Comma (rare in Java) | `,` | Left-to-Right |

---

## 2.3 Deep Analysis of Control Statements

### 2.3.1 What is "Control Flow"?

$$\text{Control Flow} = \text{the order in which individual statements, instructions, or function calls are executed in a program}$$

In the absence of control statements, every Java program follows a **linear, top-to-bottom** execution model. Control statements introduce **non-linearity**, which is the mathematical equivalent of:

$$f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$$

where the mapping from line number to next line is no longer the identity function $f(x) = x$, but a conditional function $f(x) = g(x, \text{state})$.

### 2.3.2 Control Statement — The Three-Layer Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain control statements," structure your answer in three layers:
> 1. **Selection Layer** — Decides *which* block to execute
> 2. **Iteration Layer** — Decides *how many times* to execute
> 3. **Jump Layer** — Decides *when to exit* or *skip* execution

---

## 2.4 Deep Analysis of Selection Statements

### 2.4.1 The `if` Statement — Mathematical Foundation

The `if` statement is a direct implementation of a **propositional logic implication**:

$$P \rightarrow Q \quad \text{where } P = \text{condition}, Q = \text{statement block}$$

**Syntax:**
```java
if (condition) {
    // executes if and only if condition is true
}
```

**Truth Table:**

| Condition (P) | Execution of Block (Q) |
|:-------------:|:----------------------:|
| `true` | Executed |
| `false` | Skipped |

### 2.4.2 The `if-else` Statement — Mathematical Foundation

The `if-else` statement is a **mutually exclusive binary decision**, equivalent to:

$$(P \rightarrow Q_1) \wedge (\neg P \rightarrow Q_2)$$

**Syntax:**
```java
if (condition) {
    // Block A: executes when condition is true
} else {
    // Block B: executes when condition is false
}
```

### 2.4.3 The `if-else-if` Ladder — Mathematical Foundation

A cascaded multi-way decision:

$$(P_1 \rightarrow Q_1) \wedge (\neg P_1 \wedge P_2 \rightarrow Q_2) \wedge \ldots \wedge (\neg P_1 \wedge \ldots \wedge \neg P_{n-1} \wedge P_n \rightarrow Q_n) \wedge (\neg P_1 \wedge \ldots \wedge \neg P_n \rightarrow Q_{default})$$

This guarantees **exactly one** block executes (or the `else` block as default).

### 2.4.4 The `switch` Statement — Discrete Value Dispatcher

The `switch` statement is equivalent to an **n-way equality dispatcher**:

$$\text{execute case}_i \iff \text{expression} == \text{value}_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Switch Specifics (KTU Syllabus):**
> - **Java 7+**: `switch` supports `String` operands (not just integers and enums).
> - **Java 14+**: Enhanced `switch` with arrow syntax `->` and `yield` (advanced; not typically in Module 1).
> - **Fall-through behavior**: Without `break`, execution "falls through" to the next case.
> - **Default case**: Optional but recommended for robustness.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---------|---------------------|
| **Arithmetic Operators** | Financial calculation systems, engineering simulations, scientific computing |
| **Relational & Logical** | Database query engines (`WHERE` clauses), authentication systems, validation |
| **Bitwise Operators** | Embedded systems, cryptography, network protocol design, image processing |
| **`if-else`** | ATM transaction logic, inventory threshold alerts, sensor monitoring |
| **`switch`** | Menu-driven UI, command interpreters (CLI), state machines in protocol stacks |
| **Ternary Operator** | Inline UI rendering, concise conditional assignments in `return` statements |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Operator Evaluation — Step-by-Step Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Discipline:** Every Java expression is evaluated in a **deterministic order** defined by the JLS (Java Language Specification). The following derivations show the exact evaluation order.

### 3.1.1 Trace: Pre vs. Post Increment (The Classic KTU Problem)

**Given Expression:**
```java
int a = 5;
int b = ++a + a++ + --a;
int c = a--;
```

**Step 1: Initial State**
$$a = 5, \quad b = \text{undefined}, \quad c = \text{undefined}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate `++a` (Pre-increment)**
- Increment `a` first: $a = 5 + 1 = 6$
- Use the new value in expression: returns $6$

**Step 3: Evaluate `a++` (Post-increment)**
- Use the current value of `a` (which is $6$) in expression: returns $6$
- After use, increment $a$: $a = 6 + 1 = 7$

**Step 4: Evaluate `--a` (Pre-decrement)**
- Decrement `a` first: $a = 7 - 1 = 6$
- Use the new value: returns $6$

**Step 5: Compute the Sum**
$$b = 6 + 6 + 6 = 18$$

**Step 6: Final State of `a`**
$$a = 6$$

**Step 7: Evaluate `c = a--`**
- Use the current value of `a$ ($6$) in assignment: $c = 6$
- Then decrement $a$: $a = 6 - 1 = 5$

**Final Results:**
$$a = 5, \quad b = 18, \quad c = 6$$

### 3.1.2 Trace: Compound Assignment with Implicit Casting

**Given Expression:**
```java
byte b = 10;
b = b + 5;   // Line 1 — Compilation Error!
b += 5;      // Line 2 — Compiles fine
```

**Step 1: Analyze Line 1 — `b = b + 5`**
- `b` is of type `byte` (8-bit)
- `5` is an `int` literal (32-bit)
- Java promotes `b` to `int` for the addition: $\text{int} = \text{byte} + \text{int}$
- Result is of type `int`
- Assigning `int` back to `byte` requires **explicit cast** → **Compilation Error: possible lossy conversion**

**Step 2: Analyze Line 2 — `b += 5`**
- Compound assignment operators in Java include an **implicit cast**
- The expression `b += 5` is internally equivalent to `b = (byte)(b + 5)`
- The cast happens automatically → **Compiles successfully**, result: $b = 15$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Highlight:** This is one of the most frequently tested distinctions. Remember the equivalence: $x \text{ op}= y \equiv x = (\text{type of } x)(x \text{ op } y)$.

### 3.1.3 Trace: Operator Precedence in Complex Expression

**Given Expression:**
```java
int result = 10 + 20 * 3 - 4 / 2 + (5 % 3);
```

**Step 1: Identify Operators by Precedence (Highest First)**

| Step | Sub-expression | Operator | Result |
|:----:|----------------|----------|--------|
| 1 | `20 * 3` | `*` (Level 3) | `60` |
| 2 | `4 / 2` | `/` (Level 3) | `2` |
| 3 | `5 % 3` | `%` (Level 3) | `2` |
| 4 | `10 + 60` | `+` (Level 4) | `70` |
| 5 | `70 - 2` | `-` (Level 4) | `68` |
| 6 | `68 + 2` | `+` (Level 4) | `70` |

**Step 2: Final Result**
$$\text{result} = 70$$

**Step 3: Verify with Associativity**
- All operators at the same level are left-to-right associative ✓
- Parentheses `(5 % 3)` evaluated first ✓

### 3.1.4 Trace: Short-Circuit Logical Evaluation

**Given Expression:**
```java
int x = 5, y = 10;
boolean result = (x > 10) && (y++ > 5);
System.out.println(y);
```

**Step 1: Evaluate `(x > 10)`**
- $5 > 10$ → `false`

**Step 2: Short-Circuit Decision**
- Since `&&` requires both operands `true` and the left operand is `false`
- The right operand `(y++ > 5)` is **NOT evaluated**
- Therefore, `y` is **NOT incremented**

**Step 3: Final State**
$$\text{result} = \text{false}, \quad y = 10$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** If this had been `&` (bitwise AND, non-short-circuit), the right operand would be evaluated, and `y` would become $11$. This distinction is a **guaranteed KTU question**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace: `if-else-if` Ladder

**Problem:** Grade classification based on marks.

```java
int marks = 78;
String grade;

if (marks >= 90)      grade = "A+";
else if (marks >= 80) grade = "A";
else if (marks >= 70) grade = "B+";
else if (marks >= 60) grade = "B";
else if (marks >= 50) grade = "C";
else                  grade = "F";

System.out.println(grade);
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Condition Evaluated | Result | Action Taken |
|:----:|---------------------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | `marks >= 90` → `78 >= 90` | `false` | Move to next `else if` |
| 2 | `marks >= 80` → `78 >= 80` | `false` | Move to next `else if` |
| 3 | `marks >= 70` → `78 >= 70` | `true` | Assign `grade = "B+"` and **exit ladder** |
| 4 | (Skipped) | — | — |

**Output:** `B+`

**Key Insight:** Once a `true` condition is found, **all subsequent conditions are skipped** — the ladder **terminates** at the first `true` match.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Trace: `switch` Statement with Fall-Through

**Problem:** Display the name of a weekday.

```java
int day = 3;
switch (day) {
    case 1: System.out.println("Monday");
    case 2: System.out.println("Tuesday");
    case 3: System.out.println("Wednesday");
    case 4: System.out.println("Thursday");
    case 5: System.out.println("Friday");
    default: System.out.println("Weekend");
}
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Action | Output |
|:----:|--------|--------|
| 1 | Match found: `case 3` | "Wednesday" |
| 2 | **Fall-through** (no `break`) | "Thursday" |
| 3 | **Fall-through** (no `break`) | "Friday" |
| 4 | **Fall-through** (no `break`) | "Weekend" |

**Output:**
```
Wednesday
Thursday
Friday
Weekend
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule:** Every `case` in a `switch` should end with a `break` statement unless **intentional fall-through** is desired. The `default` case is optional but recommended. The `switch` argument must be of type `byte`, `short`, `int`, `char`, `String`, or `enum`.

**Corrected Version (with `break`):**
```java
switch (day) {
    case 1: System.out.println("Monday"); break;
    case 2: System.out.println("Tuesday"); break;
    case 3: System.out.println("Wednesday"); break;
    case 4: System.out.println("Thursday"); break;
    case 5: System.out.println("Friday"); break;
    default: System.out.println("Weekend");
}
```

**Output (Corrected):** `Wednesday`

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Statement Comparison

| Feature | `if-else` | `switch` | Ternary `? :` |
|---------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **Condition Type** | Boolean (any complexity) | Equality against discrete values | Boolean (simple) |
| **Number of Branches** | 2 (binary) or multi via ladder | N-way | 2 (binary only) |
| **Data Types Supported** | Any (with overloading) | `int`, `byte`, `short`, `char`, `String`, `enum` | Any compatible types |
| **Range Checking** | ✅ Yes (e.g., `x > 10 && x < 20`) | ❌ No (only exact matches) | ✅ Yes |
| **Fall-through Behavior** | N/A | ⚠️ Yes (without `break`) | N/A |
| **Return Value** | ❌ No (statement, not expression) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (expression) |
| **Readability for Many Branches** | Poor (ladder becomes messy) | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Poor (nested ternaries are confusing) |
| **Performance** | Slightly slower (branch prediction) | Slightly faster (jump table) | Same as `if-else` |

---

## 3.5 Trace: Nested `if` vs. Logical `&&` Equivalence

**These two constructs are logically equivalent:**

```java
// Version 1: Nested if
if (a > 0) {
    if (b > 0) {
        System.out.println("Both positive");
    }
}

// Version 2: Combined with &&
if (a > 0 && b > 0) {
    System.out.println("Both positive");
}
```

**Truth Table for Equivalence:**

| `a > 0` | `b > 0` | Nested `if` | `&&` |
|:-------:|:-------:|:-----------:|:----:|
| `true` | `true` | ✅ Execute | ✅ Execute |
| `true` | `false` | ❌ Skip | ❌ Skip |
| `false` | `true` | ❌ Skip | ❌ Skip |
| `false` | `false` | ❌ Skip | ❌ Skip |

**Conclusion:** The `&&` form is more **concise and readable**. However, the nested form allows **different actions** in the outer and inner branches, which `&&` cannot do.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: `switch` Statement Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startC["Start: Evaluate switch expression"] --> matchC{"Match with case value?"}
    matchC -- "Case 1" --> caseC1["Execute Case 1 Block"] --> brkC1{"break?"}
    brkC1 -- "YES" --> endC["End"]
    brkC1 -- "NO" --> fallC["Fall-through to next case"]
    matchC -- "Case 2" --> caseC2["Execute Case 2 Block"] --> brkC2{"break?"}
    brkC2 -- "YES" --> endC
    brkC2 -- "NO" --> fallC
    matchC -- "Case 3" --> caseC3["Execute Case 3 Block"] --> brkC3{"break?"}
    brkC3 -- "YES" --> endC
    matchC -- "NO MATCH" --> defC["Execute default Block"] --> endC
    fallC --> caseC2
    fallC --> caseC3
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Control Statement Hierarchy (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootD["Java Control Statements"]
    rootD --> selD["Selection Statements"]
    rootD --> iterD["Iteration Statements"]
    rootD --> jumpD["Jump Statements"]

    selD --> selD1["if"]
    selD --> selD2["if-else"]
    selD --> selD3["if-else-if Ladder"]
    selD --> selD4["Nested if"]
    selD --> selD5["switch"]
    selD --> selD6["Ternary ? :"]

    iterD --> iterD1["while"]
    iterD --> iterD2["do-while"]
    iterD --> iterD3["for"]
    iterD --> iterD4["Enhanced for"]

    jumpD --> jumpD1["break"]
    jumpD --> jumpD2["continue"]
    jumpD --> jumpD3["return"]
    jumpD --> jumpD4["goto DEPRECATED"]
```

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: Operator Precedence Ladder (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph TD
    precRoot["Operator Precedence: Highest to Lowest"]
    precRoot --> lev1["Level 1: Postfix ++ -- ( ) [ ]"]
    precRoot --> lev2["Level 2: Unary ++ -- + - ~ !"]
    precRoot --> lev3["Level 3: Multiplicative * / %"]
    precRoot --> lev4["Level 4: Additive + -"]
    precRoot --> lev5["Level 5: Shift << >> >>>"]
    precRoot --> lev6["Level 6: Relational < <= > >="]
    precRoot --> lev7["Level 7: Equality == !="]
    precRoot --> lev8["Level 8: Bitwise AND &"]
    precRoot --> lev9["Level 9: Bitwise XOR ^"]
    precRoot --> lev10["Level 10: Bitwise OR |"]
    precRoot --> lev11["Level 11: Logical AND &&"]
    precRoot --> lev12["Level 12: Logical OR ||"]
    precRoot --> lev13["Level 13: Ternary ? :"]
    precRoot --> lev14["Level 14: Assignment = += etc"]
```

---

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram: Short-Circuit Evaluation Logic (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    scStart["Start: Evaluate Logical Expression"] --> scEvalA["Evaluate Left Operand A"]
    scEvalA --> scOp{"Operator"}
    scOp -- "&&" --> scAnd{"A is FALSE?"}
    scAnd -- "YES" --> scAndResult["Result = FALSE, Skip B"]
    scAnd -- "NO" --> scAndEvalB["Evaluate Right Operand B"]
    scAndEvalB --> scAndFinal["Result = B"]
    scOp -- "||" --> scOr{"A is TRUE?"}
    scOr -- "YES" --> scOrResult["Result = TRUE, Skip B"]
    scOr -- "NO" --> scOrEvalB["Evaluate Right Operand B"]
    scOrEvalB --> scOrFinal["Result = B"]
    scAndResult --> scEnd["End"]
    scAndFinal --> scEnd
    scOrResult --> scEnd
    scOrFinal --> scEnd
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `==` operator and `.equals()` method in Java. Why is the latter preferred for String comparison?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `==` Operator | `.equals()` Method |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Type** | Operator | Method (defined in `Object` class) |
| **Comparison** | Reference (memory address) | Content (actual value) |
| **Primitive Types** | ✅ Compares values | ❌ Cannot be used |
| **Objects** | Compares references | Compares content (overridable) |
| **Example** | `"hello" == "hello"` → may be `false` (different objects) | `"hello".equals("hello")` → always `true` |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** `.equals()` is preferred for `String` comparison because it compares the **actual character sequence** (content), whereas `==` compares **memory addresses**, which can differ even for strings with identical content created using `new` keyword.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain short-circuit evaluation in Java with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Short-circuit evaluation is an optimization technique used by the `&&` (logical AND) and `||` (logical OR) operators where the right-hand operand is **not evaluated** if the result can be determined from the left-hand operand alone.

**Rules (1 mark):**
- In `A && B`: If `A` is `false`, `B` is skipped (result is `false`).
- In `A || B`: If `A` is `true`, `B` is skipped (result is `true`).

**Example (1 mark):**
```java
int x = 0;
if (x != 0 && 10 / x > 1) {
    System.out.println("Safe");
}
```
Here, when `x = 0`, the left operand `x != 0` evaluates to `false`, so the right operand `10 / x` is **not evaluated**, preventing an `ArithmeticException` (division by zero).

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain all categories of operators in Java with suitable examples. Also explain operator precedence with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Java provides a rich set of operators classified into 8 categories based on functionality. Operators are symbols that perform operations on operands and return a result.

**Operator Categories (5 marks):**

1. **Arithmetic Operators:** `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` — perform mathematical operations.
   - Example: `int x = 10 + 5;` → `x = 15`

2. **Unary Operators:** `+`, `-`, `++`, `--`, `!`, `~` — operate on a single operand.
   - Example: `int y = -x;` → negation

3. **Relational Operators:** `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` — compare two values, return boolean.
   - Example: `boolean b = (a > b);`

4. **Logical Operators:** `&&`, `||`, `!` — combine boolean expressions.
   - Example: `if (a > 0 && b > 0)`

5. **Bitwise Operators:** `&`, `|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`, `>>>` — operate on individual bits.
   - Example: `int c = 5 & 3;` → `c = 1`

6. **Assignment Operators:** `=`, `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `%=`, `&=`, `|=`, `^=`, `<<=`, `>>=`, `>>>=` — assign values.
   - Example: `a += 5;` → equivalent to `a = a + 5;`

7. **Ternary Operator:** `? :` — shorthand for simple if-else.
   - Example: `int max = (a > b) ? a : b;`

8. **`instanceof` Operator:** checks if an object is an instance of a class.
   - Example: `if (obj instanceof String)`

**Operator Precedence (1 mark):** The order in which operators are evaluated in an expression. Higher precedence operators are evaluated first. Example:
```java
int result = 10 + 20 * 3;
```
Here, `*` has higher precedence than `+`, so `20 * 3 = 60` is evaluated first, then `10 + 60 = 70`. Final result: `70`.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write a Java program to read a character from the user and check whether it is a vowel, consonant, digit, or special character using a `switch` statement.**

**Model Solution:**
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class CharacterChecker {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a character: ");
        char ch = sc.next().charAt(0);
        
        switch (ch) {
            case 'a': case 'e': case 'i': case 'o': case 'u':
            case 'A': case 'E': case 'I': case 'O': case 'U':
                System.out.println(ch + " is a VOWEL");
                break;
            case '0': case '1': case '2': case '3': case '4':
            case '5': case '6': case '7': case '8': case '9':
                System.out.println(ch + " is a DIGIT");
                break;
            default:
                if (Character.isLetter(ch)) {
                    System.out.println(ch + " is a CONSONANT");
                } else {
                    System.out.println(ch + " is a SPECIAL CHARACTER");
                }
        }
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

- **`sc.next().charAt(0)`** (1 mark): Reads a string token and extracts the first character.
- **Multiple case labels without `break`** (2 marks): Creates fall-through grouping, so any vowel (lowercase or uppercase) executes the same block.
- **Digit case grouping** (1 mark): All ten digits share a single output block.
- **`default` case with `isLetter()`** (2 marks): If the character is not a vowel or digit, it is either a consonant (letter) or a special character (symbol).
- **`break` statements** (1 mark): Prevent fall-through between groups.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain selection statements in Java. Compare `if-else-if` ladder with `switch` statement with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Selection statements are control statements that allow the program to choose between alternative paths of execution based on whether a condition evaluates to `true` or `false`.

**Types of Selection Statements (2 marks):**
1. `if` statement — Single-path
2. `if-else` statement — Two-path
3. `if-else-if` ladder — Multi-path
4. `switch` statement — Multi-way discrete branching
5. Ternary operator `? :` — Inline two-path

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | `if-else-if` Ladder | `switch` Statement |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Condition Type | Any boolean expression | Equality only |
| Data Types | Any (with overloading) | `int`, `char`, `String`, `enum` |
| Range Checking | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Number of Branches | Unlimited | Unlimited (cases) |
| Default Block | Optional `else` | Optional `default` |
| Fall-through | N/A | ⚠️ Yes (without `break`) |
| Readability | Poor for many branches | ✅ Better for discrete values |

**Example (2 marks):**

*Using `if-else-if`:*
```java
int day = 3;
if (day == 1) System.out.println("Monday");
else if (day == 2) System.out.println("Tuesday");
else if (day == 3) System.out.println("Wednesday");
else System.out.println("Invalid");
```

*Using `switch`:*
```java
int day = 3;
switch (day) {
    case 1: System.out.println("Monday"); break;
    case 2: System.out.println("Tuesday"); break;
    case 3: System.out.println("Wednesday"); break;
    default: System.out.println("Invalid");
}
```

The `switch` version is **cleaner and more efficient** for discrete value matching.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write a Java program that reads three integers and finds the largest among them using nested `if-else` and also using the ternary operator.**

**Model Solution (Nested if-else):**
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class FindLargest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter three integers: ");
        int a = sc.nextInt();
        int b = sc.nextInt();
        int c = sc.nextInt();
        
        int largest;
        
        if (a >= b) {
            if (a >= c) {
                largest = a;
            } else {
                largest = c;
            }
        } else {
            if (b >= c) {
                largest = b;
            } else {
                largest = c;
            }
        }
        
        System.out.println("The largest number is: " + largest);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Model Solution (Ternary Operator):**
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class FindLargestTernary {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter three integers: ");
        int a = sc.nextInt();
        int b = sc.nextInt();
        int c = sc.nextInt();
        
        int largest = (a > b) ? ((a > c) ? a : c) : ((b > c) ? b : c);
        
        System.out.println("The largest number is: " + largest);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

- **Nested `if-else` logic** (3 marks): First compares `a` and `b`. If `a >= b`, then `a` is compared with `c` to find the largest. Otherwise, `b` is compared with `c`.
- **Ternary operator logic** (2 marks): `(a > b) ? ((a > c) ? a : c) : ((b > c) ? b : c)` is a nested ternary that mimics the same logic in a single line.
- **Input/Output** (2 marks): Proper use of `Scanner` class for input and `println` for formatted output.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `=` with `==`** (1 mark deduction each occurrence): Using assignment instead of comparison. Remember: `=` assigns, `==` compares.
> 2. **Missing `break` in `switch`** (2 marks deduction): Causes unintended fall-through. Always include `break` unless explicitly required.
> 3. **Integer division misconception** (1 mark deduction): `7 / 3` in integer context gives `2`, NOT `2.33`. The result is truncated.
> 4. **Operator precedence errors** (1-2 marks deduction): Not using parentheses to clarify complex expressions. When in doubt, use parentheses.
> 5. **Post vs. Pre increment confusion** (2 marks deduction): `a++` uses then increments; `++a` increments then uses. Trace step-by-step in the exam.
> 6. **Compound assignment implicit cast** (1 mark deduction): Forgetting that `b += 5` includes an implicit cast to `byte`, but `b = b + 5` does not.
> 7. **Nested ternary unreadability** (1 mark deduction): Avoid more than 2 levels of nested ternaries — use `if-else` instead for clarity.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1**

### 🔑 Operators — Key Takeaways
- Java has **8 operator categories** with **~44 operators** total.
- **Arithmetic**: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` — watch for integer division truncation.
- **Unary**: `++`, `--` (pre vs. post), `!`, `~` — pre-increments before use; post-increments after use.
- **Relational**: Always return `boolean` (not `int` like C/C++).
- **Logical**: `&&` and `||` are **short-circuit**; `&` and `|` are **non-short-circuit** bitwise operators that can also work on booleans.
- **Bitwise**: Operate on individual bits; `>>>` is unsigned right shift (fills with zeros).
- **Assignment**: `x op= y` is equivalent to `x = (type of x)(x op y)` — implicit cast included.
- **Ternary**: Only ternary operator in Java; shorthand for simple `if-else`.
- **`instanceof`**: Type checking operator; returns `boolean`.

### 🔑 Operator Precedence — Critical Order
$$\text{Postfix} > \text{Unary} > \text{Multiplicative} > \text{Additive} > \text{Shift} > \text{Relational} > \text{Equality} > \text{Bitwise} > \text{Logical} > \text{Ternary} > \text{Assignment}$$

### 🔑 Control Statements — Key Takeaways
- **Three categories**: Selection, Iteration, Jump.
- Control statements **break the default top-to-bottom sequential flow**.
- They are the foundation of all algorithmic logic.

### 🔑 Selection Statements — Key Takeaways
- **`if`**: Single-path, simplest form.
- **`if-else`**: Two-path, mutually exclusive.
- **`if-else-if` ladder**: Multi-path, cascading; **first true condition wins**.
- **`switch`**: Discrete value matching; supports `int`, `char`, `String`, `enum`; uses `break` to prevent fall-through.
- **Ternary `? :`**: Inline conditional; returns a value (it's an expression, not a statement).
- **Nested `if`**: `if` inside `if`; can be replaced by `&&` when both branches do the same thing.

### 🔑 Exam-Day Formulas to Memorize
$$\text{Pre-increment: } ++a \Rightarrow a = a + 1; \text{ return new } a$$
$$\text{Post-increment: } a++ \Rightarrow \text{return old } a; a = a + 1$$
$$\text{Compound assignment: } a \text{ op}= b \Leftrightarrow a = (\text{type}) (a \text{ op } b)$$
$$\text{Short-circuit: } A \And\& B = \text{false if } A = \text{false (skip B)}$$
$$\text{Fall-through: without break, switch executes all subsequent cases}$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iteration Statements and Jump Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Iteration Statements and Jump Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Iteration Statements — Formal Definition

**Iteration Statements** (also called *Looping Constructs*) are program control structures that allow a block of code (the **body** of the loop) to be executed repeatedly based on the evaluation of a **boolean condition** (also termed a *loop-continuation condition*). In the **C++** language — which is the prescribed implementation language for **PEEIT741** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** — iteration is provided by three primitive statements:

1. The `while` statement (entry-controlled loop).
2. The `for` statement (counter-controlled / range-based loop).
3. The `do…while` statement (exit-controlled loop).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):**
> "Iteration statements cause a block of statements to be executed repeatedly, zero or more times, until a terminating condition is satisfied. Jump statements transfer control unconditionally to another part of the program."

A loop therefore possesses **four canonical elements**: an *initialisation expression*, a *condition expression*, an *update expression*, and a *body*.

## 1.2 Jump Statements — Formal Definition

**Jump Statements** are single-token statements that unconditionally transfer program control to a different location in the source code. The C++ jump statements mandated by Module 1 are:

- `break` — terminates the **innermost** enclosing `switch` or loop statement.
- `continue` — skips the remainder of the current loop iteration and proceeds to the next.
- `goto` — unconditional transfer to a labelled statement.
- `return` — exits the current function, optionally returning a value.
- `exit()` (declared in `<cstdlib>`) — terminates the entire program.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Dish-Washing Machine"**
>
> Think of a **dish-washer** that repeats a *wash cycle* until the **"Are dishes clean?"** sensor returns `false`. The loop body is the *wash cycle*, the condition is the *sensor reading*, and the update is *advancing the timer*. If you press the **emergency stop** button, you execute a `break`. If you press **"skip dry"**, you execute a `continue` — the dishes go straight to the next wash. If you smash the machine with a hammer, you have effectively performed `exit(0)`.

This dish-washer metaphor captures every element of iteration and the disruption caused by jump statements.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded)

- **Entry-controlled loop** — condition is tested *before* the body executes.
- **Exit-controlled loop** — body executes *at least once*; condition is tested after.
- **Infinite loop** — a loop whose condition never becomes false.
- **Iteration** — one complete execution of the loop body.
- **Counter-controlled loop** — a loop whose update modifies a counter variable that ultimately drives termination.
- **Sentinel value** — a special data value used to terminate a loop when the count is unknown.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `while` Statement (Entry-Controlled)

### Syntax
```cpp
initialisation;
while ( condition ) {
    // body
    update;
}
```

### Operational Logic
1. Evaluate the `initialisation` expression (executes exactly once).
2. Test the `condition`. If it evaluates to `true` (non-zero), proceed to step 3; otherwise, exit the loop.
3. Execute the `body`.
4. Execute the `update` expression.
5. Return to step 2.

### Why and How
The `while` loop is used when the number of repetitions is **not known in advance** — for instance, reading input until a sentinel value is entered.

> [!NOTE]
> If the condition is initially `false`, the body executes **zero** times. This is the defining property of an entry-controlled loop.

## 2.2 The `for` Statement (Counter / Range Controlled)

### Syntax (Classic form)
```cpp
for ( initialisation; condition; update ) {
    // body
}
```

### Syntax (Range-based — C++11 onward, KTU 2024 relevant)
```cpp
for ( type var : container ) {
    // body
}
```

### Operational Logic
The `for` loop is *semantically identical* to the `while` loop; the three expressions `initialisation`, `condition`, and `update` are merely **co-located** for readability. The C++ standard guarantees that:

- `initialisation` executes **once** at loop entry.
- `condition` is evaluated *before* every iteration.
- `update` executes *after* every iteration, *before* the next condition test.

### Why and How
The `for` loop is preferred when the loop variable behaves as a **counter**, an **iterator**, or a **ranged traversal** — making it the most-used loop in KTU lab examinations.

## 2.3 The `do…while` Statement (Exit-Controlled)

### Syntax
```cpp
initialisation;
do {
    // body
    update;
} while ( condition );
```

### Operational Logic
1. Execute the `body` (and `update`) once **unconditionally**.
2. Evaluate the `condition`. If `true`, return to step 1; otherwise, exit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `do…while` loop guarantees **at least one execution** of the body, even when the condition is initially `false`. This makes it the canonical construct for *menu-driven programs* in KTU lab cycles.

## 2.4 Jump Statements — Detailed Behaviour

| Jump Statement | Scope of Effect | Practical Use |
|----------------|-----------------|---------------|
| `break;` | Innermost enclosing `switch` / `while` / `for` / `do…while` | Early termination; exits a loop once target is found |
| `continue;` | Innermost enclosing loop (not `switch`) | Skips remainder of iteration; proceeds to next iteration's condition |
| `goto label;` | Whole function (cannot jump over initialisations) | Multi-level loop exit; discouraged but syntactically legal |
| `return expr;` | Current function | Function exit; returns value to caller |
| `exit(code);` | Whole program (declared in `<cstdlib>`) | Emergency/abort termination of `main()` |

### Why `goto` is Discouraged
The `goto` statement produces **spaghetti code** — non-linear control flow that is virtually impossible to verify. Stroustrup's *C++ Programming Language* notes that nearly every legitimate use of `goto` is replaced by structured constructs (`break`, `continue`, functions, exceptions). KTU examiners may deduct marks for unjustified use of `goto`.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Property | `while` | `for` | `do…while` |
|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| Type | Entry-controlled | Entry-controlled | Exit-controlled |
| Minimum executions of body | **0** | **0** | **1** |
| Condition tested | Before body | Before body | After body |
| Counter style | External to loop | Inside header | External to loop |
| Infinite-loop idiom | `while(true);` | `for(;;);` | `do{}while(true);` |
| Canonical use | Sentinel loops | Counter / range loops | Menu loops |

### Engineering Real-World Utility
- **`while`** — embedded system polling; reading sensor data until EOF.
- **`for`** — array/traversal in DSP, image-processing kernels, FIR filters.
- **`do…while`** — ATM transaction menus; user-interactive REPLs.
- **`break`** — search algorithms; early exit in linear/binary search.
- **`continue`** — skipping negative/zero values in statistical filtering.
- **`goto`** — error-handling in C-style resource management (the historical but obsolete pattern).
- **`return`** — every function that is not `void`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Trace Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Trace Methodology

To solve any KTU OOP iteration question, build a **Trace Table** with the following columns:

| Step | Condition | Body Output | Update | Notes |
