|------|-----------|-------------|--------|-------|

We then iterate step-by-step until the condition becomes `false`.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace — Worked Example 1 (`for` loop)

**Code:**
```cpp
int s = 0;
for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
    s = s + i;
}
```

### Step-by-step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state: } & s = 0, \; i \; \text{uninitialised} \\
\text{Iteration 1: } & i = 1,\; 1 \le 5 \Rightarrow \text{true} \Rightarrow s = 0 + 1 = 1,\; i \rightarrow 2 \\
\text{Iteration 2: } & i = 2,\; 2 \le 5 \Rightarrow \text{true} \Rightarrow s = 1 + 2 = 3,\; i \rightarrow 3 \\
\text{Iteration 3: } & i = 3,\; 3 \le 5 \Rightarrow \text{true} \Rightarrow s = 3 + 3 = 6,\; i \rightarrow 4 \\
\text{Iteration 4: } & i = 4,\; 4 \le 5 \Rightarrow \text{true} \Rightarrow s = 6 + 4 = 10,\; i \rightarrow 5 \\
\text{Iteration 5: } & i = 5,\; 5 \le 5 \Rightarrow \text{true} \Rightarrow s = 10 + 5 = 15,\; i \rightarrow 6 \\
\text{Test: } & i = 6,\; 6 \le 5 \Rightarrow \text{false} \Rightarrow \text{EXIT}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Analytical Result
$$
s_{\text{final}} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = \frac{5 \cdot 6}{2} = \mathbf{15}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The closed-form sum $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ is the result of the **Gauss formula** — useful for predicting output in O(1) without tracing.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Trace — Worked Example 2 (`do…while` with `continue`)

**Code:**
```cpp
int i = 0;
do {
    i++;
    if (i == 3) continue;
    if (i == 5) break;
    cout << i << " ";
} while (i < 10);
```

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{i=0:} & \text{enter body} \Rightarrow i=1,\; 1\ne3,\; 1\ne5 \Rightarrow \text{print } 1,\; \text{test } 1<10 \Rightarrow \text{continue} \\
\text{i=1:} & i=2,\; 2\ne3,\; 2\ne5 \Rightarrow \text{print } 2 \\
\text{i=2:} & i=3,\; i==3 \Rightarrow \text{continue} \Rightarrow \text{skip print, jump to test} \Rightarrow 3<10 \Rightarrow \text{continue} \\
\text{i=3:} & i=4,\; 4\ne3,\; 4\ne5 \Rightarrow \text{print } 4 \\
\text{i=4:} & i=5,\; i==5 \Rightarrow \text{break} \Rightarrow \text{EXIT loop}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Output Stream
$$
\mathbf{1 \; 2 \; 4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `continue` inside `do…while` jumps **directly to the test**, not to the update — this is a frequently tested KTU subtlety.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Construct

| Scenario | Recommended Construct | Reason |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| Read integers until `-1` is entered | `while(cin>>x && x!=-1)` | Count unknown, sentinel terminates |
| Print 1 to 100 | `for(int i=1;i<=100;i++)` | Known counter range |
| Display menu at least once, repeat until exit | `do…while(choice!='Q')` | Body must run at least once |
| Skip processing of negative array elements | `if(x<0) continue;` | Skip remaining iteration logic |
| Stop searching once target is found | `if(found) break;` | Early exit, ignore rest of array |
| Multi-level nested loop immediate exit | `goto done;` (or flag variable) | Standard C++ alternative to labelled `break` (C++ lacks Java-style labelled break) |

## 3.5 The Classic `for(;;)` Infinite Loop Pattern

```cpp
for (;;) {
    // body
    if (terminating_condition) break;
}
```

### Derivation of Behaviour
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{init: } & \text{none} \\
\text{test: } & \text{empty condition} \Rightarrow \text{treated as } \text{true (non-zero)} \\
\text{update: } & \text{none} \\
\therefore \; & \text{loop runs indefinitely until } \text{break} \text{ or } \text{return}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU practical exams, omitting the `break` in an infinite `for(;;)` is a **3-mark deduction** because the program will hang during execution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Generic `while` Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Start"]) --> B["initialisation"]
    B --> C{"condition true?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z(["End / exit loop"])
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Execute body"]
    D --> E["update"]
    E --> C
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — `for` Loop (Entry-Controlled, Co-located)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Start"]) --> B["initialisation"]
    B --> C{"condition true?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z(["End"])
    C -- "Yes" --> D["body"]
    D --> E["update"]
    E --> C
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — `do…while` Loop (Exit-Controlled)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Start"]) --> B["initialisation"]
    B --> D["body"]
    D --> E["update"]
    E --> F{"condition true?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> D
    F -- "No" --> Z(["End"])
```

## 4.4 Flowchart — `break` Inside a Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Start"]) --> B["initialisation"]
    B --> C{"condition true?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z(["End"])
    C -- "Yes" --> D["body"]
    D --> G{"break encountered?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> Z
    G -- "No" --> E["update"]
    E --> C
```

## 4.5 Flowchart — `continue` Inside a Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A(["Start"]) --> B["initialisation"]
    B --> C{"condition true?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z(["End"])
    C -- "Yes" --> D["body"]
    D --> H{"continue encountered?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> J["jump to update / test"]
    H -- "No" --> E["update"]
    E --> C
    J --> C
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Decision Map for Loop Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Repetition Required?"] --> B["Count Known?"]
    B -- "Yes" --> C["for loop"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Body must run at least once?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["do-while loop"]
    D -- "No" --> F["while loop"]
    C --> G["Iterate code"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Early exit needed?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["use break"]
    H -- "No" --> J["natural termination"]
    G --> K{"Skip remainder of iteration?"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["use continue"]
```

## 4.7 State Machine — Effect of `break` vs `continue` vs `goto`

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ExecutingBody
    ExecutingBody --> ExecutingBody : normal statement
    ExecutingBody --> NextIteration : continue
    ExecutingBody --> LoopExit : break
    ExecutingBody --> LabelTarget : goto
    LabelTarget --> ExecutingBody
    NextIteration --> ConditionTest
    ConditionTest --> ExecutingBody : true
    ConditionTest --> LoopExit : false
    LoopExit --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Distinguish between the `while` loop and the `do…while` loop in C++. When is each preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `while` is **entry-controlled** — condition is tested *before* body execution; body may execute **zero** times.
- `do…while` is **exit-controlled** — body executes **at least once**; condition tested *after*.
- `while` is preferred when the iteration count is **unknown or zero** (e.g., reading until EOF).
- `do…while` is preferred when the body **must run at least once** (e.g., menu-driven programs).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**
*What is the role of the `break` statement inside a switch block and inside a loop?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Inside a **`switch`**, `break` prevents *fall-through* by terminating the case and transferring control to the statement after the switch.
- Inside a **loop**, `break` causes the **immediate termination** of the *innermost* enclosing loop (`while`, `for`, or `do…while`), transferring control to the next statement after the loop.
- In both contexts, `break` does **not** terminate an `if` statement.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]**

> **(a)** Explain the three iteration statements supported by C++ with their general syntax. Compare them using any four differentiating points. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Write a complete C++ program to find the **sum of all even numbers** and the **sum of all odd numbers** from 1 to N using a `for` loop. Display both sums. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The three iteration statements are:

1. **`while` loop** — entry-controlled.
```cpp
int i = 1;
while (i <= 10) {
    cout << i << " ";
    i++;
}
```

2. **`for` loop** — counter/range controlled.
```cpp
for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
    cout << i << " ";
}
```

3. **`do…while` loop** — exit-controlled.
```cpp
int i = 1;
do {
    cout << i << " ";
    i++;
} while (i <= 10);
```

**Four differentiating points:**

| Property | `while` | `for` | `do…while` |
|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| Control type | Entry | Entry | Exit |
| Minimum body runs | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| Counter location | Outside | Inside header | Outside |
| Use case | Sentinel-driven | Count/range | Menu-driven |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    int N;
    cout << "Enter N: ";
    cin >> N;
    int evenSum = 0, oddSum = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= N; i++) {
        if (i % 2 == 0)
            evenSum = evenSum + i;
        else
            oddSum  = oddSum  + i;
    }
    cout << "Sum of even numbers = " << evenSum << endl;
    cout << "Sum of odd numbers  = " << oddSum  << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-step derivation of output for N = 6:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{evens: } & 2 + 4 + 6 = 12 \\
\text{odds: }  & 1 + 3 + 5 = 9 \\
\text{Output: } & \text{`Sum of even numbers = 12` and `Sum of odd numbers = 9`}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

> **(a)** With a suitable C++ program, explain the use of `break` and `continue` statements inside loops. Show how `continue` differs when used inside a `for` loop versus a `do…while` loop. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Write a C++ program to read integers from the user until `-1` is entered (sentinel). Use a `do…while` loop. Compute and display the **count of positive numbers**, the **count of negative numbers**, and the **count of zeros** entered (excluding the sentinel). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Demonstrative program:**
```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    // break demonstration
    cout << "Break: ";
    for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
        if (i == 6) break;        // exits loop
        cout << i << " ";
    }
    // continue demonstration
    cout << "\nContinue: ";
    for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
        if (i % 2 == 0) continue; // skips even numbers
        cout << i << " ";
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
$$
\mathbf{1 \; 2 \; 3 \; 4 \; 5} \quad \text{(break)} \qquad \mathbf{1 \; 3 \; 5 \; 7 \; 9} \quad \text{(continue)}
$$

**Key difference — `continue` in `for` vs `do…while`:**
- In a `for` loop, `continue` transfers control to the **update expression**, then the condition is tested.
- In a `do…while` loop, `continue` transfers control **directly to the condition test**, bypassing the update. Hence, the update must be placed *inside the body* to avoid an infinite loop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    int x, pos = 0, neg = 0, zero = 0;
    do {
        cout << "Enter integer (-1 to stop): ";
        cin >> x;
        if (x == -1) break;
        if (x > 0)       pos++;
        else if (x < 0)  neg++;
        else             zero++;
    } while (true);
    cout << "Positives = " << pos  << endl;
    cout << "Negatives = " << neg  << endl;
    cout << "Zeros     = " << zero << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Step trace for inputs `5, -2, 0, 7, -1`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x=5:   & pos=1,\; neg=0,\; zero=0 \\
x=-2:  & pos=1,\; neg=1,\; zero=0 \\
x=0:   & pos=1,\; neg=1,\; zero=1 \\
x=7:   & pos=2,\; neg=1,\; zero=1 \\
x=-1:  & \text{break} \Rightarrow \text{EXIT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final output:**
$$
\text{Positives} = 2,\quad \text{Negatives} = 1,\quad \text{Zeros} = 1
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Carefully**
> 1. **Missing `break` inside `switch` / loop** — causes 3-mark deduction for "incomplete output".
> 2. **Placing `update` after `continue` in `do…while`** — silently creates an **infinite loop**; the examiner will deduct for the program *not* terminating.
> 3. **Forgetting to exclude the sentinel value** in counting logic — sentinel `-1` will wrongly inflate the *negatives* count. The `if (x == -1) break;` MUST come *before* the classification.
> 4. **Using `=` instead of `==`** in conditions (e.g., `if (i = 5)`) — compiles to an *assignment*, not a comparison; lost marks in unit tests.
> 5. **Off-by-one errors** in `for` boundaries (e.g., `i < N` vs `i <= N`) — read the problem statement *twice* before finalising the condition.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Three iteration statements** — `while`, `for`, `do…while`. Only the third is exit-controlled.
- [x] **Minimum body executions** — `while`/`for` = **0**; `do…while` = **1**.
- [x] **Infinite loop idioms** — `while(true)`, `for(;;)`, `do{}while(true)`.
- [x] **`break`** terminates the *innermost* loop or `switch`.
- [x] **`continue`** skips the rest of the current iteration; jumps to the update in `for` and to the test in `while`/`do…while`.
- [x] **`return`** exits the current function; `exit(0)` terminates the whole program.
- [x] **`goto`** is legal but heavily penalised in KTU valuation when used unnecessarily.
- [x] **Range-based `for`** (`for (auto x : v)`) introduced in C++11 — covered in Module 1 of KTU 2024.
- [x] **Closed-form shortcuts** — sum $1$ to $n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$; sum of first $n$ evens $= n(n+1)$.
- [x] **Sentinel-controlled loops** use a special terminator value, e.g., `-1` or `0`.
- [x] **Counter-controlled loops** use a known range of indices.
- [x] **Menu-driven programs** always use `do…while` to guarantee the menu appears at least once.
- [x] **Trace table columns** = *Step | Condition | Body | Update | Output*.
- [x] **Pre-test vs Post-test** — a guaranteed common 3-mark short-answer in every KTU ESE.
- [x] **Always test boundary values** — e.g., $i=0$, $i=N$, $i=N+1$ when verifying loop correctness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Oriented Programming in Java - Class Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Object Oriented Programming in Java - Class Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)** under the KTU 2024 scheme, a **Class** is formally defined as a **user-defined reference data type** that acts as a *blueprint*, *template*, or *prototype* from which individual **objects** are instantiated. A class encapsulates **state** (represented by *fields* or *attributes*) and **behavior** (represented by *methods* or *member functions*) into a single logical unit, supporting the core OOP tenets of **encapsulation**, **abstraction**, and **modularity**.

In Java, a class is declared using the keyword `class`, and the body of the class is enclosed within a pair of curly braces `{ }`. According to the **Java Language Specification (JLS)**, a class body may contain fields, methods, constructors, instance initializers, static initializers, and nested type declarations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A class is **NOT** an object. A class is a logical description; an object is the physical realization occupying memory. This distinction is a high-weightage conceptual question in KTU Module 2 examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect**.

- The **architectural blueprint** of a house is the **Class**. It defines how many rooms, the color, the layout, the door positions, etc.
- The **actual house** built from that blueprint is the **Object**. You can build 50 houses (50 objects) from one blueprint (one class), and each house can have its own paint color or furniture (its own state), but they all follow the same structural plan (the class definition).

Another simple analogy:
- A **Class** is like a *cookie cutter* (the design).
- An **Object** is the *actual cookie* (the product).
- The dough is the *memory*; the cutter shapes the dough, just as the class shapes memory into a usable object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU:** Every Java program must reside inside a class. Even the simplest `HelloWorld` program has a class named `HelloWorld`. There is no such thing as a "function outside a class" in pure Java (except static methods of utility classes).

## 1.3 Fundamental Members of a Java Class

A class is composed of the following core members:

| Member Type | Keyword | Purpose | Memory Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Instance Variable** | (none) | Stores per-object state | Heap (per object) |
| **Static Variable** | `static` | Stores class-level shared state | Method Area (one copy) |
| **Instance Method** | (none) | Operates on object state | Stored in Method Area, executed on Stack |
| **Static Method** | `static` | Operates on class-level state | Stored in Method Area |
| **Constructor** | `<ClassName>` | Initializes new objects | Heap (during `new`) |
| **Initializer Block** | `{ }` / `static { }` | Runs at object/class load | Heap / Method Area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard:** When asked to "define a class", examiners expect mention of (1) it is a blueprint, (2) it encapsulates data + methods, (3) objects are instances of it, and (4) it is a reference type in Java. Missing any one of these four points typically results in a 1-mark deduction.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

In Java Class Fundamentals, the following constants are **language-enforced** (not user-configurable):

- **Maximum method parameters (practical):** **255** arguments (per JVM specification, with limits of 64KB on a single method signature).
- **Method overloading limit:** Only distinguished by parameter list (number, type, order) — **NOT** by return type alone.
- **Access modifier levels:** Exactly **4** levels — `private`, *default* (package-private), `protected`, `public`.
- **Class name convention:** Should follow **PascalCase** (e.g., `StudentRecord`), though the compiler itself imposes no such rule.
- **Source file rule:** A source file `X.java` can have at most **one** `public` class, and that class **must** be named `X`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Class — Structured Logic Breakdown

A Java class is built up in a layered fashion. Let us break it down step by step:

### Step 1: Class Declaration
A class is declared using the access modifier + optional non-access modifiers + `class` keyword + class name.

```java
[access_modifier] [non_access_modifier] class ClassName [extends Parent] [implements Interface1, Interface2] {
    // body
}
```

**Why this ordering matters:** Java's grammar is rigid. Access modifier (`public`) must come first, followed by non-access modifiers like `abstract`, `final`, `static` (in nested contexts), then `class`. Reversing them produces a compile-time error.

### Step 2: Field Declaration (State)
Fields represent the data held by an object.

```java
[access_modifier] [static] [final] [transient] [volatile] dataType fieldName [= initializer];
```

**How they work:** `static` fields are shared across all instances; instance fields are unique per object. `final` fields must be initialized exactly once.

### Step 3: Method Declaration (Behavior)
Methods define the actions an object can perform.

```java
[access_modifier] [static] [abstract] [final] [synchronized] returnType methodName(parameterList) [throws ExceptionList] {
    // body
}
```

### Step 4: Constructor Declaration (Initialization)
A constructor has the same name as the class and no return type.

```java
[access_modifier] ClassName(parameterList) {
    // initialization code
}
```

**How it works:** The `new` operator invokes the constructor to allocate memory and initialize the new object.

## 2.2 The `this` Keyword — The Hidden Reference

Every instance method and constructor receives an implicit parameter named `this` — a reference to the **current invoking object**.

- `this.field` → refers to the current object's field
- `this.method()` → invokes current object's method
- `this()` → calls another constructor in the same class (constructor chaining)
- `this.parameter = parameter` → resolves **shadowing** (parameter hides field with same name)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** `this` is a `final` reference — you can never reassign `this` to point to another object. Writing `this = null;` causes a compile-time error.

## 2.3 Access Control — The Four Pillars

Java defines **4** access levels. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests this matrix:

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff package) | Other Classes (diff package) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *default* (no keyword) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often think `protected` = `public within package`. WRONG. `protected` adds *subclass access* on top of package access. It is **stricter** than `public` but **looser** than default.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Template | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Class Declaration** | `class Name { }` | `class Student { }` |
| **Object Creation** | `ClassName obj = new ClassName();` | `Student s1 = new Student();` |
| **Method Call** | `object.method(args);` | `s1.setName("Rahul");` |
| **Field Access** | `object.field;` | `s1.age;` |
| **`this` Resolution** | `this.field = parameter;` | `this.name = name;` |
| **Static Access** | `ClassName.member;` | `Math.PI;` |
| **Constructor Chain** | `this(args);` (must be 1st line) | `this(0);` |
| **Memory of Reference** | $S_{ref} = 8 \text{ bytes (on 64-bit JVM)}$ | Pointer-sized |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Class fundamentals form the backbone of:

- **Software Architecture:** Designing microservice entities as classes.
- **Database Modeling:** ORM frameworks like **Hibernate** map Java classes to database tables.
- **GUI Development:** Swing/JavaFX treats every UI component as a class.
- **Embedded Systems:** Even microcontroller Java (e.g., **Java ME**) uses class-based state machines.
- **Test Automation:** Frameworks like **JUnit** define test cases as annotated methods inside classes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Connection:** In the **Spring Framework** (heavily used in Kerala's IT industry), every `@Component`, `@Service`, and `@RestController` is a Java class. Understanding class fundamentals is the prerequisite to mastering enterprise Java.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Building a Java Class — Exhaustive Walkthrough

Let us derive a complete, exam-ready `Student` class step by step, explaining the design rationale of every line.

### Step 1: Define the Class Skeleton

```java
class Student {
    // body will be added below
}
```

**Logic:** We start with the minimal valid class. The `class` keyword signals the JVM that this is a reference type. Without curly braces `{ }`, the class body is undefined and compilation fails.

### Step 2: Add Instance Fields (State)

```java
class Student {
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private double cgpa;
}
```

**Logic:** Each student has three pieces of state. They are declared `private` to enforce **encapsulation** — a KTU-favorite concept. Now external code cannot directly read or modify `name`, `rollNo`, or `cgpa`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `private` and not `public`?** If we made them `public`, anyone could write `s1.cgpa = 15.0;` (an invalid CGPA). With `private`, the data is safe, and we expose controlled access via getter/setter methods.

### Step 3: Add a Default Constructor

```java
class Student {
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private double cgpa;

    public Student() {
        this.name = "Unknown";
        this.rollNo = 0;
        this.cgpa = 0.0;
    }
}
```

**Logic:** A constructor with zero parameters is called the **default constructor**. If you do not write any constructor, Java auto-generates a public no-arg one. But the moment you write a parameterized constructor, Java stops generating the default one — a common exam trap.

### Step 4: Add a Parameterized Constructor (Constructor Overloading)

```java
    public Student(String name, int rollNo, double cgpa) {
        this.name = name;        // resolves shadowing
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.cgpa = cgpa;
    }
```

**Logic:** We have two constructors with different parameter lists. This is **constructor overloading** — distinguished by parameter signature, NOT by return type. The `this.name = name;` line is critical: without `this.`, both sides would refer to the parameter `name` (the local one shadows the field), and the field would remain uninitialized.

### Step 5: Add Getters and Setters (Encapsulation Accessors)

```java
    public String getName()    { return name; }
    public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }

    public int getRollNo()        { return rollNo; }
    public void setRollNo(int r)  { this.rollNo = r; }

    public double getCgpa()       { return cgpa; }
    public void setCgpa(double c) {
        if (c >= 0.0 && c <= 10.0) {
            this.cgpa = c;
        }
    }
}
```

**Logic:** The setter for `cgpa` includes a **validation guard** — this is the practical reason encapsulation exists. We protect the invariant $0.0 \leq \text{cgpa} \leq 10.0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "demonstrate encapsulation", you MUST show (1) private fields, (2) public getters, and (3) public setters with validation. Missing validation in the setter is the most common reason for losing 1–2 marks.

### Step 6: Add a Behavioral Method

```java
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Roll: " + rollNo + ", CGPA: " + cgpa);
    }
```

**Logic:** Behavior is the third pillar of a class. A `display()` method is an instance method because it operates on per-object state.

## 3.2 Complete Final Class

```java
class Student {
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private double cgpa;

    public Student() {
        this.name = "Unknown";
        this.rollNo = 0;
        this.cgpa = 0.0;
    }

    public Student(String name, int rollNo, double cgpa) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.cgpa = cgpa;
    }

    public String getName()           { return name; }
    public void setName(String n)     { this.name = n; }
    public int getRollNo()            { return rollNo; }
    public void setRollNo(int r)      { this.rollNo = r; }
    public double getCgpa()           { return cgpa; }
    public void setCgpa(double c) {
        if (c >= 0.0 && c <= 10.0) this.cgpa = c;
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Roll: " + rollNo + ", CGPA: " + cgpa);
    }
}
```

## 3.3 Object Creation and Memory Mapping

When we write:

```java
Student s1 = new Student("Rahul", 47, 9.2);
Student s2 = new Student("Anjali", 12, 8.8);
```

The JVM performs these steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step A: } \texttt{Student} \text{ class is loaded into the Method Area (once).} \\
&\text{Step B: } \texttt{new Student(...)} \text{ allocates memory in the Heap for the new object.} \\
&\text{Step C: } \text{Fields are default-initialized (name=null, rollNo=0, cgpa=0.0).} \\
&\text{Step D: } \text{The parameterized constructor is invoked, assigning explicit values.} \\
&\text{Step E: } \text{Reference } \texttt{s1} \text{ is created on the Stack, pointing to the Heap object.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Memory Layout Schematic (Textual)

```
STACK                              HEAP                              METHOD AREA
+----------------+                +-------------------+              +----------------+
| s1  [0xA1]  |--|------------>  | name: "Rahul"     |              | Student.class  |
+----------------+                | rollNo: 47        |              |  - field info  |
| s2  [0xA2]  |--|---|--+       | cgpa: 9.2         |              |  - method info |
+----------------+    |  |       +-------------------+              |  - constructor |
                      |  |       | name: "Anjali"    |              +----------------+
                      |  +-----> | rollNo: 12        |
                      |          | cgpa: 8.8         |
                      |          +-------------------+
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Instance vs Static Members

This is a high-frequency KTU Part A question.

| Feature | Instance Member | Static Member |
|---|---|---|
| **Keyword** | (none) | `static` |
| **Copies per class** | One per object | One per class |
| **Memory** | Heap (object-wise) | Method Area (single copy) |
| **Accessed via** | `object.member` | `ClassName.member` (preferred) |
| **Can access `this`?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (compile error) |
| **Can call instance methods?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (compile error, unless via object) |
| **Lifecycle** | Born with `new`, dies with object | Born at class load, dies at class unload |
| **Use case** | Per-object state | Counters, constants, utility methods |

**Why static cannot access `this`:** `this` refers to the *current object*. Static members exist even before any object is created, so there is no "current object" to refer to. Hence, accessing `this` from a static context is a **compile-time error**.

## 3.5 The `new` Operator — Exhaustive Analysis

The `new` operator performs three discrete operations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. Memory Allocation: } M_{heap} = \text{sizeOf(Class)} \\
&\text{2. Default Initialization: } \forall \text{ fields } f: f \leftarrow \text{defaultValue}(f) \\
&\text{3. Constructor Invocation: } \text{this} \leftarrow \text{new object}; \text{ constructor}(args) \text{ executes}
\end{aligned}
$$

If step 1 (memory allocation) fails (e.g., JVM out of memory), a `OutOfMemoryError` is thrown. If step 3 (constructor) throws an exception, the allocated memory is reclaimed by the garbage collector.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Lifecycle — Block-Level Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire lifecycle of a Java class from source code to object destruction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source File: Student.java] --> B[Java Compiler: javac]
    B --> C[Bytecode File: Student.class]
    C --> D[Class Loader Subsystem]
    D --> E[Method Area: Class Metadata Stored]
    E --> F{Static Members Initialized?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Static Variables Ready]
    F -- No --> E
    G --> H[Program Calls new Student]
    H --> I[Heap: Memory Allocated for Object]
    I --> J[Fields Default-Initialized]
    J --> K[Constructor Invoked]
    K --> L[Reference Variable Assigned on Stack]
    L --> M[Object is Live and Usable]
    M --> N{Reference Still Accessible?}
    N -- Yes --> M
    N -- No --> O[Object is Unreachable]
    O --> P[Garbage Collector Eligible]
    P --> Q[Memory Reclaimed]
    Q --> R[Object Destroyed]
```

## 4.2 Class Anatomy — Internal Block Diagram

The diagram below shows the internal structure of a Java class as a hierarchical block.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLASS["class Student"]
        direction TB
        subgraph STATE["STATE: Fields"]
            F1["private String name"]
            F2["private int rollNo"]
            F3["private double cgpa"]
        end
        subgraph BEHAVIOR["BEHAVIOR: Methods"]
            M1["public Student() - Default Constructor"]
            M2["public Student(String, int, double) - Parameterized Constructor"]
            M3["public String getName()"]
            M4["public void setName(String)"]
            M5["public void display()"]
        end
        subgraph STATIC["STATIC: Class-Level"]
            S1["public static int studentCount"]
            S2["public static final String COLLEGE = KTU"]
        end
    end
    CLASS --> OBJ1["Object 1: s1 = new Student"]
    CLASS --> OBJ2["Object 2: s2 = new Student"]
    OBJ1 --> H1["Heap: own copy of name, rollNo, cgpa"]
    OBJ2 --> H2["Heap: own copy of name, rollNo, cgpa"]
    STATIC --> SH["Method Area: one shared copy"]
```

## 4.3 Object Instantiation Sequence Diagram

This sequence diagram illustrates the step-by-step message flow during object creation.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main() method
    participant Stack as Stack Memory
    participant Heap as Heap Memory
    participant Method as Method Area
    Main->>Method: 1. Load Student.class
    Method-->>Main: 2. Class loaded, static init done
    Main->>Stack: 3. Declare reference s1
    Main->>Heap: 4. new Student("Rahul", 47, 9.2)
    Heap->>Heap: 5. Allocate memory
    Heap->>Heap: 6. Default-init fields
    Heap->>Heap: 7. Invoke parameterized constructor
    Heap-->>Stack: 8. Return memory address
    Stack->>Main: 9. s1 now points to object
    Main->>Heap: 10. s1.display()
    Heap-->>Main: 11. Output printed
```

## 4.4 Access Modifier Visibility Map

This diagram summarizes the four-tier access control system in Java.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["private: Same Class Only"] --> B["default: Same Class + Same Package"]
    B --> C["protected: Adds Subclass Access across Packages"]
    C --> D["public: Accessible Everywhere"]
    A -.->|most restrictive| A
    D -.->|least restrictive| D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each successive level **adds** accessibility; it never removes. `public` is a superset of `protected`, which is a superset of `default`, which is a superset of `private`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a class in Java. How is it different from an object?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **class** in Java is a user-defined reference data type that acts as a blueprint or template for creating objects. It encapsulates **data** (fields) and **behavior** (methods) into a single logical unit.

**Difference between Class and Object:**

| Aspect | Class | Object |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Logical blueprint | Physical instance |
| Memory | No memory allocated at declaration | Memory allocated via `new` |
| Declaration | `class Student { }` | `Student s1 = new Student();` |
| Keyword | Declared with `class` | Created with `new` |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for class nature, 1 mark for object nature with example.)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** What is the significance of the `this` keyword in Java? Give an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `this` keyword in Java is an **implicit reference variable** that points to the current invoking object. It is used to:

1. **Resolve name conflicts** between instance fields and parameters (shadowing).
2. **Invoke other constructors** in the same class via `this(args)` (constructor chaining).
3. **Pass the current object** as a parameter to other methods.

**Example:**

```java
class Box {
    int length;
    Box(int length) {
        this.length = length;  // this.length refers to field, length refers to parameter
    }
}
```

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for uses, 1 mark for correct example.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** With suitable example, explain the concept of a class and object in Java. Also illustrate encapsulation using a real-world class.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the syntax for declaring a class and creating an object in Java. Write a complete Java program demonstrating the same.**

**Model Solution:**

A class in Java is declared using the `class` keyword. Its general form is:

```java
access_modifier class ClassName {
    // fields
    // constructors
    // methods
}
```

An object is created using the `new` operator, which:
1. Allocates memory in the heap.
2. Invokes the constructor.
3. Returns a reference to the newly created object.

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
class Car {
    // Fields
    String brand;
    int speed;

    // Parameterized Constructor
    public Car(String brand, int speed) {
        this.brand = brand;
        this.speed = speed;
    }

    // Method
    public void drive() {
        System.out.println(brand + " is driving at " + speed + " km/h");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car c1 = new Car("Toyota", 120);  // Object 1
        Car c2 = new Car("Honda", 100);   // Object 2
        c1.drive();
        c2.drive();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Toyota is driving at 120 km/h
Honda is driving at 100 km/h
```

**Explanation of Memory:** When `new Car("Toyota", 120)` executes, memory is allocated in the heap, fields are default-initialized, then the constructor assigns "Toyota" and 120. The reference `c1` is stored in stack.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Demonstrate encapsulation in Java using a suitable class with private fields, public getters/setters, and validation logic.**

**Model Solution:**

Encapsulation is the OOP principle of **wrapping data and methods into a single unit** and **restricting direct access** to some of the object's components.

**Program: Encapsulated `BankAccount` class:**

```java
class BankAccount {
    private String accountHolder;
    private double balance;

    public BankAccount(String accountHolder, double balance) {
        this.accountHolder = accountHolder;
        if (balance >= 0) {
            this.balance = balance;
        } else {
            this.balance = 0;
        }
    }

    public String getAccountHolder() {
        return accountHolder;
    }

    public void setAccountHolder(String accountHolder) {
        this.accountHolder = accountHolder;
    }

    public double getBalance() {
        return balance;
    }

    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            balance += amount;
            System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid deposit amount");
        }
    }

    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid withdrawal");
        }
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new BankAccount("Rahul", 5000);
        acc.deposit(2000);
        acc.withdraw(1000);
        System.out.println("Final Balance: " + acc.getBalance());
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Deposited: 2000.0
Withdrawn: 1000.0
Final Balance: 6000.0
```

**Key Encapsulation Features Demonstrated:**
- `private` fields prevent direct external modification.
- Public getters provide **read-only** access.
- Public setters and business methods (deposit, withdraw) include **validation logic** to maintain data integrity.
- External code cannot directly set `balance = -1000;` — it must go through the validated `withdraw()` method.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write a setter like `public void setBalance(double b) { balance = b; }` without validation. This **fails the encapsulation test**. The setter must enforce business rules. Another common mistake is making fields `public` and then claiming the code demonstrates encapsulation — that is just data exposure, not encapsulation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]** Explain the access modifiers in Java with a comparison table. Write a program that demonstrates the use of `this` keyword in multiple scenarios.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the four access modifiers in Java with a comparison table and example.**

**Model Solution:**

Java provides **four** access control levels, controlling the visibility of classes, methods, and fields:

1. **`private`**: Accessible only within the **same class**.
2. **Default (package-private)**: Accessible within the same class and **same package**. Used when no modifier is specified.
3. **`protected`**: Accessible within the same class, same package, and **subclasses in any package**.
4. **`public`**: Accessible from **everywhere**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Different Package) | Other Classes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *default* | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

**Example Demonstrating `private` and `public`:**

```java
package ktu.oop;

public class Employee {
    private double salary = 50000;  // cannot be accessed outside this class

    public double getSalary() {     // public accessor
        return salary;
    }
}
```

```java
package ktu.main;
import ktu.oop.Employee;

public class TestEmployee {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e = new Employee();
        // e.salary = 100000;  // COMPILE ERROR: salary is private
        System.out.println(e.getSalary());  // OK: 50000.0
    }
}
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write a program demonstrating the use of `this` keyword in three scenarios: (i) resolving shadowing, (ii) constructor chaining, (iii) passing current object.**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Student {
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private Student mentor;  // for scenario (iii)

    // Default constructor chains to parameterized via this()
    public Student() {
        this("Unassigned", 0);  // Scenario (ii): constructor chaining
    }

    // Parameterized constructor
    public Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        this.name = name;        // Scenario (i): resolving shadowing
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.mentor = this;     // Scenario (iii): this as current object reference
    }

    public void printDetails() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Roll: " + rollNo);
        System.out.println("Self-reference same? " + (this == mentor));
    }

    public Student getSelf() {
        return this;  // Scenario (iii): returning current object
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student("Anjali", 12);
        Student s2 = new Student();

        s1.printDetails();
        s2.printDetails();

        Student ref = s1.getSelf();
        System.out.println("ref == s1 ? " + (ref == s1));
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Name: Anjali, Roll: 12
Self-reference same? true
Name: Unassigned, Roll: 0
Self-reference same? true
ref == s1 ? true
```

**Explanation of the three scenarios:**

- **(i) Shadowing Resolution:** In `this.name = name;`, the left side `this.name` refers to the instance field, while the right side `name` refers to the constructor parameter.
- **(ii) Constructor Chaining:** `this("Unassigned", 0);` invokes the parameterized constructor from the default constructor. This call **must be the first statement** in the constructor.
- **(iii) Current Object Reference:** `this` can be stored, returned, or passed. `return this;` returns the current object, enabling fluent-style programming.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. Using `this()` (constructor call) on a line that is **not the first statement** → compile-time error.
> 2. Trying to use `this` inside a `static` method → compile-time error.
> 3. Confusing `this()` (constructor call) with `this.field` (field access) — they look similar but do entirely different things.
> 4. Forgetting that **`this` is `final`** — students sometimes write `this = null;` thinking it works.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Class Fundamentals**

- ✅ A **class** is a blueprint; an **object** is an instance. Never use these terms interchangeably in exams.
- ✅ A class is declared with the `class` keyword, and the source file (if `public`) **must** match the class name.
- ✅ The `new` operator performs **3 steps**: memory allocation, default initialization, constructor invocation.
- ✅ A class can have **instance members** (per-object) and **static members** (per-class).
- ✅ `static` methods **cannot** access instance variables or call instance methods directly (no `this`).
- ✅ Java has **exactly 4 access levels**: `private`, default, `protected`, `public`. Know the table by heart.
- ✅ The `this` keyword refers to the **current invoking object**; it is a `final` reference.
- ✅ `this.field = param;` resolves **shadowing** when field and parameter have the same name.
- ✅ `this(args);` enables **constructor chaining** but **must be the first statement**.
- ✅ **Encapsulation = private fields + public getters/setters + validation in setters.**
- ✅ If you write a parameterized constructor, Java **stops** auto-generating the default no-arg constructor.
- ✅ Method overloading is decided by the **parameter list** (number, type, order) — **never** by return type alone.
- ✅ Reference variables live on the **Stack**; objects live on the **Heap**; class metadata lives in the **Method Area**.
- ✅ Garbage collector reclaims objects that have **no live reference** — `System.gc()` is a hint, not a command.
- ✅ Common exam traps: confusing class vs object, `static` vs instance, `this()` placement, and forgetting the default constructor rule.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Declaring Objects # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) under the **Java** language framework prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Declaring Objects** refers to the formal process of creating a **reference variable** of a class type, which can subsequently be used to point to (or refer to) an **instance** (object) of that class. Object declaration is a **two-step conceptual process**: (1) **Declaration** of a reference variable, and (2) **Instantiation** of the class using the `new` operator, which dynamically allocates memory in the **heap** and returns a memory address that is stored in the reference variable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** A *declared object reference* in Java is a *handle* (pointer-like variable stored in the **stack**) used to manipulate an *object* (the actual data structure stored in the **heap**). Declaring an object **does not** allocate memory for the object's fields — it only reserves space in the stack for a reference variable that can later hold a memory address. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – PEEIT741):** The KTU 2024 Module 2 explicitly differentiates between *primitive variables* and *object reference variables*. Object declaration always involves the **class name as the type**, distinguishing it from primitive data type declarations. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **house blueprint** (the *Class*) and you want to build an actual house from it. - The **blueprint itself** is **not a house** — it is merely a plan. - When you say *"I want a house of type Villa"*, you are essentially **declaring an object reference** — you have reserved a name (e.g., `myVilla`) but **no physical house exists yet**. - When the construction company says *"House is built at Plot No. 42, Green Park"*, this is the act of **instantiation** using `new`. A real house now exists at a physical location (memory address). - The variable `myVilla` now stores the *address* `Plot No. 42, Green Park` — you can visit, modify, or demolish the house **only through this address reference**. ### The Real-World Analogy in Plain English | Step | Real World | Java Equivalent | |------|------------|-----------------| | 1. Plan exists | House blueprint ready | `class Student { }` is defined | | 2. Declare intent | "I want a house named `myHouse`" | `Student myHouse;` | | 3. Build it | Contractor builds at address #101 | `myHouse = new Student();` | | 4. Use it | Drive to #101, open the door | `myHouse.study();` | > [!NOTE] > At step 2, **no house exists** — `myHouse` is simply a piece of paper with the address slot empty (or pointing to `null`). Only at step 3 does the actual house (object) get constructed in memory. --- ## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Components of an Object Declaration > [!IMPORTANT] > An "object" in Java is conceptually **split** into two distinct memory entities: > 1. **The Reference Variable** — lives in the **Stack memory**, holds the memory address of the object. > 2. **The Object Itself** — lives in the **Heap memory**, contains the actual instance data (fields) and behavior references (methods). --- ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Terminology - **Default value of an uninitialized reference variable:** `null` (a literal in Java indicating "no object is currently referenced"). - **Memory address size:** Implementation-dependent (typically 32 or 64 bits), but the programmer never manipulates it directly. - **Object size:** Sum of all instance fields + object header (typically **12–16 bytes** overhead on modern JVMs). - **Keyword used for instantiation:** `new` (a Java reserved keyword). - **Default initialization of object fields:** `0` (numeric), `false` (boolean), `null` (reference types) — guaranteed by the Java Language Specification (JLS). > [!NOTE] > **Bold constants/metrics** stated: **Stack** (for references), **Heap** (for objects), **`null`** (default reference value), **`new`** (instantiation operator), **0/false/null** (default field initialization values). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Distinct Phases of Object Creation A complete object lifecycle in Java consists of three logically separable phases. **Declaring objects** specifically encompasses Phases 1 and 2. ### Phase 1: Declaration of Reference Variable - A reference variable of a class type is declared. - **Memory allocated:** Space in the **Stack** for the reference (address holder). - **Default value of reference:** `null`. - **No heap memory** is allocated to the actual object at this point. ### Phase 2: Instantiation (Dynamic Memory Allocation) - The `new` keyword is invoked, followed by a constructor call. - The JVM allocates memory in the **Heap** for the new object. - All instance variables are **default-initialized** (0, false, or null). - The object's **constructor** is invoked to perform any custom initialization. - The `new` operator **returns the memory address** (reference) of the newly created object. ### Phase 3: Assignment (Linking Reference to Object) - The reference returned by `new` is **assigned** to the reference variable declared in Phase 1. - Now the reference variable in the Stack **points to** the actual object in the Heap. --- ## 2.2 Syntax Forms for Declaring Objects The KTU 2024 Module 2 prescribes the following canonical forms: ### Form 1: Declaration First, Instantiation Later (Two Statements) ```java ClassName objectName; // Phase 1: Declaration objectName = new ClassName(); // Phase 2 + 3: Instantiation and Assignment ``` ### Form 2: Combined Declaration and Instantiation (Most Common) ```java ClassName objectName = new ClassName(); ``` ### Form 3: Declaration with Deferred Initialization (Constructor Not Yet Called) ```java ClassName objectName; // Reference exists, points to null // ... some logic ... objectName = new ClassName(); // Object created later ``` ### Form 4: Multiple Reference Variables to the Same Object ```java ClassName ref1 = new ClassName(); ClassName ref2 = ref1; // Both ref1 and ref2 point to the SAME object ``` --- ## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step ### Why do we need a separate declaration step? The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **separation of concerns**: - **Declaration** is a **compile-time** activity — the compiler verifies the type. - **Instantiation** is a **run-time** activity — the JVM allocates memory dynamically. - This separation enables **polymorphism, late binding,** and **dynamic dispatch** which are foundational OOP concepts in later modules. ### How does memory allocation work? 1. The **JVM's class loader** verifies that the class definition exists in the **Method Area** (a logical part of the heap in modern JVMs). 2. The `new` instruction triggers the JVM to: - Compute the **total size** required (sum of all instance fields + object header). - Allocate a contiguous block in the **Heap** (specifically, in the **Eden Space** of the Young Generation). - **Zero-out** the memory (default initialization). - Invoke the appropriate **constructor** to perform custom initialization. 3. The **reference** (memory address) is pushed onto the **operand stack** and assigned to the reference variable. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Understanding the declaration-instantiation split is **critical** for: > - **Memory profiling** in large-scale applications. > - **Avoiding `NullPointerException`** (NPE), the most common runtime exception in Java. > - Designing **lazy initialization** patterns. > - Implementing **singleton** and **factory** design patterns. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes all key declarative concepts, their memory locations, default values, and behaviors. This is a **high-yield reference** for the KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. | **Concept** | **Syntax Pattern** | **Memory Location** | **Default Value** | **When Allocated** | **Lifetime** | |-------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------| | Reference Variable Declaration | `ClassName ref;` | Stack | `null` | Compile-time declaration, Run-time allocation | Until scope ends | | Object Instantiation | `new ClassName()` | Heap (Eden Space) | Fields: `0`/`false`/`null` | Run-time (JVM `new` opcode) | Until Garbage Collected | | Combined Declaration | `ClassName ref = new ClassName();` | Stack (ref) + Heap (object) | As above | Run-time | ref: scope / object: GC | | Multiple References | `ref2 = ref1;` | Stack (both refs) | N/A | Assignment at run-time | Until references are overwritten | | Anonymous Object | `new ClassName().method();` | Heap (temporary) | Default initialized | Run-time, eligible for GC after expression | Garbage collected immediately | | Array of Objects | `ClassName[] arr = new ClassName[N];` | Stack (ref) + Heap (array + N objects) | All refs: `null` (or objects created) | Run-time | Until GC | ### Operator Semantics Table | **Operator / Keyword** | **Function** | **Operates On** | **Returns** | |------------------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------| | `new` | Allocates heap memory & invokes constructor | Class name + constructor arguments | Reference to newly created object | | `.` (dot) | Member access operator | Reference variable + member name | Value of the member (or void for setters) | | `=` (assignment) | Binds a reference to a variable | Reference expression | The assigned reference | ### Boundary Conditions & Special Cases | **Scenario** | **Behavior** | **KTU Pitfall** | |--------------|-------------|-----------------| | Declared but uninitialized reference | Value is `null` | Calling any method → `NullPointerException` | | `new ClassName()` with no explicit constructor | Default no-arg constructor is used | If you define a parameterized constructor, default is **lost** | | Multiple references to same object | Modifications via one ref are visible via the other | Confusing aliasing behavior — exam favorite | | Object without reference (anonymous) | Eligible for **Garbage Collection** | Cannot be accessed later — exam trap | | `ref1 == ref2` | Compares **references (addresses)**, not content | To compare content, use `.equals()` method | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Highlight:** Students frequently confuse `==` (reference comparison) with `.equals()` (content comparison). This is a **frequently tested** distinction in KTU 2024 ESE questions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Analytical Example — Tracing Object Declaration Consider the following Java class definition: ```java class Student { String name; int rollNo; double marks; Student(String n, int r, double m) { name = n; rollNo = r; marks = m; } void display() { System.out.println(name + " " + rollNo + " " + marks); } } ``` Now trace the following declarations step-by-step: ```java Student s1; // Step 1 Student s2; // Step 2 s1 = new Student("Arjun", 1, 95.5); // Step 3 s2 = s1; // Step 4 s2 = new Student("Meera", 2, 88.0); // Step 5 ``` ### Step 1: `Student s1;` **Memory State after Step 1:** - A reference variable `s1` of type `Student` is created in the **Stack**. - `s1` holds the value **`null`** (no object is referenced). - **Heap:** unchanged (no object allocated). - **Verification:** `s1 == null` returns `true`. ### Step 2: `Student s2;` **Memory State after Step 2:** - A second reference variable `s2` of type `Student` is created in the **Stack**. - `s2` also holds the value **`null`**. - **Heap:** still unchanged. ### Step 3: `s1 = new Student("Arjun", 1, 95.5);` **Memory State after Step 3:** 1. The JVM evaluates `new Student("Arjun", 1, 95.5)`. 2. Memory is allocated in the **Heap** (Eden Space) for a new `Student` object. 3. Fields are **default-initialized**: - `name` → `null` - `rollNo` → `0` - `marks` → `0.0` 4. The **parameterized constructor** is invoked with arguments `n="Arjun"`, `r=1`, `m=95.5`. 5. Constructor body executes: - `name` is assigned `"Arjun"` - `rollNo` is assigned `1` - `marks` is assigned `95.5` 6. The `new` operator **returns the memory address** of the new object (call it `0xABC100`). 7. `s1` is assigned this address `0xABC100`. **Reference Diagram:** $$\text{s1 (Stack)} \xrightarrow{\text{points to}} \text{0xABC100 (Heap)} \rightarrow \text{Student\{name="Arjun", rollNo=1, marks=95.5\}}$$ ### Step 4: `s2 = s1;` **Memory State after Step 4:** - The **value of `s1`** (which is the address `0xABC100`) is **copied** into `s2`. - Both `s1` and `s2` now point to the **same object** in the heap. - **No new object is created** — this is a **reference assignment**, not an object creation. **Reference Diagram:** $$\text{s1} \rightarrow \text{0xABC100} \leftarrow \text{s2}$$ ### Step 5: `s2 = new Student("Meera", 2, 88.0);` **Memory State after Step 5:** 1. A **new object** is created in the heap at a fresh address (e.g., `0xDEF200`). 2. The constructor initializes the new object with `name="Meera"`, `rollNo=2`, `marks=88.0`. 3. `s2` is updated to point to the new object. 4. `s1` **still points** to the original object (`0xABC100`). 5. The original Arjun object remains reachable via `s1` — **not eligible for GC**. **Final Reference Diagram:** $$\text{s1} \rightarrow \text{0xABC100} \rightarrow \text{Student\{name="Arjun", rollNo=1, marks=95.5\}}$$ $$\text{s2} \rightarrow \text{0xDEF200} \rightarrow \text{Student\{name="Meera", rollNo=2, marks=88.0\}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Memory State at Each Step | **Step** | **Statement** | **s1 Value** | **s2 Value** | **Heap Objects** | **GC Eligible** | |----------|--------------|-------------|-------------|------------------|-----------------| | 1 | `Student s1;` | `null` | — | None | None | | 2 | `Student s2;` | `null` | `null` | None | None | | 3 | `s1 = new Student("Arjun", 1, 95.5);` | `0xABC100` | `null` | Arjun (1 obj) | None | | 4 | `s2 = s1;` | `0xABC100` | `0xABC100` | Arjun (1 obj) | None | | 5 | `s2 = new Student("Meera", 2, 88.0);` | `0xABC100` | `0xDEF200` | Arjun, Meera (2 objs) | None (both reachable) | --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Object Size Formula The total memory occupied by an object in the JVM is given by: $$ \text{Object Size} = \text{Object Header} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{SizeOf}(\text{field}_i) + \text{Padding} $$ Where: - $\text{Object Header}$ typically includes the **mark word** (8 bytes), **class pointer** (4 or 8 bytes), and **length** (for arrays only, 4 bytes). - $\text{SizeOf}(\text{field}_i)$ is the size of each instance field based on its type. **Example Calculation for the Student class:** Assume a 64-bit JVM with compressed oops (ordinary object pointers): $$ \text{Header} = 12 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{mark word} + \text{class pointer}) $$ $$ \text{Fields: } \text{String name} = 4 \text{ bytes (ref)}, \text{ int rollNo} = 4 \text{ bytes}, \text{ double marks} = 8 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \text{Total Fields} = 4 + 4 + 8 = 16 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \text{Raw Total} = 12 + 16 = 28 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \text{Padding to 8-byte boundary} = 32 \text{ bytes} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\text{Object Size} = 32 \text{ bytes (aligned)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > Note that the `String` itself is a **separate object** in the heap (typically 40+ bytes for short strings), and the `name` field in `Student` is merely a **reference** to that String. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand this **reference indirection**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: `NullPointerException` Derivation Consider the following erroneous code: ```java Student s3; s3.display(); // Compile-time OK, Runtime ERROR ``` ### Step-by-Step Failure Analysis: 1. **Compile-time:** The compiler sees `s3` declared as type `Student`, and `display()` is a valid method. **No compile error.** 2. **Run-time:** The JVM attempts to dereference `s3` to invoke `display()`. Since `s3 == null`, the JVM cannot find an object to dispatch the call to. 3. The JVM throws `java.lang.NullPointerException` (NPE) at the bytecode level — specifically, the `invokevirtual` JVM instruction detects a `null` reference on the operand stack. **Bytecode-level trace:** $$ \text{aload\_1} \rightarrow \text{Push s3 onto operand stack} \rightarrow \text{invokevirtual Student.display} \rightarrow \text{NPE} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **frequently asked concept** in KTU 2024 examinations. Students are expected to know that **declaration alone does not prevent NPE** — explicit instantiation is required. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Architecture for Object Declaration The following diagram illustrates the **Stack–Heap memory architecture** when objects are declared and instantiated. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STK["STACK MEMORY (Per Thread)"] refS1["s1 = 0xABC100"] refS2["s2 = 0xDEF200"] refNull["s3 = null"] end subgraph HEP["HEAP MEMORY (Shared)"] objA["Object at 0xABC100
name=Arjun
rollNo=1
marks=95.5"] objB["Object at 0xDEF200
name=Meera
rollNo=2
marks=88.0"] end refS1 -.->|reference arrow| objA refS2 -.->|reference arrow| objB refNull -.->|points to nothing| X["NULL - No Object"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Two-Phase Object Creation Process This flowchart depicts the **two logical phases** of object declaration as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Phase 1: DECLARATION
ClassName refName;"] --> B["Reference variable created in STACK
Default value: null
No heap allocation"] B --> C{"Phase 2: INSTANTIATION
refName = new ClassName()"} C --> D["JVM allocates memory in HEAP
Eden Space"] D --> E["Default initialize all fields
0, false, null"] E --> F["Invoke constructor
for custom initialization"] F --> G["Return memory address
to reference variable"] G --> H["Object is now usable
via refName.methodName()"] style A fill:#e1f5ff style C fill:#fff4e1 style H fill:#e1ffe1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Reference vs Object This diagram represents the **functional relationship** between reference variables, the heap, and the JVM runtime system — ideal for visualizing the **Object Lifecycle**. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COMP["COMPILE TIME"] C1["Source Code: Student s;"] C2["Compiler verifies type 'Student'"] C3["Generates reference variable
allocation bytecode"] end subgraph RUNTIME["RUN TIME (JVM)"] R1["new Student() invoked"] R2["Class Loader: Load class
from Method Area"] R3["Heap Allocator: Reserve
Eden Space block"] R4["Zero-fill memory
default init fields"] R5["Invoke constructor
custom init fields"] R6["Return heap address
to reference variable"] end subgraph GC["GARBAGE COLLECTION LIFECYCLE"] G1["Reference exists
Object reachable"] G2["All references lost
Object unreachable"] G3["GC marks object
for collection"] G4["Memory reclaimed
back to heap"] end C1 --> C2 --> C3 C3 --> R1 R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 R6 --> G1 G1 -->|"references = null"| G2 G2 --> G3 --> G4 style COMP fill:#e3f2fd style RUNTIME fill:#fff3e0 style GC fill:#ffebee ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Multiple References to a Single Object (Aliasing) This diagram illustrates the **aliasing phenomenon** — a frequently tested KTU concept. ```mermaid flowchart LR ref1["ref1 (Stack)"] -->|"address 0xAA01"| sharedObject["SINGLE OBJECT in Heap
data: x=10, y=20"] ref2["ref2 (Stack)"] -->|"address 0xAA01"| sharedObject ref3["ref3 (Stack)"] -->|"address 0xAA01"| sharedObject note["All three references point to
the SAME object.
Modifications via any ref
are visible to all others."] sharedObject -.-> note ``` --- ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Object Lifecycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Declared: ClassName ref; Declared --> Instantiated: new ClassName() Instantiated --> InUse: ref.methodCall() InUse --> InUse: Multiple references InUse --> Unreachable: All refs = null / out of scope Unreachable --> GarbageCollected: GC sweep GarbageCollected --> [*] note right of Declared: Reference in Stack
Value = null
No heap object note right of Instantiated: Object in Heap
Fields initialized
Reference bound note right of Unreachable: Eligible for GC
but not yet collected ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the difference between declaring an object reference and instantiating an object in Java. Why are these treated as two separate steps?** **Model Answer:** In Java, **declaring an object reference** involves creating a variable of a class type, which is stored in the **Stack** memory and initially holds the value `null`. For example, `Student s;` declares a reference `s` of type `Student`, but **no object is created** in the heap at this point. **Instantiating an object**, on the other hand, involves using the `new` keyword to allocate memory in the **Heap**, default-initialize the instance fields, invoke a constructor for custom initialization, and return the memory address (reference) to the reference variable. For example, `s = new Student();` actually creates the `Student` object. These two steps are separated for several reasons: 1. **Compile-time vs. Run-time separation** — declaration is a compile-time activity (type checking), while instantiation is a run-time activity (memory allocation). 2. **Flexibility for polymorphism** — a reference of a parent type can be declared first and instantiated later with a child class object. 3. **Lazy initialization** — objects can be created only when needed, saving memory. 4. **Clearer control flow** — separating the two steps makes the program's logic more readable and maintainable. > [!NOTE] > **Word Count Target:** 150–200 words. Marks awarded for: correct definition (1), correct memory location mention (1), reasons for separation (1). --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **What is the default value of an object reference variable in Java? What happens if you try to invoke a method on an uninitialized reference?** **Model Answer:** The default value of an object reference variable in Java is **`null`**. The literal `null` is a special reference value that indicates the reference variable does not currently point to any object in memory. If you attempt to invoke a method on a reference variable that holds `null`, the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) will throw a **`java.lang.NullPointerException` (NPE)** at runtime. This happens because the JVM tries to dereference the `null` reference to access the object's method, but no object exists at that location. For example, the following code compiles successfully but throws an NPE at runtime: ```java Student s; // s = null s.display(); // Runtime: NullPointerException ``` To avoid NPE, the reference must be explicitly initialized using `new` before any method invocation, or a null-check should be performed using conditional statements. > [!NOTE] > **Word Count Target:** 120–150 words. Marks awarded for: default value (1), NPE behavior (1), example/mitigation (1). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **a) With suitable diagrams, explain the process of declaring and instantiating objects in Java. Differentiate clearly between stack and heap memory in this context. (7 marks)** **b) Consider the following Java class:** ```java class Box { int length, width, height; Box(int l, int w, int h) { length = l; width = w; height = h; } int volume() { return length * width * height; } } ``` **Trace the following code, showing the memory state (stack and heap) at each step:** ```java Box b1; Box b2 = new Box(2, 3, 4); b1 = b2; b1 = new Box(5, 6, 7); b2 = null; ``` **What will be the output of `b1.volume()` and `b2.volume()` after these statements? Justify your answer. (7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) Model Solution: **Declaring and Instantiating Objects in Java:** The process of creating an object in Java involves two distinct steps: **declaration** of a reference variable and **instantiation** of the class using the `new` operator. **Step 1: Declaration of a Reference Variable** A reference variable of a class type is declared using the syntax: ```java ClassName referenceName; ``` At this stage: - A variable named `referenceName` is created in the **Stack memory**. - Its initial value is **`null`**, indicating it does not point to any object. - **No memory is allocated in the heap.** - The compiler verifies that the class `ClassName` exists in the program's scope. **Step 2: Instantiation Using `new`** The `new` operator is used to create an actual object: ```java referenceName = new ClassName(arguments); ``` At this stage: - The JVM **allocates memory in the Heap** (Eden Space) for the new object. - All instance fields are **default-initialized** (`0` for int, `false` for boolean, `null` for references). - The appropriate **constructor** is invoked to perform custom initialization. - The `new` operator **returns a memory address** (reference) of the newly created object. - This address is **stored in the reference variable** in the Stack. **Combined Form:** ```java ClassName referenceName = new ClassName(arguments); ``` **Difference Between Stack and Heap Memory:** | **Aspect** | **Stack Memory** | **Heap Memory** | |-----------|------------------|-----------------| | Purpose | Stores reference variables and primitive local variables | Stores actual objects and instance data | | Allocation | Static (compile-time scope) | Dynamic (run-time allocation by `new`) | | Access Speed | Faster (LIFO structure) | Slower (garbage-collected) | | Lifetime | Until method/scope ends | Until object is garbage-collected | | Thread Safety | Private to each thread | Shared across threads | | Size | Limited, small | Large, configurable via JVM flags | **Memory Diagram for `Box b = new Box(2, 3, 4);`:** ``` STACK HEAP +--------+ +-------------------+ | b | --(address 0xA1)--> | length = 2 | +--------+ | width = 3 | | height = 4 | +-------------------+ ``` --- #### Part (b) Model Solution: **Trace Table (Memory State at Each Step):** | **Step** | **Statement** | **b1 Value** | **b2 Value** | **Heap Objects** | **b1.volume()** | **b2.volume()** | |----------|--------------|-------------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------| | 1 | `Box b1;` | `null` | — | None | NPE | — | | 2 | `Box b2 = new Box(2, 3, 4);` | `null` | `0xA1` | Box(2,3,4) | NPE | 24 | | 3 | `b1 = b2;` | `0xA1` | `0xA1` | Box(2,3,4) | 24 | 24 | | 4 | `b1 = new Box(5, 6, 7);` | `0xA2` | `0xA1` | Box(2,3,4), Box(5,6,7) | 210 | 24 | | 5 | `b2 = null;` | `0xA2` | `null` | Box(2,3,4), Box(5,6,7) | 210 | NPE | **Final Output:** - `b1.volume()` returns **`210`** (5 × 6 × 7) - `b2.volume()` throws **`NullPointerException`** because `b2` is now `null`. **Justification:** After step 5, `b1` points to the `Box(5, 6, 7)` object at `0xA2`, and `b2` holds `null`. The original `Box(2, 3, 4)` object is **no longer reachable** from any reference, making it eligible for garbage collection. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to update both references after `b1 = b2;`** — students often assume `b1` and `b2` are still independent. **Lose 1–2 marks.** > 2. **Not mentioning the original `Box(2,3,4)` becomes eligible for GC** after step 5 — KTU 2024 specifically tests GC eligibility. **Lose 1 mark.** > 3. **Confusing `b1.volume()` returning 24 vs 210** — students must carefully track which object each reference points to at each step. **Lose 2 marks.** > 4. **Forgetting NPE on `b2.volume()`** after `b2 = null` — this is a critical runtime consequence. **Lose 1 mark.** --- ### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **a) Explain with examples the different ways of declaring and initializing objects in Java. What is the significance of the `new` keyword? Discuss with reference to memory allocation. (7 marks)** **b) Given the following class definition, identify and correct the errors in the code below. Explain each error in detail:** ```java class Car { String model; int year; Car(String m, int y) { model = m; year = y; } void show() { System.out.println(model + " " + year); } } public class TestCar { public static void main(String[] args) { Car c1; c1.show(); Car c2 = new Car(); Car c3 = Car("Honda", 2022); c1 = c3; c1.model = "Toyota"; c3.show(); } } ``` **What will be the output of the corrected program? (7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) Model Solution: **Different Ways of Declaring and Initializing Objects in Java:** **Method 1: Declaration First, Instantiation Later** ```java Student s; // Declaration only s = new Student(); // Instantiation in a separate statement ``` **Method 2: Combined Declaration and Instantiation (Most Common)** ```java Student s = new Student(); ``` **Method 3: Anonymous Object (No Reference Stored)** ```java new Student().display(); // Object created, used immediately, eligible for GC ``` **Method 4: Multiple References to the Same Object** ```java Student s1 = new Student(); Student s2 = s1; // Both refer to the same object ``` **Method 5: Array of Objects** ```java Student[] arr = new Student[5]; // Array of 5 null references arr[0] = new Student(); // Each element must be individually created ``` **Significance of the `new` Keyword:** The `new` keyword is a **Java reserved keyword** that serves three critical functions: 1. **Memory Allocation** — The JVM dynamically allocates memory in the **Heap** (Eden Space) for the new object, sized to fit all instance fields plus the object header. 2. **Default Initialization** — All instance fields are automatically initialized to their default values (`0`, `false`, or `null`). 3. **Constructor Invocation** — The appropriate constructor is called to perform custom initialization logic. 4. **Return Reference** — The `new` expression evaluates to the **memory address** of the newly created object, which is then assigned to the reference variable. Without `new`, no object would be created in the heap, and only a `null` reference would exist — leading to `NullPointerException` upon use. **Memory Allocation Diagram:** ``` Before 'new': After 'new Student()': +--------+ +--------+ | s | ------> | s | --(0xB7)--> [Student Object in Heap] | (null) | +--------+ - name: null +--------+ - rollNo: 0 ``` --- #### Part (b) Model Solution: **Error Identification and Correction:** | **Line** | **Original Code** | **Error Type** | **Corrected Code** | **Explanation** | |----------|------------------|----------------|---------------------|-----------------| | `c1.show();` | `c1.show();` | **Runtime Error: NullPointerException** | `c1 = new Car("Default", 0); c1.show();` | `c1` is declared but not instantiated; calling a method on a `null` reference causes NPE. | | `Car c2 = new Car();` | `Car c2 = new Car();` | **Compile-time Error** | `Car c2 = new Car("Default", 0);` | The class has only a parameterized constructor; the default no-arg constructor is **not auto-generated** when a parameterized constructor is defined. | | `Car c3 = Car("Honda", 2022);` | `Car c3 = Car("Honda", 2022);` | **Compile-time Error** | `Car c3 = new Car("Honda", 2022);` | The `new` keyword is **mandatory** for object instantiation; `Car("Honda", 2022)` is treated as a method call, not object creation. | **Corrected Program:** ```java class Car { String model; int year; Car(String m, int y) { model = m; year = y; } void show() { System.out.println(model + " " + year); } } public class TestCar { public static void main(String[] args) { Car c1 = new Car("Default", 0); // Fixed: instantiate before use c1.show(); Car c2 = new Car("Default", 0); // Fixed: pass required args Car c3 = new Car("Honda", 2022); // Fixed: added 'new' keyword c1 = c3; // c1 now points to Honda object c1.model = "Toyota"; // Modifies the object via c1 c3.show(); // Visible: same object as c1 } } ``` **Output Trace:** | **Step** | **Statement** | **c1 points to** | **c3 points to** | **Output** | |----------|--------------|------------------|------------------|------------| | 1 | `c1 = new Car("Default", 0);` | Default object | — | — | | 2 | `c1.show();` | Default object | — | `Default 0` | | 3 | `c2 = new Car("Default", 0);` | Default object | — | — | | 4 | `c3 = new Car("Honda", 2022);` | Default object | Honda object | — | | 5 | `c1 = c3;` | Honda object | Honda object | — | | 6 | `c1.model = "Toyota";` | Toyota object (modified) | Toyota object (visible) | — | | 7 | `c3.show();` | Toyota object | Toyota object | `Toyota 2022` | **Final Output:** ``` Default 0 Toyota 2022 ``` **Justification:** After `c1 = c3;`, both `c1` and `c3` point to the **same** Honda object. When `c1.model = "Toyota"` is executed, the modification is reflected in the shared object. Hence, `c3.show()` prints `"Toyota 2022"`, not `"Honda 2022"`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Missing the `new` keyword in `Car("Honda", 2022)`** — this is a **compile-time error**, not a runtime error.
