Many students wrongly classify it as a runtime issue. **Lose 2 marks.** > 2. **Not recognizing that the default no-arg constructor is lost** when a parameterized constructor is defined — this is a **classic KTU favorite**. **Lose 1–2 marks.** > 3. **Forgetting that `c1 = c3` creates aliasing** — students often think `c3` retains its original Honda value. **Lose 2 marks.** > 4. **Final output confusion** — students may write `Honda 2022` instead of `Toyota 2022`. **Lose 1 mark.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Declaring Objects** - **Object creation is a two-step process:** (1) Declaration of reference in **Stack**, (2) Instantiation using `new` in **Heap**. - **Reference variables** store memory addresses, not the object itself; they are stored in the **Stack**. - **Objects** (actual data) are stored in the **Heap**, specifically in the **Eden Space** of the Young Generation. - The default value of any uninitialized reference variable is **`null`**. - The `new` keyword performs **memory allocation**, **default initialization**, and **constructor invocation**. - After `new ClassName()`, the returned reference must be assigned to a reference variable to be retained; otherwise, the object is **eligible for garbage collection** (anonymous object). - **Multiple references** can point to the **same object** (aliasing); modifications via one reference are visible through all others. - Declaring a reference does **not** create an object — calling a method on an uninitialized reference causes **`NullPointerException`** at runtime. - The **default no-arg constructor** is **automatically provided by the compiler only if no constructor is explicitly defined**. Once you define any constructor, the default is removed. - The `==` operator compares **references (addresses)**, not object **content**; use `.equals()` for content comparison. - Object size = **Object Header (12 bytes)** + **Sum of field sizes** + **Padding to 8-byte alignment**. - **Local reference variables** are destroyed when the method/scope ends; **objects** are destroyed only when **garbage collected** after becoming unreachable. - Arrays of objects are declared in two stages: the **array** is allocated first (with `null` references), and then each element is **individually instantiated**. - **Static variables** belong to the class (in the Method Area), not to objects — they are not part of instance object memory. > [!NOTE] > **End of Topic Notes — Declaring Objects (PEEIT741, Module 2)**

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Method**: A *method* in Java is a named, self-contained block of statements designed to perform a specific, well-defined task. It is a member of a class that combines the attributes of a *function*, a *procedure*, and a *sub-routine* into a single, object-oriented construct. Methods provide **modularity**, **reusability**, and **abstraction** in Java programs. A method is fundamentally characterized by the **6-tuple signature**: $$\text{MethodSignature} = \langle \text{AccessModifier},\ \text{StaticKeyword},\ \text{ReturnType},\ \text{MethodName},\ \text{ParameterList},\ \text{ExceptionList} \rangle$$ In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020), methods are positioned as the **primary units of behavior encapsulation** within the Object-Oriented paradigm, sitting at the intersection of the *Class* and *Object* layers of the OOP hierarchy. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: The Vending Machine (Auto-Mata)** > > Think of a method like a **vending machine**: > - **Machine Name (Method Name)**: The label "Cola-Matic" — how you refer to it. > - **Input Panel (Parameter List)**: The coin slot and selection buttons — what you provide. > - **Internal Mechanism (Method Body)**: The hidden machinery that processes your request. > - **Output Tray (Return Value)**: The can of cola that drops out — what you get back. > - **Restricted Access (Access Modifier)**: Some buttons are on the front (public), some require a service key (private). > > You don't need to know *how* the vending machine works internally (abstraction) — you just press a button (invoke the method) and receive the result. ### 1.3 Why Methods? — The Three Pillars > [!TIP] > The KTU 2024 OOP module explicitly tests the **rationale** behind methods. Memorize the "**M-R-D**" trio: > > 1. **M — Modularity**: Break a large problem into small, manageable pieces. > 2. **R — Reusability**: Write once, call many times (`$DRY$` principle — *Don't Repeat Yourself*). > 3. **D — Decomposition (Abstraction)**: Hide internal complexity, expose only the interface. --- ### 1.4 Pre-Requisite Vocabulary (KTU Module 2 Anchor Terms) | Anchor Term | Meaning | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Signature** | The unique identifier of a method (name + parameter list) | `add(int, int)` | | **Return Type** | The data type of value the method hands back | `int`, `double`, `void` | | **Formal Parameter** | The variable in the method's declaration | `int a` in `sum(int a)` | | **Actual Argument** | The real value passed during a call | `5` in `sum(5)` | | **Caller** | The code segment that invokes the method | `main()` calling `sum()` | | **Callee** | The method being invoked | `sum()` being called | | **Stack Frame** | A memory block created in call stack per invocation | Local variable storage | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Method — The 7 Mandatory/Optional Parts A complete Java method declaration consists of **up to 7 components**, parsed left-to-right by the compiler. The KTU examiner's *favorite* 3-mark question is to ask students to label these parts. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MethodDeclaration} \equiv\ & \underbrace{\text{[access\_modifier]}}_{\text{Part 1}} \underbrace{\text{[static]}}_{\text{Part 2}} \underbrace{\text{[final/abstract]}}_{\text{Part 3}} \\ & \underbrace{\text{return\_type}}_{\text{Part 4}} \underbrace{\text{method\_name}}_{\text{Part 5}} \underbrace{(\text{parameter\_list})}_{\text{Part 6}} \\ & \underbrace{\text{[throws exception\_list]}}_{\text{Part 7}} \underbrace{\{ \text{method\_body} \}}_{\text{Part 8}} \end{aligned} $$ #### Part-by-Part Breakdown 1. **Access Modifier (Mandatory Optional)** — Controls *visibility*. Allowed values: `public`, `private`, `protected`, or *default* (package-private). 2. **Static Keyword (Optional)** — When present, the method belongs to the **class**, not to instances. Called via `ClassName.methodName()`. 3. **Non-Access Modifier (Optional)** — e.g., `final` (cannot be overridden), `abstract` (must be overridden in subclass), `synchronized`. 4. **Return Type (Mandatory)** — The data type of value the method returns. Use `void` if nothing is returned. 5. **Method Name (Mandatory)** — A valid Java identifier. **KTU convention**: use *camelCase* verbs (`calculateTotal`, `getName`). 6. **Parameter List (Mandatory, but can be empty `()`)** — Comma-separated `type name` pairs. Each is a *formal parameter* acting as a *local variable*. 7. **Throws Clause (Optional)** — Declares checked exceptions the method may propagate. 8. **Method Body (Mandatory, but can be empty `{}`)** — The block of executable statements enclosed in curly braces. ### 2.2 The Method Signature — KTU's Most Tested Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Board Standard)**: The *method signature* in Java consists **only** of the **method name** and the **parameter list** (their types and order). The return type, access modifier, and exception list are **NOT** part of the signature. $$ \text{Signature} = \text{MethodName} + \text{ParameterList} \quad \text{(return type NOT included)} $$ **Consequence**: Two methods with the same name and parameter list but different return types **CANNOT coexist** in the same class — it is a **compile-time error**. ```java // VALID: Different parameter lists => Different signatures int sum(int a, int b) { return a + b; } double sum(double a, double b) { return a + b; } // INVALID: Same signature, only return type differs => COMPILE ERROR int compute(int x) { return x; } double compute(int x) { return x * 1.0; } // ❌ Duplicate method ``` ### 2.3 KTU Formula / Rule Sheet — "Methods at a Glance" | # | Rule / Formula | Statement | KTU Exam Tip | |:--|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Method Memory Cost** | Each invocation creates a new **stack frame** of size $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(p_i) + \text{sizeof}(\text{return addr})$ | Asked in 2-mark questions | | 2 | **Return Type Rule** | $\text{return\_value} \ \text{must be assignable to} \ \text{return\_type}$ | `return 5.0;` in `int f()` ❌ | | 3 | **Void Method Return** | A `void` method **may** use a bare `return;` to exit early | `return;` (no value) only in `void` | | 4 | **Parameter Pass Mechanism** | Java is **Pass-By-Value** *always* (even for object references, the reference is copied) | Highest-yield 7-mark question | | 5 | **Overloading Resolution** | $\text{Compile-time matching} = \text{Exact} \to \text{Widening} \to \text{Autoboxing} \to \text{Varargs}$ | Order matters! | | 6 | **Static Method Restriction** | A `static` method **cannot** use `this` or `super` keywords | Asked in MCQ section | | 7 | **Main Method Signature** | `public static void main(String[] args)` — fixed by JVM contract | Guaranteed 3-mark question | | 8 | **Local Variable Scope** | A local variable lives within the **smallest enclosing braces** `{}` of its declaration | Common source of bugs | ### 2.4 Classification of Methods (KTU Taxonomy) > [!NOTE] > For the 2024 scheme, methods are classified along **two axes**: > > **Axis 1: By Declaration Context** > - **Instance Methods**: Belong to an object (require `new ClassName()` to call) > - **Static (Class) Methods**: Belong to the class itself > - **Abstract Methods**: Declared without a body (must be implemented in subclass) > - **Final Methods**: Cannot be overridden by subclasses > > **Axis 2: By Operational Behavior** > - **Accessor (Getter)**: Returns a field's value (`getName()`) > - **Mutator (Setter)**: Modifies a field's value (`setName(...)`) > - **Helper/Utility**: Private methods used internally > - **Constructor (special)**: A method-like block invoked at object creation > - **Recursive Method**: Calls itself (e.g., factorial) ### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters Beyond KTU In real-world **software engineering**: - Methods underpin **microservices architecture** (each endpoint ≈ a method). - The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** maps every method call to an **invokevirtual**, **invokestatic**, or **invokeinterface** bytecode instruction — directly affecting JIT compilation and runtime performance. - The *method signature contract* is the foundation of **API design** (e.g., `java.util.Collections.sort(List)`). - **Stack-based execution** ensures methods are re-entrant and thread-safe by default for primitive locals. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1: The Simplest `void` Method > **Problem**: Write and analyze a Java method that prints a greeting. **Source Code:** ```java public class Greeter { // Method declaration begins here public static void sayHello() { System.out.println("Hello, KTU Student!"); } // Method declaration ends here } ``` **Step-by-Step Analytical Trace:** | Line | Token Parsed | Role | KTU Explanation | |:----:|:-------------|:-----|:----------------| | 1 | `public` | Part 1 — Access Modifier | Visible from any class in the application. | | 2 | `static` | Part 2 — Non-Access Modifier | Callable **without** creating an object: `Greeter.sayHello();` | | 3 | `void` | Part 4 — Return Type | The method returns **nothing**. | | 4 | `sayHello` | Part 5 — Method Name | A verb in camelCase. | | 5 | `()` | Part 6 — Parameter List | Empty, so it accepts **zero** inputs. | | 6 | `{}` | Part 8 — Method Body | Contains the executable `println` statement. | **Signature:** `sayHello()` — no parameters, no return. **Call site:** `Greeter.sayHello();` from `main()`. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Parameterized Method with Return Value > **Problem**: Write a method that computes the sum of two integers. **Source Code:** ```java public class Calculator { public static int add(int a, int b) { int sum = a + b; // local variable return sum; // return statement } public static void main(String[] args) { int result = Calculator.add(10, 20); System.out.println("Sum = " + result); // Output: Sum = 30 } } ``` **Exhaustive Execution Trace (Stack Memory Model):** Let $T_0$ be the entry into `main()`. The JVM performs the following: 1. JVM pushes a stack frame for `main()`. 2. `args` reference is stored in the local variable array of `main`. 3. Line `int result = Calculator.add(10, 20);` triggers an `invokestatic` bytecode. 4. JVM pushes a **new stack frame** for `add()` on top of `main`'s frame. 5. Formal parameters are bound: $a \leftarrow 10$, $b \leftarrow 20$ (values copied). 6. Local variable `sum` is created and computed: $sum = a + b = 10 + 20 = 30$. 7. `return sum;` copies 30 into `main`'s local variable `result` and pops the `add()` frame. 8. `System.out.println` prints the result. **Mathematical Representation of the Call:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State}(main\_frame) &= \langle args, result \rangle \\ \text{State}(add\_frame) &= \langle a = 10,\ b = 20,\ sum = 30 \rangle \\ \text{ReturnValue} &= 30 \\ \text{State}'(main\_frame) &= \langle args,\ result = 30 \rangle \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Pass-By-Value (The KTU Trap Question) > **Problem**: What is the output of the following program? *(Classic KTU trick question)* ```java public class PassByValueDemo { static void modify(int x) { x = x + 100; System.out.println("Inside modify: x = " + x); } public static void main(String[] args) { int x = 10; modify(x); System.out.println("Inside main: x = " + x); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Derivation:** Let the variable in `main` be $x_{main} = 10$ and the formal parameter in `modify` be $x_{modify}$. 1. `main` calls `modify(x)` — but Java **copies the value** of $x_{main}$ into $x_{modify}$. 2. Inside `modify`: $x_{modify} = x_{modify} + 100 = 10 + 100 = 110$. 3. `modify` prints: `Inside modify: x = 110`. 4. `modify` returns; its stack frame is destroyed. $x_{modify}$ is gone. 5. Back in `main`, $x_{main}$ is **unchanged**, still $10$. 6. `main` prints: `Inside main: x = 10`. **Expected Output:** ``` Inside modify: x = 110 Inside main: x = 10 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap**: Many students wrongly answer `x = 110` for both lines. Remember: **Java is strictly Pass-By-Value**. The formal parameter is a *separate local variable*. Modifying it cannot affect the caller's variable. ### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Method Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism) > **Problem**: Demonstrate three valid overloads of a method named `multiply`. ```java public class OverloadDemo { // Overload 1: int parameters static int multiply(int a, int b) { return a * b; } // Overload 2: double parameters (different signature) static double multiply(double a, double b) { return a * b; } // Overload 3: three int parameters (different arity) static int multiply(int a, int b, int c) { return a * b * c; } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(multiply(2, 3)); // -> 6 (calls Overload 1) System.out.println(multiply(2.5, 4.0)); // -> 10.0 (calls Overload 2) System.out.println(multiply(1, 2, 3)); // -> 6 (calls Overload 3) } } ``` **Resolution Algorithm (used by the compiler):** $$ \text{Match}(call) = \text{Phase}_1 \to \text{Phase}_2 \to \text{Phase}_3 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Phase}_1 &: \text{Exact match (no conversion)} \\ \text{Phase}_2 &: \text{Widening primitive conversion (e.g., int} \to \text{long)} \\ \text{Phase}_3 &: \text{Autoboxing / Varargs} \end{aligned} $$ > The compiler picks the **first** match in this order. A method ambiguity error occurs if two methods match at the **same phase** with equal preference. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Void vs. Value-Returning Methods | Property | `void` Method | Value-Returning Method | |:---|:---|:---| | **Declares return type?** | Implicitly `void` | Yes (`int`, `double`, etc.) | | **Must have `return value;`?** | No (optional bare `return;`) | **Yes**, unless it throws | | **Can be used in an expression?** | No | Yes, e.g., `int y = square(5);` | | **Used for** | Side-effects (printing, modifying state) | Computing and returning a result | | **Example** | `setName(String n)` | `getName()` | | **Equivalent in C** | `void func()` | `int func()` | ### 3.6 Recursion: A Method Calling Itself > **Problem**: Compute the factorial $n!$ using a recursive method. **Mathematical Recurrence:** $$ f(n) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \text{ (base case)} \\ n \times f(n-1) & \text{if } n \geq 1 \text{ (recursive case)} \end{cases} $$ **Java Implementation:** ```java public class Factorial { static long factorial(int n) { if (n == 0) return 1; // Base case return n * factorial(n - 1); // Recursive case } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println(factorial(5)); // Output: 120 } } ``` **Step-by-Step Unwinding for $n = 4$:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(4) &= 4 \times f(3) \\ &= 4 \times (3 \times f(2)) \\ &= 4 \times 3 \times (2 \times f(1)) \\ &= 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times (1 \times f(0)) \\ &= 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 \times 1 \\ &= 24 \end{aligned} $$ ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style Model]** --- **Q1.** *What is a method in Java? List any four components of a method signature with an example.* `[3 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A method in Java is a named collection of statements that performs a specific task and is a member of a class. It enables **modularity, reusability, and abstraction** in object-oriented programs. The four essential components of a method declaration are: 1. **Access Modifier** — e.g., `public`, `private`, `protected` (controls visibility). 2. **Return Type** — the data type of the value returned, e.g., `int`, `double`, `void`. 3. **Method Name** — a valid Java identifier following camelCase, e.g., `calculateArea`. 4. **Parameter List** — comma-separated list of typed inputs enclosed in parentheses, e.g., `(int radius, double pi)`. **Example:** ```java public double calculateArea(double radius) { return 3.14159 * radius * radius; } ``` Here, `public` is the access modifier, `double` is the return type, `calculateArea` is the method name, and `(double radius)` is the parameter list. --- **Q2.** *Differentiate between a `void` method and a value-returning method in Java. Give one example of each.* `[3 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | `void` Method | Value-Returning Method | |:---|:---|:---| | **Return type** | `void` (no return) | A specific data type (`int`, `double`, etc.) | | **Returns a value?** | No | Yes, via the `return` statement | | **Usable in expressions?** | No | Yes (e.g., `x = square(5);`) | | **Primary purpose** | Perform a side-effect (e.g., printing) | Compute and return a result | **Example of `void` method:** ```java public static void greet(String name) { System.out.println("Hello, " + name); } ``` **Example of value-returning method:** ```java public static int square(int n) { return n * n; } ``` --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts: (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Choose either A or B* **Q3 (a).** *Explain the general syntax of declaring a method in Java. Describe the role of access modifiers, return type, method name, and parameter list with a suitable example.* `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The general syntax for declaring a method in Java is: ```java [access_modifier] [static] [final|abstract] return_type method_name(parameter_list) [throws exception_list] { // method body [return value;] } ``` **Description of components:** 1. **Access Modifier**: Specifies the visibility of the method. The four allowed values are `public` (visible everywhere), `private` (visible only within the class), `protected` (visible within the package and subclasses), and *default* (package-private). 2. **Return Type**: Specifies the data type of the value the method returns. Use `void` if the method does not return any value. The return value must be compatible with the declared return type. 3. **Method Name**: A valid Java identifier that names the method. By KTU convention, method names should be **verbs in camelCase**, e.g., `calculateTotal`, `getName`. 4. **Parameter List**: A comma-separated list of `type name` pairs enclosed in parentheses. These are the *formal parameters* that act as local variables inside the method body. **Suitable Example:** ```java public class CircleUtility { public static double calculateCircumference(double radius) { final double PI = 3.14159; return 2 * PI * radius; } } ``` Here, `public` is the access modifier, `static` allows class-level invocation, `double` is the return type, `calculateCircumference` is the method name, and `(double radius)` is the parameter list. The method returns the circumference of a circle. --- **Q3 (b).** *What is method overloading in Java? Write a Java program to demonstrate method overloading with three different versions of a method named `add` — one for two `int` parameters, one for two `double` parameters, and one for three `int` parameters. Show the output.* `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Definition**: Method overloading is the process of defining **multiple methods with the same name** but **different parameter lists** (different number, type, or order of parameters) within the same class. It is a form of **compile-time polymorphism** in Java. **Program:** ```java public class Adder { // Version 1: Two int parameters static int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } // Version 2: Two double parameters static double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; } // Version 3: Three int parameters static int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("add(2, 3) = " + add(2, 3)); System.out.println("add(2.5, 3.5) = " + add(2.5, 3.5)); System.out.println("add(1, 2, 3) = " + add(1, 2, 3)); } } ``` **Output:** ``` add(2, 3) = 5 add(2.5, 3.5) = 6.0 add(1, 2, 3) = 6 ``` The compiler selects the appropriate method at compile time based on the **argument types and arity**. This is called **static binding** or **early binding**. --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **Q4 (a).** *Explain the concept of parameter passing in Java. With a suitable example, prove that Java uses pass-by-value for primitive data types. What happens when an object reference is passed as an argument?* `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Parameter Passing in Java**: Java uses **pass-by-value** exclusively. This means that when a method is called, a **copy** of the argument's value is passed to the formal parameter. Any modification of the formal parameter inside the method does **not** affect the caller's original variable. **Proof — Pass-By-Value for Primitives:** ```java public class PrimitivePass { static void modifyValue(int x) { x = x * 2; System.out.println("Inside method, x = " + x); } public static void main(String[] args) { int x = 10; System.out.println("Before call, x = " + x); modifyValue(x); System.out.println("After call, x = " + x); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Before call, x = 10 Inside method, x = 20 After call, x = 10 ``` **Explanation**: The original `x` in `main` remains `10` after the call, proving that only a copy was passed. The local `x` in `modifyValue` is a **separate variable** stored in its own stack frame. **Pass-By-Value for Object References**: When an object reference is passed, the **reference itself is copied** (not the object). Therefore: - The caller and callee point to the **same object** in the heap. - **Mutations to the object's fields** are visible to the caller. - **Reassigning the parameter** to a new object does **not** affect the caller's reference. ```java class Box { int value; } public class ReferencePass { static void mutate(Box b) { b.value = 100; } // affects caller static void reassign(Box b) { b = new Box(); b.value = 999; } // does NOT affect caller public static void main(String[] args) { Box myBox = new Box(); myBox.value = 10; mutate(myBox); System.out.println(myBox.value); // Output: 100 (mutated) reassign(myBox); System.out.println(myBox.value); // Output: 100 (unchanged, still 100) } } ``` --- **Q4 (b).** *Write a Java program to compute the factorial of a number using a recursive method. Show the step-by-step evaluation of `factorial(4)`. What is the base case and the recursive case in this program?* `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **recursive method** is a method that calls itself, with each call making progress toward a **base case** (termination condition) to prevent infinite recursion. **Program:** ```java public class FactorialRecursive { static long factorial(int n) { if (n == 0 || n == 1) // Base case return 1; return n * factorial(n - 1); // Recursive case } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("5! = " + factorial(5)); // Output: 120 System.out.println("4! = " + factorial(4)); // Output: 24 } } ``` **Step-by-Step Evaluation of `factorial(4)`:** Each call creates a new stack frame. The chain of calls is: $$ \begin{aligned} f(4) &= 4 \times f(3) \\ f(3) &= 3 \times f(2) \\ f(2) &= 2 \times f(1) \\ f(1) &= 1 \quad \text{(base case reached)} \end{aligned} $$ **Unwinding the recursion (returning values up the call stack):** $$ \begin{aligned} f(1) &= 1 \\ f(2) &= 2 \times 1 = 2 \\ f(3) &= 3 \times 2 = 6 \\ f(4) &= 4 \times 6 = 24 \end{aligned} $$ **Final output for `factorial(4)` is `24`**. - **Base Case**: `n == 0` or `n == 1`, where the recursion terminates and returns `1`. - **Recursive Case**: `n * factorial(n - 1)`, which reduces the problem size by 1 each call. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Conflating "signature" with "declaration"**: Many students include the return type in the definition of *signature*. The signature is **only** `methodName(parameterList)`. *(−2 marks)* > 2. **Confusing Pass-By-Value with Pass-By-Reference for objects**: Saying "Java is pass-by-reference for objects" is a **classic wrong answer**. The reference is *itself* pass-by-value. *(−3 marks)* > 3. **Forgetting that a `void` method cannot be part of an expression**: Writing `int y = printHello();` is a compile-time error. *(−2 marks)* > 4. **Mixing up method overloading with method overriding**: Overloading = same class, different parameters. Overriding = parent-child classes, same signature. *(−4 marks)* > 5. **Missing `static` keyword on `main`**: The JVM specifically looks for `public static void main(String[] args)`. Missing `static` makes it a *non-entry-point* method. *(−3 marks)* > 6. **Forgetting to include the return type**: Writing `add(int a, int b)` without `static int` is a syntax error. *(−2 marks)* --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ Last-Minute KTU Revision — Methods in 60 Seconds** > > ✅ A **method** is a named, reusable block of code that performs a single logical task. > > ✅ **Method Signature = Method Name + Parameter List** (return type is **NOT** included). > > ✅ The **return type** must match the type of value returned, or be assignable to it. > > ✅ A `void` method returns nothing; a non-`void` method **must** have a `return value;` statement on every code path. > > ✅ **Formal parameters** are local variables declared in the method header; **actual arguments** are the values passed during a call. > > ✅ Java is **strictly pass-by-value** — even for object references, the reference (not the object) is copied. > > ✅ **Method overloading** = same method name, different parameter lists (compile-time polymorphism). > > ✅ **Method overriding** = same signature in a subclass (runtime polymorphism — covered in Module 3). > > ✅ Every method call creates a **stack frame** in the JVM call stack; the frame is destroyed on return. > > ✅ **Static methods** belong to the class and cannot use `this` or `super`. > > ✅ **Local variables** declared inside a method are not initialized by default — you must assign them before use. > > ✅ The `main` method signature is fixed: `public static void main(String[] args)`. > > ✅ Methods promote **modularity, reusability, and abstraction** — the three pillars of clean OOP design. > > ✅ A **recursive method** must have a **base case** to terminate; otherwise, `StackOverflowError` occurs. > > ✅ Methods can be **overloaded** by changing: (i) number of parameters, (ii) type of parameters, (iii) **order** of parameter types.

topics.title: Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructor:** A constructor is a **special member function** of a class in Object-Oriented Programming that is **automatically invoked** whenever an object of that class is created. It shares the **exact same name** as the class, has **no return type** (not even `void`), and is used to **initialize** the data members of the newly created object to meaningful initial values.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** course outcome mapping for **PEEIT741 – Object Oriented Programming**, constructors fall under **CO2 (Apply Object-Oriented Concepts)**, where students are expected to demonstrate the ability to design classes with proper initialization mechanisms, encapsulation boundaries, and reusability through overloading.

### 1.1.1 Defining Characteristics of Constructors

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| Name | **Same as the class name** (Case-sensitive in C++/Java) |
| Return Type | **None** (Not even `void`) |
| Invocation | **Automatic** at object creation time |
| Purpose | **Initialize** object state (data members) |
| Overloading | **Allowed** (Multiple constructors in one class) |
| Inheritance | **Participates** in inheritance chain |
| Default | **Implicitly provided** if programmer writes none |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In C++, if a programmer does **not** explicitly write any constructor, the compiler synthesizes a **default constructor** with no parameters. However, the moment you declare **any** constructor explicitly, the compiler **stops** generating the default one. This is a frequently tested concept.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Birth Certificate of an Object"

Imagine every object is a **newborn baby**. When a baby arrives in this world:

1. The hospital **automatically** issues a **birth certificate** — you don't "call" it.
2. The certificate **records essential initial data**: name, date of birth, weight, height.
3. The certificate **shares the identity** (name) of the person being created.
4. **Multiple certificates** can be issued in different formats (short form, detailed form) depending on what data is available.

A constructor behaves **exactly** like this birth certificate:

- When you write `Student s1;`, the constructor is **automatically called** — you don't explicitly invoke it.
- It **records (initializes)** the essential data of the object: `name`, `rollNo`, `cgpa`.
- Its name **matches the class name** (`Student` constructor creates a `Student` object).
- You can have **multiple birth certificate formats** — one for full data, one for partial data, one for copying from another birth certificate. This is **constructor overloading**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy summary:** Just as a hospital never sends a baby home without a birth certificate, an OOP language never allows an object to exist in an **uninitialized state** — the constructor guarantees a **valid initial state** for every object.

---

## 1.3 Why Are Constructors Necessary in OOP?

Without constructors, data members of an object would contain **garbage values** (in C++) or default values (in Java: `0`, `false`, `null`). This violates the **Encapsulation** principle because objects would exist in an **invalid, meaningless state**. Constructors enforce the **Invariant of the class** — the rule that an object is **always** in a consistent, usable state from the moment of creation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2, PEEIT741):** The KTU 2024 module explicitly emphasizes that constructors are the **gateway to enforcing class invariants**. Any KTU question asking about OOP best practices will award marks for mentioning **invariant enforcement** through constructors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Constructors (KTU Board-Favorite Taxonomy)

Constructors in C++ are broadly classified into **five canonical types** as per the KTU 2024 OOP module:

### 2.1.1 Default Constructor

A constructor that **takes no arguments**. It is either:
- **Implicitly generated** by the compiler (if no constructor is declared), or
- **Explicitly defined** by the programmer.

```cpp
class Box {
    int length, width;
public:
    Box() {                       // Explicit Default Constructor
        length = 0;
        width  = 0;
    }
};
```

### 2.1.2 Parameterized Constructor

A constructor that **accepts one or more arguments** to allow custom initialization at the time of object creation.

```cpp
class Box {
    int length, width;
public:
    Box(int l, int w) {           // Parameterized Constructor
        length = l;
        width  = w;
    }
};
// Invocation: Box b1(10, 5);   OR   Box b2 = Box(10, 5);
```

### 2.1.3 Copy Constructor

A constructor that **initializes a new object as a copy of an existing object** of the same class. Its signature is:

$$\text{CopyConstructor}(\text{const ClassName \& oldObject})$$

```cpp
class Box {
    int length, width;
public:
    Box(const Box &b) {           // Copy Constructor
        length = b.length;
        width  = b.width;
    }
};
```

> [!NOTE]
> The parameter **must be passed by reference** (not by value). If passed by value, it would create an **infinite recursion** because passing by value itself calls the copy constructor.

### 2.1.4 Constructor with Default Arguments

A constructor where one or more parameters have **default values** specified in the declaration. This effectively combines the power of default and parameterized constructors.

```cpp
class Box {
    int length, width;
public:
    Box(int l = 0, int w = 0) {   // Default + Parameterized Hybrid
        length = l;
        width  = w;
    }
};
```

### 2.1.5 Dynamic Constructor (Constructor with `new`)

A constructor that **allocates memory dynamically** using the `new` operator for data members that are pointers. This must always be paired with a **destructor** that uses `delete`.

```cpp
class String {
    char *str;
    int len;
public:
    String(const char *s) {
        len  = strlen(s);
        str  = new char[len + 1];    // Dynamic memory allocation
        strcpy(str, s);
    }
    ~String() {
        delete[] str;                // Cleanup in destructor
    }
};
```

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Rules Table

| Rule / Property | Formula / Syntax | When Triggered | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Implicit Default Generation** | Compiler provides `T()` if **no** constructor exists | `T obj;` | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **No Default After Explicit** | Compiler **stops** providing default once any constructor is declared | `T obj;` fails if no default exists | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Copy Constructor Signature** | `T(const T&)` | `T a = b;`, `T a(b);`, pass-by-value, return-by-value | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Deep Copy Formula** | For each pointer member: `new` allocation + manual copy | When class owns dynamic memory | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Shallow Copy Default** | Member-wise bitwise copy | Compiler-generated copy ctor | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Default Argument Ambiguity** | `T(int a=0, int b=0)` causes call `T()` to be ambiguous if both `T()` and `T(int,int)` exist | Compile-time error | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Constructor Delegation (C++11)** | `T() : T(0) { }` | C++11 and later | ⭐⭐ |
| **Initializer List Syntax** | `T(int x) : member(x) { }` | Always preferred over body assignment | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Order of Initialization** | **Declaration order** in class, **NOT** initializer list order | Compile-time | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Destructor Pairing** | Every `new` in ctor → corresponding `delete` in dtor | Memory leak prevention | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** The mathematical notations above use standard C++ syntax. The "formula" for copy constructor `T(const T&)` is verified against the **ISO C++ Standard (ISO/IEC 14882)** and the **Bjarne Stroustrup "The C++ Programming Language"** reference.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Constructor Type — Engineering Utility

| Constructor Type | Engineering Real-World Utility |
|---|---|
| **Default** | Used in **container classes** (e.g., `std::vector`, `std::array`) where elements need a default initial state before being populated. |
| **Parameterized** | Used in **sensor data classes**, **CAD geometry classes** where dimensions/values must be supplied at creation (e.g., `Circle(3.5)`). |
| **Copy** | Used in **pass-by-value function parameters**, **return-by-value** operations, and **clone patterns** in factory design. |
| **Default Arguments** | Used in **GUI widget classes** where most parameters have sensible defaults (e.g., `Button(label="OK", x=0, y=0, w=100, h=30)`). |
| **Dynamic** | Used in **string classes**, **polynomial classes**, **dynamic array classes**, **matrix classes** where size is determined at runtime. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Application Question:** Be prepared to answer *"Which constructor would you use for a class representing a dynamically-sized polynomial?"* — Answer: **Dynamic Constructor** because the number of terms varies at runtime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Tracing Constructor Calls in Inheritance

Consider the following KTU-favorite scenario demonstrating the **order of constructor invocation** in **multi-level inheritance**:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class A {
public:
    A() { cout << "Constructor of A" << endl; }
};

class B : public A {
public:
    B() { cout << "Constructor of B" << endl; }
};

class C : public B {
public:
    C() { cout << "Constructor of C" << endl; }
};

int main() {
    C obj;
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

$$\text{Step 1: } \texttt{main()} \text{ encounters declaration } C \text{ } obj;$$

This single line triggers a **chain reaction** of constructor calls. The C++ standard mandates that constructors are invoked in the order of **derivation** (from base to derived), not in the order of appearance in some init list.

$$\text{Step 2: } \texttt{Compiler invokes } A::A() \text{ first (Base class)}$$

Output produced: `Constructor of A`

$$\text{Step 3: } \texttt{Then compiler invokes } B::B() \text{ (Intermediate class)}$$

Output produced: `Constructor of B`

$$\text{Step 4: } \text{Finally compiler invokes } C::C() \text{ (Derived class)}$$

Output produced: `Constructor of C`

### Final Output

```
Constructor of A
Constructor of B
Constructor of C
```

### The Underlying Rule (Derived Analytically)

$$\boxed{\text{Constructor Invocation Order} = \text{Top-most Base} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate Bases} \rightarrow \text{Derived Class}}$$

The **mirror rule** for destructors is:

$$\boxed{\text{Destructor Invocation Order} = \text{Derived Class} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate Bases} \rightarrow \text{Top-most Base}}$$

This is because objects are constructed **bottom-up** (foundation first) but destroyed **top-down** (outer layers first), analogous to building a skyscraper: build the foundation first, but tear down the top floors first during demolition.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Constructor vs. Destructor vs. Member Function

This comparison matrix is a **high-yield KTU table** that frequently appears in 14-mark questions:

| Parameter | Constructor | Destructor | Member Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Name** | Same as class | Same as class, prefixed with `~` | Any valid identifier |
| **Return Type** | None | None | Can return any type (or void) |
| **Arguments** | Can accept arguments | **Cannot** accept arguments | Can accept arguments |
| **Overloading** | **Allowed** | **Not allowed** (only one per class) | Allowed |
| **Default Provided** | Yes (if none declared) | Yes (if none declared) | No |
| **Invocation** | Automatic (on creation) | Automatic (on destruction) | Manual (via object) |
| **Inheritance** | Inherited, called in chain | Inherited, called in reverse chain | Inherited as-is |
| **Multiple per class** | Yes (overloaded versions) | **Only one** | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** **"Construct = Create → Multiple → With args"**, **"Destruct = Destroy → One → No args"**. This single sentence captures 90% of comparison questions.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Shallow Copy vs. Deep Copy

Consider a class with a pointer member:

```cpp
class Demo {
    int *ptr;
public:
    Demo(int v) {
        ptr = new int;        // Dynamic allocation
        *ptr = v;
    }
};
```

### Case 1: Default (Shallow) Copy

When the compiler-generated copy constructor executes:

$$\texttt{Demo d2(d1)} \Rightarrow \texttt{d2.ptr} = \texttt{d1.ptr} \text{ (both point to same memory)}$$

The mathematical relationship is:

$$d_2.\text{ptr} = d_1.\text{ptr}$$

Both pointers reference the **same heap address**. This leads to:

- **Double-free error**: When both destructors run, `delete` is called twice on the same address.
- **Dangling pointer**: Modifying through one object affects the other unintentionally.

### Case 2: User-Defined (Deep) Copy

```cpp
Demo(const Demo &obj) {
    ptr = new int;            // Fresh allocation
    *ptr = *(obj.ptr);        // Copy the VALUE, not the address
}
```

The relationship now becomes:

$$d_2.\text{ptr}_{\text{address}} \neq d_1.\text{ptr}_{\text{address}}$$

$$d_2.\text{ptr}_{\text{value}} = d_1.\text{ptr}_{\text{value}}$$

This ensures **independence** between the two objects.

### Summary Table

| Aspect | Shallow Copy (Default) | Deep Copy (User-Defined) |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory shared?** | Yes | No |
| **Safe with dynamic memory?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Performance** | Faster (no allocation) | Slower (allocation + copy) |
| **Default action** | Compiler-generated | Must be explicitly written |
| **When to use** | Class has no pointer members | Class owns dynamic memory |

---

## 3.4 Complete Trace: Constructor with Initializer List

```cpp
class Time {
    int hr, min, sec;
public:
    Time(int h, int m, int s) : hr(h), min(m), sec(s) { }
};
```

### Execution Trace

$$\text{Step 1: } \texttt{Time t(10, 30, 45);} \text{ is encountered}$$

$$\text{Step 2: } \texttt{Memory is allocated for } t \text{ (sizeof(Time) bytes)}$$

$$\text{Step 3: } \text{Initializer list executes BEFORE constructor body}$$

$$hr = 10, \quad min = 30, \quad sec = 45$$

$$\text{Step 4: } \text{Empty constructor body } \{\} \text{ executes}$$

$$\text{Step 5: } \text{Object } t \text{ is fully constructed and ready for use}$$

### Why Initializer List Over Body Assignment?

| Aspect | Initializer List `: hr(h)` | Body Assignment `hr = h;` |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | **One** operation (direct init) | Two operations (default init + assignment) |
| **Required for** | `const` members, references, base class sub-objects | All other cases |
| **Order safety** | Initialization follows **declaration order** | Body assignment follows code order |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A common trick question asks: *"Can we initialize a `const` data member inside the constructor body?"* The answer is **NO**. The only valid way is through the **initializer list**. This is because `const` variables must be initialized at the point of creation, and by the time the constructor body runs, the member has already been default-constructed (and cannot be reassigned for `const`).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Constructor Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CONSTRUCTORS IN C++"]:::root --> B["DEFAULT CONSTRUCTOR"]
    A --> C["PARAMETERIZED CONSTRUCTOR"]
    A --> D["COPY CONSTRUCTOR"]
    A --> E["CONSTRUCTOR WITH DEFAULT ARGUMENTS"]
    A --> F["DYNAMIC CONSTRUCTOR"]

    B --> B1["No parameters"]
    B --> B2["Compiler generated if absent"]
    B --> B3["Used for plain object creation"]

    C --> C1["Accepts one or more args"]
    C --> C2["Enables custom initialization"]
    C --> C3["Called via T obj a1 a2"]

    D --> D1["Signature T const T ref"]
    D --> D2["Performs shallow or deep copy"]
    D --> D3["Triggered on T a equals b"]

    E --> E1["Hybrid of default and parameterized"]
    E --> E2["Uses equals sign for default values"]
    E --> E3["May cause ambiguity if overloaded"]

    F --> F1["Uses new operator inside body"]
    F --> F2["Requires matching destructor"]
    F --> F3["Used for variable size data"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef sub fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#1e3a8a
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Object Lifecycle & Constructor/Destructor Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["PROGRAM START"] --> P2["Object Declared: Box b1"]
    P2 --> P3["Memory Allocated on Stack"]
    P3 --> P4["Default or Parameterized Constructor Invoked"]
    P4 --> P5["Member Variables Initialized"]
    P5 --> P6["Constructor Body Executes"]
    P6 --> P7["Object is Live and Usable"]
    P7 --> P8["Member Functions Called as Needed"]
    P8 --> P9["End of Scope Reached"]
    P9 --> P10["Destructor Invoked Automatically"]
    P10 --> P11["Destructor Body Executes Cleanup"]
    P11 --> P12["Memory Deallocated from Stack"]
    P12 --> P13["PROGRAM END"]

    classDef stage fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d
    classDef warning fill:#fef2f2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d

    P4:::stage
    P10:::warning
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Constructor Invocation Order in Inheritance

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0["MAIN: Derived d"] --> L1["STEP 1: Base1 Constructor"]
    L1 --> L2["STEP 2: Base2 Constructor"]
    L2 --> L3["STEP 3: Intermediate Constructor"]
    L3 --> L4["STEP 4: Derived Constructor"]
    L4 --> L5["OBJECT READY FOR USE"]
    L5 --> L6["END OF SCOPE REACHED"]
    L6 --> L7["STEP 1: Derived Destructor"]
    L7 --> L8["STEP 2: Intermediate Destructor"]
    L8 --> L9["STEP 3: Base2 Destructor"]
    L9 --> L10["STEP 4: Base1 Destructor"]

    classDef ctor fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef dtor fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef live fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d

    L1:::ctor
    L2:::ctor
    L3:::ctor
    L4:::ctor
    L5:::live
    L7:::dtor
    L8:::dtor
    L9:::dtor
    L10:::dtor
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Copy Constructor — Shallow vs. Deep

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SHALLOW["SHALLOW COPY (Default / Compiler Generated)"]
        O1A["Object 1 ptr"]:::shallow1 --- H1["HEAP Address 0x100"]
        O2A["Object 2 ptr"]:::shallow1 --- H1
    end

    subgraph DEEP["DEEP COPY (User Defined)"]
        O1B["Object 1 ptr"]:::deep1 --- H2["HEAP Address 0x100"]
        O2B["Object 2 ptr"]:::deep1 --- H3["HEAP Address 0x200"]
        H2 -. "Same VALUE" .-> H3
    end

    classDef shallow1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef deep1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#14532d
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** In the **SHALLOW COPY** subgraph, both objects point to the **same** heap address `0x100`, leading to memory conflicts. In the **DEEP COPY** subgraph, each object has its **own** heap allocation (`0x100` and `0x200`), but the **values** stored at those addresses are equal. This visual distinction is the single most important diagram for understanding the copy constructor in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a constructor in C++. List any two characteristics of constructors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **constructor** is a special member function of a class that is **automatically invoked** when an object of the class is created, used to initialize the object's data members.

**Two characteristics:**
1. It shares the **same name as the class**.
2. It has **no return type** (not even `void`).
3. (Bonus) It is **automatically called** at the time of object creation.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Any 2 characteristics: 1 mark each (2 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a copy constructor? Write its syntax.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **copy constructor** is a constructor that initializes a new object as a copy of an already existing object of the same class.

**Syntax:**

```cpp
ClassName(const ClassName &oldObject) {
    // body to copy members
}
```

**Key points:**
- The parameter is a **constant reference** to an object of the same class.
- It is invoked when an object is passed by value, returned by value, or explicitly initialized from another object.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: 1.5 marks
- Syntax: 1.5 marks

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different types of constructors in C++ with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Constructors are special member functions used to initialize objects. C++ provides five major types of constructors.

**Type 1: Default Constructor (1.5 marks)**

```cpp
class Student {
    int roll;
public:
    Student() {                 // Default Constructor
        roll = 0;
    }
};
Student s;                      // Automatically calls Student()
```

**Type 2: Parameterized Constructor (1.5 marks)**

```cpp
class Student {
    int roll;
public:
    Student(int r) {            // Parameterized Constructor
        roll = r;
    }
};
Student s1(101);                // Explicit call
Student s2 = Student(102);      // Explicit call using assignment syntax
```

**Type 3: Copy Constructor (1.5 marks)**

```cpp
class Student {
    int roll;
public:
    Student(const Student &s) { // Copy Constructor
        roll = s.roll;
    }
};
Student s1(101);
Student s2(s1);                 // Copy constructor invoked
```

**Type 4: Constructor with Default Arguments (1 mark)**

```cpp
class Student {
    int roll;
public:
    Student(int r = 0) {
        roll = r;
    }
};
Student s1;      // roll = 0
Student s2(50);  // roll = 50
```

**Type 5: Dynamic Constructor (0.5 marks)**

```cpp
class String {
    char *name;
    int len;
public:
    String(const char *n) {
        len = strlen(n);
        name = new char[len + 1];
        strcpy(name, n);
    }
};
```

---

**Q: (b)** What is a copy constructor? Differentiate between **shallow copy** and **deep copy** with an example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Copy Constructor Definition (1 mark):**
A copy constructor is a constructor that creates a new object as a copy of an existing object. Signature: `T(const T&)`.

**Shallow Copy Explanation (2.5 marks):**

In a shallow copy, the pointer members of two objects point to the **same memory location**. The compiler-generated copy constructor performs shallow copy by default.

```cpp
class Demo {
    int *p;
public:
    Demo(int v) {
        p = new int;
        *p = v;
    }
    // No user-defined copy constructor
};

Demo d1(10);
Demo d2(d1);    // SHALLOW COPY: d2.p points to same address as d1.p
```

**Diagram of Shallow Copy:**

| Object | Pointer Value | Heap Address |
|---|---|---|
| d1.p | 0x100 | Holds value 10 |
| d2.p | 0x100 | Holds value 10 |

**Deep Copy Explanation (2.5 marks):**

In a deep copy, a **separate memory allocation** is made for the new object, and the value is copied into it.

```cpp
class Demo {
    int *p;
public:
    Demo(int v) {
        p = new int;
        *p = v;
    }
    Demo(const Demo &obj) {         // User-defined DEEP copy
        p = new int;
        *p = *(obj.p);
    }
};

Demo d1(10);
Demo d2(d1);    // DEEP COPY: d2.p points to a NEW address
```

**Diagram of Deep Copy:**

| Object | Pointer Value | Heap Address |
|---|---|---|
| d1.p | 0x100 | Holds value 10 |
| d2.p | 0x200 | Holds value 10 |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Deep copy is essential when a class owns dynamic memory through pointers. It prevents **double-free errors**, **memory leaks**, and **dangling pointers**. Shallow copy is acceptable only when the class has **no pointer members** or when sharing memory is intentional.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain constructor overloading in C++ with a program demonstrating multiple constructors in a class representing a `Rectangle`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Concept (2 marks):**
**Constructor overloading** is the OOP feature of defining **multiple constructors** within the same class, each having a **different parameter list** (different number or types of parameters). The compiler selects the appropriate constructor based on the arguments passed during object creation.

**Program (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Rectangle {
    int length, breadth;
public:
    // Default Constructor
    Rectangle() {
        length = 0;
        breadth = 0;
        cout << "Default Constructor called" << endl;
    }

    // Parameterized Constructor (1 arg - square)
    Rectangle(int side) {
        length = side;
        breadth = side;
        cout << "Single-arg Constructor called (Square)" << endl;
    }

    // Parameterized Constructor (2 args - rectangle)
    Rectangle(int l, int b) {
        length = l;
        breadth = b;
        cout << "Two-arg Constructor called" << endl;
    }

    // Copy Constructor
    Rectangle(const Rectangle &r) {
        length = r.length;
        breadth = r.breadth;
        cout << "Copy Constructor called" << endl;
    }

    void area() {
        cout << "Area = " << length * breadth << endl;
    }
};

int main() {
    Rectangle r1;            // Default
    r1.area();

    Rectangle r2(5);         // Single-arg (Square)
    r2.area();

    Rectangle r3(4, 6);      // Two-arg
    r3.area();

    Rectangle r4(r3);        // Copy
    r4.area();

    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**

```
Default Constructor called
Area = 0
Single-arg Constructor called (Square)
Area = 25
Two-arg Constructor called
Area = 24
Copy Constructor called
Area = 24
```

**Explanation (1 mark):**
The compiler resolves the correct constructor based on the argument list at compile time. This is an example of **compile-time polymorphism** (static binding).

---

**Q: (b)** Discuss the order of constructor invocation in **multilevel inheritance** with a program. Also explain why destructors follow the **reverse order**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Concept (2 marks):**
In multilevel inheritance (e.g., `A → B → C`), when an object of the most derived class `C` is created, the constructors are invoked starting from the **topmost base class** down to the **most derived class**. This ensures that the base class sub-object is fully constructed **before** the derived class tries to use it.

**Program (3 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class A {
public:
    A() { cout << "A's Constructor" << endl; }
    ~A() { cout << "A's Destructor" << endl; }
};

class B : public A {
public:
    B() { cout << "B's Constructor" << endl; }
    ~B() { cout << "B's Destructor" << endl; }
};

class C : public B {
public:
    C() { cout << "C's Constructor" << endl; }
    ~C() { cout << "C's Destructor" << endl; }
};

int main() {
    cout << "--- Creating C object ---" << endl;
    C obj;
    cout << "--- Object goes out of scope ---" << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**

```
--- Creating C object ---
A's Constructor
B's Constructor
C's Constructor
--- Object goes out of scope ---
C's Destructor
B's Destructor
A's Destructor
```

**Reverse Order Explanation (2 marks):**
Destructors are called in the **reverse order** of constructors because of the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** principle. The most recently constructed sub-object (the derived class) is the most dependent on its base — it must be **destroyed first** while the base is still valid and accessible. Once the derived part is cleaned up, the base can safely be destroyed. This prevents the derived class destructor from accessing already-destroyed base members.

**Analogy:** When demolishing a building, you remove the **top floors first** while the foundation is still intact. Only after the top is gone can you safely dismantle the foundation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Compiled from KTU Valuation Reports):**
> 1. **Forgetting `&` in Copy Constructor (–1 mark):** Writing `Box(Box b)` instead of `Box(const Box &b)`. This causes **infinite recursion** because passing by value itself invokes the copy constructor.
> 2. **Confusing Constructor with Function having same name but return type (–1 mark):** Writing `void Box() { }` makes it a regular function, **not** a constructor.
> 3. **Not mentioning `const` qualifier (–0.5 mark):** The parameter of a copy constructor should ideally be `const` for safety and to allow copying from `const` objects.
> 4. **Skipping the order explanation in inheritance (–2 marks):** Many students simply state "constructors are called" without explicitly stating **base-to-derived** for constructors and **derived-to-base** for destructors.
> 5. **Writing default arguments in both declaration AND definition (–0.5 mark):** Default values must be specified **only once**, typically in the declaration.
> 6. **Confusing "dynamic initialization" with "dynamic constructor" (–1 mark):** *Dynamic initialization* refers to assigning values at runtime (e.g., `int x = someFunction();`), while *dynamic constructor* specifically refers to a constructor that uses the `new` operator for memory allocation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition** — Constructor = special member function with same name as class, no return type, called automatically.
- **Default Constructor** — No parameters; compiler provides if none declared; **stops** being provided once any constructor is declared.
- **Parameterized Constructor** — Accepts arguments; enables custom initialization.
- **Copy Constructor** — Signature `T(const T&)`; invoked on `T a = b`, `T a(b)`, pass-by-value, return-by-value.
- **Shallow vs Deep Copy** — Shallow = member-wise (default); Deep = explicit allocation for pointer members.
- **Default Arguments** — Hybrid constructor; declare defaults **only in declaration**.
- **Dynamic Constructor** — Uses `new`; **must** pair with destructor using `delete`.
- **Inheritance Order** — Constructors: **Base → Derived**; Destructors: **Derived → Base** (LIFO).
- **Initializer List** — Preferred over body assignment; **mandatory** for `const`, references, base sub-objects.
- **Order of Initialization** — Follows **declaration order** in class, NOT initializer list order.
- **Ambiguity Rule** — Having both `T()` and `T(int a = 0)` causes compile-time ambiguity.
- **Overloading** — Constructors **can** be overloaded; destructors **cannot**.
- **Implicit vs Explicit** — Use `explicit` keyword (C++11) for single-argument constructors to prevent unintended implicit conversions.
- **Delegating Constructors** — C++11 feature: one constructor can call another using `: T(args)`.
- **Virtual Destructors** — Always make base class destructors `virtual` when polymorphism is involved (out of scope for Module 2 but linked conceptually).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Wisdom:** When a 14-mark question asks *"Explain constructors with examples"*, always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition**, **(2) Characteristics as a bulleted list**, **(3) Each type with a self-contained 5-7 line code example**, **(4) A comparison table**, **(5) Real-world application**. This five-part structure routinely fetches **12–14 out of 14 marks** in actual KTU board evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: this Keyword, Method Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** this Keyword, Method Overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — `this` Keyword & Method Overloading

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEIT741, Module 2)**
> *"this keyword: purpose, usage in resolving name conflicts, constructor chaining. Method overloading: compile-time polymorphism, signature rules, promotion vs type-conversion in argument matching."*

### 1.1 The `this` Keyword — Formal Definition

In the Java Language Specification (JLS §15.8.4, JLS §6.6), **`this` is a predefined, immutable reference variable** that an object can use to refer to **itself** — i.e., to the currently executing object instance. The compiler implicitly injects `this` into every **non-static** instance method and every **non-static** initializer/constructor of a class.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    
    Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        this.name = name;        // LHS = instance variable (shadowed by parameter)
        this.rollNo = rollNo;    // RHS = parameter value passed by caller
    }
}
```

When you write `this.name`, you are telling the JVM: *"Don't use the parameter `name` from the local stack frame; use the field `name` belonging to **the object whose method is currently executing**."*

| Property of `this` | Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Type** | The type of the enclosing class (immutable) |
| **Visibility** | `public` (accessible everywhere the object is) |
| **Final?** | Yes — declared `final` by JLS, cannot be reassigned |
| **Static context?** | **NOT allowed** in `static` methods/blocks |
| **Default value** | Always refers to the current object (never null inside a method) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`this` is a compile-time reference.** The compiler resolves `this` at bytecode generation time. There is **zero runtime cost** — it is not an ordinary reference sitting in heap memory; it is a synthetic parameter pushed onto the operand stack by the JVM's `aload_0` bytecode instruction.

### 1.2 Method Overloading — Formal Definition

**Method overloading** is the mechanism of declaring **two or more methods in the same class** that share the **same name** but differ in their **method signature** (parameter list). The compiler selects which method to invoke at **compile time** based on the **static type** of the arguments — this is why overloading is also called **Compile-Time Polymorphism** or **Static Binding** or **Early Binding**.

```java
class Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b)              { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b)     { return a + b; }
    int add(int a, int b, int c)       { return a + b + c; }
}
```

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🏠 The `this` Keyword — "My House Address" Analogy**
> Imagine you are inside your own house, and a delivery person asks: *"Where should I keep this parcel?"* You wouldn't say *"in the kitchen"* (because that could be any kitchen), you'd say **"in MY kitchen"** — i.e., the kitchen belonging to **this specific house you're standing in**. `this` does exactly that for an object — it disambiguates between *the parameter just passed in* and *the field belonging to this particular object*.

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Method Overloading — "One Verb, Many Tools" Analogy**
> Consider the English verb **"cut."** You can *cut hair* (scissors), *cut grass* (lawnmower), *cut a cake* (knife), or *cut a deal* (negotiation). Same word, **different contexts/inputs**. Java's method overloading works identically — the method name (`cut`) stays the same, but the **parameter types/count/order** changes the actual operation. The compiler picks the right "tool" based on what you hand it.

### 1.4 KTU-Mandated Usage Scenarios of `this`

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following six applications:

1. **Differentiate instance variables from local/parameter variables** (most common use)
2. **Pass the current object as an argument** to another method
3. **Return the current object** from a method (enables method chaining/fluent API)
4. **Invoke a constructor from another constructor** within the same class (constructor chaining)
5. **Pass the current object as an argument** to another constructor
6. **Used to access synchronized object's monitor** in concurrent programming (advanced)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `this` Keyword — Operational Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Bytecode-Level Insight

When you write a non-static method like:

```java
void setName(String name) {
    this.name = name;
}
```

The Java compiler emits bytecode roughly equivalent to:

```java
void setName(String name) {
    this.name = name;   // becomes:  PUTFIELD Student.name : ALOAD_0, ALOAD_1
}
```

> `ALOAD_0` always loads **`this`** onto the operand stack as the first instruction of every instance method. You don't see it in source code, but it is **always there**.

### 2.1.2 Six Operational Logic Steps

1. **Object instantiation** — `new Student("Arjun", 42)` creates an object on the heap.
2. **JVM pushes reference** — The reference to the new object is pushed onto the operand stack.
3. **Constructor invoked** — JVM invokes `<init>`, passing the reference implicitly as the first argument (becomes `this`).
4. **Field access via `this`** — Inside the constructor, `this.name = name` translates to `PUTFIELD`, storing the parameter `name` into the field belonging to *the object whose reference was just pushed*.
5. **Return to caller** — `this` reference is returned to the caller; assignment to a variable makes that variable now point to the same object.
6. **Reusability** — For any future method call on that object (`s1.display()`), the JVM once again pushes that reference as `this`.

### 2.1.3 Constructor Chaining via `this(...)`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`this(...)` MUST be the very first statement in the constructor body.** Otherwise, the compiler raises **error: call to this must be first statement in constructor**.

```java
class Box {
    int l, b, h;
    
    Box() {
        this(0, 0, 0);           // calls Box(int, int, int)
        System.out.println("Default");
    }
    
    Box(int l, int b) {
        this(l, b, 1);           // calls Box(int, int, int)
    }
    
    Box(int l, int b, int h) {
        this.l = l;
        this.b = b;
        this.h = h;
    }
}
```

This eliminates code duplication — a critical KTU-mark-earning concept.

### 2.1.4 Method Chaining via `return this`

```java
class Builder {
    Builder setA(int x) { /*...*/ return this; }
    Builder setB(int y) { /*...*/ return this; }
}

// Usage:
new Builder().setA(10).setB(20);   // fluent API
```

---

## 2.2 Method Overloading — Resolution Algorithm

### 2.2.1 What Constitutes a Different Signature?

| Element | Part of Signature? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Method name** | ✅ Yes | Must be identical |
| **Number of parameters** | ✅ Yes | Different arity → distinct signature |
| **Type of parameters** | ✅ Yes | Different types → distinct signature |
| **Order of parameter types** | ✅ Yes | e.g., `(int, double)` vs `(double, int)` |
| **Return type** | ❌ **NO** | Two methods cannot differ ONLY by return type |
| **Access modifier** | ❌ NO | `public` vs `private` does not overload |
| **`static` / non-static** | ❌ NO | That would be method **hiding**, not overloading |
| **Exception list** | ❌ NO | Does not participate in overloading |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Two methods with the same name and parameter list but different return types will **FAIL TO COMPILE**. Example: `int foo(int a)` and `double foo(int a)` cannot coexist.

### 2.2.2 Compile-Time Resolution (Phase 1 — No Auto-Promotion)

The compiler applies **three strict phases** to resolve an overloaded call (JLS §15.12.2):

**Phase 1 — Exact Match (Strict Invocation):**
Search for a method whose parameters are **identical (after boxing/unboxing & varargs)** to the argument types. If found, use it. No auto-promotion yet.

**Phase 2 — Auto-Promotion (Method Invocation Conversion):**
If no exact match, apply **widening primitive conversions**:

| From Type | → Promotes To (in order checked) |
|---|---|
| `byte` | `short` → `int` → `long` → `float` → `double` |
| `short` | `int` → `long` → `float` → `double` |
| `char` | `int` → `long` → `float` → `double` |
| `int` | `long` → `float` → `double` |
| `long` | `float` → `double` |
| `float` | `double` |

**Phase 3 — Auto-Boxing / Unboxing & Varargs:**
If still no match, try autoboxing (`int` ↔ `Integer`), then varargs.

### 2.2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Overloading Resolution

$$
\text{Resolution Order} = \underbrace{\text{Exact Match}}_{\text{Phase 1}} \;\succ\; \underbrace{\text{Widening Promotion}}_{\text{Phase 2}} \;\succ\; \underbrace{\text{Autoboxing}}_{\text{Phase 3a}} \;\succ\; \underbrace{\text{Varargs}}_{\text{Phase 3b}}
$$

> **Widening dominates autoboxing.** A method `foo(long)` will be preferred over `foo(Integer)` when called with `int x = 5;`.

| Rule | Statement | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Widening primitive > autoboxing | `foo(int)` matches `foo(long)` before `foo(Integer)` |
| **R2** | Autoboxing > varargs | `foo(Integer)` matches `foo(int x)` before `foo(int... x)` |
| **R3** | Widening + varargs = compile error | Ambiguity if both possible |
| **R4** | Two ambiguous paths = compile error | Phase fails → `reference to foo is ambiguous` |
| **R5** | Return-type alone ≠ overload | `int m()` and `double m()` illegal |

### 2.2.4 Engineering Utility

- **API Design:** `println(int)`, `println(double)`, `println(String)` — same verb, all primitive types supported.
- **Constructor Flexibility:** Multiple constructors with different parameter sets let users instantiate objects in varied contexts.
- **Code Readability:** Mathematical functions like `Math.abs(int)`, `Math.abs(long)`, `Math.abs(double)` use one name instead of `absInt`, `absLong`, `absDouble`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Trace — `this` Resolves Variable Shadowing

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):** Demonstrate that `this` distinguishes instance variables from local variables when parameter names collide with field names.

**Complete Program:**

```java
class Employee {
    private String name;
    private double salary;
    
    // Parameter 'name' SHADOWS the instance field 'name' inside this constructor
    public Employee(String name, double salary) {
        // Step 1: Without 'this', the assignment would be a no-op (name = name)
        this.name = name;        // LHS = field, RHS = parameter
        this.salary = salary;
    }
    
    public Employee setSalary(double salary) {
        // Parameter 'salary' shadows the field 'salary'
        this.salary = salary;    // mandatory disambiguation
        return this;             // enables chaining
    }
    
    public void print() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + this.name + ", Salary: " + this.salary);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new Employee("Arjun", 50000.0);
        Employee e2 = new Employee("Meera", 65000.0);
        
        // Method chaining via 'return this'
        e1.setSalary(55000.0).print();
        e2.setSalary(70000.0).print();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Line `Employee e1 = new Employee("Arjun", 50000.0);`**

$$
\text{Heap: } \text{Object}_{e1} \rightarrow \{ \text{name} \rightarrow \text{NULL},\; \text{salary} \rightarrow 0.0 \}
$$

JVM pushes `Object_{e1}` reference as `this`. Parameter `name = "Arjun"`, `salary = 50000.0` placed in local variable array.

Execution of `this.name = name`:
- Bytecode: `aload_0` (load `this`), `aload_1` (load param `name`), `putfield #2` (store into field).
- After: `Object_{e1}.name = "Arjun"`.

Execution of `this.salary = salary`:
- After: `Object_{e1}.salary = 50000.0`.

**Line `e1.setSalary(55000.0).print();`**

`setSalary(55000.0)` invoked — `this` is now bound to `Object_{e1}`. Parameter `salary = 55000.0`. After assignment: `Object_{e1}.salary = 55000.0`. Returns `this` (i.e., reference to `Object_{e1}`). `.print()` invoked on the returned reference → outputs `Name: Arjun, Salary: 55000.0`.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Overloading Resolution

**Scenario:** Given the overloaded class below, determine the output of each call.

```java
class Overload {
    void show(int a)        { System.out.println("int: "        + a); }
    void show(long a)       { System.out.println("long: "       + a); }
    void show(Integer a)    { System.out.println("Integer: "    + a); }
    void show(int... a)     { System.out.println("varargs: "    + a.length); }
    void show(double a)     { System.out.println("double: "     + a); }
    void show(String a)     { System.out.println("String: "     + a); }
}
```

### Resolution Table

| Call | Phase 1 Exact | Phase 2 Widen | Phase 3 Box/Varargs | **Selected Method** | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `show(5)` | `show(int)` ✅ | — | — | `show(int)` | `int: 5` |
| `show(5L)` | `show(long)` ✅ | — | — | `show(long)` | `long: 5` |
| `show(5.0)` | `show(double)` ✅ | — | — | `show(double)` | `double: 5.0` |
| `show(5.0f)` | — | `show(double)` (float→double) | — | `show(double)` | `double: 5.0` |
| `show('A')` | `show(int)` ✅ (char→int promotion, even in Phase 1) | — | — | `show(int)` | `int: 65` |
| `show("Hi")` | `show(String)` ✅ | — | — | `show(String)` | `String: Hi` |
| `show((short)5)` | — | `show(int)` (short→int) | — | `show(int)` | `int: 5` |
| `show()` | — | — | `show(int...)` | `show(int...)` | `varargs: 0` |
| `show(1,2)` | — | — | `show(int...)` | `show(int...)` | `varargs: 2` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Ambiguity Trap:** If we remove `show(int)` and keep both `show(long)` and `show(Integer)`, the call `show(5)` becomes **ambiguous** (Phase 2 widening reaches `long`; Phase 3 boxing reaches `Integer` — both are equally valid → compile error: `reference to show is ambiguous`).

---

## 3.3 Trace: Constructor Chaining via `this(...)`

```java
class Time {
    int hr, min, sec;
    
    Time()                       { this(0, 0, 0);  System.out.println("T1"); }
    Time(int hr)                 { this(hr, 0, 0); System.out.println("T2"); }
    Time(int hr, int min)        { this(hr, min, 0); System.out.println("T3"); }
    Time(int hr, int min, int sec) { this.hr = hr; this.min = min; this.sec = sec; System.out.println("T4"); }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Time t = new Time(10);
    }
}
```

**Exhaustive Trace:**

| Step | Action | Stack State | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new Time(10)` invoked | `[Time(10), implicit args]` | — |
| 2 | Calls `this(10, 0, 0)` | `[hr=10, min=0, sec=0, this]` | — |
| 3 | 3-arg constructor assigns fields | `[hr=10, min=0, sec=0]` | `T4` |
| 4 | Returns to 1-arg constructor | `[hr=10]` | `T2` |
| 5 | 1-arg constructor exits | — | — |

**Final Output Sequence:** `T4 T2`

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Model — Overload Selection as a Decision Function

Let $S = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n\}$ be the set of overloaded methods with same name $f$. For a call $f(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k)$ with argument types $A_i$:

$$
\text{Select}(f, A_1, \ldots, A_k) = \arg\min_{m_j \in S} \, \text{Cost}(A_i, P_j^{(i)})
$$

where $P_j^{(i)}$ is the parameter type of method $m_j$ at position $i$, and:

$$
\text{Cost}(A, P) =
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } A = P \text{ (exact match)} \\
1, & \text{if } A \xrightarrow{\text{widen}} P \text{ (widening)} \\
2, & \text{if } A \leftrightarrow P \text{ (autoboxing)} \\
3, & \text{if } P = T\ldots \text{ (varargs)} \\
\infty, & \text{if no conversion exists}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Ambiguous} \iff \exists\, m_a, m_b \in S \text{ such that } \text{Cost}(A, P_a) = \text{Cost}(A, P_b) < \infty
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formalization is the basis for KTU numerical-type questions where students must justify why a particular overload is chosen.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 `this` Keyword — Object Reference Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Caller: Employee e1 = new Employee Arjun, 50000] --> B[JVM: heap allocates Object_e1]
    B --> C[Push Object_e1 reference as this onto operand stack]
    C --> D[Invoke constructor Employee String, double]
    D --> E[this.name = name]
    D --> F[this.salary = salary]
    E --> G[PUTFIELD stores Arjun in Object_e1.name]
    F --> H[PUTFIELD stores 50000.0 in Object_e1.salary]
    G --> I[Return reference to caller]
    H --> I
    I --> J[e1 now refers to fully initialized Object_e1]

    K[Future call: e1.setSalary 55000.0] --> L[JVM pushes Object_e1 as this]
    L --> M[this.salary = 55000.0 updates field]
    M --> N[return this returns Object_e1 reference]
    N --> O[Chained .print executes on Object_e1]
```

## 4.2 Method Overloading — Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Method Call f arg1, arg2...] --> P1{Phase 1: Exact Match?}
    P1 -- Yes --> END1[Bind to exact match method]
    P1 -- No --> P2{Phase 2: Widening Promotion?}
    P2 -- Yes --> END2[Bind to widened method]
    P2 -- No --> P3{Phase 3a: Autoboxing or Unboxing?}
    P3 -- Yes --> END3[Bind to boxed method]
    P3 -- No --> P3B{Phase 3b: Varargs Match?}
    P3B -- Yes --> END4[Bind to varargs method]
    P3B -- No --> ERR[COMPILE ERROR: cannot resolve]
    
    P2 -- Ambiguous? --> AMB[COMPILE ERROR: ambiguous]
    P3 -- Ambiguous? --> AMB
```

## 4.3 Constructor Chaining via `this(...)`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time t = new Time 10] --> B[Time int hr]
    B -- this hr, 0, 0 --> C[Time int hr, int min, int sec]
    C -- this hr, min, 0 --> D[Time int hr, int min, int sec final]
    D --> E[Assign fields hr=10, min=0, sec=0]
    E -- print T4 --> F[Return to Time int hr]
    F -- print T2 --> G[Return to main]
    G -- print none --> H[t now references fully built Time object]
```

## 4.4 Overload Selection Decision Matrix

