```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[int literal 5]
        I2[long literal 5L]
        I3[float literal 5.0f]
        I4[double literal 5.0]
        I5[String literal Hi]
    end
    
    subgraph Methods
        M1[show int]
        M2[show long]
        M3[show Integer]
        M4[show double]
        M5[show String]
        M6[show int...]
    end
    
    I1 --> M1
    I1 -.widening.-> M2
    I1 -.autoboxing.-> M3
    I1 -.varargs.-> M6
    
    I2 --> M2
    I3 -.float to double.-> M4
    I4 --> M4
    I5 --> M5
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Variable Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MethodScope[Method or Constructor Scope]
        P[Local Parameters: name, salary]
        L[Local Variables]
    end
    
    subgraph ObjectScope[Object Instance Scope]
        F1[Field: this.name]
        F2[Field: this.salary]
    end
    
    P -- Unqualified name refers to --> P
    F1 -- Qualified this.name refers to --> F1
    F2 -- Qualified this.salary refers to --> F2
    
    COMPILER{JVM Compiler Identifier Resolution}
    P --> COMPILER
    F1 --> COMPILER
    F2 --> COMPILER
    COMPILER --> RULE[Rule: Closest scope wins; use this to escape]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Explain the purpose of the `this` keyword in Java. List any **four** uses with examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`this` is a reference variable in Java that refers to the **current object** — the instance on which the currently executing method or constructor was invoked. It is implicitly passed by the JVM as the first argument to every non-static method.

**Four uses:**

1. **Differentiating instance variable from parameter** (when names clash):
```java
void setName(String name) { this.name = name; }
```

2. **Passing current object as argument** to another method:
```java
s.register(this);   // 'this' = current Student object
```

3. **Returning current object** for method chaining:
```java
Builder setX(int x) { this.x = x; return this; }
```

4. **Constructor chaining** within the same class:
```java
Box(int a) { this(a, 10); }   // calls Box(int, int)
```

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1 mark) + 4 uses × 0.5 mark each (2 marks).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is method overloading? Can two methods be overloaded if they differ only in return type? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Method overloading** is a feature that allows a class to have **multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists** (different number, type, or order of parameters). It is an example of **compile-time polymorphism**.

**Answer to sub-question:** **No**, two methods cannot be overloaded if they differ **only** in return type. The Java compiler determines which method to call based on the **method signature** (method name + parameter list). Return type is **not** part of the signature.

```java
int  sum(int a, int b);     // ❌ Compile error if both exist
double sum(int a, int b);   // "duplicate method sum(int,int)"
```

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1 mark) + Justification (1.5 marks) + Example (0.5 mark).

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in ESE. **Attempt either Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable Java program, illustrate the use of the `this` keyword for:
   (i) Resolving name conflicts between instance variables and parameters.
   (ii) Constructor chaining within the same class.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain method overloading in Java. Discuss the rules for valid overloading. Why does overloading qualify as **compile-time polymorphism**? Provide an example involving both *widening promotion* and *autoboxing*.

---

#### 📌 Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Program demonstrating both uses:**

```java
class Rectangle {
    private double length;
    private double breadth;
    
    // (i) Resolving name conflict — parameters shadow fields
    public Rectangle(double length, double breadth) {
        this.length = length;     // LHS = field, RHS = parameter
        this.breadth = breadth;
    }
    
    // (ii) Constructor chaining — this() must be first statement
    public Rectangle() {
        this(1.0, 1.0);          // chains to 2-arg constructor with default 1x1
    }
    
    public Rectangle(double side) {
        this(side, side);         // chains to 2-arg constructor (square)
    }
    
    public Rectangle(double length, double breadth) {
        // already defined above
    }
    
    public double area() { return this.length * this.breadth; }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle(5.0, 3.0);   // direct 2-arg → area = 15.0
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(4.0);         // square → area = 16.0
        Rectangle r3 = new Rectangle();            // default → area = 1.0
        System.out.println(r1.area() + " " + r2.area() + " " + r3.area());
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

- In `Rectangle(double length, double breadth)`, the parameters `length` and `breadth` **shadow** the instance fields of the same name. Without `this.`, the assignment `length = length` would be a no-op (assigning parameter to itself).
- In `Rectangle()`, the call `this(1.0, 1.0)` invokes the 2-arg constructor, eliminating duplicate initialization logic — a hallmark of clean OOP design.

> **Marking Scheme:** Program correctness (3) + `this` for shadowing explained (2) + `this()` chaining explained (2).

---

#### 📌 Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Method Overloading Definition:**
Method overloading allows multiple methods in the same class to share the **same name** but differ in their **parameter signature** — i.e., number of parameters, types of parameters, or order of parameter types.

**Rules for Valid Overloading:**

1. ✅ Different number of parameters.
2. ✅ Different types of parameters.
3. ✅ Different order of parameter types.
4. ❌ Different return type **alone** → compile error.
5. ❌ Different access modifier **alone** → compile error.
6. ❌ Different exception list **alone** → compile error.

**Why Compile-Time Polymorphism?**
The compiler resolves the method call at **compile time** by examining the **static types** of the arguments. The actual method to be invoked is **fixed in the .class file** at compile time — there is no runtime decision-making. This is called **static binding** or **early binding**.

**Example with Widening vs Autoboxing:**

```java
class Demo {
    void show(int x)        { System.out.println("int: "        + x); }
    void show(long x)       { System.out.println("long: "       + x); }
    void show(Integer x)    { System.out.println("Integer: "    + x); }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Demo d = new Demo();
        d.show(5);       // Phase 1 exact match → show(int) → "int: 5"
        d.show(5L);      // Phase 1 exact match → show(long) → "long: 5"
        d.show(Integer.valueOf(5));  // exact match → "Integer: 5"
    }
}
```

**Key Resolution Rule:** Widening primitive conversion (`int → long`) takes **precedence** over autoboxing (`int → Integer`). If `show(int)` is removed and only `show(long)` and `show(Integer)` remain, the call `d.show(5)` becomes **ambiguous** — compile error.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (2) + Rules (2) + Compile-time polymorphism explanation (2) + Example (1).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **phases of method resolution** in Java overloading with a suitable example. What happens when both widening and autoboxing are equally applicable?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a Java program, demonstrate the use of `this` to:
   (i) Return the current object from a method (enabling method chaining).
   (ii) Pass the current object as a parameter to another method.
   Show the corresponding output and explain each line.

---

#### 📌 Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**The Three-Phase Resolution Algorithm (JLS §15.12.2):**

**Phase 1 — Strict / Exact Match:** Compiler looks for an exact type match for each argument (after boxing/unboxing for literals). If found, the method is selected.

**Phase 2 — Widening Primitive Conversion:** If no Phase 1 match, the compiler applies widening primitive conversions (`byte → short → int → long → float → double`, `char → int`, etc.) to find a match.

**Phase 3 — Autoboxing / Varargs:** If still no match, the compiler tries autoboxing (`int ↔ Integer`) and finally varargs.

**Example:**

```java
class Test {
    static void m(long x)    { System.out.println("long");    }
    static void m(Integer x) { System.out.println("Integer"); }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        m(5);      // Phase 2 wins → widening int → long → "long"
    }
}
```

**Output:** `long`

**Reason:** Phase 2 (widening primitive `int → long`) is **more specific** than Phase 3 (autoboxing `int → Integer`). The compiler always prefers the **earlier phase**.

**What if both are equally applicable?**

```java
class Test {
    static void m(long x)    { System.out.println("long");    }
    static void m(Integer x) { System.out.println("Integer"); }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        m(5);      // AMBIGUOUS — Phase 2 reaches long; Phase 3 reaches Integer
    }
}
```

**Result:** `error: reference to m is ambiguous, both method m(long) and method m(Integer) match`. The compiler refuses to guess.

> **Marking Scheme:** Three phases explained (3) + Example (2) + Ambiguity case (2).

---

#### 📌 Model Solution — Q.B(b)

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    
    Student(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }
    
    // (i) Returning 'this' — enables method chaining (fluent API)
    Student celebrate() {
        System.out.println(this.name + " celebrated!");
        return this;     // returns current Student object
    }
    
    Student study() {
        System.out.println(this.name + " studied hard!");
        return this;
    }
    
    // (ii) Passing 'this' to another method
    void register(Department d) {
        d.enroll(this);   // passes current Student reference
    }
}

class Department {
    void enroll(Student s) {
        System.out.println(s.name + " enrolled in department.");
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student("Arjun");
        
        // Method chaining — returns 'this' each time
        s.celebrate().study().celebrate();
        
        // Pass current object to another class's method
        s.register(new Department());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Arjun celebrated!
Arjun studied hard!
Arjun celebrated!
Arjun enrolled in department.
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

1. `s.celebrate()` → `this` = `s`. Prints `"Arjun celebrated!"`, returns `s`.
2. `.study()` called on returned `s` → `this` = `s`. Prints `"Arjun studied hard!"`, returns `s`.
3. `.celebrate()` called again → prints `"Arjun celebrated!"`, returns `s`.
4. `s.register(new Department())` → `register()` calls `d.enroll(this)`, passing the current `Student` reference `s` to `Department.enroll()`.

> **Marking Scheme:** Program (3) + Output (1) + Return `this` explained (1.5) + Pass `this` explained (1.5).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Writing `this(...)` NOT as the first statement** → compile error → 0 marks for that sub-question.
> 2. **Forgetting that return type alone ≠ overloading** → students lose 1–2 marks in Part A.
> 3. **Confusing method overloading with method overriding** — overloading is **same class**, overriding is **parent-child** inheritance. Marks deducted if confused.
> 4. **Skipping the resolution phases** in long answers — examiner expects explicit mention of *Phase 1 (exact) → Phase 2 (widening) → Phase 3 (boxing/varargs)*.
> 5. **Ambiguous method calls** — students often forget to verify ambiguity. If a call is ambiguous, the program does not compile.
> 6. **Using `this` inside `static` context** — compile error: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context"*.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **`this`** is an **implicit, final reference** to the **current object**, auto-injected by the JVM as the **first parameter** of every non-static method/constructor.
- ✅ **`this` cannot be used in `static` methods, static blocks, or static variable initializers.**
- ✅ **`this(...)`** invokes another constructor in the **same class**; **must be the first statement** in the constructor body.
- ✅ **`this` as return value** enables **fluent method chaining** (e.g., `StringBuilder.append().append()`).
- ✅ **Method overloading = same method name + different parameter signature**, in the **same class**.
- ✅ Overloading **differs from overriding** — overloading is **compile-time polymorphism** (static binding); overriding is **runtime polymorphism** (dynamic binding, covered in Module 3).
- ✅ **Resolution order:** Exact Match → Widening Primitive Promotion → Autoboxing/Unboxing → Varargs.
- ✅ **Widening beats autoboxing** in priority.
- ✅ **Return type alone ≠ overload** — causes compile error.
- ✅ **Ambiguity = compile error** — if two applicable methods are equally specific.
- ✅ **Six canonical uses of `this`:** variable disambiguation, argument passing, object return, constructor chaining, constructor argument, monitor lock in synchronization.
- ✅ **Engineering utility:** Overloading enables intuitive APIs (`println`, `Math.abs`), while `this` enables fluent APIs (Lombok, Stream builders, JPA query DSL).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Access Control (KTU 2024 Scheme — PEEIT741 Definition)**
> Access Control is a fundamental Object-Oriented Programming mechanism that regulates the visibility and accessibility of **classes, methods, constructors, and data members** through access specifiers (also called access modifiers). It enforces the OOP principle of **Encapsulation** by restricting unauthorized access to the internal state of an object, thereby ensuring **data hiding**, **modularity**, and **controlled interface exposure**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEIT741 — Object Oriented Programming), Access Control is studied primarily in the context of **Java** (with comparative references to **C++**). The four canonical access levels are:

| # | Access Specifier | Keyword | Restrictiveness |
|---|------------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Private | `private` | Most restrictive |
| 2 | Default (Package-Private) | *(no keyword)* | Package-restricted |
| 3 | Protected | `protected` | Inheritance-aware |
| 4 | Public | `public` | Least restrictive |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House & Rooms" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Think of a software class as a **house**, and its members (variables and methods) as **rooms** inside it.

- **`private` rooms** → Your **personal bedroom**. Only *you* (the class itself) can enter. Family members (other classes), neighbours (different packages), and guests (subclasses outside the package) are **NOT allowed**.
- **`default` (package-private) rooms** → A **shared living room** in an *apartment complex* (package). Only residents of the *same apartment* (same package) may enter. Outsiders from other buildings (other packages) cannot.
- **`protected` rooms** → A **family heirloom room**. Only you and your *direct family* (subclasses — even if they live in a different apartment / package via inheritance) can access it.
- **`public` rooms** → A **public park** or **road**. *Anyone*, anywhere, at any time, can access it.

This single analogy maps **every** access rule in Java. Memorize it before reading further.

---

## 1.3 Why Access Control Exists — The Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Syllabus Highlight**
> Access control is **not** a syntax formality. It is a **design contract** that:
> 1. Protects the **invariants** of a class (e.g., a bank balance can never be negative).
> 2. Reduces **coupling** between classes — internal implementation can change without breaking external code.
> 3. Enables **secure API design** — only sanctioned methods are exposed to the user.
> 4. Supports **maintainability** — clear ownership boundaries simplify debugging and refactoring.

The standard industry metric cited in KTU viva panels: **a well-encapsulated class exposes < 20% of its members as `public`**.

---

## 1.4 Access Control vs. Access Specifiers — Terminology Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> These two terms are often used interchangeably in textbooks, but KTU examiners appreciate precision:
> - **Access Specifier** → The keyword itself (`private`, `protected`, `public`, or default).
> - **Access Modifier** → A broader category that also includes `static`, `final`, `abstract`, `synchronized`, etc.
> - **Access Control** → The overarching *mechanism / policy* that uses specifiers to govern visibility.

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus lists the topic as **"Access Control"** — so in answer sheets, prefer the umbrella term "Access Control Mechanism" when introducing the concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Four Access Levels — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 `private` Access

- **Scope**: Declared members are accessible **only within the same class** in which they are declared.
- **Visibility outside class**: ❌ Not accessible to subclasses, same-package classes, or any other class.
- **Typical use case**: Data fields (instance variables) that store the internal state.
- **KTU Board Tip**: Examiners frequently ask *"Can a `private` member be inherited?"* — The answer is **Yes, it is inherited but NOT directly accessible**. The subclass must use `public`/`protected` getter methods.

### 2.1.2 Default (Package-Private) Access

- **Scope**: No keyword is written. The member is accessible to **all classes within the same package** but inaccessible outside the package.
- **Also called**: *Package-private* or *friendly* access (though "friendly" is obsolete terminology).
- **Typical use case**: Helper classes and package-internal utility methods.
- **KTU Pitfall**: Many students wrongly write `default` as a keyword. **There is no `default` keyword for access in Java** — the absence of a specifier *is* the default access. (The `default` keyword exists only in `switch` statements and interface methods from Java 8+.)

### 2.1.3 `protected` Access

- **Scope**: Accessible within the **same package** AND by **subclasses in any package** (even outside the original package).
- **Inheritance interaction**: This is the only specifier (other than `public`) that respects inheritance across package boundaries.
- **Typical use case**: Methods designed to be overridden by subclasses but hidden from unrelated clients.
- **Important Caveat**: `protected` access across packages applies **only to inherited members**, not to objects of the superclass type. (This is the famous "protected hole" rule tested in KTU exams.)

### 2.1.4 `public` Access

- **Scope**: Accessible from **everywhere** — same class, same package, different package, different module.
- **Typical use case**: The API surface — methods that constitute the public contract of the class.
- **Engineering principle**: *"Make a member `public` only when you are willing to support it forever."* — Effective Java, Item 15.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Access Control Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most-tested artifact in KTU PEEIT741 Module 2.** Memorize it row-by-row.

| Visibility Scenario | `private` | `default` | `protected` | `public` |
|---------------------|:---------:|:---------:|:-----------:|:--------:|
| Same class | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Same package (subclass) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Same package (non-subclass) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Different package (subclass via inheritance) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Different package (non-subclass) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Different package (subclass accessing via superclass reference) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

**Legend**: ✅ = Accessible, ❌ = Not Accessible

### 2.2.1 Class-Level Access Control

> [!NOTE]
> Classes (top-level) can be declared **only** as `public` or default. They **cannot** be `private` or `protected` (a `protected` top-level class is a compile-time error in Java). Inner classes, however, can use all four specifiers.

| Class Type | Allowed Specifiers |
|------------|-------------------|
| Top-level class | `public`, default |
| Inner (nested) class | `public`, `protected`, `private`, default |

### 2.2.2 Constructor Access Control

- Constructors follow the same rules as methods. A `private` constructor is used in:
  - **Singleton Design Pattern** (only one instance allowed).
  - **Static Factory Methods**.
  - **Utility classes** that should never be instantiated (e.g., `java.lang.Math`).

### 2.2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Use of Access Control |
|-----------------|----------------------|
| Banking Software | `private` fields for account balance, `public` methods for transactions |
| Android SDK | `public` API surface vs. `@hide` (package-private) internal classes |
| Java Standard Library | `public` interfaces, `protected` `clone()` method in `Object` |
| Game Engines (Unity/Unreal) | `public` engine API, `private` internal state machines |

---

## 2.3 Default vs. Protected — The Most Confused Pair

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Confusion Point**
>
> | Feature | `default` | `protected` |
> |---------|-----------|-------------|
> | Cross-package subclass access | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
> | Same-package non-subclass access | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
> | Keyword required | No (absence = default) | Yes (`protected`) |
> | Affects inheritance | No | Yes |
>
> **Rule of thumb**: If you need cross-package inheritance access, you **must** use `protected`. `default` will silently break compilation.

---

## 2.4 Access Control in C++ (Comparative Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 2 sometimes asks comparative questions between Java and C++ access specifiers.

| Feature | Java | C++ |
|---------|------|-----|
| Number of access specifiers | 4 (incl. default) | 3 (`private`, `protected`, `public`) |
| Default access for `class` | package-private | `private` |
| Default access for `struct` | package-private | `public` |
| Multiple specifiers per class | ❌ (one default per class) | ✅ (multiple `public:`, `private:` blocks allowed) |
| `friend` mechanism | ❌ None | ✅ Yes |
| Access via inheritance type | Specifier-based | `public`, `protected`, `private` inheritance |

In C++, **`private` and `protected` members are technically *inherited* but the access level can be further restricted by the inheritance type** — a uniquely C++ concept.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Comprehensive Visibility Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark question pattern**: Write a program demonstrating all four access specifiers and verify the visibility matrix.

### 3.1.1 Class Under Test

```java
// File: MasterClass.java (Package: university.core)
package university.core;

public class MasterClass {
    private   int a = 1;   // Private
              int b = 2;   // Default (package-private)
    protected int c = 3;   // Protected
    public    int d = 4;   // Public

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Inside MasterClass:");
        System.out.println("a = " + a);  // ✅ private - same class
        System.out.println("b = " + b);  // ✅ default  - same class
        System.out.println("c = " + c);  // ✅ protected - same class
        System.out.println("d = " + d);  // ✅ public   - same class
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Same-Package Non-Subclass Access

```java
// File: SamePackageTest.java (Package: university.core)
package university.core;

public class SamePackageTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MasterClass obj = new MasterClass();
        // System.out.println(obj.a);  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR - private
        System.out.println(obj.b);     // ✅ default - same package
        System.out.println(obj.c);     // ✅ protected - same package
        System.out.println(obj.d);     // ✅ public
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**
- **Step 1:** `obj.a` → `a` is declared `private` in `MasterClass`. The compiler enforces that only `MasterClass` itself can access `a`. `SamePackageTest` is a *different class*, so access is denied. **Compile error: `a has private access in MasterClass`**.
- **Step 2:** `obj.b` → `b` has default access. `SamePackageTest` resides in `university.core` — the same package. **Allowed**.
- **Step 3:** `obj.c` → `c` is `protected`. Same-package access is always allowed regardless of inheritance. **Allowed**.
- **Step 4:** `obj.d` → `d` is `public`. **Allowed** from anywhere.

### 3.1.3 Cross-Package Subclass Access

```java
// File: ChildClass.java (Package: university.derived)
package university.derived;
import university.core.MasterClass;

public class ChildClass extends MasterClass {
    public void test() {
        // System.out.println(a);  // ❌ private - not accessible
        // System.out.println(b);  // ❌ default  - different package
        System.out.println(c);     // ✅ protected - inherited, accessible
        System.out.println(d);     // ✅ public
    }
}
```

**Analytical derivation of the `c` access:**
- **Step 1:** `c` is declared `protected` in `MasterClass` (package `university.core`).
- **Step 2:** `ChildClass` resides in package `university.derived` — a **different package**.
- **Step 3:** However, `ChildClass extends MasterClass`, so it is a **subclass in a different package**.
- **Step 4:** Per the Java Language Specification (§6.6.2), a `protected` member is accessible to a subclass in another package **only if the access is through an inheritance-based reference** (i.e., the member must be accessed via a reference of the subclass type, not the superclass type).
- **Step 5:** Inside `ChildClass`, `c` is being accessed without any explicit qualifier — this resolves to the inherited field of `this`, which is of type `ChildClass`. **Allowed**.

### 3.1.4 Cross-Package Non-Subclass Access

```java
// File: ForeignClass.java (Package: university.foreign)
package university.foreign;
import university.core.MasterClass;

public class ForeignClass {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MasterClass obj = new MasterClass();
        // System.out.println(obj.a); // ❌ private
        // System.out.println(obj.b); // ❌ default
        // System.out.println(obj.c); // ❌ protected - no inheritance
        System.out.println(obj.d);    // ✅ public only
    }
}
```

**Derivation logic for `c`:**
- `c` is `protected`. `ForeignClass` is in a different package and **does not extend** `MasterClass`. Therefore, the inheritance-based exception does not apply. **Denied at compile time.**

### 3.1.5 The "Protected Hole" — Subclass Accessing Superclass Reference

```java
// File: ProtectedHoleTest.java (Package: university.derived)
package university.derived;
import university.core.MasterClass;

public class ProtectedHoleTest extends MasterClass {
    public void probe(MasterClass ref) {
        // System.out.println(ref.c);  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR!
        System.out.println(this.c);     // ✅ Works fine
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- **Step 1:** The parameter `ref` is of type `MasterClass` — the superclass.
- **Step 2:** Even though `ProtectedHoleTest` is a subclass of `MasterClass`, the Java compiler does **not** allow accessing `protected` members through a **superclass-typed reference** from a different package.
- **Step 3:** This rule exists to prevent a subclass in another package from accessing protected members of *other* subclasses or *other instances* of the superclass.
- **Step 4:** Accessing `this.c` works because `c` is being accessed through the inheriting class's own context.
- **Conclusion**: The access succeeds **only** via `this` or a subclass-typed reference.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The Encapsulation Pattern (Getter/Setter)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **second most-asked KTU pattern**: Show how `private` data is exposed safely via `public` methods.

```java
// File: Student.java
package university.model;

public class Student {
    private String name;     // Hidden state
    private int rollNo;

    public void setName(String name) {
        if (name != null && !name.trim().isEmpty()) {
            this.name = name;     // Validation enforced
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid name");
        }
    }

    public String getName() {
        return this.name;
    }

    public void setRollNo(int rollNo) {
        if (rollNo > 0) {
            this.rollNo = rollNo;
        }
    }

    public int getRollNo() {
        return this.rollNo;
    }
}
```

**Analytical walkthrough:**

- **Step 1:** The fields `name` and `rollNo` are declared `private` — they cannot be touched directly from outside.
- **Step 2:** The setter methods are `public` — they are the *only* sanctioned entry points.
- **Step 3:** Inside `setName`, a **validation guard** is applied. Without encapsulation, the caller could set `name = null` and crash downstream code.
- **Step 4:** The getter methods are `public` but **read-only** — they return a copy (or immutable `String` reference) without exposing a setter.
- **Step 5:** This pattern embodies the OOP principle: *"Hide the data, expose the behaviour."*

**Test driver:**

```java
// File: StudentTest.java
package university.app;
import university.model.Student;

public class StudentTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student();
        s.setName("Ananya");
        s.setRollNo(47);
        System.out.println(s.getName() + " - " + s.getRollNo());

        s.setRollNo(-1);    // Validation rejects this
        System.out.println(s.getRollNo());  // Still 47
    }
}
```

**Output derivation:**
- Line 1 prints: `Ananya - 47`
- `setRollNo(-1)` triggers the validation guard, the assignment is skipped.
- Line 2 prints: `47` (the previous valid value remains).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Access Control with Inheritance Chain

```java
package level1;
public class A {
    protected int x = 10;
    private   int y = 20;
}

package level2;
import level1.A;
public class B extends A {
    void demo() {
        System.out.println(x);   // ✅ inherited protected
        // System.out.println(y); // ❌ private - not accessible
    }
}

package level3;
import level2.B;
public class C extends B {
    void test() {
        System.out.println(x);   // ✅ still accessible (inherited transitively)
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**
- **Step 1:** `x` is `protected` in `A`. `B` is a subclass → can access.
- **Step 2:** `C` is a subclass of `B`, which is a subclass of `A`. So `x` is *transitively inherited* and remains `protected` in `C` (assuming `public` inheritance).
- **Step 3:** `y` is `private` in `A`. It is *technically inherited* in the bytecode sense, but **never accessible** outside `A` — not by `B`, not by `C`, not by anyone.
- **Step 4:** Therefore, `System.out.println(y)` fails to compile in both `B` and `C`.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Specifier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix as a checklist when designing classes for KTU lab exams and assignments.**

| If the member is... | Use this specifier | Rationale |
|---------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| An internal data field | `private` | Hide state, enable validation |
| A package-internal helper | default | Limit visibility to same package |
| A method meant for overriding in any package | `protected` | Inheritance-friendly access |
| Part of the public API | `public` | Full exposure, stable contract |
| A class constant | `public static final` | Global, immutable, accessible |
| A constructor (singleton) | `private` | Restrict instantiation |
| An abstract method | `public` (or `protected` rarely) | Must be overridable |
| An overridden `Object` method (`equals`, `toString`) | `public` | Match superclass contract |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Access Surface Ratio

> [!NOTE]
> This is a metric sometimes asked in viva: *Calculate the public-to-private member ratio of a class.*

**Problem:** A class `Invoice` has 3 `public` methods, 1 `protected` method, 2 default methods, 4 `private` data fields, and 1 `public` constant.

**Solution derivation:**

$$
\text{Total members} = 3 + 1 + 2 + 4 + 1 = 11
$$

$$
\text{Public members} = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

$$
\text{Public surface ratio} = \frac{\text{Public members}}{\text{Total members}} = \frac{4}{11} \approx 36.36\%
$$

**Evaluation against KTU best practice benchmark (< 20% public surface):**
- **Result**: 36.36% > 20% threshold.
- **Recommendation**: Mark 2 of the 3 `public` methods as `private` (refactor into helper methods) to bring the public surface below 20%.

This shows that access control is not just a syntactic concern — it is **measurable** and **auditable**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Java Access Control Visibility Funnel

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 1**: This Mermaid block renders the four access levels as concentric rings. The inner ring (most restrictive) is `private`; the outer ring (least restrictive) is `public`. Each ring lists the contexts in which that access level is allowed.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef privateStyle fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef defaultStyle fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc8400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef protectedStyle fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#b3a300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef publicStyle fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#008000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    O(["PUBLIC — Any class, any package, anywhere"])

    P(["PROTECTED — Same package + Subclasses in any package"])

    D(["DEFAULT — Same package only"])

    PR(["PRIVATE — Same class only"])

    O --> P
    P --> D
    D --> PR

    O --- O1["Allowed: All classes globally"]
    P --- P1["Allowed: university.core package + any subclass of this class"]
    D --- D1["Allowed: university.core package only"]
    PR --- PR1["Allowed: Declaring class only"]

    class O publicStyle
    class P protectedStyle
    class D defaultStyle
    class PR privateStyle
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Package Inheritance Access Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 2**: This diagram models the flow of access from a subclass in a foreign package accessing a protected member of its superclass in another package. The `Protected Hole` is highlighted.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef pkg1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99
    classDef pkg2 fill:#e5ccff,stroke:#6600cc
    classDef deny fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    classDef allow fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#008000

    subgraph PkgCore["Package: university.core"]
        A["MasterClass<br/>protected int c<br/>public int d"]
    end

    subgraph PkgDerived["Package: university.derived"]
        B["ChildClass extends MasterClass<br/>Inherits c and d"]
        H["Protected Hole Test<br/>MasterClass ref = new MasterClass()"]
    end

    subgraph PkgForeign["Package: university.foreign"]
        F["ForeignClass<br/>Does NOT extend MasterClass"]
    end

    B -- "this.c -- ALLOWED" --> A
    B -- "this.d -- ALLOWED" --> A
    H -- "ref.c -- DENIED" --> A
    F -- "obj.c -- DENIED" --> A
    F -- "obj.d -- ALLOWED" --> A

    class A pkg1
    class B,H pkg2
    class F pkg2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Access Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 3**: A block-level functional architecture showing how access control integrates with the OOP stack: source code → compiler enforcement → runtime access resolution → encapsulation guarantee.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef src fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99
    classDef comp fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#b3a300
    classDef run fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#008000
    classDef goal fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000

    S1["Source Code<br/>class declaration with specifiers"]:::src
    S2["Member Declaration<br/>private / default / protected / public"]:::src

    C1["Java Compiler<br/>Bytecode Generation"]:::comp
    C2["Symbol Table<br/>Stores access flags per member"]:::comp
    C3["Access Check<br/>Validates visibility at compile time"]:::comp

    R1["JVM Class Loader<br/>Loads class with access flags"]:::run
    R2["Reflection Layer<br/>setAccessible bypasses checks"]:::run
    R3["Security Manager<br/>Runtime policy enforcement"]:::run

    G1["Encapsulation<br/>Data hiding achieved"]:::goal
    G2["Modularity<br/>Loose coupling between classes"]:::goal
    G3["Maintainability<br/>Internal changes are safe"]:::goal

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 -->|Allowed| R1
    C3 -->|Denied| C4["Compile-time Error"]:::goal
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Encapsulation Workflow (Getter/Setter Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4**: Sequence-flow style block diagram showing how an external client safely reads/writes a `private` field through `public` getter/setter methods, with validation logic embedded.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef client fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99
    classDef api fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#008000
    classDef data fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000

    CLIENT["External Client<br/>e.g., StudentTest class"]:::client

    SETTER["public void setRollNo(int r)<br/>Validation: r > 0"]:::api
    GETTER["public int getRollNo()<br/>Returns this.rollNo"]:::api

    FIELD["private int rollNo<br/>Hidden state"]:::data

    CLIENT -->|"s.setRollNo(-1)"| SETTER
    SETTER -->|"Validation fails"| REJECT["Reject - no assignment"]:::data
    SETTER -->|"Validation passes"| ASSIGN["rollNo = r"]:::data
    ASSIGN --> FIELD

    CLIENT -->|"s.getRollNo()"| GETTER
    GETTER --> FIELD
    FIELD -->|"return value"| CLIENT
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Explain the different access specifiers available in Java. Mention their visibility scope.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Java provides four access specifiers that control the visibility of classes, methods, and data members:

1. **`private`** (1 mark): Members declared `private` are accessible **only within the same class** in which they are declared. They cannot be accessed by any other class, including subclasses.

2. **Default / Package-Private** (1 mark): When no access specifier is mentioned, the member has *default* access. It is accessible to all classes within the **same package** but inaccessible outside the package.

3. **`protected`** (0.5 marks): A `protected` member is accessible within the **same package** and also by **subclasses in different packages** through inheritance.

4. **`public`** (0.5 marks): A `public` member is accessible from **everywhere** — any class in any package.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *What is the difference between `private` and `protected` access specifiers in Java? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `private` | `protected` |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Same class | ✅ Accessible | ✅ Accessible |
| Same package (other classes) | ❌ Not accessible | ✅ Accessible |
| Different package (subclass) | ❌ Not accessible | ✅ Accessible |
| Different package (non-subclass) | ❌ Not accessible | ❌ Not accessible |

**Example of `private`** (1 mark):
```java
class Account {
    private double balance;  // Only Account can access
}
```

**Example of `protected`** (1 mark):
```java
class Animal {
    protected void speak() {  // Accessible in subclasses
        System.out.println("...");
    }
}
```

The key distinction is that `protected` supports inheritance-based access across packages, while `private` does not.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, Part B questions of 14 marks offer an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided below. Each carries sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### 📘 Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
> *(a)* Explain the four access specifiers in Java with a suitable code example illustrating how each restricts visibility across packages. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* What is encapsulation? Demonstrate with a Java program how access control enforces encapsulation through getter and setter methods with validation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
Java provides four access specifiers: `private`, default (package-private), `protected`, and `public`. These control the visibility of class members across classes and packages.

**Step 2 — Code example (4 marks):**

```java
// Package: college.dept
package college.dept;
public class Faculty {
    private   int id = 101;
              String name = "Dr. Rao";   // default
    protected String dept = "CSE";
    public    String college = "KTU";

    public void show() {
        System.out.println(id + " " + name + " " + dept + " " + college);
    }
}
```

```java
// Package: college.dept (same package)
package college.dept;
public class SamePackageDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Faculty f = new Faculty();
        // f.id ❌ private
        System.out.println(f.name);    // ✅ default
        System.out.println(f.dept);    // ✅ protected
        System.out.println(f.college); // ✅ public
    }
}
```

```java
// Package: college.foreign
package college.foreign;
import college.dept.Faculty;
public class ForeignDemo extends Faculty {
    public void test() {
        // id ❌ private, name ❌ default
        System.out.println(dept);     // ✅ protected via inheritance
        System.out.println(college);  // ✅ public
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Visibility analysis (2 marks):**
- `id` (private) — accessible only inside `Faculty`.
- `name` (default) — accessible inside `college.dept` package only.
- `dept` (protected) — accessible in same package and in subclasses in any package.
- `college` (public) — accessible from anywhere.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Encapsulation is the OOP principle of **binding data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class)** and **restricting direct external access** to the data via access specifiers.

**Step 2 — Code (4 marks):**

```java
package banking.model;

public class BankAccount {
    private String accountHolder;
    private double balance;

    public void setAccountHolder(String name) {
        if (name != null && !name.isEmpty()) {
            this.accountHolder = name;
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid name");
        }
    }

    public String getAccountHolder() {
        return accountHolder;
    }

    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            balance += amount;
            System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid deposit amount");
        }
    }

    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0 && amount <= balance) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Insufficient funds or invalid amount");
        }
    }

    public double getBalance() {
        return balance;
    }
}
```

```java
package banking.app;
import banking.model.BankAccount;

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new BankAccount();
        acc.setAccountHolder("Anjali");
        acc.deposit(5000);
        acc.withdraw(1500);
        acc.withdraw(10000);  // Rejected by validation
        System.out.println("Final Balance: " + acc.getBalance());
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Explanation of how access control enforces encapsulation (2 marks):**
- The `balance` field is `private`, so external classes cannot directly read or modify it.
- All mutations must go through the `public` methods `deposit()` and `withdraw()`, which embed **validation logic**.
- The getter `getBalance()` exposes the value *read-only* — there is no `setBalance()`.
- This pattern guarantees the invariant: *balance can never be negative, and account holder is never null/empty*.

---

### 📗 Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**
> *(a)* Compare and contrast access specifiers in Java and C++. Which access specifier is the default for a `class` and a `struct` in C++? *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Explain the "protected access across packages" rule in Java with an example. What is the "protected hole" and how does Java resolve it? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Java access specifiers (2 marks):**
Java has four — `private`, default, `protected`, `public`. Default applies when no keyword is written. They apply uniformly to all members and are checked at compile time.

**Step 2 — C++ access specifiers (2 marks):**
C++ has three — `private`, `protected`, `public`. The default for a `class` is `private`; the default for a `struct` is `public`. C++ also supports `friend` declarations and inheritance-type modifiers (public/protected/private inheritance).

**Step 3 — Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Java | C++ |
|---------|------|-----|
| Number of access specifiers | 4 (incl. default) | 3 |
| Default for `class` | package-private | `private` |
| Default for `struct` | package-private | `public` |
| `friend` mechanism | ❌ | ✅ |
| Multiple specifier blocks per class | ❌ | ✅ |
| Access modification via inheritance type | ❌ | ✅ |
| Compile-time enforcement | ✅ | ✅ |

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
C++ provides finer-grained control via `friend` and inheritance types, while Java's model is simpler and more uniform, relying on package boundaries and a single inheritance model.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — The basic protected rule (2 marks):**
A `protected` member of a class is accessible:
- Within the same package (regardless of inheritance).
- Within subclasses in *any* package, **but only through inheritance** (i.e., the access must be through a reference of the subclass type, not the superclass type).

**Step 2 — Code example (3 marks):**

```java
// Package: p1
package p1;
public class SuperX {
    protected int value = 100;
}
```

```java
// Package: p2
package p2;
import p1.SuperX;
public class SubY extends SuperX {
    public void show() {
        System.out.println(this.value);  // ✅ Allowed — inherited
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — The protected hole explained (2 marks):**
Consider a method inside `SubY` that receives a `SuperX` reference:

```java
public class SubY extends SuperX {
    public void show() {
        System.out.println(this.value);  // ✅ Allowed
    }
    public void showExternal(SuperX ref) {
        // System.out.println(ref.value);  // ❌ DENIED!
    }
}
```

Even though `SubY` inherits `value`, accessing it through a `SuperX` reference from a different package is **denied** by the Java compiler. This is the **"protected hole"** — the rule exists to prevent a subclass from accessing protected state of *other* unrelated instances of the superclass.

**Resolution:** To access `value` from `SubY` in another package, always use `this.value` (or a `SubY`-typed reference). If cross-package access via a superclass reference is genuinely needed, use a `public` getter method instead.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Access Control Questions**
>
> 1. **Writing `default` as a keyword** — There is no `default` access keyword. The *absence* of a specifier is default access. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing `protected` and `default`** — `protected` allows cross-package subclass access; `default` does not. Many students write "they are the same" — full 1-mark penalty.
>
> 3. **Forgetting that `private` members are inherited but inaccessible** — A common question: *"Are private members inherited?"* The technically correct answer is **yes, but not accessible**. Writing a flat "No" costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the protected hole** — In 14-mark questions involving cross-package inheritance, students often overlook the rule that `protected` access must be via subclass-typed references.
>
> 5. **Using `private` for top-level classes** — Top-level classes in Java **cannot** be `private` or `protected`. Only nested classes can.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the visibility table** — KTU examiners award marks for a **clean, complete table** showing all four specifiers and their visibility. A purely textual answer loses presentation marks.
>
> 7. **Confusing access specifiers with access modifiers** — `static`, `final`, and `abstract` are *modifiers*, not specifiers. Be precise with terminology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEEIT741 Module 2: Access Control**

- ☐ Java has **four** access levels: `private`, **default** (no keyword), `protected`, `public`.
- ☐ **`private`** → Same class only.
- ☐ **Default** → Same package only. **No keyword is written.**
- ☐ **`protected`** → Same package + subclasses in any package (via inheritance).
- ☐ **`public`** → Everywhere, no restrictions.
- ☐ Top-level classes can be **only `public` or default** — not `private`/`protected`.
- ☐ Constructors follow the same rules as methods; `private` constructors enable the **Singleton pattern**.
- ☐ **`private` members ARE inherited** but are **NOT accessible** outside the declaring class.
- ☐ The **"protected hole"**: cross-package subclass access to `protected` members is allowed **only via subclass-typed references**, not via superclass-typed references.
- ☐ Encapsulation = data hiding + controlled public interface (getters/setters with validation).
- ☐ C++ has **three** specifiers: `private`, `protected`, `public`. Default for `class` is `private`; default for `struct` is `public`. C++ also supports `friend` and inheritance-type modifiers.
- ☐ Best practice: **public surface should be < 20%** of total class members for a well-encapsulated class.
- ☐ Keyword `default` in Java exists only in `switch` statements and interface methods — **not** as an access specifier.
- ☐ In KTU 14-mark answers, always include: **(i)** a visibility table, **(ii)** code with multiple packages, **(iii)** explanation of `protected` cross-package inheritance, and **(iv)** a note on the protected hole.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Final Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Final Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Variables (Java / C++ Context)** are variables whose value, once assigned, cannot be modified thereafter. They represent *compile-time constants* when initialized at declaration, and *blank final constants* when deferred to constructors or initialization blocks.

In Java, a variable declared with the keyword `final` is called a **final variable**. Once a value is assigned to it, the compiler enforces that no further reassignment can occur. This immutability is a core pillar of writing robust, thread-safe, and maintainable object-oriented code, and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

## Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

A **final variable** in Java is a constant identifier whose binding to a value is *permanent*. There are **three distinct categories** recognized by the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus:

1. **Static Final Variable (Class Constant):** Declared with `static final`. Memory is allocated once in the *Method Area / Class Area*, shared across all objects.
2. **Instance Final Variable (Blank Final):** Declared with `final` inside a class but outside any method. Must be initialized at declaration *or* in every constructor.
3. **Local Final Variable:** Declared inside a method/block. Must be initialized before use; cannot be reassigned.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention (KTU Standard):** All `final` variables — both static and instance — must be written in **UPPERCASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES** (e.g., `MAX_LIMIT`, `PI_VALUE`). This is graded strictly in KTU board evaluations.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Engraved Stone Tablet"

Imagine you have a **stone tablet** that you commission a sculptor to engrave a name onto.

- **Regular Variable (non-final):** Like writing with chalk on a blackboard. You can erase it tomorrow and write something new.
- **Final Variable:** Like carving a name into stone. Once the chisel strikes, the name is *permanently fixed*. No future stroke can alter it.
- **Static Final (Class Constant):** Like a *public monument* — a single stone tablet visible to the entire village (all objects share one copy).
- **Instance Final (Blank Final):** Like a *personal family stone* — each family (object) gets their own stone, and the name carved on it cannot be changed.
- **Local Final:** Like a *temporary nameplate* used only inside one room (method). It is carved while you're in the room, and discarded when you leave.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in OOP?** Final variables eliminate an entire class of bugs caused by accidental mutation. They make programs **predictable**, **thread-safe**, and **easier to reason about** — three qualities heavily rewarded in KTU's outcome-based assessment.

| Property | Regular Variable | Final Variable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reassignable? | Yes | **No** |
| Must be initialized? | Before use | At declaration OR in constructor (if blank) |
| Memory Region | Heap/Stack | Heap/Stack (same) |
| Thread-Safe by Default? | No | **Yes (if reference is final)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Categories — Operational Breakdown

### Category 1: Static Final Variables (Class Constants)

Declared as `public static final` (or package-private). These are constants belonging to the *class itself*, not to any object. The JVM stores them in the **Class Constant Pool** inside the *Method Area* (JDK 8+ Metaspace).

**Operational Logic:**
1. Memory is allocated **exactly once** when the class is loaded by the ClassLoader.
2. All instances of the class share the *same memory location*.
3. They are inlined by the compiler at compile time wherever used (if `static final` and primitive or `String`).
4. Cannot be reassigned under any circumstance.

### Category 2: Instance Final Variables (Blank Final Variables)

Declared as `final` (without `static`) at the class level.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Each object receives its **own copy** in the heap memory.
2. If initialized at the point of declaration → behaves like a per-object constant.
3. If **not** initialized at declaration → it is called a **"blank final"**, and the compiler **mandates** initialization inside **every constructor** of the class.
4. Once set in the constructor, it cannot be modified for the lifetime of the object.

### Category 3: Local Final Variables

Declared inside methods, constructors, or blocks.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Stored in the **stack frame** of the method.
2. Must be definitely assigned before use (compiler-enforced).
3. Often used in **anonymous inner classes** (a heavily tested KTU concept) because Java requires captured variables to be *effectively final*.

## 2.2 Final Reference Variables — The Subtle Trap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Favorite Trap:** Declaring a reference variable as `final` does **NOT** make the object it points to immutable. It only freezes the *reference (the arrow)*, not the *contents (the object)*.

**Example mental model:**
- The reference is like the **address** of a house painted on a stone.
- The address (reference) is permanent — you cannot repaint it.
- But the people living inside (object state) can change all they want.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax Form | Initialization Site | Memory Location | Reassignable? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Static Final Constant | `static final TYPE NAME = value;` | At declaration only | Method Area (Class Constant Pool) | **No** |
| Instance Final (Initialized) | `final TYPE NAME = value;` | At declaration | Heap (per object) | **No** |
| Instance Final (Blank) | `final TYPE NAME;` | In every constructor | Heap (per object) | **No** |
| Local Final | `final TYPE NAME = value;` | Before use in method | Stack (per invocation) | **No** |
| Final Reference | `final MyClass obj = new MyClass();` | At declaration | Stack/Heap (reference in stack) | Reference: No; Object State: Yes |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Embedded Systems** | Hardware register addresses declared as `final static int`. |
| **Cryptography** | Modulus constants (e.g., prime $p$, generator $g$) made `final`. |
| **Configuration** | `MAX_CONNECTIONS`, `TIMEOUT_MS`, `DEFAULT_LOCALE` as `public static final`. |
| **Multithreading** | Immutable shared state in worker threads. |
| **Scientific Computing** | Physical/mathematical constants ($\pi$, $c$, $G$). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In the Java standard library itself, `Math.PI` is declared as `public static final double PI = 3.14159265358979323846;`. This is the gold-standard pattern for class constants and is **guaranteed** to appear in some form in KTU board questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

Since "Final Variables" is a conceptual Java topic, the "derivations" here take the form of **exhaustive code-trace walkthroughs** — a skill directly tested in KTU's 14-mark descriptive questions.

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Static Final Constant

```java
class Circle {
    public static final double PI = 3.14159;
    private double radius;

    Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }

    double area() { return PI * radius * radius; }
}
```

**Step-by-step evaluation** when `new Circle(5.0).area()` is invoked:

$$\text{Area} = \pi \times r^2$$

$$\text{Area} = 3.14159 \times (5.0)^2$$

$$\text{Area} = 3.14159 \times 25.0$$

$$\text{Area} = 78.53975$$

**Textual Logic Trace:**
1. Class `Circle` is loaded by the JVM ClassLoader.
2. `PI` is allocated **once** in the Method Area with value $3.14159$.
3. Object is created on the heap; `radius = 5.0`.
4. Method `area()` executes: retrieves `PI` (compile-time inlined), retrieves `radius`, performs multiplication.
5. Returns $78.53975$ to caller.
6. Any attempt to write `Circle.PI = 3.14;` → **Compile-Time Error**.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Blank Final Variable (Constructor Initialization)

```java
class Student {
    private final int rollNo;   // blank final
    private String name;

    Student(int r, String n) {
        rollNo = r;             // MUST initialize here
        name = n;
    }

    Student(int r) {
        rollNo = r;             // MUST initialize here too (every constructor)
        name = "Unknown";
    }
}
```

**Compiler-Enforced Logic:**
- The compiler tracks whether each blank final has been *definitely assigned* on every constructor exit path.
- If **any** constructor forgets to assign `rollNo`, the program **fails to compile**.
- This is a classic **definite assignment analysis** that the Java compiler performs at compile time.

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — Final Reference Trap (Theoretical Trace)

```java
class Box {
    int value;
    Box(int v) { this.value = v; }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final Box b = new Box(10);
        b.value = 20;        // ✅ ALLOWED — modifying object state
        // b = new Box(30);  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR — reference is final
    }
}
```

**Analytical Trace:**

| Step | Operation | Allowed? | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `final Box b = new Box(10);` | ✅ | Reference `b` is created and bound. |
| 2 | `b.value = 20;` | ✅ | The reference (arrow) is unchanged; only the object's internal state changes. |
| 3 | `b = new Box(30);` | ❌ | Attempting to rebind `b` to a new object violates the final reference contract. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked "Can a final reference variable's object be modified?", the correct answer is **YES** for object state and **NO** for reassignment of the reference. Marks are split: 2 marks for the reference rule, 2 marks for the object-state rule.

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4 — Effectively Final in Lambda Expressions

```java
public void demo() {
    int counter = 0;          // effectively final
    Runnable r = () -> {
        System.out.println(counter);  // ✅ Allowed
        // counter++;                  // ❌ Compile Error
    };
    r.run();
}
```

**Compiler Logic:**
1. Lambda captures the local variable `counter`.
2. Java requires captured variables to be *effectively final* (never modified after initialization).
3. The line `counter++` would break the effectively-final rule → compile error.
4. This restriction exists because lambdas may run on different threads; allowing mutation would break thread-safety.

## 3.5 Summary of Compilation Outcomes

$$\text{Valid Final Usage} = \{\text{Initialize once}\} \cap \{\text{No reassignment}\} \cap \{\text{Definite assignment before use}\}$$

Any violation of the right-hand conditions triggers a **compile-time error**, never a runtime error. This makes final variables a *design-time safety mechanism*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Final Variables

