<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a final variable in Java. Differentiate between static final and instance final variables.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **final variable** in Java is a variable whose value cannot be changed once it has been assigned. The `final` keyword is used to declare it, and any attempt to reassign it results in a compile-time error.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Static Final | Instance Final |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Keyword | `static final` | `final` |
| Memory | Single copy in Method Area | One copy per object in Heap |
| Access | Accessed via class name | Accessed via object reference |
| Initialization | At declaration only | At declaration or in every constructor |

**Example:**
```java
static final double PI = 3.14;        // Static final
final int rollNo;                      // Instance final (blank)
```

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks differentiation table = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain what is meant by a "blank final variable" with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **blank final variable** is an instance-level `final` variable that is **not initialized at the point of declaration**. Instead, it must be initialized inside **every constructor** of the class. If even one constructor fails to initialize it, the program will not compile.

```java
class Employee {
    final int empId;           // blank final — not initialized here
    String name;
    Employee(int id, String n) {
        empId = id;            // initialized in constructor
        name = n;
    }
}
```

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark example + 1 mark rule = 3 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**"Explain final variables in Java with reference to static final, instance final, blank final, and local final variables. Illustrate your answer with appropriate examples."**

#### Part (a) — Static Final and Instance Final (7 Marks)

**Static Final Variables:**

A `static final` variable is a class-level constant. It is created once when the class is loaded and shared across all objects. It must be initialized at the point of declaration.

```java
class MathConstants {
    public static final double PI = 3.14159265358979;
    public static final int MAX_VALUE = 2147483647;
}
```

It is accessed as `MathConstants.PI` — through the class name, not an object. The JVM stores it in the **Class Constant Pool**, and the compiler may inline its value at compile time for performance.

**Instance Final Variables:**

An `final` variable declared at class level (without `static`) is an instance final. Each object gets its own copy, and once assigned (at declaration or in the constructor), it cannot be changed.

```java
class Account {
    final long accountNumber;   // instance final
    String holderName;

    Account(long accNo, String name) {
        accountNumber = accNo;   // assigned in constructor
        holderName = name;
    }
}
```

*(3 marks for static final explanation + code, 4 marks for instance final explanation + code = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Blank Final and Local Final Variables (7 Marks)

**Blank Final Variables:**

A blank final is an instance final that is **not initialized at declaration**. The Java compiler enforces that it must be assigned in **every constructor** of the class.

```java
class Student {
    final int rollNo;
    final String branch;
    String name;

    Student(int r, String b, String n) {
        rollNo = r;
        branch = b;
        name = n;
    }

    // If we add another constructor, it MUST also assign rollNo and branch.
}
```

**Local Final Variables:**

A local final variable is declared inside a method or block. It must be definitely assigned before use, and it cannot be reassigned. It plays a critical role in **lambda expressions**, where captured variables must be *effectively final*.

```java
void greet(String userName) {
    final String greeting = "Hello, ";    // local final
    System.out.println(greeting + userName);
    // greeting = "Hi, ";                // ❌ Compile Error
}
```

**Real-world analogy:** A blank final is like a unique ID assigned to each customer at the time of registration — fixed at creation time, never changed again.

*(3 marks for blank final explanation + code, 2 marks for local final explanation + code, 2 marks for analogy and lambda context = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Discuss the concept of final variables in Java. Explain with examples how final variables behave differently with primitives, objects, and references. Also explain the role of 'effectively final' variables in lambda expressions."**

#### Part (a) — Final Variables with Primitives and Objects (7 Marks)

**Final Variables with Primitives:**

When a primitive variable is declared `final`, its value becomes a true constant. Any reassignment is rejected at compile time.

```java
final int MAX = 100;
// MAX = 200;    // ❌ Compile Error: cannot assign a value to final variable MAX
```

**Final Variables with References:**

When a reference variable is declared `final`, the *reference itself* is locked, but the *object it points to* can still be modified. This is one of the most misunderstood aspects of `final`.

```java
class Point { int x, y; }

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final Point p = new Point();
        p.x = 10; p.y = 20;        // ✅ Allowed — modifying object state
        // p = new Point();        // ❌ Compile Error — cannot reassign final reference
    }
}
```

**Tabular Comparison:**

| Aspect | Primitive Final | Reference Final |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| What is frozen? | The value itself | The reference (memory address) |
| Can the value/contents change? | No | Yes (object's internal state can change) |
| Reassign the variable? | Compile Error | Compile Error |

*(2 marks primitive example + 3 marks reference example with table + 2 marks conceptual explanation = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Effectively Final Variables and Lambdas (7 Marks)

**Effectively Final Variables:**

A local variable that is **never modified after its initialization** is said to be *effectively final*, even without the `final` keyword. Java treats such variables identically to `final` variables in certain contexts.

**Role in Lambda Expressions:**

Lambda expressions can capture local variables from their enclosing scope. For thread-safety and predictability, Java requires these captured variables to be effectively final.

```java
public class LambdaDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int count = 5;                    // effectively final (never reassigned)
        // count = 10;                    // ❌ Would break effectively-final rule

        Runnable r = () -> {
            for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
                System.out.println("Iteration: " + i);
            }
        };
        r.run();
    }
}
```

**Why the restriction?**

1. Lambdas may execute on different threads than the one that created them.
2. Local variables live on the stack of the creating thread; they may be gone by the time the lambda runs.
3. Java solves this by *copying* the value of effectively final variables into the lambda's closure — making it a snapshot.
4. Allowing mutation would create inconsistent views across threads → thread-safety violation.

**Without `final` keyword (implicitly effectively final):**

```java
void method() {
    String msg = "Hello";        // effectively final
    Runnable r = () -> System.out.println(msg);   // ✅ Works
}
```

*(2 marks effective final definition + 3 marks lambda code + explanation + 2 marks thread-safety reasoning = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing `b = new Box(30);` in your explanation:** Many students incorrectly state that final references make objects immutable. They are penalized **2 marks** for this confusion. Always clarify: reference is frozen, object state is not.
> 2. **Forgetting the constructor rule for blank finals:** A common error is showing a blank final initialized in *only one* constructor out of two. This would not compile; examiners deduct **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Missing the lambda context:** When asked about local finals, students often skip the connection to *anonymous inner classes* and *lambda expressions*. This connection is worth **2 marks** in most 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Naming convention oversight:** Writing `final int max = 100;` instead of `final int MAX = 100;` — examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for not following the KTU-standard UPPERCASE convention.
> 5. **Confusing `final` with `finally` and `finalize`:** Three different Java keywords. Spelling mistakes cost **1 mark** each occurrence.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **final variable** in Java is a constant whose value cannot be reassigned after initialization.
- ✅ There are **three types**: `static final`, instance `final` (including blank final), and local `final`.
- ✅ **Static final** variables are class-level constants stored in the Method Area; initialized **once** at declaration.
- ✅ **Instance final** variables are per-object constants; must be initialized at declaration **or** in every constructor.
- ✅ **Blank final variables** are instance finals not initialized at declaration; the compiler enforces initialization in every constructor through *definite assignment analysis*.
- ✅ **Local final variables** are stack-allocated; must be definitely assigned before use; cannot be reassigned.
- ✅ A **final reference** locks the reference (arrow), **not** the object's internal state — this is the most commonly tested nuance.
- ✅ **Effectively final** variables are local variables never modified after initialization; required for capture in lambda expressions and anonymous inner classes.
- ✅ Naming convention: `UPPERCASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES` for all final variables (e.g., `MAX_LIMIT`).
- ✅ `final`, `finally`, and `finalize()` are **three different things** — do not confuse them.
- ✅ Violations of final rules cause **compile-time errors**, never runtime errors — this makes them powerful design-time safety tools.
- ✅ Real-world examples: `Math.PI`, `Integer.MAX_VALUE`, configuration constants in enterprise applications.
- ✅ Final variables contribute to **immutability**, **thread-safety**, and **code clarity** — three pillars of high-quality OOP design emphasized in KTU's CO-based assessment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inner Classes, Inheritance - Super Class
Sub Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sub Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sub Class** (also known as a *Derived Class*, *Child Class*, or *Extended Class*) in Object-Oriented Programming is a class that **inherits** attributes (fields/data members) and behaviors (methods/member functions) from another class, referred to as its **Super Class** (or *Base Class* / *Parent Class*). The subclass mechanism is the foundational syntactic construct that enables the OOP principle of **Inheritance** — one of the four pillars of OOP (alongside Encapsulation, Polymorphism, and Abstraction).

In Java (the prescribed language for KTU PEEIT741), a subclass is declared using the `extends` keyword.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"A subclass is a class that acquires the properties and methods of another class through inheritance, allowing code reusability and establishing an 'is-a' relationship between classes."*

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Parent (Father)** who has certain properties:
- Eye color: Brown
- Height: 5'10"
- Surname: Sharma
- Skills: Cooking, Driving

Now, his **Child (Son)** naturally inherits:
- Surname: Sharma
- Possibly eye color: Brown
- Height (genetic influence)

But the child can **add** his own properties:
- Own skills: Programming, Painting
- Own phone number

And can **override** (modify) inherited behaviors:
- The child cooks differently than the father

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sub Class Analogy — Key Mapping:**
>
> | Real World | OOP Concept |
> |------------|-------------|
> | Parent | Super Class / Base Class |
> | Child | Sub Class / Derived Class |
> | Inherited traits | Inherited fields & methods |
> | New traits of child | Sub class-specific members |
> | Modified behavior | Method Overriding |

> [!TIP]
> 💡 **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** "**Sub Class = Child = extends**" — In Java, the child class uses the keyword `extends` to inherit from the parent.

---

## 🔍 The "is-a" Relationship

A subclass represents an **"is-a"** relationship with its superclass. For example:
- A `Dog` **is-a** `Animal` ✅
- A `Car` **is-a** `Vehicle` ✅
- A `SavingsAccount` **is-a** `BankAccount` ✅
- A `Square` **is-a** `Shape` ✅

This is contrasted with the **"has-a"** relationship (Composition/Aggregation), where one class *contains* another.

---

## 📐 Basic Syntax (Java — KTU Prescribed Language)

```java
// Super Class definition
class SuperClass {
    // fields
    // methods
}

// Sub Class definition using 'extends'
class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    // inherited members (accessible)
    // new members (subclass-specific)
    // overridden methods
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse `extends` with `implements`. Remember: `extends` is for **class-to-class inheritance**, while `implements` is for **class-to-interface** realization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧩 Anatomy of a Sub Class — Structural Breakdown

When a `SubClass extends SuperClass`, the subclass acquires:

1. **All `public` members** of the superclass
2. **All `protected` members** of the superclass
3. **Package-private (default) members** — *only* if both classes are in the **same package**
4. **`private` members are NOT inherited directly** — but can be accessed via `public/protected` getter/setter methods

### 🔑 The Five Pillars of Sub Class Behavior

| # | Concept | Definition | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Member Inheritance** | Sub class automatically receives accessible members of super class | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Method Overriding** | Sub class provides specific implementation of super class method | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **`super` Keyword** | Refers to immediate parent class members/constructor | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Constructor Chaining** | Sub class constructor implicitly/explicitly calls super class constructor | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Access Control** | Visibility rules govern what is inherited | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 🏗️ Types of Inheritance Supported in Java (KTU Critical)

Java supports **four** types of inheritance, but only **three** are achievable through class extension:

| Type | Supported in Java? | Description | Diagram |
|------|-------------------|-------------|---------|
| **Single** | ✅ Yes | One subclass inherits one superclass | `A → B` |
| **Multilevel** | ✅ Yes | Chain of inheritance: A → B → C | `A → B → C` |
| **Hierarchical** | ✅ Yes | Multiple subclasses inherit one superclass | `A → B`, `A → C` |
| **Multiple** | ❌ No (for classes) | One class inherits from multiple classes | ❌ Not allowed |
| **Hybrid** | ⚠️ Partial | Mix of types — achieved via interfaces | Only via interfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Hot Question:** *"Why does Java not support multiple inheritance using classes?"*
>
> **Model Answer:** To avoid the **Diamond Problem** — ambiguity when two parent classes have a method with the same name, and the child class doesn't know which one to inherit. Java resolves this by allowing multiple inheritance only through **interfaces**, where conflicts are resolved by the implementing class.

---

## 🧪 Access Modifier Matrix for Sub Class Inheritance

This is a **high-frequency KTU question**. The visibility rules are:

| Super Class Member | Same Package Sub Class | Different Package Sub Class |
|--------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| `private` | ❌ Not inherited | ❌ Not inherited |
| Default (no modifier) | ✅ Inherited | ❌ Not inherited |
| `protected` | ✅ Inherited | ✅ Inherited |
| `public` | ✅ Inherited | ✅ Inherited |

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule:** The `private` members **exist** in the subclass object (memory is allocated), but they are **not directly accessible** by subclass code. They can only be reached through public/protected accessor methods.

---

## 🔧 The `super` Keyword — Three Critical Uses

The `super` keyword is the **gateway** from subclass to superclass. It has **three** primary applications:

### Use 1: `super` to call parent class **variable**
```java
super.variableName;
```
Used when subclass has a field with the same name as the parent class (name shadowing).

### Use 2: `super` to call parent class **method**
```java
super.methodName(arguments);
```
Used when subclass has overridden a method but still needs the parent's version.

### Use 3: `super()` to call parent class **constructor**
```java
super(arguments);  // must be FIRST line
```
Used to initialize inherited members. If not written explicitly, Java **automatically inserts** `super()` (no-arg call) as the first statement.

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Pitfall:** If the super class has **only** a parameterized constructor (no default no-arg constructor), and the subclass does NOT explicitly call `super(args)`, the program will throw a **compilation error**.

---

## 🧬 Method Overriding Rules (KTU-Favorite Topic)

For a method in a subclass to **properly override** a method in the superclass, the following rules apply (these are tested every KTU exam):

| Rule | Requirement |
|------|-------------|
| Method name | Must be **identical** |
| Parameter list | Must be **identical** (same number, type, order) |
| Return type | Must be **same** or a **covariant** (subtype) return type |
| Access modifier | Cannot be **more restrictive** than the parent |
| `static` methods | **Cannot** be overridden (they are hidden) |
| `final` methods | **Cannot** be overridden |
| Exception handling | Can throw **fewer or narrower** checked exceptions, not broader |

---

## 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Example |
|---------|---------------|---------|
| Sub Class Declaration | `class Child extends Parent` | `class Dog extends Animal` |
| `super` to call variable | `super.fieldName` | `super.name` |
| `super` to call method | `super.method(args)` | `super.display()` |
| `super` to call constructor | `super(args);` (first line) | `super("Hello");` |
| Prevent inheritance | `final` keyword | `final class A {}` |
| Prevent method overriding | `final` on method | `final void show() {}` |
| Reference of parent type | `Parent ref = new Child();` | `Animal a = new Dog();` |
| instanceof check | `obj instanceof ClassName` | `d instanceof Animal` |
| Covariant return | Override return type can be subclass | `Animal → Dog` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Pipes:** KTU expects strict LaTeX compliance. The expression `super(arg) \vert \vert this()` would use the LaTeX `\vert` notation if written in a math context. For code, use the double pipe `||` as a logical OR operator.

---

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

The subclass mechanism is used extensively in:

1. **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX, Swing):** `MyButton extends Button`
2. **JDBC API:** `MyConnection extends Connection`
3. **Servlet Programming:** `MyServlet extends HttpServlet`
4. **Android Development:** `MainActivity extends AppCompatActivity`
5. **Software Design Patterns:** Template Method, Factory, Strategy — all leverage inheritance
6. **Framework Development:** Spring, Hibernate — use subclassing for customization

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🛠️ Exhaustive Walkthrough 1: Basic Sub Class Creation

### Step 1: Define the Super Class

```java
// File: Vehicle.java
class Vehicle {
    String brand;
    int year;
    
    Vehicle() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle default constructor called");
    }
    
    Vehicle(String brand, int year) {
        this.brand = brand;
        this.year = year;
        System.out.println("Vehicle parameterized constructor called");
    }
    
    void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Brand: " + brand);
        System.out.println("Year: " + year);
    }
}
```

### Step 2: Define the Sub Class Using `extends`

```java
// File: Car.java
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int numberOfDoors;
    
    Car() {
        super();  // Explicitly calls Vehicle's no-arg constructor
        System.out.println("Car default constructor called");
    }
    
    Car(String brand, int year, int doors) {
        super(brand, year);  // Calls Vehicle's parameterized constructor
        this.numberOfDoors = doors;
        System.out.println("Car parameterized constructor called");
    }
    
    void displayCarInfo() {
        displayInfo();  // Inherited from Vehicle
        System.out.println("Doors: " + numberOfDoors);
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Demonstration (Main Class)

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car myCar = new Car("Toyota", 2022, 4);
        myCar.displayCarInfo();
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Trace the Output

```
Vehicle parameterized constructor called
Car parameterized constructor called
Brand: Toyota
Year: 2022
Doors: 4
```

### Step 5: Logical Breakdown of Constructor Chaining

$$\text{Execution Order} = \text{Super Class Constructor} \rightarrow \text{Sub Class Constructor Body}$$

The chain is:
1. `new Car("Toyota", 2022, 4)` is invoked
2. Control enters `Car(String, int, int)` constructor
3. The first line `super(brand, year)` is executed
4. Control jumps to `Vehicle(String, int)` constructor
5. `Vehicle`'s fields (`brand`, `year`) are initialized
6. `Vehicle` constructor body finishes
7. Control returns to `Car` constructor
8. `this.numberOfDoors = doors` is executed
9. `Car` constructor body finishes
10. Object is fully constructed

---

## 🛠️ Exhaustive Walkthrough 2: Method Overriding in Sub Class

### Step 1: Super Class with a Method

```java
class Animal {
    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}
```

### Step 2: Sub Class Overriding the Method

```java
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override  // Annotation — compiler safety check (best practice)
    void speak() {
        super.speak();  // Optional: call parent version first
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Demonstration of Polymorphic Behavior

```java
public class TestOverride {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal genericAnimal = new Animal();
        genericAnimal.speak();
        // Output: Animal makes a sound
        
        Dog dog = new Dog();
        dog.speak();
        // Output:
        // Animal makes a sound
        // Dog barks
        
        // Upcasting — Reference of super type holding sub object
        Animal polymorphicRef = new Dog();
        polymorphicRef.speak();
        // Output:
        // Animal makes a sound
        // Dog barks
        // (Dynamic Method Dispatch — runtime polymorphism)
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Dynamic Method Dispatch — The Underlying Mechanism

Dynamic Method Dispatch is the JVM mechanism that decides **at runtime** which overridden method to invoke, based on the **actual object type**, not the reference type.

Let $R$ = reference type, $O$ = actual object type, $M$ = method called.

$$\text{Method Invoked} = M(O) \text{ where } O \in \text{Object Hierarchy of } R$$

In our example:
- $R = \text{Animal}$, $O = \text{Dog}$
- The JVM inspects $O = \text{Dog}$
- It walks up the class hierarchy: `Dog.speak()` found ✅
- `Dog.speak()` is invoked (which internally calls `super.speak()` → `Animal.speak()`)

---

## 🛠️ Exhaustive Walkthrough 3: Use of `super` to Resolve Name Shadowing

```java
class Parent {
    int value = 100;
}

class Child extends Parent {
    int value = 200;  // Shadows parent's 'value'
    
    void printValues() {
        System.out.println("Child's value: " + value);        // 200
        System.out.println("Parent's value: " + super.value); // 100
    }
}
```

**Resolution Rule:**

$$\text{Without super} \rightarrow \text{Local} \rightarrow \text{This class field} \rightarrow \text{Parent field (compile error)}$$

$$\text{With super} \rightarrow \text{Directly accesses immediate parent class member}$$

---

## 🛠️ Exhaustive Walkthrough 4: The `final` Keyword and Sub Classes

```java
// Case 1: Final class cannot be extended
final class SecureClass {
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Cannot be inherited");
    }
}

// class Hacker extends SecureClass { }  // ❌ Compilation error
```

```java
// Case 2: Final method cannot be overridden
class Base {
    final void show() {
        System.out.println("Final method");
    }
}

class Derived extends Base {
    // void show() { }  // ❌ Compilation error — cannot override final method
}
```

---

## 🛠️ Exhaustive Walkthrough 5: Multilevel Inheritance Chain

```java
class Grandparent {
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Grandparent");
    }
}

class Parent extends Grandparent {
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Parent");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    void print() {
        System.out.println("Child");
    }
}

// Usage
Child c = new Child();
c.display();  // Inherited from Grandparent
c.show();     // Inherited from Parent
c.print();    // Own method
```

**Inheritance Depth Diagram:**

$$\text{Grandparent} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{Parent} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{Child}$$

The `Child` class transitively inherits **all** accessible members of both `Parent` and `Grandparent`.

---

## 🧠 Analytical Matrix: Sub Class vs. Other Class Relationships

| Feature | Sub Class (`extends`) | Inner Class | Anonymous Class | Composition (`has-a`) |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Keyword** | `extends` | none | none | field reference |
| **Relationship** | is-a | logically nested | one-time subclass | has-a |
| **Code Reuse** | Inherits all | Can access outer | Inherits + custom | Uses reference |
| **Coupling** | Tight | Tight | Tight | Loose |
| **Multiple** | ❌ Not in Java | ✅ Allowed | ❌ Single | ✅ Allowed |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Design Principle:** Prefer **composition over inheritance** when there is no clear "is-a" relationship. Inheritance breaks encapsulation if not used carefully.

---

## ⚖️ Advantages & Disadvantages of Sub Classing

### ✅ Advantages
1. **Code Reusability** — write once, inherit many times
2. **Method Overriding** — supports runtime polymorphism
3. **Extensibility** — easy to add new features
4. **Logical Hierarchy** — mirrors real-world taxonomy

### ❌ Disadvantages
1. **Tight Coupling** — subclass depends on super class implementation
2. **Fragile Base Class Problem** — changes in super class can break subclass
3. **No Multiple Inheritance** — Java limitation
4. **Hierarchy can become complex** — hard to maintain deep chains

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a subclass. How is it declared in Java? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **subclass** is a class that inherits properties (fields) and behaviors (methods) from another class, called the **superclass**, thereby enabling code reuse and establishing an "is-a" relationship.

In Java, a subclass is declared using the `extends` keyword.

**Example:**
```java
class Animal {  // Super class
    void eat() { System.out.println("Eating..."); }
}

class Dog extends Animal {  // Sub class
    void bark() { System.out.println("Barking..."); }
}
```

Here, `Dog` is a subclass of `Animal` and inherits the `eat()` method.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition (1.5 marks) + Java declaration syntax (0.5 marks) + Example (1 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the difference between `super` and `this` keyword in Java?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `this` | `super` |
|---------|--------|---------|
| **Refers to** | Current object of the same class | Immediate parent class object |
| **Use Case** | Differentiate instance variables from local variables | Access overridden members of parent |
| **Constructor Call** | `this()` calls another constructor in same class | `super()` calls parent class constructor |
| **Position** | Can be used anywhere | First line if calling constructor |

**Example:**
```java
class A {
    int x = 10;
}
class B extends A {
    int x = 20;
    void show() {
        System.out.println(this.x);   // 20 (current class)
        System.out.println(super.x);  // 10 (parent class)
    }
}
```

---

## 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the concept of inheritance in Java. With a suitable example, demonstrate single inheritance where the subclass overrides a method of the superclass and uses the `super` keyword to invoke the overridden method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Inheritance Concept (3 marks):**
Inheritance is an OOP mechanism that allows a new class (subclass) to acquire the properties and methods of an existing class (superclass). It promotes:
- **Code Reusability**
- **Method Overriding (Polymorphism)**
- **Hierarchical Classification**

In Java, inheritance is achieved using the `extends` keyword.

**Code Example (4 marks):**

```java
// Super Class
class Shape {
    String color;
    
    Shape(String color) {
        this.color = color;
    }
    
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape with color: " + color);
    }
}

// Sub Class
class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    
    Circle(String color, double radius) {
        super(color);  // Call parent constructor
        this.radius = radius;
    }
    
    @Override
    void draw() {
        super.draw();  // Call parent draw method
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle with radius: " + radius);
    }
    
    double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

// Main class
public class TestInheritance {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle("Red", 5.0);
        c.draw();
        System.out.println("Area: " + c.area());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Drawing a generic shape with color: Red
Drawing a circle with radius: 5.0
Area: 78.53981633974483
```

**Explanation of Key Elements:**
- `Circle extends Shape` → establishes inheritance
- `super(color)` → calls parent constructor
- `@Override` → annotation ensuring proper overriding
- `super.draw()` → invokes parent class method explicitly

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Discuss the different types of inheritance supported by Java. Why is multiple inheritance not supported using classes? How is it achieved using interfaces?**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Inheritance in Java (4 marks):**

1. **Single Inheritance:** One subclass inherits one superclass
   - `class B extends A`

2. **Multilevel Inheritance:** Chain of inheritance
   - `class C extends B extends A`

3. **Hierarchical Inheritance:** Multiple subclasses from one superclass
   - `class B extends A` and `class C extends A`

4. **Multiple Inheritance:** One class extends multiple classes — ❌ Not supported in Java

5. **Hybrid Inheritance:** Mix of types — ⚠️ Partially supported via interfaces

**Why Multiple Inheritance is Not Supported (2 marks):**

Java prohibits multiple inheritance through classes to avoid the **Diamond Problem** (also called the **Deadly Diamond of Death**):

```
       A
      / \
     B   C
      \ /
       D
```

If both `B` and `C` have a method `display()`, and `D extends B, C`, then ambiguity arises: which `display()` should `D` inherit? This creates **compile-time ambiguity**.

**Achieving Multiple Inheritance via Interfaces (1 mark):**

```java
interface Printable {
    void print();
}

interface Showable {
    void show();
}

class Document implements Printable, Showable {
    public void print() { System.out.println("Printing"); }
    public void show() { System.out.println("Showing"); }
}
```

Here, `Document` achieves multiple inheritance of type by implementing two interfaces.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Write a Java program to demonstrate multilevel inheritance. Create a class `Person` with fields `name` and `age`, a class `Employee` that extends `Person` with field `empId`, and a class `Manager` that extends `Employee` with field `department`. Display all details using a method in the `Manager` class.**

**Model Solution:**

```java
// Level 1: Super class
class Person {
    String name;
    int age;
    
    Person(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
    }
}

// Level 2: Intermediate subclass
class Employee extends Person {
    int empId;
    
    Employee(String name, int age, int empId) {
        super(name, age);  // Call Person constructor
        this.empId = empId;
    }
}

// Level 3: Final subclass
class Manager extends Employee {
    String department;
    
    Manager(String name, int age, int empId, String department) {
        super(name, age, empId);  // Call Employee constructor
        this.department = department;
    }
    
    void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);          // From Person
        System.out.println("Age: " + age);            // From Person
        System.out.println("Employee ID: " + empId);  // From Employee
        System.out.println("Department: " + department); // Own
    }
}

// Main class
public class MultilevelDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Manager mgr = new Manager("Alice", 35, 101, "IT");
        mgr.displayDetails();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Name: Alice
Age: 35
Employee ID: 101
Department: IT
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Person class (1.5 marks)
- Employee class (1.5 marks)
- Manager class (2.5 marks)
- Main class and explanation (1.5 marks)

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Explain method overriding with its rules. Provide an example demonstrating dynamic method dispatch using a superclass reference holding a subclass object.**

**Model Solution:**

**Method Overriding Definition (1.5 marks):**
Method overriding occurs when a subclass defines a method with the **same name, same parameters, and same return type** (or covariant return type) as a method in its superclass. The subclass version **replaces** (overrides) the parent's version for subclass objects.

**Rules of Method Overriding (3 marks):**

1. Method name and parameter list must be **identical**
2. Return type must be the **same or covariant** (subtype)
3. Access level **cannot be more restrictive** (e.g., `public` in parent → `protected` in child is illegal)
4. `static`, `final`, and `private` methods **cannot be overridden**
5. Can throw **same, fewer, or narrower checked exceptions**, but not broader
6. Use `@Override` annotation for compile-time safety

**Dynamic Method Dispatch Example (2.5 marks):**

```java
class Vehicle {
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle starting...");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Car starting with key...");
    }
}

class Bike extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Bike starting with kick...");
    }
}

public class DynamicDispatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle ref;  // Superclass reference
        
        ref = new Car();
        ref.start();  // Calls Car's start() — Output: Car starting...
        
        ref = new Bike();
        ref.start();  // Calls Bike's start() — Output: Bike starting...
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Car starting with key...
Bike starting with kick...
```

**Key Concept:** At compile time, the compiler checks the **reference type** (`Vehicle`) for the existence of `start()`. At runtime, the JVM checks the **actual object type** (`Car` or `Bike`) and invokes the **overridden version**. This is **runtime polymorphism**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `super()` as first line:** If parent has only a parameterized constructor, not calling `super(args)` explicitly causes compilation failure. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Using `extends` with `final` class:** Attempting to inherit a `final` class throws compile error. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 3. **More restrictive access in overriding:** Reducing visibility (e.g., `public` → `private`) in subclass is **illegal** and loses marks. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Confusing `Overloading` vs `Overriding`:** Overloading = same name, **different parameters** (compile-time). Overriding = same name, **same parameters** (runtime). **(-2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Static method overriding claim:** Static methods are **hidden**, not overridden. Saying "static methods can be overridden" costs marks. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Private member inheritance misconception:** Students write "private members are not inherited" — they ARE present in the object memory but are NOT directly accessible. Wording matters. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 7. **Multiple inheritance declaration error:** Writing `class C extends A, B` is a **syntax error** in Java. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 8. **Not using `@Override` annotation:** Although optional, KTU expects best practices. Missing it may cost 0.5 marks in some valuations.

---

## 🏁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist — Sub Class (Module 2)**

- ✅ A **sub class** inherits from a **super class** using the `extends` keyword
- ✅ Java supports **single, multilevel, and hierarchical** inheritance through classes
- ✅ Java does **NOT** support **multiple inheritance** through classes (Diamond Problem)
- ✅ Multiple inheritance is achieved through **interfaces** using `implements`
- ✅ `private` members are **not directly accessible** in subclass (use getters/setters)
- ✅ `protected` members are accessible in subclass (even across packages)
- ✅ **`super`** keyword has three uses: variable, method, constructor
- ✅ `super()` must be the **first line** in a subclass constructor
- ✅ If super has no default constructor, subclass **must** explicitly call `super(args)`
- ✅ Method **overriding** requires identical name, parameters, and covariant return type
- ✅ Access modifier in overriding **cannot be more restrictive**
- ✅ `final` methods **cannot be overridden**; `final` classes **cannot be extended**
- ✅ `static` methods are **hidden**, not overridden (no polymorphism)
- ✅ **Dynamic Method Dispatch** = JVM invokes overridden method based on actual object type
- ✅ `Parent ref = new Child();` is valid (upcasting) and enables polymorphism
- ✅ Constructor chaining: **Super class constructor runs first**, then sub class
- ✅ The `is-a` relationship defines inheritance; `has-a` defines composition
- ✅ Prefer **composition over inheritance** for loose coupling (design principle)
- ✅ `@Override` annotation is a **best practice** for compiler safety
- ✅ `instanceof` operator checks if an object is an instance of a class

---

> **📊 Quick-Reference Mnemonic for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 
> **"E.S.C.F.O.A.P"** — The 7 keywords/concepts for Sub Class:
> - **E** → `extends`
> - **S** → `super`
> - **C** → Constructor chaining
> - **F** → `final` (prevents inheritance/overriding)
> - **O** → Overriding
> - **A** → Access modifiers
> - **P** → Polymorphism (Dynamic dispatch)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Keyword super
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Keyword super

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, the **`super` keyword** is a built-in reference variable that always points to the **immediate superclass (parent class) object**. It acts as a bridge that allows a subclass (child class) to explicitly access the members (fields, methods) and constructors of its direct parent class, bypassing the normal name-resolution rules of inheritance.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEIT741 – Module 2: Inheritance & Polymorphism), `super` is a cornerstone of inheritance, providing controlled access upward in the class hierarchy while preserving the encapsulation discipline of Object-Oriented Programming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Standard):**
> The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class instance**. It is implicitly provided by the JVM (Java Virtual Machine) for every non-static context inside a subclass, and it is the only legal way to explicitly invoke a parent's overridden method, hidden field, or parameterized constructor.

## 1.2 The Three Principal Uses of `super`

The `super` keyword has **exactly three** sanctioned applications, each of which is a high-frequency KTU exam topic:

| # | Use Case | Syntax Form | Purpose |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | Access parent class **variable** (field) | `super.variableName` | Disambiguates when subclass and superclass share a field with the same name. |
| 2 | Invoke parent class **method** (overridden) | `super.methodName(args)` | Calls the parent's version of a method that has been overridden in the child. |
| 3 | Invoke parent class **constructor** | `super(arg1, arg2, ...)` | Explicitly chains the parent's constructor as the first step in the child's construction. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Heirloom

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Child Calling Out to the Parent"**
>
> Imagine a child (subclass) who has been given the **same toy car** that his father (superclass) already owns. The child has repainted his own version in red, while the father's car is blue. If the child wants to show off the **original blue car** to his friends, he must explicitly point to it and say: *"I want **my father's** car"* — that pointing gesture is the `super` keyword.
>
> - **`super.carColor`** → "Show me father's color, not my red one."
> - **`super.drive()`** → "Run father's driving method, not my custom turbo version."
> - **`super()`** → "First, build the father's car (parent object) before I add my custom paint job."

Without `super`, the child would have no way to deliberately access the parent's version because Java's default name resolution always picks the **closest scope** (the child itself).

## 1.4 Why Does `super` Exist? The Engineering Motivation

In inheritance, a subclass **inherits** the accessible members of its parent. However, two complications arise:

1. **Name Shadowing:** If the child declares a field/method with the same name as the parent's, the child's version hides the parent's.
2. **Initialization Dependency:** The parent portion of every subclass object **must be fully constructed** before the child-specific logic runs, because the child may depend on inherited state.

The `super` keyword solves both problems in a type-safe, compiler-verified manner — no reflection, no casting hacks, no fragile string-based lookups.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How `super` Resolves at Runtime

The Java compiler treats `super` as a **statically bound, pre-resolved reference**. The exact superclass is determined at **compile time** using the inheritance chain, while the actual memory address is filled in at object instantiation.

### Logical Flow of a `super` Call

1. **Compile-time resolution:** The compiler reads the class declaration (e.g., `class Dog extends Animal`) and records that for any `Dog` object, `super` refers to the `Animal` portion.
2. **Bytecode emission:** The compiler emits an `invokespecial` or `getfield` JVM instruction qualified with the superclass symbolic reference.
3. **Runtime binding:** The JVM, while executing, walks the object header to locate the parent subobject and dispatches the call.
4. **Access check:** The JVM enforces access modifiers — a `private` parent member is **never** reachable via `super`, even though it is inherited as a memory region.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** `super` cannot be used inside a `static` method, `static` block, or main method, because `super` is an *instance* reference — it requires a live object on the heap. Attempting it produces the compile-time error: *"non-static variable super cannot be referenced from a static context."*

## 2.2 The Three Operational Modes — Detailed Mechanics

### Mode 1: `super.fieldName` — Variable Access

When a subclass declares a field with the **same identifier** as a parent field, the subclass field **hides** (not overrides) the parent's. To read or write the parent's hidden field, you must qualify it with `super`.

- Useful when the subclass needs to **preserve** the parent's value while also keeping its own parallel value.
- Common in GUI frameworks where a base `Component` has a `width` and a derived `Window` redefines `width` for borders.

### Mode 2: `super.methodName(args)` — Method Access

If a subclass **overrides** a parent's method, calling the method by its bare name invokes the child's version. To call the parent's original implementation — typically to **extend** it rather than replace it — you use `super.method()`.

This is the foundation of the **Template Method** design pattern, where the parent defines the algorithm skeleton and the child fills in the steps but may invoke the default step from the parent.

### Mode 3: `super(args)` — Constructor Chaining

This is the **most heavily tested** aspect in KTU exams. When a subclass constructor begins, the very first executable statement is **either an explicit `super(args)` call or an implicit `super()` call** (the no-argument parent constructor).

- If you write `super(args)`, it must be the **first line** of the constructor body.
- If you write nothing, the compiler silently inserts `super();` as the first line.
- If the parent has **no no-argument constructor** and you fail to write an explicit `super(args)`, the program fails to compile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructor Chaining — The Cardinal Rule of `super()`:**
> Construction in Java always proceeds **top-down** through the inheritance hierarchy. Before a `Dog` object is fully created, the `Animal` subobject is created first, and before that, the `Object` subobject is created. This guarantees that no inherited method ever runs on uninitialized inherited state.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Property | Specification | Exam Tip |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| Keyword type | Reserved literal in Java | Cannot be used as a variable name |
| Refers to | Immediate parent class object | Not the root `Object` unless direct parent |
| Valid contexts | Instance methods, constructors, instance initializers | **Invalid** in `static` contexts |
| Must be the first line? | Yes, for `super(args)` constructor call | Otherwise, compile error |
| Default insertion | Compiler inserts `super();` if no explicit call | Matches the parent's no-arg constructor |
| Access to `private` members | **Disallowed** | Use `protected` or `public` getters |
| Overriding vs Hiding | Methods are overridden, fields are hidden | `super` works on both |
| Chaining direction | Top-down (root → leaf) | Always from superclass to subclass |
| Can `this()` and `super()` coexist? | **No** — both must be first line | Pick exactly one per constructor |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `super` keyword is the silent backbone of virtually every Java framework in production:

- **Swing / JavaFX:** Every custom component overrides `paintComponent(Graphics g)` and calls `super.paintComponent(g)` so the parent's default rendering runs first, then the child adds custom drawing.
- **Servlets (HTTP):** `HttpServlet` subclasses call `super.init(config)` to preserve container-managed initialization before adding their own resources.
- **Spring Framework:** Bean post-processors and abstract base classes use `super` to chain lifecycle hooks.
- **Android:** `Activity.onCreate()` universally begins with `super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)` to ensure the framework's window initialization completes before the app layer sets its own UI.

Without `super`, none of these framework extension patterns would be possible without resorting to fragile, reflection-based hacks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Constructor Chaining — Exhaustive Trace

We will trace the exact execution order of a multi-level inheritance chain. This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** pattern in KTU ESE.

### Source Code (Illustrative)

```java
class Grandparent {
    Grandparent() {
        System.out.println("Grandparent constructor");
    }
}

class Parent extends Grandparent {
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

When the statement `new Child();` executes, the JVM does the following:

**Step 1 — Call site begins**
The `main` method issues the `new Child()` instruction. Control jumps to the `Child()` constructor.

**Step 2 — Implicit `super()` insertion**
The compiler has already inserted `super();` (calling `Parent()`) as the very first line of `Child()`. Control now jumps to `Parent()`.

**Step 3 — Recursive implicit `super()`**
Inside `Parent()`, the compiler has again inserted `super();` (calling `Grandparent()`). Control jumps to `Grandparent()`.

**Step 4 — Top of the chain**
Inside `Grandparent()`, the compiler has inserted `super();` (calling `Object()`, since `Grandparent` extends `Object` implicitly). The `Object` constructor runs silently.

**Step 5 — Unwind begins**
Now `Object()` completes. `Grandparent()` body prints **"Grandparent constructor"** and returns.

**Step 6 — Middle of the chain**
Control returns to `Parent()`. Its body prints **"Parent constructor"**.

**Step 7 — Bottom of the chain**
Control returns to `Child()`. Its body prints **"Child constructor"**.

### Output Order

$$
\text{Output: Grandparent constructor} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Parent constructor} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Child constructor}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Model Insight:** Even though the printed output appears top-down, the **object is built from the inside out** — the `Object` subobject occupies the lowest memory address, the `Grandparent` subobject sits above it, then `Parent`, then `Child` at the top. The `super` reference at any point always points to the subobject immediately below the current class in memory.

## 3.2 Disambiguating Hidden Fields — Analytical Trace

### Problem Setup

```java
class Vehicle {
    int speed = 60;
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    int speed = 120;

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Car speed:    " + speed);
        System.out.println("Vehicle speed: " + super.speed);
    }
}
```

### Resolution Analysis

**Step 1 — Identifier resolution at line `speed`**
The compiler searches the **lexical scope** starting from the current class outward. The first `speed` found is `Car.speed = 120`. Bare `speed` resolves to **120**.

**Step 2 — Identifier resolution at `super.speed`**
The `super` qualifier forces the compiler to skip the current class and look in the **immediate parent** `Vehicle`. The variable `speed` there has the value **60**.

### Resulting Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Car speed:}    &\quad 120 \\
\text{Vehicle speed:} &\quad 60
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Extending an Overridden Method — Analytical Trace

### Problem Setup

```java
class Account {
    double calculateInterest(double principal) {
        return principal * 0.04;   // base 4% interest
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    @Override
    double calculateInterest(double principal) {
        double base = super.calculateInterest(principal);  // call parent
        return base + (principal * 0.02);                  // add 2% bonus
    }
}
```

### Analytical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Subclass method invoked**
Calling `savings.calculateInterest(1000.0)` enters `SavingsAccount.calculateInterest()`.

**Step 2 — Explicit `super` call**
The line `double base = super.calculateInterest(principal);` delegates the **base calculation** to `Account.calculateInterest()`.

**Step 3 — Parent executes base logic**
`Account.calculateInterest()` returns `1000 * 0.04 = 40.0`. This is the parent's answer.

**Step 4 — Subclass extends the result**
`SavingsAccount` computes its bonus: `1000 * 0.02 = 20.0`. Final return: `40.0 + 20.0 = 60.0`.

### Final Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Interest} &= \underbrace{P \times r_{\text{parent}}}_{\text{super call}} + \underbrace{P \times r_{\text{bonus}}}_{\text{child logic}} \\
&= P \times (r_{\text{parent}} + r_{\text{bonus}})
\end{aligned}
$$

For $P = 1000$, $r_{\text{parent}} = 0.04$, $r_{\text{bonus}} = 0.02$:

$$
\text{Total Interest} = 1000 \times (0.04 + 0.02) = 1000 \times 0.06 = 60.0
$$

## 3.4 Pitfall Analysis — `this()` vs `super()` Mutual Exclusivity

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** A constructor can use **either** `this(args)` **or** `super(args)`, but **never both**, because both must occupy the first line. The legal workaround is the *"constructor chaining across overloaded constructors"* pattern: constructor A calls `this(...)` which in turn calls `super(...)`.

### Verification Example

```java
class Box {
    Box()               { System.out.println("Default Box"); }
    Box(int size)       { this();  System.out.println("Sized Box: " + size); }
    // Box(double size) { super(); this(); }   // ILLEGAL — compile error
}
```

The commented line produces: *"call to this must be first statement in constructor"*, because `super()` already occupies the first slot.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Constructor Chaining Flow (Top-Down Build)

This diagram maps the **call sequence** of constructors across a 3-level inheritance hierarchy, showing how `super()` propagates the call upward before unwinding.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef callNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef execNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef endNode  fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    startNode([main invokes new Child]):::callNode
    callChild[Call Child constructor]:::callNode
    implicitSC1[Compiler inserts super to Parent]:::callNode
    callParent[Call Parent constructor]:::callNode
    implicitSC2[Compiler inserts super to Grandparent]:::callNode
    callGP[Call Grandparent constructor]:::callNode
    implicitSC3[Compiler inserts super to Object]:::callNode
    objectC[Object constructor runs]:::execNode
    gpBody[Print Grandparent constructor]:::execNode
    parentBody[Print Parent constructor]:::execNode
    childBody[Print Child constructor]:::execNode
    finalNode[Object fully constructed]:::endNode

    startNode --> callChild
    callChild --> implicitSC1
    implicitSC1 --> callParent
    callParent --> implicitSC2
    implicitSC2 --> callGP
    callGP --> implicitSC3
    implicitSC3 --> objectC
    objectC --> gpBody
    gpBody --> parentBody
    parentBody --> childBody
    childBody --> finalNode
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Method Override Resolution with `super`

This block diagram illustrates how the JVM dispatches an overridden method call when the child explicitly invokes `super.method()`.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef client fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef child  fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef parent fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef grand  fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#fff

    clientA[Client Code calls child.method]:::client
    childBox[Subclass overridden method]:::child
    superCall[super keyword qualifies call]:::child
    parentBox[Parent class original method]:::parent
    grandBox[Grandparent method if further chained]:::grand

    clientA --> childBox
    childBox -.invokes.-> superCall
    superCall --> parentBox
    parentBox -.optional super.-> grandBox
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of `super` Usage

This diagram maps the **three usage categories** of `super` to their respective memory and resolution targets within a parent–child object.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef usage  fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    useVar[Usage 1: super.fieldName]:::usage
    useMethod[Usage 2: super.methodName args]:::usage
    useCons[Usage 3: super args in constructor]:::usage

    targetField[Access parent hidden field in parent subobject]:::target
    targetMethod[Invoke parent overridden method via vtable]:::target
    targetCons[Chain parent constructor before child body]:::target

    useVar   --> targetField
    useMethod --> targetMethod
    useCons  --> targetCons
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Solid arrows represent direct control flow or data reference. Dotted arrows represent optional further chaining. The yellow node is the external caller, blue nodes are subclass-side actions, and green/purple nodes are superclass-side targets.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*What is the purpose of the `super` keyword in Java? List its uses.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class object** of the current instance. It provides controlled upward access in an inheritance hierarchy.

**The three uses are:**

1. **`super.fieldName`** — to access a parent class field that has been hidden by a same-named field in the subclass.
2. **`super.methodName(args)`** — to invoke the parent class version of a method that has been overridden in the subclass.
3. **`super(args)`** — to explicitly call a parameterized constructor of the parent class as the first statement of a subclass constructor.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing the three uses with one-line explanation each.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Why must the call `super()` (or `super(args)`) be the first statement inside a constructor? What happens if it is omitted?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The call `super()` or `super(args)` must be the **first statement** inside a subclass constructor because the parent class subobject must be **fully initialized before any child-specific initialization logic runs**. This is a language-enforced invariant that guarantees no inherited method can ever execute on uninitialized inherited state.

**If omitted:** the Java compiler **implicitly inserts a call to the parent's no-argument constructor** (`super();`) at the first line. If the parent class does not declare a no-argument constructor and the subclass fails to write an explicit `super(args)`, the program **fails to compile** with the error *"no suitable constructor found for Parent()"* or *"implicit super constructor Parent() is not visible"*.

*(1 mark for the rule, 1 mark for the reasoning, 1 mark for the implicit-insertion behavior and compile error.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation, Model Paper Pattern A]**
> Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the three different uses of the `super` keyword in Java with suitable code examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following class hierarchy. Predict the exact output of the program and justify the order of execution step by step. *(7 marks)*

```java
class A {
    A() { System.out.println("A constructor"); }
    A(int x) { System.out.println("A param: " + x); }
}
class B extends A {
    B() {
        super(10);
        System.out.println("B constructor");
    }
}
class C extends B {
    C() {
        System.out.println("C constructor");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new C();
    }
}
```

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The Three Uses of `super`** *(7 marks)*

The `super` keyword is a reference to the immediate parent class instance and has three principal applications.

**Use 1 — Accessing a hidden parent field (`super.fieldName`):**

```java
class Employee {
    double salary = 30000;
}
class Manager extends Employee {
    double salary = 80000;          // hides parent's salary
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Manager salary: " + salary);
        System.out.println("Employee salary: " + super.salary);
    }
}
```

Here, bare `salary` resolves to `80000` (the Manager's own field), while `super.salary` forces lookup in the parent `Employee` class, yielding `30000`. *(2 marks)*

**Use 2 — Calling an overridden parent method (`super.method()`):**

```java
class Printer {
    void print() { System.out.println("Base print"); }
}
class ColorPrinter extends Printer {
    @Override
    void print() {
        super.print();                                  // parent's version
        System.out.println("Color print extension");
    }
}
```

The child invokes `super.print()` to retain the parent's base behavior and then adds color-specific logic. *(2 marks)*

**Use 3 — Invoking the parent constructor (`super(args)`):**

```java
class Animal {
    Animal(String name) { System.out.println("Animal: " + name); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog() {
        super("Generic Dog");       // explicit parent constructor call
        System.out.println("Dog created");
    }
}
```

The `super("Generic Dog")` call must be the first statement. It selects the parameterized parent constructor instead of the default one. *(3 marks)*

**Part (b) — Output Prediction and Justification** *(7 marks)*

**Predicted Output:**

$$
\text{A param: 10} \;\rightarrow\; \text{B constructor} \;\rightarrow\; \text{C constructor}
$$

**Step-by-step Justification:**

**Step 1:** `main` invokes `new C()`. Control enters the `C()` constructor. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** `C()` contains no explicit `super()` call, so the compiler inserts `super();` (the no-arg `B()` constructor). Control jumps to `B()`. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** `B()`'s first line is `super(10);` — an **explicit** call to the parameterized `A(int)` constructor. Control jumps to `A(int)`. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** `A(int)` prints **"A param: 10"** and returns. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5:** Control returns to `B()`. The `System.out.println("B constructor")` line executes, printing **"B constructor"**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6:** Control returns to `C()`. The `System.out.println("C constructor")` line executes, printing **"C constructor"**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 7:** Object construction is complete. Note that the implicit `Object()` super-call is satisfied automatically at the top of `A`. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords in Java. When would you prefer one over the other? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating constructor chaining using `super()` in a multi-level inheritance hierarchy (`University → Department → Faculty`). Explain the order of construction with a diagram. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Difference Between `this` and `super`** *(7 marks)*

| Aspect | `this` keyword | `super` keyword |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Refers to | Current object instance | Immediate parent class object |
| Purpose | Disambiguates instance members of the **same** class | Accesses members of the **parent** class |
| Constructor use | `this(args)` calls **another constructor of the same class** | `super(args)` calls a **parent class constructor** |
| Default insertion | `this();` is **not** automatically inserted | `super();` is **automatically inserted** by the compiler |
| Position rule | Must be the first line, mutually exclusive with `super()` | Must be the first line, mutually exclusive with `this()` |
| Static context | Invalid (same reason as `super`) | Invalid |
| Accesses `private` members of own class | Allowed | Not applicable to parent |

**When to prefer which:** Use `this` when resolving ambiguity **within the current class** (e.g., parameter shadowing a field, calling an overloaded constructor). Use `super` when you need to reach **upward** to invoke the parent's overridden method, hidden field, or constructor — especially in the *Template Method* pattern or when extending parent behavior without replacing it. *(7 marks: 5 for table, 2 for the preference discussion.)*

**Part (b) — Multi-Level Constructor Chaining Program** *(7 marks)*

**Program:**

```java
class University {
    University() {
        System.out.println("University established");
    }
}
class Department extends University {
    Department() {
        System.out.println("Department created");
    }
}
class Faculty extends Department {
    Faculty() {
        System.out.println("Faculty appointed");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Faculty();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\text{University established} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Department created} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Faculty appointed}
$$

**Construction Order Diagram (Block Architecture):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef tier  fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef finalT fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

    objNode[Object constructed silently]:::tier
    uniNode[University constructor body runs]:::tier
    deptNode[Department constructor body runs]:::tier
    facNode[Faculty constructor body runs]:::finalT

    objNode --> uniNode
    uniNode --> deptNode
    deptNode --> facNode
```

**Explanation:** Construction begins at the **root** of the hierarchy (`Object`, silently) and proceeds **top-down**. The `super()` call is implicitly inserted by the compiler at the first line of every constructor that lacks an explicit `this()` or `super()` call. This guarantees the parent subobject is fully constructed before the child-specific logic runs. *(7 marks: 2 for code, 2 for output, 3 for explanation with diagram.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on `super` Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that `super()` must be the first line.** Writing `System.out.println(...)` before `super(x);` produces a compile error. Many students lose 2 marks by misordering lines.
> 2. **Confusing `super.field` with overriding.** Fields are **hidden**, not overridden. There is no polymorphism for fields — `super.field` is the only way to access the parent's value when shadowed.
> 3. **Believing `super` can call a grandparent's constructor directly.** It cannot. To reach the grandparent's constructor, you must call `super(args)` in the parent, which in turn calls its own parent. The chain is always **one level at a time**.
> 4. **Writing `super` inside a `static` method or main.** This is a **compile-time error**. Always wrap the demonstration inside an instance method or a constructor.
> 5. **Using both `this()` and `super()` in the same constructor.** Both demand the first line; only one can occupy that slot. Use the *"constructor chaining across overloads"* pattern: constructor A calls `this(...)`, and that chained constructor calls `super(...)`.
> 6. **Omitting `super(args)` when the parent has no default constructor.** This is the **most common compilation failure** in exam programs. Always verify that the parent has either a no-arg constructor or that the child explicitly chains a parameterized one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`super` is a reference to the immediate parent class object**, not to `Object` or to the root of the hierarchy.
- It has **exactly three uses**: field access, method access, constructor invocation.
- **`super(args)` must be the first statement** of a constructor; otherwise, the program fails to compile.
- **The compiler implicitly inserts `super();`** when no explicit constructor call is written — but only if the parent has an accessible no-arg constructor.
- **`super` cannot be used in `static` contexts** — it requires a live instance.
- **Fields are hidden, methods are overridden**; `super` can reach either, but the resolution mechanism differs.
- **Constructor chaining is top-down**: root → intermediate → leaf class. Object construction proceeds from the inside out in memory.
- **`this()` and `super()` are mutually exclusive** in the same constructor — both want the first line.
- **A subclass cannot directly invoke a grandparent's constructor** via `super`; it must transitively chain through the parent.
- **The `super` keyword is the foundation of the Template Method design pattern** and is used pervasively in framework code (Swing, Servlets, Spring, Android).
- **Static methods can be hidden but not overridden**; `super` is irrelevant in static contexts and cannot be used to call a hidden static method polymorphically.
- **Access modifiers still apply**: a `private` parent member is never reachable via `super`, even though it physically exists in the object.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions**, always pair your code with a **step-by-step execution trace** and a **block/memory diagram** — this alone can earn the full 7 marks on the constructor-chaining sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: protected Members
Calling Order of Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calling Order of Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**, the **Calling Order of Constructors** refers to the deterministic, well-defined sequence in which **constructors of base (parent) classes and the derived (child) class are invoked** when an object of a derived class is instantiated. The order is governed by the inheritance hierarchy defined in the class declaration, and it ensures that inherited data members are **properly initialized before** the derived class's own initialization logic executes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Understand the order in which constructors (and destructors) are invoked in single, multilevel, multiple, and hierarchical inheritance — including implicit invocations and explicit parameterized constructor calls."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Building Construction Metaphor"

Imagine constructing a **multi-storey building**:

- You **cannot** start building the 5th floor until the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th floors are complete.
- The **foundation (base class)** must be laid first, then each **intermediate floor (intermediate base classes)**, and finally the **top floor (most derived class)**.
- Each floor has its own "construction team" (constructor) that runs in sequence.

In OOP, when you write `new Derived()`, the JVM does **not** jump straight to `Derived()` — it first walks **up the inheritance chain** from derived → base, calling each constructor in hierarchical order. Once the entire chain has executed, control unwinds back, and the **derived class constructor finishes last**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule of Thumb:** *Constructors execute from the **root (most-base) class downward** to the **most-derived class**. Destructors execute in the **exact reverse order**.*

## 1.3 Why This Order Matters — Engineering Perspective

- **Memory Safety:** Base class sub-objects must be initialized before any derived class logic tries to use them.
- **Encapsulation Integrity:** Inherited members are private/protected; only base class constructors can legally initialize them.
- **Determinism:** Same input → same constructor execution trace → predictable object state.
- **Resource Management:** In C++, this order prevents undefined behaviour when base class destructors own resources (RAII pattern).

> [!TIP]
> In real-world engineering systems (e.g., embedded firmware, hardware abstraction layers), this same "parent-before-child" rule applies to **device drivers** — the parent bus driver must initialize before the child device driver.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Rules Governing Constructor Calling Order

The order is **not arbitrary**. It is dictated by the following four hierarchical rules:

### Rule 1 — Single Inheritance (Linear Chain)
- When `Derived` extends `Base`, creating a `Derived` object causes `Base()` → `Derived()` to execute.
- The compiler **implicitly inserts** a call to the base class's **no-argument (default) constructor**.

### Rule 2 — Multilevel Inheritance (Vertical Chain)
For `GrandChild extends Child extends Parent`:
- Execution order is: `Parent()` → `Child()` → `GrandChild()`.
- Each level must complete before the next begins.

### Rule 3 — Multiple Inheritance (Diamond/Linear Fork)
- The order follows the **order of base classes listed in the inheritance declaration**, **not** the order of their appearance in the code body.
- In Java (which forbids class multiple inheritance), this applies to **interface implementation order** and constructor chaining via `super()`.

### Rule 4 — Explicit Parameterized Constructor Calls
- If the base class has **no default constructor**, the derived class **must explicitly call** a base constructor using `super(args)` in Java or the initializer list `: Base(args)` in C++.
- Failure to do so → **compile-time error**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implicit vs Explicit Calls:**
> - **Implicit** → Compiler inserts `super()` (no-arg) automatically.
> - **Explicit** → Programmer writes `super(x, y)` to forward arguments to a parameterized base constructor.

## 2.2 Destructor Calling Order (Reverse Rule)

Destructors are called in **exactly the reverse** order of constructors:
- Constructors: Base → Derived
- Destructors: Derived → Base

**Reason:** The derived class may have references/pointers to base class resources. The derived class must clean up its own state **before** the base class's resources are released.

## 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Constructor Call Ordering Cheat Table

| Inheritance Type | Class Hierarchy | Constructor Call Order | Destructor Call Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single | `B` extends `A` | `A()` → `B()` | `~B()` → `~A()` |
| Multilevel | `C` extends `B` extends `A` | `A()` → `B()` → `C()` | `~C()` → `~B()` → `~A()` |
| Multiple | `C` extends `A`, `B` (in that order) | `A()` → `B()` → `C()` | `~C()` → `~B()` → `~A()` |
| Hierarchical | `B`, `C` both extend `A` | `A()` → `B()` or `A()` → `C()` | Reverse of above |
| Hybrid/Diamond | `D` extends `B`, `C`; both extend `A` | `A()` → `B()` → `C()` → `D()` (one `A()` only due to virtual inheritance in C++) | Reverse of above |

| Java Keyword | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `super()` | Calls base class **no-arg** constructor | `super();` |
| `super(args)` | Calls base class **parameterized** constructor | `super(name, age);` |
| `this()` | Calls **same-class** overloaded constructor | `this(10);` |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Software Engineering

- **Framework Design:** Java Spring/Hibernate framework extensively relies on constructor chaining for bean initialization.
- **GUI Toolkits:** Swing/AWT — `JFrame` constructor chains up `Component → Container → JComponent → JFrame`.
- **Game Engines:** Unity/Unreal component hierarchies follow the same rule.
- **Embedded Systems:** RTOS task creation mirrors this base-before-derived initialization to guarantee hardware dependency order.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case Study 1 — Single Inheritance (Java)

Consider the following **trace-able** Java program:

```java
class Animal {
    Animal() {
        System.out.println("Animal constructor");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog() {
        System.out.println("Dog constructor");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Step | Action | Output | Stack State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `main()` begins; `new Dog()` is invoked | — | `main` active |
| 2 | JVM allocates memory for `Dog` (includes `Animal` sub-object) | — | `main`, `Dog()` active |
| 3 | First statement of `Dog()` is **implicit `super()`** → calls `Animal()` | `Animal constructor` | `main`, `Dog()`, `Animal()` active |
| 4 | `Animal()` body finishes, returns to `Dog()` | — | `main`, `Dog()` active |
| 5 | `Dog()` body executes | `Dog constructor` | `main` active |
| 6 | `main()` resumes | — | — |

**Final Console Output:**
```
Animal constructor
Dog constructor
```

### Mathematical Representation

Let the constructor call be modeled as a function $C(\text{class})$:

$$
\text{Order} = C(\text{Animal}) \;\prec\; C(\text{Dog})
$$

Where $\prec$ denotes "executes before". For any linear chain $L = \{A, B, C, \ldots, Z\}$:

$$
C(A) \prec C(B) \prec C(C) \prec \cdots \prec C(Z)
$$

---

## 3.2 Case Study 2 — Multilevel Inheritance

```java
class A {
    A() { System.out.println("A"); }
}
class B extends A {
    B() { System.out.println("B"); }
}
class C extends B {
    C() { System.out.println("C"); }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        C obj = new C();
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace (Detailed)

**Step 1:** `new C()` is encountered.
**Step 2:** Inside `C()`, the compiler-injected first line is `super();` which calls `B()`.
**Step 3:** Inside `B()`, the compiler-injected first line is `super();` which calls `A()`.
**Step 4:** `A()` executes → prints `A`.
**Step 5:** Control returns to `B()` → prints `B`.
**Step 6:** Control returns to `C()` → prints `C`.

**Output:**
```
A
B
C
```

**Order formula:**

$$
C(A) \prec C(B) \prec C(C)
$$

**Destructor (reverse) formula:**

$$
D(C) \prec D(B) \prec D(A)
$$

---

## 3.3 Case Study 3 — Explicit Parameterized Constructor Call

```java
class Person {
    String name;
    Person(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Person: " + name);
    }
}

class Student extends Person {
    int roll;
    Student(String name, int roll) {
        super(name);          // Explicit call
        this.roll = roll;
```
