```java
        System.out.println("Student: " + roll);
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student("Karthik", 42);
    }
}
```

### Analytical Trace

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new Student("Karthik", 42)` invoked | — |
| 2 | First line of `Student()` is `super("Karthik")` → invokes `Person(String)` | — |
| 3 | `Person(String)` sets `this.name = "Karthik"`, prints | `Person: Karthik` |
| 4 | Returns to `Student()`, sets `this.roll = 42`, prints | `Student: 42` |

**Final Output:**
```
Person: Karthik
Student: 42
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `super()` must be the first statement?**
> Java enforces this rule to guarantee base class initialization completes **before** any derived-class-specific logic that might depend on inherited fields. This is a **compile-time enforcement**, not a runtime check.

---

## 3.4 Case Study 4 — C++ Multiple Inheritance (Initializer List)

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Base1 {
public:
    Base1() { cout << "Base1" << endl; }
};
class Base2 {
public:
    Base2() { cout << "Base2" << endl; }
};
class Derived : public Base1, public Base2 {
public:
    Derived() { cout << "Derived" << endl; }
};

int main() {
    Derived d;
    return 0;
}
```

### Order Analysis

In C++, when `Derived` inherits as `: public Base1, public Base2`, the constructor order follows the **declaration order**, not the textual order of the initializer list.

**Order:**

$$
C(\text{Base1}) \prec C(\text{Base2}) \prec C(\text{Derived})
$$

**Output:**
```
Base1
Base2
Derived
```

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Cases

| Scenario | Hierarchy | Output Order | Java Keyword Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single | `B → A` | `A`, `B` | Implicit `super()` |
| Multilevel | `C → B → A` | `A`, `B`, `C` | Implicit `super()` at each level |
| Parameterized | `B → A(named)` | `A(name)`, `B` | Explicit `super(name)` |
| Multiple (C++) | `D → B1, B2` | `B1`, `B2`, `D` | Initializer list order |
| Diamond (C++ virtual) | `D → B,C → A` | `A`, `B`, `C`, `D` | `virtual` inheritance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Single & Multilevel Constructor Call Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([main method invoked new GrandChild]) --> callGC[GrandChild constructor]
    callGC --> superGC{super called?}
    superGC -- yes --> callPC[Parent constructor]
    callGC --> bodyGC[GrandChild body executes]
    callPC --> superPC{super called?}
    superPC -- yes --> callGP[GrandParent constructor]
    superPC -- no --> bodyPC[Parent body executes]
    callGP --> bodyGP[GrandParent body executes]
    bodyGP --> retGP[Return to Parent]
    bodyPC --> retPC[Return to GrandChild]
    bodyGC --> retGC[Return to main]
    retGC --> endNode([Execution complete])

    style startA fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style endNode fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style callGC fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style callPC fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style callGP fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Multiple Inheritance Constructor Order (C++)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mainNode([new Derived]) --> derC[Derived constructor]
    derC --> b1C[Base1 constructor]
    derC --> b2C[Base2 constructor]
    b1C --> b1Body[Base1 body]
    b2C --> b2Body[Base2 body]
    derC --> derBody[Derived body]
    b1Body --> ret1[Return]
    b2Body --> ret2[Return]
    derBody --> endM([End])

    style mainNode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style derC fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style b1C fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style b2C fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style endM fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Destructor Reverse-Order (C++ Stack Unwinding)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startD([Object goes out of scope]) --> derD[Derived destructor runs]
    derD --> b2D[Base2 destructor runs]
    derD --> b1D[Base1 destructor runs]
    b2D --> b2Done[Base2 resources freed]
    b1D --> b1Done[Base1 resources freed]
    derD --> derDone[Derived resources freed]
    b2Done --> endD([Memory deallocated])
    b1Done --> endD
    derDone --> endD

    style startD fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style derD fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style b1D fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style b2D fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style endD fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inheritance & Constructor Initialization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HierArch["Inheritance Hierarchy"]
        H1[Base Class Layer]
        H2[Intermediate Class Layer]
        H3[Derived Class Layer]
        H1 --> H2
        H2 --> H3
    end

    subgraph InitSeq["Constructor Initialization Sequence"]
        S1[Step 1 Base class data members initialized]
        S2[Step 2 Base class constructor body executes]
        S3[Step 3 Intermediate class members initialized]
        S4[Step 4 Intermediate class constructor body executes]
        S5[Step 5 Derived class members initialized]
        S6[Step 6 Derived class constructor body executes]
        S1 --> S2
        S2 --> S3
        S3 --> S4
        S4 --> S5
        S5 --> S6
    end

    HierArch --> InitSeq

    style H1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style H2 fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style H3 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style S1 fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style S6 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the order in which constructors are invoked in a multilevel inheritance hierarchy. What happens if the base class has a parameterized constructor and no default constructor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In multilevel inheritance, constructors are invoked from the **topmost base class downward to the most-derived class**. For a hierarchy `C extends B extends A`, the order is `A() → B() → C()`.

If the base class has only a parameterized constructor and no default constructor, the **derived class must explicitly call** the base class constructor using `super(parameters)` in Java (or the initializer list in C++). Failure to do so results in a **compile-time error**, as the compiler cannot insert an implicit `super()` call.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why must the `super()` call be the first statement in a Java constructor? What is the consequence if a derived class constructor does not call `super()` explicitly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `super()` call must be the **first statement** to ensure that base class data members are fully initialized **before** any derived-class logic that may depend on them is executed. This preserves the **encapsulation and initialization safety** contract.

If a derived class constructor does not call `super()` explicitly, the Java compiler **automatically inserts** an implicit call to the **no-argument constructor** of the base class. If the base class lacks such a constructor, a **compilation error** occurs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the order of constructor invocation in **single, multilevel, and multiple inheritance** with suitable Java code examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating **explicit parameterized constructor chaining** using `super()`. Show the output and explain the flow of execution. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Model Solution:**

In **single inheritance**, when a derived class object is created, the base class constructor executes first, followed by the derived class constructor. Example:

```java
class Vehicle {
    Vehicle() { System.out.println("Vehicle"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    Car() { System.out.println("Car"); }
}
// Creating Car object: Output → "Vehicle" then "Car"
```

In **multilevel inheritance**, constructors execute **top-down** through the chain. For `C extends B extends A`, the order is `A() → B() → C()`.

In **multiple inheritance** (supported in C++, simulated in Java via interfaces), the order follows the **declaration order of base classes**. In Java, since multiple class inheritance is not allowed, the rule applies to the order of `extends` and `implements` clauses combined with chained `super()` calls.

**(b) Model Solution:**

```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    Employee(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Employee: " + name);
    }
}
class Manager extends Employee {
    String dept;
    Manager(String name, String dept) {
        super(name);
        this.dept = dept;
        System.out.println("Manager of: " + dept);
    }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Manager m = new Manager("Anjali", "CSE");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Employee: Anjali
Manager of: CSE
```

**Explanation:** When `new Manager(...)` is called, control first reaches `super(name)` which invokes `Employee(String)`, initializing the inherited `name` field. After `Employee()` completes, the derived class `Manager()` sets `dept` and prints. This ensures **base class initialization precedes derived class logic**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram and Java code, explain how constructors and destructors are invoked in a **three-level inheritance hierarchy**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is the role of the `super` keyword in Java? Differentiate between `super()` and `this()` with examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Model Solution:**

For a hierarchy `ResearchScholar → Student → Person`:

```java
class Person {
    Person() { System.out.println("Person"); }
    protected void finalize() { System.out.println("Person destroyed"); }
}
class Student extends Person {
    Student() { System.out.println("Student"); }
    protected void finalize() { System.out.println("Student destroyed"); }
}
class ResearchScholar extends Student {
    ResearchScholar() { System.out.println("ResearchScholar"); }
    protected void finalize() { System.out.println("ResearchScholar destroyed"); }
}
```

**Constructor Order:** `Person()` → `Student()` → `ResearchScholar()`
**Destructor Order (when GC runs):** Reverse — `ResearchScholar destroyed` → `Student destroyed` → `Person destroyed`

**Diagram (textual):**
```
ResearchScholar → Student → Person
   (Derived)    (Mid)     (Base)
```

The constructor chain moves **upward from derived to base**, while destructors run **downward from derived to base** (reverse).

**(b) Model Solution:**

The `super` keyword in Java refers to the **immediate parent class**. It serves two purposes:
1. **Call parent class constructor:** `super()` or `super(args)`
2. **Access parent class member (when overridden/hidden):** `super.methodName()` or `super.fieldName`

**Difference between `super()` and `this()`:**

| Feature | `super()` | `this()` |
|---|---|---|
| Refers to | Parent class constructor | Same-class constructor |
| Position | Must be first statement | Must be first statement |
| Use case | Initialize inherited members | Constructor overloading within same class |
| Conflict | Cannot coexist in same constructor | Cannot coexist with `super()` |

**Example:**
```java
class Box {
    int side;
    Box() { this(5); }              // Calls Box(int)
    Box(int side) { this.side = side; }
}
class ColoredBox extends Box {
    String color;
    ColoredBox() {
        super();                     // Calls Box()
        this.color = "Red";
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing constructor order with method call order** — Marks lost if a student writes "derived class method is called first".
> 2. **Forgetting implicit `super()` insertion rule** — If base has no default constructor, mentioning "compiler gives error" fetches full marks; ignoring this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Destructor order confusion** — Always state: **destructors run in REVERSE order of constructors**. This is a favourite 2-mark question.
> 4. **Java does NOT support multiple class inheritance** — If a student writes C++-style multiple inheritance code in Java, they will lose marks. Mention interfaces if needed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Constructor Order Rule:** Top-down — base class constructor always runs **before** derived class constructor.
- **Destructor Order Rule:** Bottom-up — derived class destructor runs **before** base class destructor (reverse).
- **Implicit `super()`:** Java compiler auto-inserts a no-arg call to the parent constructor if not written explicitly.
- **Explicit `super(args)`:** Mandatory when base class has no default constructor or when passing parameters.
- **`super()` position:** Must be the **first statement** in a constructor — non-negotiable in Java.
- **Multiple inheritance order (C++):** Follows the **declaration order** of base classes, not the initializer list textual order.
- **Diamond inheritance (C++):** Use `virtual` inheritance to ensure the grandparent constructor runs **only once**.
- **Static members:** Static initialization happens **once** when the class is first loaded — separate from instance constructor order.
- **Final keyword:** A class declared `final` cannot be inherited — hence its constructor is never called as a base.
- **Abstract classes:** Can have constructors, and these constructors **ARE invoked** when a concrete subclass is instantiated.
- **Practical tip for exams:** Always trace the **constructor chain visually** with arrows before writing the output — this prevents misordering errors.
- **Real-world takeaway:** This order is not academic trivia — it underpins every major Java framework (Spring Beans, Servlets, JPA Entities) and is the backbone of safe inheritance design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overriding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overriding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Method Overriding** is a core feature of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) that enables a **subclass (derived class)** to provide a *specific implementation* of a method that is **already declared** in its **superclass (base class)**. The overridden method in the subclass must share an **identical signature** (name, parameter list, and return type) with the method in the parent class.

This mechanism is the foundational pillar of **Dynamic Polymorphism** (also called *Run-Time Polymorphism* or *Late Binding*), where the decision of *which* method implementation to invoke is deferred until **runtime**, based on the actual object type rather than the reference type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Method Overriding is tested heavily under **CO2 – Apply the concepts of inheritance and polymorphism to design object-oriented programs.** Marks are frequently allotted to identifying valid overrides, demonstrating dynamic dispatch, and writing correct code involving the `super` keyword.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Method Overriding like a **family recipe passed down from a parent to a child**.

- The **parent (superclass)** owns the original recipe for making tea: *boil water, add tea leaves, add sugar, serve hot.*
- The **child (subclass)** learns this recipe but decides to *customize* it: *boil water, add tea leaves, add **ginger and cardamom**, add sugar, serve hot.*

The "core signature" (the method name `makeTea()`) remains the same — but the child has **redefined the internal steps** to suit their own taste. When someone asks the child to *make tea*, the child performs *their* version, not the parent's. This is exactly how method overriding works in Java/C++/Python — the JVM (or compiler) resolves the actual method body based on the **runtime object**, not the declared reference variable.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Superclass Method** | The original method in the parent class, also called the *overridden* or *hidden* method. |
| **Subclass Method** | The new method in the child class that *replaces* the parent's version, called the *overriding* method. |
| **Upcasting** | Referencing a subclass object using a superclass reference variable: `Animal a = new Dog();` |
| **Dynamic Dispatch** | The mechanism by which the JVM selects the correct overridden method at runtime. |
| **`super` keyword** | A reference variable used to invoke the parent class's version of an overridden method. |
| **Covariant Return Type** | Allowing the overriding method to return a *subtype* of the original return type. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Method Overriding — Step-by-Step

To override a method correctly, the JVM follows a strict resolution pipeline. Let's break down the **"Why"** and **"How"**:

1. **Step 1 — Inheritance Establishment**
   - A subclass must extend a superclass (or implement an interface). Overriding has *no meaning* outside an inheritance relationship.
   - *Why:* The overriding method needs access to the parent's method signature contract.

2. **Step 2 — Signature Matching**
   - The subclass method must have the **exact same name**, **same parameter list (number, type, order)**, and a **compatible return type** (identical or covariant).
   - *How:* The compiler uses *name + parameter descriptor* to perform the binding lookup.

3. **Step 3 — Access Modifier Non-Restriction Rule**
   - The overriding method can have the **same** or a **wider (less restrictive)** access level, but never narrower.
   - *Why:* Java enforces *Liskov Substitution Principle* — a child cannot be less accessible than its parent.

4. **Step 4 — Runtime Dynamic Dispatch**
   - When a method is called via a superclass reference pointing to a subclass object, the JVM inspects the **actual object on the heap** (not the reference type) to decide which method to execute.
   - *How:* This is achieved via the **virtual method table (vtable)** stored in the Class object.

5. **Step 5 — Invocation of `super` (Optional)**
   - The subclass can optionally invoke the parent's version using `super.methodName()`.
   - *Why:* To **extend** the parent's behavior rather than completely replace it.

6. **Step 6 — Resolution at Runtime**
   - The method binding is finalized only when the program runs. This is why overriding implements **Run-Time Polymorphism**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Method Overriding is a programming construct, the "formulas" are essentially **rule-based invariants** that must be satisfied for a valid override.

$$
\boxed{\text{Valid Override} \iff \left( N_{sub} = N_{super} \right) \wedge \left( P_{sub} \equiv P_{super} \right) \wedge \left( R_{sub} \preceq R_{super} \right) \wedge \left( A_{sub} \succeq A_{super} \right)}
$$

Where:

| Symbol | Represents | Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{sub}$, $N_{super}$ | Method Name | Must be **identical** |
| $P_{sub}$, $P_{super}$ | Parameter List | Must be **identical** (types, count, order) |
| $R_{sub}$, $R_{super}$ | Return Type | Must be identical **OR** $R_{sub}$ is a subtype of $R_{super}$ (Covariant Return) |
| $A_{sub}$, $A_{super}$ | Access Modifier | Must be **same or wider** (e.g., `protected` $\rightarrow$ `public` allowed; `public` $\rightarrow$ `private` **NOT** allowed) |
| $\wedge$ | Logical AND | All conditions must hold simultaneously |
| $\preceq$, $\succeq$ | Subtype / Supertype ordering | Used for covariance and access modifier rules |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The relation $\equiv$ above on parameter lists denotes *structural equality* — not just same names. Changing a parameter from `int` to `double` converts an *override* into an *overload*, which is a frequent exam trap.

### 2.3 Methods That **CANNOT** Be Overridden

| Method Type | Reason |
|---|---|
| `static` methods | Belong to the class, not the object; undergo *method hiding*, not overriding. |
| `final` methods | Explicitly locked by the parent to prevent modification (security/contract reasons). |
| `private` methods | Not visible to the subclass; any "similar" method in the subclass is treated as a new method. |
| Constructors | Constructors are not inherited; hence they cannot be overridden. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Framework Design:** Frameworks like Spring (Java) and Django (Python) use method overriding to let developers customize template methods (the *Template Method Design Pattern*).
- **GUI Programming:** Subclasses of `JFrame` or `Activity` override lifecycle methods like `onCreate()`, `paint()`, etc.
- **Plugin Architectures:** Applications expose overridable hooks so third-party developers can extend behavior without modifying source code (Open/Closed Principle).
- **API Contracts:** Defining overridable methods in abstract base classes enforces a behavioral contract across all implementations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because Method Overriding is a programming concept, the "derivations" here take the form of **exhaustive code walkthroughs**, syntactic validations, and resolution logic — each step explained in plain text.

### 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Demonstrating Basic Method Overriding (Java)

**Problem Context:** Model a `Shape` superclass and a `Circle` subclass. The superclass declares a `draw()` method, and the subclass overrides it to provide a specialized drawing routine.

```java
// Step 1: Define the superclass
class Shape {
    // Step 2: Declare the method that will be overridden
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}

// Step 3: Define the subclass
class Circle extends Shape {
    // Step 4: Override the draw() method
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle with radius and center");
    }
}

// Step 5: Driver class
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 6: Upcasting — superclass reference, subclass object
        Shape myShape = new Circle();
        // Step 7: Dynamic dispatch — Circle's draw() is invoked
        myShape.draw();
    }
}
```

**Execution Logic Step-by-Step:**

- **Line-by-Line Evaluation:**
  - `Shape myShape = new Circle();` — The **reference variable** `myShape` is of type `Shape` (compile-time type). The **object** on the heap is of type `Circle` (runtime type).
  - `myShape.draw();` — At compile time, the compiler verifies that `draw()` exists in `Shape` (it does). At runtime, the JVM consults the vtable of the actual object (`Circle`) and invokes `Circle.draw()`.
  - **Output:** `Drawing a circle with radius and center`

- **Key Insight:** The output is the subclass version, even though the reference is of the parent type — this is **dynamic polymorphism in action**.

### 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Using `super` to Invoke the Parent's Overridden Method

```java
class Vehicle {
    public void start() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle started");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    public void start() {
        // Step A: Invoke parent class method first
        super.start();
        // Step B: Then add subclass-specific behavior
        System.out.println("Car engine ignited, ready to drive");
    }
}
```

**Conversion Logic:**

- `super.start()` explicitly calls `Vehicle.start()`, ensuring the parent's logic is *preserved and extended*.
- This pattern is critical when the parent's method performs essential initialization that the child **must not skip**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Valid vs. Invalid Overrides

Given a superclass method:
```java
public class Parent {
    public Number display(int x) { return x; }
}
```

Evaluate the following subclass declarations:

| # | Subclass Method | Valid Override? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `public Integer display(int x) { return x; }` | ✅ **YES** | `Integer` is a subtype of `Number` → **Covariant Return Type** allowed. |
| 2 | `public Number display(int x) { return x; }` | ✅ **YES** | Identical signature. |
| 3 | `protected Number display(int x) { return x; }` | ❌ **NO** | Narrowing access (`public` → `protected`) violates the access-widening rule. |
| 4 | `public Number display(double x) { return x; }` | ❌ **NO** | Parameter type changed (`int` → `double`) → this is an **overload**, not an override. |
| 5 | `public static Number display(int x) { return x; }` | ❌ **NO** | `static` methods cannot be overridden; this causes *method hiding*. |
| 6 | `public final Number display(int x) { return x; }` | ❌ **NO** | `final` methods are immutable and cannot be overridden. |
| 7 | `private Number display(int x) { return x; }` | ❌ **NO** | `private` methods are not inherited; the subclass declares a brand new method. |

### 3.4 Walkthrough 3 — Method Overriding vs. Method Overloading (Most Frequent KTU Comparison)

```java
class Calculator {
    // Overload 1
    public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    // Overload 2 (different parameter count)
    public int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; }
}

class ScientificCalculator extends Calculator {
    // Override of Overload 1
    @Override
    public int add(int a, int b) {
        System.out.println("Scientific add invoked");
        return a + b;
    }
}
```

**Resolution Logic Table:**

| Invocation | Compile-Time Bind? | Run-Time Bind? | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Calculator c = new Calculator(); c.add(2, 3);` | Yes (`Calculator.add`) | N/A | Returns `5`, no message. |
| `Calculator c = new ScientificCalculator(); c.add(2, 3);` | Yes (`Calculator.add` by signature) | Yes (overridden body in `ScientificCalculator`) | Returns `5` with message "Scientific add invoked". |
| `Calculator c = new ScientificCalculator(); c.add(2, 3, 4);` | Yes (3-arg version in `Calculator`) | No override exists | Returns `9`, no message. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 7-mark question asks students to predict the output of code involving **both** overloading and overriding. The student must demonstrate understanding that *overload resolution happens at compile time*, while *override resolution happens at runtime*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class Hierarchy & Dynamic Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Superclass Shape"] -->|extends| B["Subclass Circle"]
    A -->|extends| C["Subclass Rectangle"]
    A -->|extends| D["Subclass Triangle"]

    A --> A1["Method draw declared"]
    B --> B1["Method draw overridden"]
    C --> C1["Method draw overridden"]
    D --> D1["Method draw overridden"]

    E["Reference Variable of type Shape"] -->|points to heap object| F["Which version of draw is invoked?"]
    F -->|Actual Object Circle| G["Invokes Circle.draw"]
    F -->|Actual Object Rectangle| H["Invokes Rectangle.draw"]
    F -->|Actual Object Triangle| I["Invokes Triangle.draw"]

    J["Compiler Stage"] --> J1["Verifies draw exists in Shape - Compile Time Binding"]
    K["JVM Stage"] --> K1["Reads vtable of actual object - Run Time Binding"]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **left column** shows the inheritance hierarchy: `Shape` is the superclass; `Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle` are subclasses.
- Each subclass provides its **own overridden `draw()`** method (shown in blue boxes).
- The **right side** illustrates dynamic dispatch: a single superclass reference (`Shape`) can point to *any* subclass object, and the JVM dynamically invokes the *correct* overridden version at runtime.

### 4.2 Virtual Method Table (vtable) Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph vtableShape["VTable for Shape"]
        S1["draw -> Shape.draw"]
    end
    subgraph vtableCircle["VTable for Circle"]
        C1["draw -> Circle.draw"]
    end
    subgraph vtableRectangle["VTable for Rectangle"]
        R1["draw -> Rectangle.draw"]
    end

    REF["Reference: Shape s = new Circle"] --> CHECK["Compiler confirms draw exists"]
    CHECK --> LOOKUP["JVM looks up vtable of actual object Circle"]
    LOOKUP --> INVOKE["Invokes Circle.draw correctly"]

    style vtableShape fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style vtableCircle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style vtableRectangle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Explanation:**

- Each class loaded by the JVM has its **own vtable** — a structure mapping method signatures to actual code addresses.
- When `Circle` overrides `draw`, its vtable entry for `draw` points to `Circle.draw` instead of `Shape.draw`.
- When `s.draw()` is called, the JVM uses the reference's declared type (`Shape`) only to *find the vtable slot*, then uses the *object's class* to *find the actual method address*.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Override Validation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["Subclass Method Declaration"] --> Q1{"Same Method Name?"}
    Q1 -->|No| O1["Compile Error: New Method"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Same Parameter List?"}
    Q2 -->|No| O2["Method Overload, Not Override"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{"Return Type Identical or Covariant?"}
    Q3 -->|No| O3["Compile Error: Incompatible Return"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{"Access Modifier Same or Wider?"}
    Q4 -->|No| O4["Compile Error: Cannot Reduce Visibility"]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5{"Method Non-static, Non-final, Non-private?"}
    Q5 -->|No| O5["Compile Error or Warning"]
    Q5 -->|Yes| OK["VALID METHOD OVERRIDE"]

    style OK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style O1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
    style O2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
    style O3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
    style O4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
    style O5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
```

This block diagram acts as a **decision flowchart** a student can mentally apply during exams to validate whether a given code snippet demonstrates correct method overriding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Method Overriding. State any two rules that must be satisfied for a valid method override in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

**Method Overriding** is a mechanism in object-oriented programming that allows a subclass to provide a *specific implementation* of a method that is already defined in its superclass, using the *same method signature* (name, parameter list, and return type).

**Rules for Valid Override:**

1. **Same Signature:** The overriding method must have the **same name** and **identical parameter list** as the overridden method.
2. **Access Modifier Restriction:** The overriding method cannot have a *more restrictive* access modifier than the overridden method (e.g., overriding a `public` method with `private` is illegal).

*(Any other valid rules like covariant return type, exception restriction, etc., also accepted.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Method Overloading and Method Overriding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Binding Time | Compile-time (Static Polymorphism) | Run-time (Dynamic Polymorphism) |
| Signature | Must differ in **parameter list** | Must be **identical** |
| Inheritance | Can occur within the **same class** | Requires **inheritance** (subclass–superclass) |
| Return Type | Can be different (no constraint) | Must be same or covariant |
| Access Modifiers | No restrictions | Cannot be more restrictive |
| Purpose | Adds multiple ways to call a similar operation | Customizes inherited behavior |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Method Overriding in Java with a suitable example. Discuss how dynamic method dispatch works at runtime. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
Method Overriding allows a subclass to redefine a method inherited from its superclass. The overriding method must have the **same signature** as the parent method. This enables **runtime polymorphism** — the JVM determines which version of the method to invoke based on the *actual object type*, not the reference type.

**Example:**
```java
class Animal {
    public void speak() {
        System.out.println("Animal speaks");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void speak() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}

class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void speak() {
        System.out.println("Cat meows");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal a;
        a = new Dog();
        a.speak();  // Output: Dog barks
        a = new Cat();
        a.speak();  // Output: Cat meows
    }
}
```

**Dynamic Method Dispatch Mechanism:**

1. The reference variable `a` is declared as type `Animal` at compile time.
2. At runtime, the JVM inspects the **actual object** pointed to by `a`.
3. Using the **virtual method table (vtable)**, the JVM locates the `speak()` method corresponding to the object's true class.
4. If the subclass has overridden `speak()`, that version is invoked; otherwise, the parent's version is used.
5. This late binding mechanism is what enables polymorphism in Java.

**(b)** What is the role of the `super` keyword in method overriding? Illustrate with an example where the subclass extends the behavior of the parent's overridden method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of `super` keyword:**
The `super` keyword in Java is used to explicitly invoke the **parent class's version** of an overridden method. This is essential when the subclass wants to **extend** (not completely replace) the inherited behavior — i.e., perform the parent's logic first, then add new functionality.

**Example:**
```java
class BankAccount {
    public void calculateInterest() {
        System.out.println("Calculating generic bank interest at 4%");
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    @Override
    public void calculateInterest() {
        super.calculateInterest();  // Invoke parent's logic
        System.out.println("Adding senior citizen bonus at 0.5%");
        System.out.println("Final savings interest: 4.5%");
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount();
        sa.calculateInterest();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Calculating generic bank interest at 4%
Adding senior citizen bonus at 0.5%
Final savings interest: 4.5%
```

**Explanation:**
- `super.calculateInterest()` ensures the parent's interest computation logic runs first.
- The subclass then **augments** this result with additional rules.
- Without `super`, the parent logic would be **completely replaced**, which is undesirable in this extension scenario.
- This pattern embodies the **Open/Closed Principle** — open for extension, closed for modification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** List and explain any **four rules** that must be followed for a valid method override. For each rule, provide a small code snippet showing either a valid override or a violation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Rule 1: Identical Method Signature**
- The overriding method must have the **same name** and **same parameter list** as the parent.
- *Valid Override:*
```java
class A { public void show(int x) {} }
class B extends A { public void show(int x) {} }  // VALID
```
- *Violation:*
```java
class B extends A { public void show(double x) {} }  // This is an OVERLOAD, not override
```

**Rule 2: Access Modifier Cannot Be Narrowed**
- The overriding method must have **same or wider access**.
- *Violation:*
```java
class A { public void run() {} }
class B extends A { private void run() {} }  // ERROR: cannot reduce visibility
```

**Rule 3: Covariant Return Types Allowed**
- The return type can be a **subtype** of the original.
- *Valid Override:*
```java
class A { public Object get() { return null; } }
class B extends A { @Override public String get() { return "Hello"; } }  // VALID (String is-a Object)
```

**Rule 4: Static, Final, and Private Methods Cannot Be Overridden**
- *Violation:*
```java
class A { public static void display() {} }
class B extends A { public static void display() {} }  // Method hiding, NOT overriding
```

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating method overriding with **three levels** of inheritance (grandparent → parent → child). Explain the order in which `super.methodName()` calls resolve up the chain. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Program:**
```java
class Grandparent {
    public void message() {
        System.out.println("Message from Grandparent");
    }
}

class Parent extends Grandparent {
    @Override
    public void message() {
        super.message();  // Calls Grandparent's message()
        System.out.println("Message from Parent");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    @Override
    public void message() {
        super.message();  // Calls Parent's message()
        System.out.println("Message from Child");
    }
}

public class ChainDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
        c.message();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Message from Grandparent
Message from Parent
Message from Child
```

**Resolution Order Explanation:**

1. `Child.message()` is invoked first.
2. `super.message()` inside `Child` refers to the **immediate parent** (`Parent`), so `Parent.message()` executes next.
3. Inside `Parent.message()`, `super.message()` refers to **its** parent (`Grandparent`), so `Grandparent.message()` executes.
4. `Grandparent` has no super call, so execution unwinds back to `Parent`, which prints its own statement, and then to `Child`.
5. The output appears in **top-down hierarchical order**: Grandparent → Parent → Child.
6. This demonstrates that `super` always refers to the **direct parent** in the inheritance chain, not the topmost ancestor.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Method Overriding Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding** — Changing the parameter type makes it an *overload*, not an *override*. Examiners frequently deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** — While not mandatory in Java, its absence in answers often signals to the examiner that the student may not have actually tested the code. Use it consistently.
> 3. **Wrong Access Modifier Direction** — Writing `private` to override a `public` method is a classic trap. Remember: *you can widen visibility, never narrow it*.
> 4. **Trying to Override `static` or `final` Methods** — Many students write code attempting to override these and fail to mention that it's *illegal*. Always explicitly state *why* it's illegal.
> 5. **Misusing `super`** — Writing `super.super.method()` (chained super calls) is **not allowed in Java**. Only the direct parent's method can be accessed via `super`.
> 6. **Ignoring Exception Handling Rules** — The overriding method cannot throw *new or broader* checked exceptions than the parent. This is a frequent 2-mark short-answer topic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Method Overriding = Runtime Polymorphism.** The method to invoke is decided at runtime based on the actual object type, not the reference variable type.
- **Mandatory Conditions:** Same method name + identical parameter list + same or covariant return type + access modifier same or wider.
- **`super` Keyword** is used inside the subclass to call the **parent's overridden method** — useful for extending rather than replacing behavior.
- **Cannot Override:** `static` (method hiding instead), `final` (locked by parent), `private` (not inherited), and **constructors**.
- **Covariant Return Types** allow the subclass method to return a **subtype** of the parent's return type (since Java 5).
- **Virtual Method Table (vtable)** is the internal JVM data structure that enables dynamic method dispatch.
- **Difference from Overloading:** Overloading happens at **compile time** within the same class; Overriding happens at **runtime** across inheritance.
- **Use Cases:** Implementing polymorphic behavior, designing framework hooks, applying the Open/Closed Principle, customizing inherited APIs.
- **Best Practice:** Always use the `@Override` annotation to catch signature mismatches at compile time.
- **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP):** Any place the parent class is expected, a subclass instance must work seamlessly — overriding must preserve this contract.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Classes and Methods
Using final with Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using final with Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
In the Java programming language, the `final` keyword is a **non-access modifier** that places a permanent lock on entities such as classes, methods, and variables. In the context of inheritance, `final` is used to **prohibit further extension (subclassing) or method overriding**, thereby enforcing immutability, design contracts, and security guarantees at the class-hierarchy level.

When applied with inheritance, `final` operates at three distinct structural layers of an Object-Oriented Program:

1. **`final` Class** — A class declared as `final` cannot be inherited. No subclass can extend it.
2. **`final` Method** — A method declared as `final` cannot be overridden by any subclass, although it can be inherited normally.
3. **`final` Variable** — A variable declared as `final` becomes a *constant* whose value cannot be reassigned after initialization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Inheritance):**
> The `final` keyword is an integral part of inheritance control in Java. It enables a designer to "seal" certain parts of the class hierarchy to prevent unauthorized extension or behavioural modification — a key design pillar in the JDK (e.g., `java.lang.String` is a `final` class).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the `final` keyword as a **"Sealed Envelope"** in a bureaucratic organization:

- Imagine a **top-secret blueprint** (a `final` class). Once sealed, no engineer in the company is permitted to create a derivative prototype from it. The blueprint remains untouched forever — its design is **final**.
- Now consider a **specific instruction inside that blueprint** (a `final` method). Even if a junior engineer inherits the entire blueprint, they are **forbidden from rewriting** that one specific instruction. They may use it as-is, but they cannot modify it.
- Finally, picture a **fixed serial number stamped on the blueprint** (a `final` variable). Once stamped during creation, the number is permanent — no one can overwrite it later.

In short: `final` means **"No Further Changes Allowed — Design is Locked."**

### 1.3 Standard Java Metrics & Behavioural Constants

| Entity | Behaviour | Common JDK Example |
|---|---|---|
| `final` Class | Cannot be extended (no subclassing) | `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.Math` |
| `final` Method | Cannot be overridden (can be inherited & overloaded) | `Object.getClass()` |
| `final` Variable | Cannot be reassigned (must be initialized) | `final int MAX = 100;` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU exams:** Questions on `final` typically test whether a student understands the *direction of restriction* — i.e., whether the lock applies vertically (class extension) or horizontally (method overriding) or locally (variable reassignment). Confusing these three directions is the single biggest source of lost marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Modes of `final`

#### 2.1.1 `final` Class — Vertical Lock (Inheritance Direction)
When a class is declared `final`, the Java compiler **disallows any `extends` clause** that references it. The class becomes the **terminal node** (leaf) of the inheritance hierarchy.

**Syntax:**
```java
final class SecurityToken {
    // class body
}

// The following line causes a compile-time error:
// class EnhancedToken extends SecurityToken { }   // ❌ Cannot inherit from final class
```

**Why this exists (Engineering Rationale):**
- **Security:** Prevents malicious subclasses from altering sensitive behaviour (e.g., `String` is `final` to prevent tampering with string-pool behaviour).
- **Immutability Guarantee:** Combined with `private` fields, it produces truly immutable objects (a cornerstone of functional & concurrent programming).
- **Design Sealing:** Communicates to other developers that this class is complete and not meant for extension.

#### 2.1.2 `final` Method — Horizontal Lock (Overriding Direction)
When a method is declared `final`, it can still be **inherited** by subclasses, but it **cannot be overridden**. The implementation is permanently fixed at the level where `final` is declared.

**Syntax:**
```java
class PaymentGateway {
    public final void validateTransaction() {
        // core security logic — cannot be changed
    }
}

class SubGateway extends PaymentGateway {
    // The following method causes a compile-time error:
    // public void validateTransaction() { }   // ❌ Cannot override final method
}
```

**Engineering Rationale:**
- Used in the **Template Method Pattern** to lock the algorithmic skeleton while allowing subclasses to override specific steps.
- The JVM may perform **inlining optimizations** on `final` methods (though not guaranteed in modern JIT compilers).

#### 2.1.3 `final` Variable — Local Lock (Reassignment Direction)
A `final` variable is a **compile-time constant** (if initialized with a literal) or a **blank final** (if initialized later). Either way, **reassignment is forbidden**.

**Three Sub-Categories:**

| Sub-Type | Initialization Rule |
|---|---|
| **Static Final Constant** | Must be initialized in a `static` block or at declaration |
| **Instance Final Variable** | Must be initialized in every constructor or at declaration |
| **Final Parameter** | Cannot be reassigned inside the method body |
| **Blank Final Variable** | A `final` variable not initialized at declaration; must be assigned exactly once in constructor/initializer |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Declaration | Restriction | Direction | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Final Class | `final class A { }` | Cannot use `extends A` | Vertical (Inheritance) | Forgetting that nested classes can still be inherited inside a `final` outer class |
| Final Method | `final void m() { }` | Cannot override `m()` in subclass | Horizontal (Polymorphism) | `final` does NOT prevent method **overloading** |
| Final Variable | `final int x = 5;` | Cannot reassign `x` | Local (Reassignment) | Blank `final` must be assigned exactly once |
| Final Parameter | `void m(final int x) { }` | Cannot reassign parameter | Local (Reassignment) | Often confused with method-level scope |
| Static Final | `static final double PI = 3.14;` | True compile-time constant | Class-level | Convention: Name in `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:**
> - `final` method ≠ `private` method. A `private` method is implicitly `final` because it cannot be accessed by subclass, but it is not the same declaration.
> - `final` class ≠ `abstract` class. These are mutually exclusive — a class cannot be both `final` and `abstract`.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Banking Systems:** `final` is used on transaction-validation methods to prevent subclasses from bypassing security checks.
- **Cryptography Libraries:** Classes like `java.security.MessageDigest` use `final` to lock the algorithm.
- **String Pool Optimization:** `String` is `final` so the JVM can safely intern strings and share references.
- **API Versioning:** Frameworks use `final` on framework-extension hooks to maintain backward compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1: Final Class Behaviour

**Problem Context:** Demonstrate that a `final` class cannot be extended and identify the compile-time error produced.

**Step 1 — Declare a final class:**
```java
final class Vehicle {
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle started");
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Attempt to extend the final class:**
```java
class Car extends Vehicle {   // ❌ COMPILE-TIME ERROR
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Car started");
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Compiler Diagnostic Output:**
```
error: cannot inherit from final class Vehicle
    class Car extends Vehicle {
          ^
1 error
```

**Logical Derivation:**
$$
\text{If } C \text{ is declared } \texttt{final} \implies \forall S : S \text{ extends } C \text{ is invalid at compile-time}
$$

The compiler rejects the inheritance relationship at the **type-checking phase**, before any bytecode is generated. This is a **static (compile-time) restriction**, not a runtime check.

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2: Final Method Behaviour

**Step 1 — Declare a class with a final method:**
```java
class BankAccount {
    final void calculateInterest() {
        System.out.println("Standard interest calculation logic");
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Inherit and attempt to override the final method:**
```java
class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    void calculateInterest() {   // ❌ COMPILE-TIME ERROR
        System.out.println("Custom interest calculation");
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Compiler Diagnostic Output:**
```
error: calculateInterest() in SavingsAccount cannot override calculateInterest() in BankAccount
  void calculateInterest() {
       ^
  overridden method is final
1 error
```

**Logical Derivation:**
$$
\text{If } m \text{ is declared } \texttt{final} \text{ in } C \implies \forall S \subseteq C : S.m() \text{ override is invalid}
$$

**Important:** The subclass `SavingsAccount` can still **inherit** `calculateInterest()` and **call** it, but cannot **redefine** its body.

### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 3: Final Variable Behaviour

**Step 1 — Declare and test a final variable:**
```java
class Circle {
    final double PI = 3.14159;
    
    void modify() {
        PI = 3.14;   // ❌ COMPILE-TIME ERROR
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Compiler Diagnostic Output:**
```
error: cannot assign a value to final variable PI
        PI = 3.14;
        ^
1 error
```

**Logical Derivation for Blank Final Variable:**
$$
\text{If } v \text{ is a blank final} \implies v \text{ must be assigned exactly once, before use}
$$

**Example of Blank Final:**
```java
class Employee {
    final String employeeId;   // blank final — not initialized at declaration
    
    Employee(String id) {
        employeeId = id;       // ✅ Assigned exactly once in constructor
    }
    
    Employee() {
        // ❌ If we never assign employeeId, compile error: "variable employeeId might not have been initialized"
    }
}
```

### 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Scenario | Code Snippet | Result | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extend final class | `class B extends final A` | ❌ Compile Error | Vertical lock on inheritance |
| Override final method | `void m() override final m()` | ❌ Compile Error | Horizontal lock on polymorphism |
| Reassign final variable | `final int x = 5; x = 10;` | ❌ Compile Error | Local lock on reassignment |
| Inherit final method | Call `super.finalMethod()` | ✅ Allowed | Final ≠ Private; still accessible |
| Overload final method | Same name, different params | ✅ Allowed | `final` does not affect overloading |
| Hide final static method | `static final m()` vs `static m()` | ❌ Compile Error | Static hiding also blocked |
| Construct final class | `new FinalClass()` | ✅ Allowed | `final` only restricts inheritance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Final Class Blocking Inheritance (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Object Root Class] --> nodeB[Vehicle Final Class]
    nodeC[Car Attempted Subclass] -.->|X BLOCKED| nodeB
    nodeD[Truck Attempted Subclass] -.->|X BLOCKED| nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeE[Vehicle Instance Allowed]
    style nodeB fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeC fill:#ecf0f1,stroke:#7f8c8d,color:#2c3e50
    style nodeD fill:#ecf0f1,stroke:#7f8c8d,color:#2c3e50
```

**Interpretation:** The red node `Vehicle` is sealed. Both `Car` and `Truck` dashed arrows represent failed extension attempts blocked at compile-time.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Final Method Blocking Override (Polymorphism Lock)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP[Parent Class] --> nodeM1[Final Method ValidateTransaction]
    nodeP --> nodeM2[Regular Method Display]
    nodeC[Child Class] --> nodeM3[Inherited Final Method]
    nodeC --> nodeM4[Overridden Display]
    nodeC -.->|X BLOCKED OVERRIDE| nodeM1
    style nodeM1 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeM3 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#27ae60,color:#1b4332
    style nodeM4 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#2874a6,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** The child can **inherit** (green node) the final method but cannot **redefine** it. The dashed red arrow shows the blocked override. Other non-final methods like `Display` can be freely overridden (blue node).

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Final Variable Lock Direction (Reassignment Prevention)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeD[Declaration final int MAX 100] --> nodeA[Assignment Allowed Once]
    nodeA --> nodeR[Reassignment Blocked]
    nodeR -.->|X COMPILE ERROR| nodeD
    style nodeD fill:#3498db,stroke:#1f618d,color:#ffffff
    style nodeA fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#27ae60,color:#1b4332
    style nodeR fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** A `final` variable flows from declaration → single assignment → permanent locked state. Any attempt to reassign loops back to a compile error.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the purpose of the `final` keyword when applied to a class in Java? Provide one real-world example from the Java Standard Library.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

When the `final` keyword is applied to a class declaration, it prevents the class from being inherited by any subclass. This means no other class can use the `extends` keyword with a `final` class as its parent. The class becomes the terminal (leaf) node of the inheritance hierarchy, ensuring that its implementation cannot be altered through subclassing.

The primary purposes are:
1. **Security:** Prevents malicious or accidental modification of sensitive class behaviour through subclassing.
2. **Immutability:** Ensures the object's state and behaviour remain unchanged, which is critical for thread-safety and caching optimizations.
3. **Design Finality:** Communicates to other developers that the class is complete and not intended for extension.

**Real-World Example:** `java.lang.String` is a `final` class. If `String` were not `final`, a subclass could override its `equals()` or `hashCode()` methods, breaking the fundamental contract of string comparison and corrupting the String Constant Pool used by the JVM. Making `String` `final` guarantees predictable, secure, and efficient string handling across the entire Java ecosystem.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a `final` method and a `private` method in Java with respect to inheritance. Can a `final` method be overloaded?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `final` Method | `private` Method |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | Can be inherited and called by subclass | Cannot be inherited or accessed by subclass |
| Overriding | Explicitly forbidden by the compiler | Implicitly impossible (since subclass cannot see it) |
| Access from Subclass | Accessible via direct call or super reference | Not accessible at all |
| Polymorphic Dispatch | Participates in dynamic dispatch if not static | Not applicable (private methods are not virtual) |

**Yes, a `final` method can be overloaded.** The `final` modifier restricts **overriding** (changing the implementation in a subclass), but it does not restrict **overloading** (defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists within the same class).

Example:
```java
class Calculator {
    final int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    final double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }   // ✅ Valid overloading
}
```

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the three uses of the `final` keyword in Java with appropriate syntax and code examples. Discuss why the `String` class in Java is declared as `final`. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of the `final` keyword with:
   - A `final` class and show that it cannot be inherited.
   - A `final` method and show that it cannot be overridden.
   - A blank `final` variable initialized inside a constructor.

Provide the complete program along with the expected compile-time errors for the invalid cases. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Three Uses of `final` Keyword — Detailed Explanation**

The `final` keyword in Java is a non-access modifier used to apply restrictions at three structural levels:

**Use 1 — `final` Variable (Local Lock):**
A variable declared `final` becomes a constant. It must be initialized exactly once and cannot be reassigned. If it is a class-level constant, it is conventionally named in `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE`.

**Use 2 — `final` Method (Horizontal Lock):**
A method declared `final` cannot be overridden by any subclass. This is used to lock critical algorithmic steps in the Template Method Pattern.

**Use 3 — `final` Class (Vertical Lock):**
A class declared `final` cannot be extended. This prevents subclass-based modification of its behaviour.

**Why `String` is `final`:**
The `java.lang.String` class is declared `final` for three critical engineering reasons:
- **Security:** String values are used in class loading, network connections, file paths, and database queries. If `String` were extensible, a malicious subclass could alter `equals()` or `hashCode()`, leading to security vulnerabilities.
- **String Pool Optimization:** The JVM maintains a String Constant Pool that interns string literals. If `String` were mutable through subclassing, shared references in the pool could be modified, breaking immutability guarantees.
- **Caching Hashcode:** `String` caches its `hashCode()` after first computation. This caching is safe only because `String` cannot be subclassed to alter the character content.

**(b) Complete Java Demonstration Program:**

```java
// File: FinalKeywordDemo.java

// 1. Final Class Demonstration
final class ImmutableConfig {
    void showConfig() {
        System.out.println("Configuration loaded.");
    }
}

// ❌ The following class declaration would cause a COMPILE ERROR:
// class ExtendedConfig extends ImmutableConfig {
//     void showConfig() {
//         System.out.println("Modified configuration.");
//     }
// }
// Compile Error: "cannot inherit from final class ImmutableConfig"

// 2. Final Method Demonstration
class PaymentProcessor {
    final void authenticateTransaction() {
        System.out.println("Standard authentication applied.");
    }
    
    void logTransaction() {
        System.out.println("Transaction logged.");
    }
}

class PremiumProcessor extends PaymentProcessor {
    // ❌ The following override would cause a COMPILE ERROR:
    // void authenticateTransaction() {
    //     System.out.println("Custom authentication applied.");
    // }
    // Compile Error: "cannot override the final method from PaymentProcessor"
    
    // ✅ Valid override of non-final method
    @Override
    void logTransaction() {
        System.out.println("Premium transaction logged with priority.");
    }
}

// 3. Blank Final Variable Demonstration
class BankCustomer {
    final String customerId;        // blank final — not initialized at declaration
    final String accountType;       // blank final
    
    BankCustomer(String id, String type) {
        customerId = id;            // ✅ Assigned once in constructor
        accountType = type;         // ✅ Assigned once in constructor
    }
    
    void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Customer ID: " + customerId);
        System.out.println("Account Type: " + accountType);
        
        // ❌ The following reassignment would cause a COMPILE ERROR:
        // customerId = "NEW123";
        // Compile Error: "cannot assign a value to final variable customerId"
    }
}

// Main class to run the demonstration
public class FinalKeywordDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Final class instantiation (allowed; only inheritance is blocked)
        ImmutableConfig config = new ImmutableConfig();
        config.showConfig();
        
        // Final method demonstration
        PremiumProcessor processor = new PremiumProcessor();
        processor.authenticateTransaction();  // Inherited, not overridden
        processor.logTransaction();           // Overridden
        
        // Blank final variable demonstration
        BankCustomer customer = new BankCustomer("CUST001", "Savings");
        customer.displayDetails();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Configuration loaded.
Standard authentication applied.
Premium transaction logged with priority.
Customer ID: CUST001
Account Type: Savings
```

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **blank final variables** in Java. Explain the rules governing their initialization with a suitable example. Differentiate between a blank final variable and a static final variable. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following scenario: A banking application uses a base class `Account` with a `final` method `calculateInterest()`. A derived class `FixedDepositAccount` needs to use the same interest-calculation logic but should not be allowed to modify it. Design and implement this hierarchy in Java. Justify why `final` is the correct design choice here. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Blank Final Variables — Concept and Rules:**

A **blank final variable** in Java is a `final` variable that is **declared but not initialized at the point of declaration**. Unlike a regular `final` variable (which must be initialized immediately), a blank final variable defers initialization but is still subject to the "assign exactly once" rule.

**Governing Rules:**
1. A blank `final` instance variable must be assigned in **every constructor** of the class, or in an **instance initializer block**, before the constructor completes.
2. A blank `final` static variable must be assigned in a **static initializer block**, since static constructors don't exist in Java.
3. Once assigned, the blank final variable behaves exactly like a regular `final` variable — it cannot be reassigned.

**Example:**
```java
class Student {
    final String rollNumber;        // blank final
    final String name;              // blank final
    
    Student(String roll, String nm) {
        rollNumber = roll;
        name = nm;
    }
    
    void updateName(String newName) {
        // name = newName;   // ❌ Compile error — already assigned
    }
}
```

**Difference between Blank Final and Static Final:**

| Aspect | Blank Final (Instance) | Static Final |
|---|---|---|
| Belongs to | Object instance | Class itself |
| Number of Copies | One per object | One per class |
| Initialization | In constructor or instance block | In static block or at declaration |
| Memory Allocation | Heap (per object) | Method Area (class-level) |
| Access | Through object reference | Through class name |

**(b) Banking Hierarchy Design with Final Method:**

```java
// Base class with final interest-calculation logic
class Account {
    private double principal;
    private double rate;
    private int years;
    
    Account(double p, double r, int y) {
        principal = p;
        rate = r;
        years = y;
    }
    
    // Final method — locked algorithm
    public final double calculateInterest() {
        return (principal * rate * years) / 100.0;
    }
    
    public void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Principal: ₹" + principal);
        System.out.println("Interest: ₹" + calculateInterest());
    }
}

// Derived class — can use but not modify the final method
class FixedDepositAccount extends Account {
    private int tenure;
    
    FixedDepositAccount(double p, double r, int y, int t) {
        super(p, r, y);
        tenure = t;
    }
    
    // ❌ INVALID — cannot override the final method
    // public double calculateInterest() {
    //     return principal * rate * tenure;   // Compile error
    // }
    
    @Override
    public void displayDetails() {
        super.displayDetails();
        System.out.println("FD Tenure: " + tenure + " years");
    }
}

// Main demonstration class
public class BankingApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FixedDepositAccount fd = new FixedDepositAccount(100000, 6.5, 5, 5);
        fd.displayDetails();
        
        double interest = fd.calculateInterest();   // ✅ Inherited call works
        System.out.println("Calculated Interest: ₹" + interest);
    }
}
```

**Justification for Using `final`:**

The `calculateInterest()` method is declared `final` because:

1. **Regulatory Compliance:** Banking interest calculations are governed by RBI regulations. Allowing subclasses to override the formula could lead to non-compliant behaviour. `final` enforces a single, auditable formula across all account types.

2. **Consistency Guarantee:** All account subclasses — Savings, Fixed Deposit, Recurring Deposit — must use the *exact same* formula. If overriding were allowed, different subclasses could compute different interest for the same inputs, breaking financial consistency.

3. **Security:** A malicious subclass could override the method to return artificially low interest values, defrauding customers. `final` prevents this attack vector at compile-time.

4. **Maintainability:** If the formula needs to change, it is changed in **one place only** (the base class). Subclasses automatically inherit the updated logic without any code duplication or risk of inconsistency.

5. **Template Method Pattern:** This is a textbook example of the Template Method design pattern, where the algorithm skeleton is fixed (`final`) but specific steps (like `displayDetails()`) can be customized in subclasses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse `final` with `finally` or `finalize()`** — these are three completely different constructs. `final` is a modifier, `finally` is a block used with exception handling, and `finalize()` is an `Object` class method called by the garbage collector.
> 2. **`final` method can still be OVERLOADED** — many students incorrectly write that `final` prevents overloading. It only prevents OVERRIDING.
> 3. **A `final` class can still have its instances created** — students often think `final` class means it cannot be instantiated. Only inheritance is blocked, not instantiation.
> 4. **Blank `final` variables are NOT the same as `static final` constants** — initialization rules differ significantly.
> 5. **`private` methods are implicitly `final`** but the keyword is not written — students should explicitly mention this distinction in theory answers.
> 6. **Always show the compile-time error message** in code-based answers — KTU examiners award marks for demonstrating that you understand *why* the code fails, not just *that* it fails.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `final` is a **non-access modifier** in Java used to enforce restrictions on classes, methods, and variables.
- ✅ A **`final` class** cannot be inherited (no subclassing). Example: `java.lang.String`.
- ✅ A **`final` method** cannot be overridden but CAN be inherited and CAN be overloaded.
- ✅ A **`final` variable** must be initialized exactly once and cannot be reassigned.
- ✅ A **blank final variable** is declared without initialization but must be assigned in every constructor.
- ✅ A **`final` parameter** cannot be reassigned inside the method body.
- ✅ `final` does **NOT** affect method overloading, only overriding.
- ✅ `final` class can be **instantiated**; only extension is blocked.
- ✅ `final` and `abstract` are **mutually exclusive** — a class cannot be both.
- ✅ `private` methods are implicitly `final`, but the keyword is conventionally not written.
- ✅ Common JDK examples of `final`: `String`, `Math`, all wrapper classes (`Integer`, `Double`, etc.).
- ✅ The **Template Method Design Pattern** relies heavily on `final` methods.
- ✅ For KTU exams: always pair code examples with **compile-time error messages** to demonstrate full understanding.
- ✅ Remember the **direction of lock**: Vertical (Class) → Horizontal (Method) → Local (Variable).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packages and Interfaces - Defining Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packages and Interfaces - Defining Package

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📦 Packages and Interfaces — Defining a Package

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PEEIT741 / Module 3)**
> This topic falls under *Module 3: Packages and Interfaces*. Students are expected to understand the **conceptual definition**, **syntax for creating**, **naming conventions**, **CLASSPATH mechanism**, and the **access control implications** of packages in Java, as it lays the foundation for modular, large-scale OOP software design.

---

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

A **package** in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related **classes**, **interfaces**, **enumerations**, and **sub-packages**. Mechanically, a package corresponds to a **directory hierarchy** in the underlying file system, where the package name maps directly to the folder path, separated by dots (`.`).

In formal KTU / JLS-aligned terms:

> *"A package is a grouping of related types providing access protection and namespace management. It is declared using the `package` keyword as the first statement in a source file."*

The general syntax to **define a package** is:

```java
package <package_name>;
```

This statement **must be the very first non-comment, non-whitespace line** in the Java source file.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Library Building" Model 🏢

Imagine a massive university library:

| Real-World Element | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The whole library building | Java's entire class library (JDK) |
| Different floors (CS, Mechanical, Civil) | Different **packages** (`java.util`, `java.io`, `java.awt`) |
| Each floor's catalog | **Interfaces and classes** within the package |
| Specific bookshelves on a floor | **Sub-packages** (e.g., `javax.swing` inside `javax`) |
| A visitor's library pass | **CLASSPATH / import statement** |
| Restricted access sections | **Access modifiers** (`public`, `protected`, default) |

> [!TIP]
> **Without packages**, it would be like throwing every book in the library into one giant pile — finding anything would be chaos! Packages give us **structure**, **organization**, and **controlled access**, exactly like labeled floors in a building.

---

## 1.3 Why Packages? The Three Pillars

1. **Namespace Management** — Prevents class name collisions. Two programmers can both define a class called `List` as long as one is in `mypkg.utils` and the other in `vendor.lib`.
2. **Access Control** — Provides visibility boundaries using access modifiers coupled with package-level default visibility.
3. **Reusability & Modularity** — Logical grouping of related types, enabling easier maintenance and code distribution (e.g., JAR files map 1:1 to packages).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Why do we need packages?"*, always list these **three benefits** explicitly with one-line justification for each. Examiners award marks for each distinct benefit mentioned.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Defining & Working with Packages

---

## 2.1 The `package` Statement — Rules of Engagement

The package declaration in Java is governed by **strict lexical rules** enforced by the Java compiler (`javac`):

1. **Position Rule:** The `package` statement must appear **before any import or type declaration**. It must be the **first Java statement** in the file.
2. **Uniqueness Rule:** A source file can declare **only one package** it belongs to. Multiple `package` declarations cause a compile-time error.
3. **Naming Rule:** Package names are written in **all lowercase** by convention to avoid conflict with class names (which begin with uppercase).
4. **Hierarchy Rule:** Package names use **dot-separated identifiers** that mirror the directory structure on disk.
5. **Optional Rule:** If no `package` statement is provided, the class resides in the **default (unnamed) package**.

### Syntax Template

```java
package <top_level_package>.<sub_package>.<sub_sub_package>;

public class MyClass {
    // class body
}
```

---

## 2.2 Hierarchical Directory Mapping

The Java compiler **physically enforces** that the package name mirrors the folder structure:

| Package Declaration | Required Directory Structure |
|---|---|
| `package mypkg;` | `.../mypkg/MyClass.java` |
| `package com.ktu.oop.module3;` | `.../com/ktu/oop/module3/MyClass.java` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often declare `package ktu.oop;` but save the file in `ktu/Module3/`. The compiler will reject it with:
> `error: cannot find symbol` or `class MyClass is not in the package ktu.oop`.

---

## 2.3 Reverse Domain Naming Convention (Industry Standard)

For **uniquely identifying** packages across organizations, Java follows the **reverse Internet domain naming convention**:

$$\text{Package Name} = \text{Reverse Domain} + \text{Organization Specific Path}$$

For example, KTU's domain `ktu.edu.in` becomes:

```java
package in.edu.ktu.oop.module3.assignment;
```

This guarantees global uniqueness — no two organizations will accidentally collide on package names.

---

## 2.4 The CLASSPATH Variable — The Address Finder 🗺️

**CLASSPATH** is an **environment variable** (or command-line argument) that tells the **JVM and Java compiler** where to **search for user-defined packages** (the JDK's `rt.jar` and standard libraries are automatically available).

### Setting CLASSPATH

**Windows:**
```cmd
set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myprojects\libs
```

**Linux / macOS:**
```bash
export CLASSPATH=.:/home/student/myprojects/libs
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dot (`.`) at the beginning represents the **current directory** and is **mandatory** to access classes in your working folder. Without it, you will get `NoClassDefFoundError` even if the class file is right there!

### Compiling & Running with Packages

**Compile** a class in a package from the **root** directory:
```bash
javac -d . MyClass.java
```
The `-d .` flag tells the compiler to place the generated `.class` file in the appropriate sub-directory based on its package.

**Run** the class using its Fully Qualified Name (FQN):
```bash
java com.ktu.oop.module3.MyClass
```

---

## 2.5 Package-Level Access (Default Access Modifier)

When no access modifier is specified, Java applies **package-private (default)** access:

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Diff Pkg) | Other Packages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *default* (no modifier) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (via inheritance) | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequent Question:** *"Explain the access control provided by packages."* — Always draw this table or write the four-tier hierarchy explicitly. Full marks require all four tiers.

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Defining Packages

| Concept | Rule / Syntax | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Package declaration | `package name.subname;` | Must be **first** statement |
| Default package | (no `package` statement) | Cannot be imported |
| Sub-package syntax | `package a.b.c;` → folder `a/b/c/` | Dots = directory separator |
| CLASSPATH format | `.:/path/to/libs` (Linux) | `.` = current dir mandatory |
| Compile flag | `javac -d . File.java` | `-d` = destination directory |
| Run command | `java pkg.subpkg.ClassName` | Use FQN, **not** file path |
| Naming convention | reverse-DNS, all lowercase | e.g., `in.edu.ktu.cs` |
| Access (default) | Same package only | No `public` keyword needed |
| Import package | `import pkg.subpkg.*;` | `*` = all types in package |
| Static import | `import static pkg.ClassName.member;` | Since Java 5 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples

This section walks through the **complete mechanics** of defining, compiling, and using a package — exactly as a KTU lab exam or ESE would expect.

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Creating a Custom Package

### Step 1 — Decide the Package Name

We want a package for **KTU OOP Module 3 utilities**:
$$\text{Package Name} = \texttt{in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils}$$

### Step 2 — Create the Directory Structure

From your project's **root folder** (let's call it `MyProject`):

```text
MyProject/
└── in/
    └── edu/
        └── ktu/
            └── oop/
                └── m3/
                    └── utils/
```

### Step 3 — Write the Class with Package Declaration

Save the following file as `MyProject/in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utils/Calculator.java`:

```java
package in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils;

public class Calculator {

    public int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    public int multiply(int a, int b) {
```
