```java
        return a * b;
    }
}
```

### Step 4 — Compile with `-d` flag

Open the terminal in the **root** folder `MyProject/`:

```bash
javac -d . in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utils/Calculator.java
```

**Logic behind the command:**

- `javac` → invokes the Java compiler.
- `-d .` → **destination** is the current directory; the compiler auto-creates `in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utils/` and drops the `.class` file there.
- The full source path is required so the compiler can locate the file.

After compilation, the folder becomes:
```text
MyProject/
└── in/
    └── edu/
        └── ktu/
            └── oop/
                └── m3/
                    └── utils/
                        ├── Calculator.java
                        └── Calculator.class   ← NEW
```

### Step 5 — Use the Package from a Different Class

Now create a **client class** `MyProject/TestPackage.java`:

```java
import in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils.Calculator;

public class TestPackage {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator calc = new Calculator();

        int sum = calc.add(10, 20);
        int product = calc.multiply(5, 4);

        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
        System.out.println("Product = " + product);
    }
}
```

### Step 6 — Compile and Run the Client

```bash
javac TestPackage.java
java TestPackage
```

**Output:**
```text
Sum = 30
Product = 20
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that we did **NOT** run from inside the `in/edu/ktu/...` subfolder. The JVM uses the **CLASSPATH** (which by default contains `.` = current directory) to locate `in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utils/Calculator.class` by following the dot-separated package name as a folder path.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: `import` vs Fully Qualified Name (FQN)

Consider two equivalent ways to use a packaged class:

| Approach | Syntax | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| `import` statement | `import in.edu.ktu...Calculator;` | Cleaner code, reusable | Possible name conflict |
| Fully Qualified Name (FQN) | `in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils.Calculator calc = new in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils.Calculator();` | No ambiguity, no import needed | Verbose, hard to read |
| Wildcard import | `import in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils.*;` | Imports all types of package | Increases compile time, may shadow names |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Best Practice:** Use **specific single-type imports** (option 1). Wildcard imports are discouraged in production code by the official Java Code Conventions.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Package Access Protection

Let's mathematically analyze visibility. Define:

$$V(c, p) = \text{visibility of class } c \text{ from package } p$$

Given four access modifiers $M = \{ \text{private}, \text{default}, \text{protected}, \text{public} \}$, the visibility function expands as:

$$
V(c, p) =
\begin{cases}
\text{true}, & \text{if } M(c) = \text{public} \\
\text{true}, & \text{if } M(c) = \text{default and } p = P(c) \\
\text{true}, & \text{if } M(c) = \text{protected and } (p = P(c) \text{ or } c \text{ inherited}) \\
\text{true}, & \text{if } M(c) = \text{private and } c \text{ is inner class of accessing class} \\
\text{false}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $P(c)$ is the **owning package** of class $c$.

### Demonstration with Two Packages

**Package A:** `in.edu.ktu.pkgA`
```java
package in.edu.ktu.pkgA;

class DefaultClass {           // default access
    void display() {           // default access method
        System.out.println("Hello from DefaultClass");
    }
}

public class PublicClass {
    public void show() {
        System.out.println("Hello from PublicClass");
    }
}
```

**Package B:** `in.edu.ktu.pkgB`
```java
package in.edu.ktu.pkgB;
import in.edu.ktu.pkgA.*;

public class TestAccess {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PublicClass obj = new PublicClass();
        obj.show();                    // ✅ WORKS — public class & method

        // DefaultClass d = new DefaultClass();
        // d.display();                 // ❌ ERROR — not public
    }
}
```

**Compiler Errors Generated:**
1. `DefaultClass` is not public in `in.edu.ktu.pkgA`; cannot be accessed from outside package.
2. `display()` is not public in `DefaultClass`; cannot be accessed from outside package.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting that **`class` declarations also need `public`** to be visible across packages. Students often make methods public but keep the class default — which makes the whole class invisible!

---

## 3.4 Compilation Flow Algorithm

The complete logic the JVM/compiler follows when resolving a class:

$$
\text{Resolve}(className) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Search CLASSPATH} \to \text{find } className.class & \text{if imported/FQN} \\
\text{Search default package (current dir)} & \text{if no package declared} \\
\text{Search } \texttt{java.lang} \text{ implicitly} & \text{always} \\
\text{Throw } \texttt{ClassNotFoundException} & \text{if not found}
\end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Package Definition & Directory Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Root Directory: MyProject"] --> B["Folder: in"]
    B --> C["Folder: edu"]
    C --> D["Folder: ktu"]
    D --> E["Folder: oop"]
    E --> F["Folder: m3"]
    F --> G["Folder: utils"]
    G --> H["File: Calculator.java with package declaration"]
    G --> I["File: Calculator.class compiled output"]

    J["Source Code Top Line"] --> K["package in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils;"]
    K --> L["Maps 1-to-1 with folder hierarchy"]

    M["javac -d . Compiler Flag"] --> N["Auto-creates folder structure"]
    N --> G

    O["CLASSPATH environment variable"] --> P["Tells JVM where to search for .class files"]
    P --> I

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#16a34a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#ea580c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style K fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Package Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Java Source File"]:::src --> PKG["package statement parser"]:::proc
    PKG --> COMP["javac compiler with -d flag"]:::proc
    COMP --> FS["File System: Creates matching directory tree"]:::fs
    FS --> CLASS["Generates .class file in correct folder"]:::out

    CLIENT["Client Class"]:::src --> IMP["import statement or FQN"]:::proc
    IMP --> CP["CLASSPATH lookup"]:::proc
    CP --> RES{"Class found?"}:::decision
    RES -->|Yes| LOAD["Load bytecode into JVM"]:::out
    RES -->|No| ERR["Throws ClassNotFoundException"]:::err
    LOAD --> RUN["Execute main method"]:::out

    classDef src fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef fs fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#10b981,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#ef4444,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Access Control Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PUB["PUBLIC: Visible everywhere"]:::pub
    PRO["PROTECTED: Same package OR subclass in any package"]:::pro
    DEF["DEFAULT no keyword: Same package only"]:::def
    PRI["PRIVATE: Same class only"]:::pri

    PUB --> PRO
    PRO --> DEF
    DEF --> PRI

    PUB -.-> E1["Cross-package classes can use freely"]:::ex
    PRO -.-> E2["Used in inheritance across packages"]:::ex
    DEF -.-> E3["Default package-private restriction"]:::ex
    PRI -.-> E4["Encapsulation: data hiding principle"]:::ex

    classDef pub fill:#16a34a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef pro fill:#0891b2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef def fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef pri fill:#dc2626,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef ex fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Package Compilation & Execution Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Term as Terminal
    participant Comp as javac Compiler
    participant FS as File System
    participant JVM as Java Runtime

    Dev->>Term: Write Calculator.java with package declaration
    Dev->>Term: Execute: javac -d . Calculator.java
    Term->>Comp: Invoke compiler
    Comp->>FS: Create in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utils/ directory
    Comp->>FS: Generate Calculator.class
    FS-->>Comp: File saved
    Comp-->>Term: Compilation successful

    Dev->>Term: Execute: java in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utils.Calculator
    Term->>JVM: Load class using FQN
    JVM->>FS: Search CLASSPATH for class file
    FS-->>JVM: Return bytecode
    JVM->>JVM: Verify, link, initialize
    JVM-->>Dev: Program execution output
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a package in Java. Explain the need for packages with any two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A package in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages. It is declared using the `package` keyword as the first statement in a source file.

**Need for packages:**
1. **Namespace management:** Packages prevent naming conflicts. Two classes with the same name can coexist if placed in different packages (e.g., `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`).
2. **Access control:** Packages provide visibility boundaries, allowing default (package-private) access for encapsulation.
3. **Modularity & reusability:** Logically grouped types are easier to maintain, distribute, and reuse across projects.

*(Any two reasons — 1.5 marks each; definition — 1 mark. Full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of the `-d` flag used during compilation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `-d` flag in the `javac` command specifies the **destination directory** where the compiler should place the generated `.class` files.

- When compiling a class that belongs to a package (e.g., `in.edu.ktu.oop.utils.Calculator`), the compiler uses `-d .` to **automatically create the matching folder hierarchy** (`in/edu/ktu/oop/utils/`) and place `Calculator.class` inside it.
- Without `-d`, the `.class` file would be dumped into the current directory, breaking the package structure and causing `ClassNotFoundException` at runtime.

**Example:**
```bash
javac -d . Calculator.java
```

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for folder auto-creation logic, 1 mark for consequences of omission.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Module 3 ESE Part B questions typically give **internal choice**. We provide **TWO independent alternatives** — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure for creating and using a user-defined package in Java with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of CLASSPATH in Java. How do you set it on a Linux/Windows system? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Procedure for Creating and Using a User-Defined Package (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Plan the Package Name**
Choose a reverse-DNS-based package name, e.g., `in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utility`.

**Step 2 — Create the Directory Structure**
From your project root:
```text
MyProject/in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utility/
```

**Step 3 — Define the Package in Source Code**
Create `MyProject/in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utility/Message.java`:

```java
package in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utility;

public class Message {
    public void printWelcome(String name) {
        System.out.println("Welcome to KTU OOP, " + name + "!");
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Compile with the `-d` Flag**
From the **root** `MyProject` directory:
```bash
javac -d . in/edu/ktu/oop/m3/utility/Message.java
```
This generates `Message.class` inside the proper folder.

**Step 5 — Use the Package in Another Class**
Create `MyProject/TestMsg.java`:
```java
import in.edu.ktu.oop.m3.utility.Message;

public class TestMsg {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Message m = new Message();
        m.printWelcome("Student");
    }
}
```

**Step 6 — Compile and Run**
```bash
javac TestMsg.java
java TestMsg
```

**Output:** `Welcome to KTU OOP, Student!`

*(Marks distribution: Step 1 — 1, Step 2 — 1, Steps 3–4 — 2, Steps 5–6 — 2, Explanation — 1.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**CLASSPATH in Java (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
CLASSPATH is an environment variable that tells the **JVM** and **Java compiler** where to locate user-defined `.class` files and library JAR files. It is essential because Java does not search the entire filesystem — it only searches paths listed in CLASSPATH.

**Setting CLASSPATH (3 marks):**

**On Windows (Command Prompt):**
```cmd
set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myprojects\bin
```

**On Linux/macOS (Bash):**
```bash
export CLASSPATH=.:/home/student/myprojects/bin
```

To make it **permanent**:
- **Windows:** `System Properties → Environment Variables → New`
- **Linux:** Add the `export` line to `~/.bashrc` or `~/.profile`.

**Role and Importance (2 marks):**
1. The leading `.` represents the **current directory** — without it, classes in the working folder cannot be found.
2. Multiple paths are separated by `:` (Linux) or `;` (Windows).
3. CLASSPATH can also be set inline using `-cp` flag:
   ```bash
   java -cp .:/libs/mylib.jar TestMsg
   ```

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by confusing CLASSPATH separator: `:` on Linux vs `;` on Windows.
> - Forgetting the **leading dot `.`** is the #1 reason for `ClassNotFoundException` in labs — examiners **deduct 1 full mark** if not mentioned.
> - Never confuse **PATH** (for executables like `javac`) with **CLASSPATH** (for `.class` files). This mistake costs **2–3 marks**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between `import` statement and Fully Qualified Name (FQN) approach to access a packaged class. Provide an example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain package-level (default) access control in Java with a suitable example demonstrating why a non-public class cannot be accessed from outside its package. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**`import` vs Fully Qualified Name (FQN) — 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | `import` Statement | Fully Qualified Name (FQN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Syntax** | `import pkg.subpkg.ClassName;` | `pkg.subpkg.ClassName obj = new pkg.subpkg.ClassName();` |
| **Location** | After `package`, before class | Inline at usage site |
| **Code length** | Cleaner, shorter | Verbose |
| **Reusability** | Use class name directly throughout file | Must repeat FQN every time |
| **Name conflict** | May require explicit FQN if collision exists | No ambiguity possible |
| **Wildcard option** | `import pkg.*;` available | No wildcard concept |

**Example (3 marks):**

```java
// Approach 1: Using import
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();  // direct usage
        list.add("KTU");
    }
}

// Approach 2: Using Fully Qualified Name (no import)
public class Demo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.util.ArrayList<String> list = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
        list.add("OOP");
    }
}
```

Both produce identical functionality, but `Demo1` is more readable.

*(Marks: 3 for table, 3 for example, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Package-Level (Default) Access Control — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
When no access modifier is specified for a class or its members, Java applies **package-private (default)** access. Such elements are accessible **only within the same package** and hidden from all other packages.

**Example (4 marks):**

**Package:** `in.edu.ktu.pkgA`
```java
package in.edu.ktu.pkgA;

class Helper {          // default class
    void show() {       // default method
        System.out.println("Helper class in pkgA");
    }
}

public class PublicClass {
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("PublicClass in pkgA");
    }
}
```

**Package:** `in.edu.ktu.pkgB`
```java
package in.edu.ktu.pkgB;
import in.edu.ktu.pkgA.*;

public class TestAccess {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PublicClass p = new PublicClass();
        p.display();          // ✅ Compiles — public access

        // Helper h = new Helper();    // ❌ Compile-time error
        // h.show();                   // ❌ Cannot find symbol
    }
}
```

**Compiler Output:**
```text
error: Helper is not public in in.edu.ktu.pkgA;
       cannot be accessed from outside package
```

**Why this happens (1 mark):**
The JVM/compiler checks the access modifier at compile time. Since `Helper` has no `public` modifier, its visibility is bounded to `in.edu.ktu.pkgA`. Class `TestAccess` in a different package `in.edu.ktu.pkgB` cannot instantiate or invoke its members.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write `class Helper` without realizing it is package-private. Always mention **"default access is package-private"**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that if the **class is default**, no member — even `public` ones — can be accessed externally. The class-level restriction takes precedence.
> - **Marks Lost:** Typically **2 marks** are deducted if students don't explain *why* the compile error occurs (visibility check during compilation).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ A **package** is a namespace for grouping related Java types.
- ✅ Declared using `package name.subname;` — must be the **first statement** in the source file.
- ✅ Package name ↔ Directory structure mapping is **mandatory** (dotted name = folder path).
- ✅ The **`-d` flag** with `javac` auto-creates the directory hierarchy for compiled `.class` files.
- ✅ **CLASSPATH** tells the JVM where to find user-defined classes; **leading `.`** is essential.
- ✅ Separator in CLASSPATH: **`:` on Linux/macOS**, **`;` on Windows**.
- ✅ Use **FQN** (Fully Qualified Name) to run a packaged class: `java pkg.ClassName`.
- ✅ **Reverse-DNS convention** ensures global uniqueness of package names.
- ✅ **Default access** (no modifier) = package-private = same package only.
- ✅ `import` is for **compile-time** convenience; runtime resolution uses CLASSPATH + FQN.
- ✅ Wildcard `import pkg.*;` imports all types of a package — discouraged in production.
- ✅ **Static import** (`import static`) was introduced in Java 5 for accessing static members directly.
- ✅ Sub-packages (`a.b.c`) have **no special access relationship** — they are independent namespaces for access-control purposes.
- ✅ The **default (unnamed) package** exists only when no `package` statement is used; it cannot be imported by named packages.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importing Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Package**: A package in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related **classes**, **interfaces**, **enumerations**, and **sub-packages**. It serves as a **container** or **logical folder** for grouping similar types and preventing **naming conflicts**, while providing **access control** and **modular code organization**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Importing Packages**: Importing packages is the mechanism by which a Java source file references and gains access to classes, interfaces, and members defined in other packages — either **built-in (JDK) packages** or **user-defined packages** — without requiring fully qualified names every time.

There are **three fundamental approaches** to access classes from another package in Java:

| # | Approach | Syntax Style | Usage Frequency |
|---|----------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Fully Qualified Name** | `java.util.Scanner sc;` | Inline / one-off usage |
| 2 | **Specific Import** | `import java.util.Scanner;` | Most common in KTU exams |
| 3 | **Wildcard Import** | `import java.util.*;` | Discouraged but allowed |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of packages like office filing cabinets in a large company.**
>
> Imagine you are a new intern at KTU Tech Solutions. You walk into the office and need a pen to write with.
>
> - The **stationery cabinet** holds pens, staplers, notepads — all related items.
> - The **HR cabinet** holds employee records, leave applications, salary slips.
> - The **Engineering cabinet** holds technical diagrams, code repositories, circuit blueprints.
>
> Each cabinet is a **package**. Each file inside is a **class**. Now, suppose you need a pen. Instead of walking to the stationery cabinet and saying "Stationery cabinet, drawer 3, pen," you simply say **"I need a pen"** because you have been **given access** to that cabinet.
>
> In Java, when you **import** a package, you are essentially telling the compiler:
> *"Hey! Whenever I mention `Scanner` from now on, please look inside the `java.util` cabinet for me."*

## 1.3 Why Packages Are Mandatory Knowledge for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEIT741 Module 3):** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 explicitly covers *Packages — defining, creating, accessing, importing, and CLASSPATH management*. Questions appear frequently as **3-mark direct questions** and as **7-mark sub-parts in 14-mark questions**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Package Structure

Java packages follow a **hierarchical dotted naming convention** that mirrors the **directory structure** on the disk. Each dot (`.`) represents a directory level.

```text
java
 └── util
      ├── Scanner
      ├── ArrayList
      └── HashMap
```

The package `java.util` physically corresponds to the path:
`JDK_INSTALL_DIR/java/util/`

## 2.2 The Three Import Strategies — Detailed Breakdown

### Strategy 1 — Fully Qualified Name (No Import)

```java
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in);
    }
}
```

**Characteristics:**
- **No import statement** is required.
- The class name is prefixed with its **complete package path**.
- **Pros**: Useful when **two classes share the same simple name** from different packages (resolves ambiguity).
- **Cons**: Verbose, reduces code readability.

### Strategy 2 — Specific Class Import (Recommended by KTU Examiners)

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
    }
}
```

**Characteristics:**
- Imports **one specific class** from the package.
- This is the **best practice** and the approach most KTU model answers prefer.
- Improves **readability** without polluting the namespace.

### Strategy 3 — Wildcard Import (`*`)

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
    }
}
```

**Characteristics:**
- Imports **all public classes** from the specified package.
- Does **NOT** import sub-packages recursively.
- **Cons**: Can cause **naming conflicts** and slightly increases compilation time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** `import java.util.*;` does **NOT** import `java.util.regex.*` automatically. Students frequently lose marks by assuming wildcards work recursively. This is **sub-package blind**.

## 2.3 Static Imports — Special Case

Java 5.0 introduced **static imports** to access **static members** (fields and methods) of a class directly without class name qualification.

```java
import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;
import static java.lang.System.out;

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double result = sqrt(25);   // No need for Math.sqrt
        out.println(result);        // No need for System.out
    }
}
```

## 2.4 Built-in (Implicit) Imports

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every Java source file automatically imports the `java.lang` package.** This is why you can use `String`, `System`, `Math`, `Object`, etc., without explicitly writing `import java.lang.String;`.

## 2.5 Default Package (Unnamed Package)

A class with **no package declaration** belongs to the **default package**. Classes in the default package:
- Can be accessed only by other classes within the **same default package**.
- **Cannot** be imported by named packages — this is a **compilation error**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---------|--------|----------------------------|
| Package Declaration | `package com.ktu.mypkg;` | Must be the **first statement** (excluding comments). |
| Specific Import | `import java.util.Scanner;` | Imports one class. |
| Wildcard Import | `import java.util.*;` | Imports all classes in that package only. |
| Static Import | `import static java.lang.Math.PI;` | For static members only. |
| Fully Qualified Use | `java.util.Date d = new java.util.Date();` | No import needed. |
| Implicit Import | `java.lang.*` | Automatic for every file. |
| Directory Mapping | `com.ktu.mypkg` | Folder path: `com/ktu/mypkg/` |
| Classpath | `-classpath .;C:\libs` | Tells JVM where to find packages. |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In enterprise engineering (B.Tech project level and beyond), packages are used for:
- **API design** — separating `model`, `view`, `controller`, `service` packages.
- **Library distribution** — JAR files are essentially zipped package hierarchies.
- **Microservices** — each microservice uses packages for internal modularity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Compilation Resolution Order

When the Java compiler encounters a class reference like `Scanner sc;`, it resolves the class in the following strict order:

| Resolution Priority | Source | Example |
|---------------------|--------|---------|
| **Priority 1** | Current package (same folder) | Local `Scanner` overrides everything. |
| **Priority 2** | Explicit single-class imports | `import java.util.Scanner;` |
| **Priority 3** | Wildcard imports (`*`) | `import java.util.*;` |
| **Priority 4** | `java.lang.*` (implicit) | `String`, `Math`, `Object` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** If a class name exists in **Priority 2** and also in **Priority 3 (wildcard)**, the **explicit import ALWAYS wins**. The compiler will issue an **error** if two Priority 2 imports conflict (e.g., importing `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date` together).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Naming Conflict Resolution

### Problem Scenario
You need both `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date` in the same file.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the conflict.
Both packages contain a class named `Date`.

**Step 2:** Choose the resolution strategy.

Option A — **Use fully qualified names inline:**

```java
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.util.Date utilDate = new java.util.Date();
        java.sql.Date sqlDate = new java.sql.Date(System.currentTimeMillis());
    }
}
```

Option B — **Import one explicitly and qualify the other:**

```java
import java.util.Date;

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Date utilDate = new Date();
        java.sql.Date sqlDate = new java.sql.Date(System.currentTimeMillis());
    }
}
```

**Step 3:** Validate the logic.
- The explicit import removes ambiguity for `Date`.
- The fully qualified name handles the second case.
- No wildcard imports are used here to avoid further conflict.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Content — Complete Package Import Demonstration

### Step A: Directory Setup

Suppose the user-defined package structure is:
```
C:\MyProject\
    ├── mypack\
    │     └── MyClass.java
    └── Main.java
```

### Step B: Package Definition (MyClass.java)

```java
package mypack;

public class MyClass {
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Hello from mypack.MyClass");
    }
}
```

### Step C: Compilation

Open terminal at `C:\MyProject\` and execute:

```text
javac -d . mypack\MyClass.java
```

This generates the folder `mypack\` containing `MyClass.class`.

### Step D: Importing and Using (Main.java)

```java
import mypack.MyClass;

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyClass obj = new MyClass();
        obj.display();
    }
}
```

### Step E: Execution with CLASSPATH

```text
javac -classpath . Main.java
java  -classpath . Main
```

**Expected Output:**

```text
Hello from mypack.MyClass
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the `-classpath .` flag:**
> The `.` represents the **current working directory**. The compiler/JVM searches this directory for the `mypack` folder. Without setting the classpath, the JVM would only search the **JDK library folders** and the **current directory by default**.

## 3.4 The Compilation Resolution Equation

The probability of finding a class $C$ in a Java program can be expressed conceptually as:

$$
P(\text{Found}) = P(\text{Same Package}) + P(\text{Explicit Import}) + P(\text{Wildcard Import}) + P(\text{java.lang})
$$

Subject to the boundary conditions:

$$
P(\text{Same Package}) \;\vert\; P(\text{Explicit Import}) \;\vert\; P(\text{Wildcard Import}) \;\vert\; P(\text{java.lang}) \in \{0, 1\}
$$

And the binding constraint:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} P(i) \;\geq\; 1 \quad \text{(at least one must resolve)}
$$

If two sources both resolve to $1$, the **lower-priority source is ignored automatically**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Package Import Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Source File Begins] --> B{Package Declared?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Locate matching directory on classpath]
    B -- No --> D[Assign to default unnamed package]
    C --> E[Scan for Explicit Imports]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Class Found in Explicit?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Bind class reference]
    F -- No --> H[Scan Wildcard Imports]
    H --> I{Class Found in Wildcard?}
    I -- Yes --> G
    I -- No --> J[Check java.lang implicitly]
    J --> K{Class Found in java.lang?}
    K -- Yes --> G
    K -- No --> L[Compilation Error: Cannot Find Symbol]
    G --> M[Continue Compilation]
```

## 4.2 Architecture: Package Hierarchy and Import Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph JDKROOT[JDK Root Library]
        JAVALANG[java lang Package]
        JAVAUTIL[java util Package]
        JAVASQL[java sql Package]
        JAVAIo[java io Package]
    end

    subgraph USERPKG[User Defined Package]
        MYPACK[mypack Package]
        MYCLASS1[MyClass]
        MYCLASS2[Helper]
    end

    SRC[Main java Source] -->|import mypack MyClass| MYPACK
    SRC -->|implicit java lang| JAVALANG
    SRC -->|optional java util| JAVAUTIL

    MYPACK --> MYCLASS1
    MYPACK --> MYCLASS2
    JAVAUTIL -.contains.-> SCANNER[Scanner]
    JAVAUTIL -.contains.-> ARRAYLIST[ArrayList]
```

## 4.3 Import Strategy Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Need external class?] -->|Yes| Q2{Single class or many?}
    Q1 -->|No| END1[No import needed]
    Q2 -->|Single| Q3[Use Specific Import]
    Q2 -->|Many| Q4{Naming conflict likely?}
    Q4 -->|No| Q5[Use Wildcard Import]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Fully Qualified Names]
    Q3 --> END2[Recommended]
    Q5 --> END3[Allowed but discouraged]
    Q6 --> END4[Use when ambiguous]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Explain the difference between **fully qualified name access** and **import statement** in Java with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

> A **fully qualified name** uses the complete package path inline (e.g., `java.util.Scanner sc = new java.util.Scanner(System.in);`) without any import statement. It is verbose but avoids ambiguity.
>
> An **import statement** (e.g., `import java.util.Scanner;`) allows the class to be referenced using its simple name (`Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);`), improving readability.
>
> Imports must appear **after** the package declaration and **before** any class declaration.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the significance of the `import static` statement in Java? Give an example.

**Model Answer:**

> The `import static` statement enables direct access to **static members** (fields and methods) of a class without qualifying them with the class name.
>
> **Example:**
> ```java
> import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;
> import static java.lang.System.out;
>
> public class Demo {
>     public static void main(String[] args) {
>         out.println(sqrt(144));
>     }
> }
> ```
> Here, `sqrt(144)` is equivalent to `Math.sqrt(144)` and `out.println(...)` is equivalent to `System.out.println(...)`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **packages in Java**. Discuss the different ways to access classes from another package with appropriate code examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a user-defined package called `shapes` containing a class `Circle` with a method `area(double r)`. Import this package into another class to compute and display the area of a circle with radius 7.5. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Definition:**
A **package** in Java is a mechanism to group related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages into a single namespace. It provides:
- **Namespace management** to avoid name conflicts.
- **Access control** through `public`, `protected`, and package-private modifiers.
- **Modular organization** of large codebases.

**Three ways to access classes from another package:**

**Method 1 — Without import (using fully qualified name):**

```java
public class Test1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.util.ArrayList<Integer> list = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
    }
}
```

**Method 2 — Using specific class import:**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class Test2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
    }
}
```

**Method 3 — Using wildcard import:**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Test3 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ArrayList<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
        HashMap<String, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
    }
}
```

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1:** Create folder structure
```
C:\ShapesProject\
    ├── shapes\
    │     └── Circle.java
    └── AreaDemo.java
```

**Step 2:** Define the package file — `shapes/Circle.java`

```java
package shapes;

public class Circle {
    public double area(double r) {
        return 3.14159 * r * r;
    }
}
```

**Step 3:** Compile the package

```text
cd C:\ShapesProject
javac -d . shapes\Circle.java
```

**Step 4:** Create `AreaDemo.java` importing the package

```java
import shapes.Circle;

public class AreaDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle();
        double result = c.area(7.5);
        System.out.println("Area of Circle = " + result);
    }
}
```

**Step 5:** Compile and execute

```text
javac -classpath . AreaDemo.java
java  -classpath . AreaDemo
```

**Expected Output:**

```text
Area of Circle = 176.7144375
```

---

### Question B — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **specific import** and **wildcard import** in Java. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **CLASSPATH** in Java. How does it relate to importing user-defined packages? Demonstrate with an example where CLASSPATH is set to a custom directory. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

| Feature | Specific Import | Wildcard Import |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Syntax | `import java.util.Scanner;` | `import java.util.*;` |
| Scope | Imports **one** class. | Imports **all** public classes in the package. |
| Readability | High — explicit. | Lower — implicit. |
| Compilation Speed | Faster lookup. | Slightly slower (broader search). |
| Conflict Risk | Low. | High — may collide with local classes. |

**Specific Import:**
- **Advantage:** Clear, explicit, no ambiguity.
- **Disadvantage:** Repetitive if many classes from the same package are needed.

**Wildcard Import:**
- **Advantage:** Concise — single statement covers many classes.
- **Disadvantage:** Can cause naming conflicts; does **not** import sub-packages.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**CLASSPATH Definition:**
The **CLASSPATH** is an environment variable (or command-line argument) that tells the **Java compiler (`javac`)** and the **Java Virtual Machine (`java`)** where to locate `.class` files (compiled Java bytecode) and `.jar` archives.

**Relationship with Importing:**

When you write `import shapes.Circle;`, the JVM must locate `shapes\Circle.class` at runtime. The CLASSPATH provides the **root directory** from which this path is resolved.

**Demonstration:**

**Step 1:** Suppose compiled `Circle.class` exists in `D:\MyLib\shapes\Circle.class`.

**Step 2:** Compile a Java program from any directory:

```text
javac -classpath D:\MyLib AreaDemo.java
```

**Step 3:** Execute with the same classpath:

```text
java -classpath .;D:\MyLib AreaDemo
```

Here:
- `.` → current directory (for `AreaDemo.class`).
- `D:\MyLib` → directory containing the `shapes` package folder.

> [!NOTE]
> The **separator** is `;` on Windows and `:` on Linux/macOS.

**Alternative — Setting CLASSPATH as Environment Variable:**

On Windows:
```text
set CLASSPATH=.;D:\MyLib
```

On Linux/macOS:
```text
export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/MyLib
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Writing the package statement after import** — This is a **compilation error**. Package must be the **first statement**.
> 2. **Assuming `import java.util.*;` imports sub-packages** — It does NOT.
> 3. **Forgetting the `-d` flag** while compiling the package source — the `.class` file never gets placed in the correct directory.
> 4. **Confusing CLASSPATH with PATH** — they are different environment variables serving different purposes.
> 5. **Writing `import java.lang.String;`** — wasting a line, as `java.lang` is auto-imported.
> 6. **Using `default` keyword for package access** — `default` is used inside `switch` statements, not for packages.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Importing Packages**
>
> - A **package** is a namespace for organizing Java types; it maps to a directory structure.
> - The `package` declaration must be the **first non-comment statement** in a source file.
> - **Three ways** to use external classes: fully qualified name, specific import, wildcard import.
> - **Specific import** (`import java.util.Scanner;`) is the **recommended best practice**.
> - **Wildcard import** (`import java.util.*;`) imports only that level, **not sub-packages**.
> - **`java.lang.*`** is **implicitly imported** in every Java file — no need to write it.
> - **Static imports** allow direct access to static members without class qualification.
> - **Default (unnamed) package** classes cannot be imported by named packages.
> - **CLASSPATH** tells JVM/compiler where to locate package directories.
> - **Compiler resolution priority:** Same Package → Explicit Import → Wildcard Import → `java.lang`.
> - **Naming conflicts** are resolved using fully qualified names or by careful import selection.
> - Always compile with `javac -d . FileName.java` to generate the correct package directory structure.
> - For KTU exams, **always show the directory tree** in your answer when dealing with user-defined packages.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfaces, Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfaces, Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interfaces — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> An **interface** in Java is a completely abstract reference type that defines a *contract* of method signatures which implementing classes must honor. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, an interface may contain **abstract methods**, **default methods** (Java 8+), **static methods** (Java 8+), **private methods** (Java 9+), and **public static final** constants. Interfaces enable full **multiple inheritance of type** in Java (a class may implement any number of interfaces), thereby resolving the *Diamond Problem* that arises with single class inheritance.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Job Description"

Imagine you walk into a large multinational company and the HR department hands you a **Job Description (JD)** document. The JD lists responsibilities like *"Must code in Java,"* *"Must attend stand-ups,"* *"Must review PRs."* It tells you **what** you must do, but never **how**. The actual implementation is left to the engineer you hire.

A Java **interface is exactly that JD**:

- The methods declared (with no body) are the *responsibilities*.
- The class that **implements** the interface is the *hired employee* who must fulfil every responsibility.
- Two completely different employees (`class A` and `class B`) can fulfil the same JD in entirely different ways — this is **polymorphism**.

---

## 1.2 Checked Exceptions — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A **Checked Exception** is an exception that the **Java compiler verifies at compile-time**. The compiler forces the programmer to either **handle** the exception using a `try-catch` block or **declare** it using the `throws` keyword in the method signature. All checked exceptions are subclasses of `java.lang.Exception` (but NOT subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`). Classic examples include `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `ParseException`, and `InterruptedException`.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Security Gate at the Airport"

Picture an **airport security checkpoint**:

- The TSA officer (the **compiler**) inspects your bag **before you board** (i.e., **before the program runs**).
- If you are carrying a prohibited item (a **checked exception**), the officer will literally **stop you from boarding** until you either:
  1. **Dispose of it safely** — equivalent to a `try-catch` block.
  2. **Declare that you are carrying it** — equivalent to adding `throws` to your method signature so that your *caller* takes responsibility.

In contrast, *unchecked* exceptions (Runtime Exceptions) are like items that only get detected by a **random mid-flight check** — the compiler doesn't care about them at build-time.

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Property | Interface | Checked Exception |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Keyword** | `interface` | `extends Exception` |
| **Instantiable?** | **No** | **No** (must be thrown) |
| **Detection Time** | Compile-time | Compile-time |
| **Mandatory Handling?** | All abstract methods must be implemented | Must `catch` or `throws` |
| **Multiple Inheritance?** | Yes (a class can implement N interfaces) | A class cannot extend two exception classes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**
> KTU 2024 expects students to clearly differentiate **Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions**, write custom checked exceptions, and chain interfaces for multiple inheritance scenarios. This is a **high-weightage topic** in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Interface — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Declaration Phase** — The keyword `interface` is used. By default, every method is `public abstract` and every variable is `public static final`.
2. **Contract Phase** — Implementing classes must override **every** abstract method (or declare themselves `abstract`).
3. **Extension Phase** — An interface can extend multiple interfaces using `extends` (comma-separated).
4. **Implementation Phase** — A class uses `implements` (also comma-separated for multiple interfaces).
5. **Default Method Phase (Java 8+)** — Interfaces can have concrete methods with the `default` keyword for backward compatibility.
6. **Static Method Phase (Java 8+)** — Interfaces can have static utility methods, called via `InterfaceName.method()`.
7. **Private Method Phase (Java 9+)** — Helper methods within an interface that cannot be overridden.

### The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

- **Why interface methods are abstract by default?** To enforce a contract — the compiler guarantees every implementer provides behavior.
- **Why use `default` methods?** When new methods need to be added to existing interfaces without breaking older implementations (e.g., `forEach` was added to `Collection`).
- **Why allow multiple interface inheritance?** Java classes can extend only one class (single inheritance), so interfaces provide an escape hatch.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of Checked Exception Handling — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identification Phase** — The compiler scans every statement. If it calls a method that declares `throws SomeCheckedException`, the compiler flags it.
2. **Resolution Phase** — The programmer must either wrap the call in `try { ... } catch (...) { ... }` or propagate the exception by declaring `throws` in the calling method.
3. **Execution Phase** — At runtime, if the exception occurs, the JVM creates an exception object and the **call stack unwinds** until a matching `catch` block is found.
4. **Cleanup Phase** — The `finally` block executes regardless of whether an exception occurred (used for resource release).

### The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

- **Why checked exceptions?** They represent recoverable conditions outside the program's direct control (file missing, network down, database unreachable). Forcing the developer to acknowledge them produces more robust code.
- **Why not check `RuntimeException`?** Because they usually indicate programming bugs (e.g., `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`) — *the developer's fault*, not an external factor.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### Interface Syntax Templates

| Construct | Syntax Template |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Basic Interface | `interface Name { void method(); }` |
| Implementation | `class A implements Name { public void method() { ... } }` |
| Multiple Inheritance (class) | `class A implements I1, I2 { ... }` |
| Interface extending Interface | `interface I3 extends I1, I2 { ... }` |
| Default Method | `default void m() { ... }` |
| Static Method | `static void util() { ... }` |
| Constant Field | `int MAX = 100;` *(implicitly `public static final`)* |

### Checked Exception Syntax Templates

| Construct | Syntax Template |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Try-Catch | `try { ... } catch (ExceptionType e) { ... }` |
| Multi-catch (Java 7+) | `catch (IOException \vert SQLException e) { ... }` |
| Try-with-resources (Java 7+) | `try (Resource r = new Resource()) { ... }` |
| Throw | `throw new IOException("msg");` |
| Throws declaration | `void read() throws IOException { ... }` |
| Custom Checked Exception | `class MyException extends Exception { ... }` |
| Finally block | `finally { /* always runs */ }` |

### Java Exception Hierarchy — Critical Facts

| Level | Class | Checked? |
|-------|-------|----------|
| 0 | `java.lang.Object` | — |
| 1 | `java.lang.Throwable` | — |
| 2 | `java.lang.Exception` | **Parent of all checked** |
| 2 | `java.lang.Error` | Unchecked (JVM-level) |
| 3 | `java.lang.RuntimeException` | **Parent of all unchecked** |
| 3 | `java.io.IOException` | **Checked** |
| 3 | `java.sql.SQLException` | **Checked** |
| 3 | `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException` | **Checked** |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Interfaces** drive the entire **Java Collection Framework** (`List`, `Set`, `Map` are interfaces). Spring Boot, Hibernate, and JDBC rely heavily on interface-based design (Dependency Injection).
- **Checked Exceptions** are critical in **enterprise applications** — banking software must handle `SQLException`, distributed systems must handle `IOException`, and parsers must handle `ParseException`. They make failure modes **explicit and documented**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Exception Propagation Through the Call Stack

Let us model the call stack as a vertical chain of method frames:

$$
\text{CallStack} = \{ M_1 \rightarrow M_2 \rightarrow M_3 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow M_n \}
$$

When an exception occurs in method $M_n$, the JVM executes a **stack-unwinding algorithm**:

$$
\text{Unwind}(e) = \begin{cases} \text{Match found at } M_k & \text{if } \exists \, k \in [1, n] \text{ where } M_k \text{ has a matching catch}(e) \\ \text{Propagate to JVM} & \text{otherwise (program terminates)} \end{cases}
$$

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1** — Method $M_n$ encounters the fault condition and executes:

```java
throw new IOException("Disk failure");
```

**Step 2** — The JVM creates an object of type `IOException` and begins searching the current method frame for a matching `catch` block.

**Step 3** — If no matching catch is found in $M_n$, the current frame is **popped** from the call stack, and control returns to the calling method $M_{n-1}$.

**Step 4** — The search continues frame-by-frame upwards until a match is found (Step 4a) or the stack is empty (Step 4b).

**Step 5** — If the stack empties without a match, the default `ExceptionHandler` (in `java.lang.Thread`) prints the stack trace and terminates the program.

### Mathematical Cost of Stack Unwinding

The time complexity of exception propagation is linear in the depth of the call stack:

$$
T_{\text{propagation}} = O(d), \quad \text{where } d = \text{call stack depth}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why throwing exceptions inside performance-critical loops is **anti-pattern**. Use conditional checks instead. The unwinding cost is negligible for occasional failures but catastrophic in tight loops.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Interface vs Abstract Class vs Concrete Class

| Dimension | Interface | Abstract Class | Concrete Class |
|-----------|:---------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|
| Keyword | `interface` | `abstract class` | `class` |
| Methods allowed | Abstract, default, static, private | Abstract + concrete | Concrete only |
| Variables | `public static final` only | Any | Any |
| Inheritance form | Multiple (via `implements`) | Single (via `extends`) | Single |
| Constructor | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** | **Allowed** |
| Access modifiers for methods | `public` only (pre-Java 9) | Any | Any |
| When to choose? | "Can-do" capability (e.g., `Serializable`) | Shared base state + partial implementation | Full implementation |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction of a Custom Checked Exception

Let us derive the anatomy of a well-designed custom checked exception following KTU best practices:

**Step 1 — Class Declaration:**

$$
\text{class} \; \text{MyException} \;\text{extends}\; \text{Exception}
$$

Extending `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) makes it **checked**.

**Step 2 — Default Constructor:**

Provides a no-argument constructor for minimal instantiation:

```java
public MyException() {
    super();
}
```

**Step 3 — Message Constructor:**

Provides a constructor accepting a `String` message, forwarded to `Throwable`'s `super(message)` constructor:

```java
public MyException(String message) {
    super(message);
}
```

**Step 4 — Cause-Chaining Constructor (KTU High-Yield):**

Accepts both a message and a `Throwable` cause, enabling **exception chaining**:

```java
public MyException(String message, Throwable cause) {
    super(message, cause);
}
```

### Exception Chaining — Mathematical Representation

Let $E_1$ be the original exception and $E_2$ be the wrapper exception. The chained exception maintains a reference:

$$
E_2.\text{cause} = E_1
$$

This allows debugging tools to reconstruct the **entire causal chain**:

$$
E_n \rightarrow E_{n-1} \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow E_2 \rightarrow E_1 \rightarrow \text{root cause}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Try-Catch-Finally Execution Sequence

Let $T$ be the try block execution, $C_i$ be the $i$-th catch block, and $F$ be the finally block. The execution is governed by:

$$
\text{Result} = \begin{cases} F(\text{after } T \text{ completes normally}) & \text{if } T \text{ returns normally} \\ F(\text{after } C_i) & \text{if } T \text{ throws and } C_i \text{ matches} \\ F(\text{after re-throw}) & \text{if no catch matches} \end{cases}
$$

The key invariant:

$$
\forall \text{ paths} : F \text{ is ALWAYS executed last}
$$

This guarantee is what makes `finally` the **canonical location for resource cleanup** in pre-Java 7 code.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
