## 2.2 The Three Cardinal Rules

1. **Rule of Specificity (Most-Derived-First):**  
   Subclass exception handlers must precede superclass exception handlers.

2. **Rule of Single Match:**  
   Only **one** `catch` block executes per thrown exception. The first match absorbs the exception.

3. **Rule of Exact-Type Preference:**  
   The compiler resolves at compile time whether a `catch` block is **reachable**. Unreachable catches cause a compile-time error: `exception java.io.IOException has already been caught`.

## 2.3 Exception Class Hierarchy (Java — KTU High-Yield Diagram in Text)

```
java.lang.Object
        |
        |
java.lang.Throwable
        |
        +----- java.lang.Exception  (Checked)
        |            |
        |            +----- java.io.IOException
        |            |            |
        |            |            +----- java.io.FileNotFoundException
        |            |
        |            +----- java.sql.SQLException
        |            |
        |            +----- java.lang.ClassNotFoundException
        |            |
        |            +----- java.lang.RuntimeException  (Unchecked)
        |                         |
        |                         +----- java.lang.ArithmeticException
        |                         |
        |                         +----- java.lang.NullPointerException
        |                         |
        |                         +----- java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        |                         |
        |                         +----- java.lang.NumberFormatException
        |
        +----- java.lang.Error  (Unchecked, fatal)
                     |
                     +----- java.lang.OutOfMemoryError
                     |
                     +----- java.lang.StackOverflowError
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:**  
> Because `ArithmeticException` is a subclass of `RuntimeException`, which in turn is a subclass of `Exception`, a `catch(Exception e)` will catch *every* checked and unchecked exception. Placing such a generic handler **above** specific handlers makes them unreachable.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Example |
|---------|----------------|---------|
| Order of catch blocks | Specific → General | `ArithmeticException` before `Exception` |
| Java Multi-catch syntax | `catch (A \| B e)` | `catch (IOException \| SQLException e)` |
| C++ catch by reference | `catch (const std::exception& e)` | Mandatory to avoid slicing |
| `Throwable` parameter | Catches **both** `Exception` and `Error` | `catch (Throwable t)` — use cautiously |
| Effect of unreachable catch | Compile-time error | `Unreachable catch block for IOException` |
| Type matching test | `e instanceof T` | Equivalent to: thrown object is-a handler type |
| Java checked exceptions | Subclasses of `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `IOException`, `SQLException` |
| Java unchecked exceptions | Subclasses of `RuntimeException` or `Error` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |
| C++ catch-all | `catch (...)` | Must be the **last** handler |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production-grade software systems (e.g., banking software, embedded controllers, web servers), multiple catch clauses enable **layered error recovery**:
- **Specific handlers** trigger domain-specific recovery (retry network operation, re-authenticate user).
- **Generic handlers** trigger fail-safe fallback (log + alert admin).
- **Catch-all (`catch(...)` or `catch(Throwable t)`)** ensures the application never crashes silently.

This pattern is the foundation of the **Robustness Principle** ("be conservative in what you do, be liberal in what you accept").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Determining the Correct Catch Order

### Problem Setup
Given the Java exception hierarchy, derive the correct ordering for a `try` block that may throw:
- `ArithmeticException`
- `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`
- `IOException` (checked)

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Identify the inheritance chain of each exception.**

$$
\text{ArithmeticException} \;\rightarrow\; \text{RuntimeException} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Exception} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Throwable}
$$

$$
\text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} \;\rightarrow\; \text{IndexOutOfBoundsException} \;\rightarrow\; \text{RuntimeException} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Exception}
$$

$$
\text{IOException} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Exception} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Throwable}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the partial order using "is-a" relation.**

$$
\text{ArithmeticException} \preceq \text{RuntimeException} \preceq \text{Exception} \preceq \text{Throwable}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rule: leaf (most specific) before root (most general).**

The valid linear extension (top-to-bottom catch order) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1st:} \quad & \text{catch (ArithmeticException ae)} \\
\text{2nd:} \quad & \text{catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aie)} \\
\text{3rd:} \quad & \text{catch (IOException ioe)} \\
\text{4th:} \quad & \text{catch (Exception e)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any permutation that places a parent class before its child produces an **unreachable catch** compile-time error.

### Worked Numerical-Style Example (Compile-Time Verification)

Consider the **incorrect** snippet:

```java
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;     // throws ArithmeticException
} catch (Exception e) {  // (A)
    System.out.println("Generic");
} catch (ArithmeticException ae) {  // (B) ← ERROR
    System.out.println("Specific");
}
```

**Verification:**  
At compile time, the compiler asks: *"Is `ArithmeticException` a subclass of `Exception`?"*  
Answer: **Yes.**  
Therefore, any `ArithmeticException` thrown in the `try` block will already be caught by `(A)`.  
Therefore, `(B)` is **unreachable** → compile error:

$$
\text{error: unreachable catch block for ArithmeticException,} \quad \text{it is already handled by the catch block for Exception}
$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Program Walkthrough

### Complete Java Program Demonstrating Multiple catch Clauses

```java
import java.io.*;

class MultipleCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
            int a = 10, b = 0;
            
            System.out.println("--- Test 1: Arithmetic Exception ---");
            int result = a / b;          // Line A → throws ArithmeticException
            
            System.out.println("--- Test 2: Array Exception ---");
            System.out.println(arr[5]);   // unreached if Line A throws
            
            System.out.println("--- Test 3: IO Exception ---");
            FileReader fr = new FileReader("nofile.txt"); // unreached normally
        } 
        catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
            System.out.println("Caught ArithmeticException: " + ae.getMessage());
        } 
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aie) {
            System.out.println("Caught ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: " + aie.getMessage());
        } 
        catch (IOException ioe) {
            System.out.println("Caught IOException: " + ioe.getMessage());
        } 
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Caught Generic Exception: " + e.getMessage());
        } 
        finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block always executes.");
        }
        
        System.out.println("Program continues normally after try-catch-finally.");
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace

| Line | Action | Effect |
|------|--------|--------|
| `int a = 10, b = 0` | Normal assignment | OK |
| `int result = a / b` | Division by zero | JVM throws `ArithmeticException` |
| Search catch #1 | `ArithmeticException ae` | **MATCH** → handler executes |
| Search catch #2, #3, #4 | Skipped | Not evaluated |
| `finally` | Always runs | Prints "Finally block always executes." |
| After try-catch | Control resumes | Prints "Program continues normally..." |

**Output:**
```
--- Test 1: Arithmetic Exception ---
Caught ArithmeticException: / by zero
Finally block always executes.
Program continues normally after try-catch-finally.
```

### Multi-catch (Java 7+) Variant

```java
try {
    // code that may throw either IOException or SQLException
} catch (IOException | SQLException e) {
    System.out.println("Database or I/O failure: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-catch vs Multiple catch:**
> - **Multi-catch** (`A | B`) creates **ONE** handler accepting either type; the parameter `e` is **effectively final**.
> - **Multiple catch** creates **separate handlers** with separate bodies; useful when recovery logic differs per exception type.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table

| Feature | Java — Multiple catch | Java — Multi-catch (7+) | C++ — Multiple catch |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Introduced in | Java 1.0 | Java 7 | C++ (always) |
| Number of handlers | One per type | One shared handler | One per type |
| Different bodies? | **Yes** | No (single body) | **Yes** |
| Order matters? | **Yes** | No (no subtype relation issues) | **Yes** |
| Catch-all syntax | `catch (Throwable t)` | Not applicable | `catch (...)` |
| Compile-time safety | Strong | Strong | Weaker (no checked exceptions) |
| Subtype of exception | Cannot repeat parent before child | All types must be unrelated (no subtype relation) | None may be an unambiguous base of another |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Diagram — Exception Search Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enter try block] --> B[Execute statement 1]
    B --> C{Exception thrown?}
    C -- No --> D[Execute remaining try statements]
    D --> E[Skip all catch blocks]
    E --> F[Execute finally block]
    F --> G[Continue program after try-catch-finally]
    
    C -- Yes --> H[Save exception object e]
    H --> I[Inspect catch #1 parameter type]
    I --> J{e is-a Catch1 type?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Execute Catch #1 body]
    K --> F
    J -- No --> L[Inspect catch #2 parameter type]
    L --> M{e is-a Catch2 type?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Execute Catch #2 body]
    N --> F
    M -- No --> O[More catch blocks?]
    O -- Yes --> P[Inspect next catch]
    P --> Q{Matches?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Execute matched catch]
    R --> F
    Q -- No --> O
    O -- No --> S[Exception propagates to caller]
    S --> F
```

## 4.2 Architecture Block Diagram — Layered Exception Handlers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRY["Try Block - Critical Section"]
        T1[Statement 1]
        T2[Statement 2 - May throw]
        T3[Statement 3]
    end
    
    subgraph CATCHES["Catch Block Cascade"]
        C1["Catch 1: ArithmeticException"]
        C2["Catch 2: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"]
        C3["Catch 3: IOException"]
        C4["Catch 4: Exception - Generic Fallback"]
    end
    
    subgraph FIN["Finally Block"]
        FIN1[Cleanup Resources]
        FIN2[Close Streams]
        FIN3[Release Locks]
    end
    
    T2 -->|throws| MATCH{Type Matcher}
    MATCH -->|ArithmeticException| C1
    MATCH -->|ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException| C2
    MATCH -->|IOException| C3
    MATCH -->|Other Exception| C4
    
    C1 --> FIN
    C2 --> FIN
    C3 --> FIN
    C4 --> FIN
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram — Java Exception Tree (Module-3 Focus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OBJ[java.lang.Object]
    THR[java.lang.Throwable]
    EX[java.lang.Exception]
    RT[java.lang.RuntimeException]
    ARITH[ArithmeticException]
    AIOOBE[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    NPE[NullPointerException]
    IOE[IOException]
    FNFE[FileNotFoundException]
    ERR[java.lang.Error]
    OOM[OutOfMemoryError]
    
    OBJ --> THR
    THR --> EX
    THR --> ERR
    EX --> RT
    EX --> IOE
    RT --> ARITH
    RT --> AIOOBE
    RT --> NPE
    IOE --> FNFE
    ERR --> OOM
    
    style ARITH fill:#ffe4e1
    style AIOOBE fill:#ffe4e1
    style NPE fill:#ffe4e1
    style IOE fill:#e0f7fa
    style FNFE fill:#b2ebf2
    style EX fill:#fff9c4
    style RT fill:#fff59d
    style ERR fill:#ffcdd2
```

## 4.4 Correct vs Incorrect Ordering — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORRECT["CORRECT ORDER - Compile Success"]
        direction TB
        C_T1["try block"]
        C_C1["catch ArithmeticException - specific"]
        C_C2["catch ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException - specific"]
        C_C3["catch IOException - specific"]
        C_C4["catch Exception - generic fallback"]
        C_C1 --> C_C2
        C_C2 --> C_C3
        C_C3 --> C_C4
    end
    
    subgraph INCORRECT["INCORRECT ORDER - Compile Error"]
        direction TB
        I_T1["try block"]
        I_C1["catch Exception - generic first"]
        I_C2["catch ArithmeticException - UNREACHABLE"]
        I_C3["Compiler ERROR"]
        I_C1 -.->|blocks all subclasses| I_C2
        I_C2 --> I_C3
    end
    
    style C_C1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style C_C2 fill:#c8e6c9
    style C_C3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style C_C4 fill:#c8e6c9
    style I_C1 fill:#ffcdd2
    style I_C2 fill:#ffcdd2
    style I_C3 fill:#d32f2f,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> *Why must `catch` blocks for subclass exceptions appear before `catch` blocks for superclass exceptions in Java? What is the result of violating this rule?*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)
Java uses **first-match-wins** semantics for `catch` clauses. A `catch (Exception e)` block can handle **all** subclasses of `Exception`, including `ArithmeticException`, `IOException`, etc. If the parent-class catch is placed first, every exception will be consumed by it, leaving the more specific subclass catches **unreachable**.

The compiler enforces this rule at compile time and reports an error such as:
> *"Unreachable catch block for ArithmeticException. It is already handled by the catch block for Exception."*

This rule ensures that the **most appropriate handler** (with the most specific recovery logic) gets the opportunity to run. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
> *What is multi-catch in Java? Write its syntax and state one advantage over traditional multiple catch clauses.*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Multi-catch** (introduced in Java 7) allows a single `catch` block to handle **multiple unrelated exception types** in one handler, reducing code duplication when the recovery logic is identical.

**Syntax:**
```java
try {
    // code
} catch (IOException | SQLException e) {
    logger.log(e.getMessage());
}
```

**Advantage:** Eliminates duplicated catch bodies when the handling logic is identical for multiple exception types, making the code shorter and easier to maintain. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 Internal Choice Pattern:**  
> Every Part-B question for Module 3 carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Sub-parts `(a)` and `(b)` each carry **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Write a Java program demonstrating **multiple catch clauses** that handles `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, and a generic `Exception` for the following `try` block:
- Read two integers from command-line arguments
- Compute their division
- Access an array element at a user-specified index

Explain the flow of execution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

```java
class MultipleCatchExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int x = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
            int y = Integer.parseInt(args[1]);
            int z = x / y;
            int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
            System.out.println("Division result: " + z);
            System.out.println("Array element: " + arr[z]);
        }
        catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
            System.out.println("Arithmetic Error: " + ae.getMessage());
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException aie) {
            System.out.println("Array Index Error: " + aie.getMessage());
        }
        catch (NumberFormatException nfe) {
            System.out.println("Number Format Error: " + nfe.getMessage());
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("Cleanup in finally block.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Flow-of-Execution Explanation (7 Marks):**
1. The `try` block contains four risk points: `args[0]`, `args[1]`, `x/y`, and `arr[z]`.
2. When an exception is thrown at any of these points, the JVM **terminates** the rest of the `try` block.
3. The JVM examines `catch` blocks **sequentially from top to bottom**.
4. The first `catch` whose parameter type matches the thrown exception (via `is-a` test) executes; the rest are skipped.
5. `NumberFormatException` is placed **before** `Exception` because it is a subclass.
6. The `finally` block runs irrespective of whether an exception was caught.
7. Program execution resumes at the statement following the entire try-catch-finally construct. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the **Java exception class hierarchy** and identify which of the exceptions are **checked** vs **unchecked**. Why is `RuntimeException` considered special? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Java Exception Hierarchy:**

```
Object
  └── Throwable
        ├── Exception (Checked)
        │     ├── IOException
        │     ├── SQLException
        │     ├── ClassNotFoundException
        │     └── RuntimeException (Unchecked)
        │           ├── ArithmeticException
        │           ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        │           ├── NullPointerException
        │           └── NumberFormatException
        └── Error (Unchecked, fatal)
              ├── OutOfMemoryError
              └── StackOverflowError
```

**Checked vs Unchecked:**

| Type | Examples | Compiler Enforcement |
|------|----------|----------------------|
| **Checked** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | Must be either caught or declared with `throws`. |
| **Unchecked (Runtime)** | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | No compile-time obligation. |
| **Error** | `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError` | Unchecked; usually not caught. |

**Why `RuntimeException` is special:**  
`RuntimeException` and its subclasses represent programming bugs (e.g., divide by zero, null dereference) that are **predictable at code-review time**. The compiler deliberately does **not** force programmers to catch or declare them, to avoid cluttering the code. The Java designers' philosophy: *programmer errors should be fixed at the source, not caught at run-time.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is an **unreachable catch block**? Provide an example that triggers a compile-time error. Rewrite the code so it compiles successfully. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

An **unreachable catch block** is a `catch` clause that **cannot be executed** because a previous `catch` clause in the same `try-catch` chain has already handled the exception type (or its parent type). The Java compiler detects this at compile time and refuses to compile the code.

**Example that fails to compile:**
```java
try {
    int a = 10 / 0;   // throws ArithmeticException
}
catch (Exception e) {            // ← catches EVERYTHING
    System.out.println("Generic");
}
catch (ArithmeticException ae) {  // ← UNREACHABLE
    System.out.println("Arithmetic");
}
```

**Compiler error:**
```
error: unreachable catch block for ArithmeticException
       catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
       ^
   (it is already handled by the catch block for Exception)
```

**Corrected version (specific-to-general order):**
```java
try {
    int a = 10 / 0;
}
catch (ArithmeticException ae) {   // ← now first
    System.out.println("Arithmetic: " + ae.getMessage());
}
catch (Exception e) {              // ← generic fallback
    System.out.println("Generic: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

This now compiles and produces:
```
Arithmetic: / by zero
``` **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Compare **multiple catch clauses** with **multi-catch (Java 7+)**. Discuss the **catch-all** mechanism in C++ (`catch (...)`) and in Java (`catch (Throwable t)`), and explain when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Multiple catch | Multi-catch `A \| B` |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| Handlers | One per exception type | One shared handler |
| Body | Can differ per type | Single shared body |
| When exception types are unrelated | Use multiple catch | Use multi-catch |
| When exception types are related (subclass) | Use specific-to-general order | **Not allowed** (compile error: alternatives are not disjoint) |
| Readability | More verbose | More concise |

**Catch-All Mechanisms:**

- **C++:** `catch (...) { /* handles everything */ }` — must be the **last** handler; commonly used as a safety net to ensure no exception escapes a critical function.
- **Java:** `catch (Throwable t) { ... }` — catches both `Exception` and `Error` (including `OutOfMemoryError`). Discouraged in application code because catching `Error` masks fatal JVM problems.

**When each is preferred:**

| Scenario | Recommended Approach |
|----------|----------------------|
| Different recovery logic per exception type | **Multiple catch** with distinct bodies |
| Identical handling for unrelated exception types | **Multi-catch** |
| Logging + rethrow as last-resort safety net | **C++ `catch(...)` or Java `catch(Throwable t)`** |
| Production library with strict error contracts | **Specific multi-catch + logged rethrow** |

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Multiple catch Clauses Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong order of catch blocks (–3 marks):** Placing `catch(Exception e)` *before* specific handlers → compile error → examiner deducts full marks for non-compiling code.
> 2. **Missing `finally` discussion (–2 marks):** Many students forget to explain that `finally` always runs — KTU frequently awards 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 3. **Confusing `throw` with `throws` (–2 marks):** `throw` is a statement; `throws` is a method-level clause. Examiners note this mistake repeatedly.
> 4. **Not drawing the exception hierarchy (–2 marks):** When asked to "explain with a neat diagram," omitting the tree costs easy marks.
> 5. **Forgetting checked vs unchecked distinction (–2 marks):** A common KTU sub-question explicitly tests this — students must state which exceptions the compiler forces you to handle.
> 6. **C++ vs Java confusion (–2 marks):** KTU Module 3 may include either language; mixing syntax (`try-catch-finally` is universal, but `catch(...)` is C++ only) loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Multiple catch Clauses:**
>
> - A `try` block can have **multiple `catch` blocks**, each handling a **different exception type**.
> - The JVM performs a **sequential top-to-bottom search** and invokes the **first matching handler** (first-match-wins semantics).
> - **Catch order is mandatory:** Most-specific (subclass) exception handlers MUST appear **first**; generic (superclass) handlers appear **last**.
> - Violating the order causes a **compile-time error: unreachable catch block**.
> - The parameter of a `catch` block is **implicitly `final`** (in multi-catch, explicitly final).
> - In Java, the parameter type determines what is caught — it follows the **is-a** relation with the thrown exception.
> - Only **one** `catch` block executes per exception; the rest are bypassed.
> - The `finally` block executes **always** — whether or not an exception occurred or was caught.
> - **Java 7 Multi-catch** (`catch (A | B e)`) is a **single handler** for multiple unrelated exception types.
> - In **C++**, use **references** (`catch (const std::exception& e)`) to avoid object slicing.
> - In **C++**, the catch-all `catch(...)` must be the **last** handler.
> - `RuntimeException` and its subclasses are **unchecked** — the compiler does NOT force you to catch or declare them.
> - `Error` and its subclasses (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) generally should **not** be caught — they indicate fatal JVM problems.
> - **Common exam-ready exceptions to remember:**
>   - Checked: `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`
>   - Unchecked: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`
>   - Errors: `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`, `VirtualMachineError`
> - **Mnemonic for ordering:** *"Children before Parents"* — same as inheritance tree drawn top-down (root last).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throw
throws and finally, Input/Output - I/O Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** throws and finally, Input/Output - I/O Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The `throws` Keyword

### Formal Definition
The **`throws`** keyword in Java is a **declarative mechanism** used in a method's signature to inform the compiler (and the calling code) that the method may propagate one or more **checked exceptions** back to its caller. It does **not throw** the exception itself — it merely *advertises* the possibility, transferring the obligation of handling to the caller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The `throws` clause is mandatory for checked exceptions (subclasses of `Exception` excluding `RuntimeException`). It is purely a *compile-time enforcement contract* — it does nothing at runtime.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **"Danger: Wet Floor" signboard** placed outside a room. The sign **does not make the floor wet**; it simply **warns everyone entering** that they should be careful (or take responsibility for slipping). Similarly, `throws` does not create or handle the exception — it only **declares** that the method *might* produce one, and the caller must be prepared.

---

## 1.2 The `finally` Block

### Formal Definition
The **`finally`** block in Java is an **optional block** that always accompanies a `try` (and optionally a `catch`) block. It contains code that **must be executed regardless of whether an exception occurs**, is caught, or even when a `return` statement is encountered. It is the primary mechanism for **resource cleanup** in Java (prior to try-with-resources).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Guaranteed Execution:** The body of a `finally` block is executed under all circumstances **except two**: (1) invocation of `System.exit()` and (2) abrupt JVM termination/crash.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **pilot performing pre-flight checks**. Whether the flight takes off, gets delayed, or is cancelled due to bad weather, the pilot must **always reset the cockpit instruments and log the flight data** afterwards. That mandatory post-task reset is what `finally` does — it is the **"always do this"** block.

---

## 1.3 Input/Output (I/O) Basics

### Formal Definition
**Java I/O** is the subsystem of the `java.io` (and `java.nio`) package that enables a program to **read data from sources** (input) and **write data to destinations** (output). The fundamental abstraction is the **Stream** — a logical conduit through which data flows sequentially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stream Definition:** A *stream* is a sequence of data elements made available over time. Java treats data flowing *into* a program as an **Input Stream** and data flowing *out* as an **Output Stream**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **water pipeline**. The *tap* (source) sends water to your *bucket* (program) — that is an **Input Stream**. When you pour water *from* your bucket into a *drain* (destination), that is an **Output Stream**. Just as pipes carry water byte-by-byte or in bulk, Java streams carry data **byte-by-byte (8-bit)** or **character-by-character (16-bit Unicode)**.

### Two Categories of Streams

| Stream Type | Unit | Base Classes | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Byte Streams** | 8-bit bytes | `InputStream`, `OutputStream` | Binary data (images, audio, executables) |
| **Character Streams** | 16-bit Unicode chars | `Reader`, `Writer` | Text data (`.txt`, `.csv`, source code) |

### The Three Standard Streams
Every Java program automatically has access to:
- **`System.in`** → Standard Input Stream (keyboard, type `InputStream`)
- **`System.out`** → Standard Output Stream (console, type `PrintStream`)
- **`System.err`** → Standard Error Stream (console, type `PrintStream`)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `System.in` is a raw byte stream. To read a full line of text from the keyboard, you must wrap it — e.g., `BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `throws` Clause — Operational Rules

### Syntax
```java
returnType methodName(parameters) throws ExceptionType1, ExceptionType2, ... {
    // method body
}
```

### Operational Logic (Why and How)
- **Why `throws` exists:** Java enforces a **checked-exception contract**. If your method body contains code that may throw a checked exception, you must either (a) wrap it in `try-catch` OR (b) declare it using `throws`.
- **How it works:** When a method declares `throws IOException`, the compiler checks that **every caller** either catches `IOException` or further declares it. Failure to do so = **compile-time error**.
- **Multiple exceptions:** You can declare multiple types separated by commas: `throws IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException`.
- **Subclass exception rule:** You can declare a **parent** class to throw a **broader** range: `throws Exception` covers all checked exceptions.
- **Inheritance contract:** An overriding method in a subclass can **only throw the same or narrower** set of checked exceptions as the parent (Liskov Substitution Principle).

### Use Cases in Engineering
- **File I/O methods** → declare `throws IOException`
- **Database methods** → declare `throws SQLException`
- **Network methods** → declare `throws MalformedURLException`
- **Thread `run()`** → overrides `throws` from `Runnable`; cannot throw checked exceptions

---

## 2.2 The `finally` Block — Operational Rules

### When `finally` Executes (The Execution Matrix)

| Scenario | Does `finally` execute? |
|---|---|
| No exception in `try` | **Yes** |
| Exception occurs and is caught | **Yes** |
| Exception occurs and is **not** caught (propagated) | **Yes** |
| `return` statement inside `try` | **Yes** |
| `return` statement inside `catch` | **Yes** |
| `System.exit(0)` called | **No** |
| JVM crashes (e.g., `Runtime.getRuntime().halt(0)`) | **No** |
| Infinite loop/deadlock in `try` | **No** (cannot reach finally) |
| Thread killed via `Thread.stop()` (deprecated) | **No** |

### `finally` vs `return` — The Critical Override
If both `try` and `finally` contain `return` statements, **the `finally` block's return value wins** because it executes last. The `try`'s return is *temporarily held*, then overridden by `finally`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Placing a `return` statement inside a `finally` block is **considered bad practice** and often results in lost return values from `try`/`catch`. It may also suppress exceptions thrown in `try`.

### The `try-finally` Construct (No Catch)
Java allows a `try` block to be paired with `finally` **without** a `catch`:
```java
try {
    // risky code
} finally {
    // cleanup — always runs
}
```
This pattern is heavily used for **resource management** (closing file handles, network sockets).

---

## 2.3 I/O Stream Class Hierarchy

### Byte Stream Hierarchy
```
java.lang.Object
  └── java.io.InputStream (abstract)
        ├── FileInputStream
        ├── ByteArrayInputStream
        ├── FilterInputStream
        │     ├── BufferedInputStream
        │     └── DataInputStream
        └── ObjectInputStream
  └── java.io.OutputStream (abstract)
        ├── FileOutputStream
        ├── ByteArrayOutputStream
        ├── FilterOutputStream
        │     ├── BufferedOutputStream
        │     └── DataOutputStream
        ├── PrintStream
        └── ObjectOutputStream
```

### Character Stream Hierarchy
```
java.lang.Object
  └── java.io.Reader (abstract)
        ├── InputStreamReader
        │     └── FileReader
        ├── BufferedReader
        └── StringReader
  └── java.io.Writer (abstract)
        ├── OutputStreamWriter
        │     └── FileWriter
        ├── BufferedWriter
        ├── PrintWriter
        └── StringWriter
```

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Methods & Equivalents

| Operation | Byte Stream Method | Character Stream Method |
|---|---|---|
| Read single unit | `int read()` | `int read()` |
| Read into array | `int read(byte[] b)` | `int read(char[] c)` |
| Read a line | Not available | `BufferedReader.readLine()` |
| Write single unit | `void write(int b)` | `void write(int c)` |
| Write array | `void write(byte[] b)` | `void write(char[] c)` |
| Close stream | `close()` | `close()` |
| Flush buffer | `flush()` | `flush()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL DIFFERENCE:** Byte stream `read()` returns `int` (0–255 or -1 for EOF). Character stream `read()` returns `int` (0–65535 or -1 for EOF). Both return `-1` to signal **End-Of-File (EOF)**.

### Common Constructor Patterns (KTU High-Yield)

| Class | Constructor | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `FileInputStream` | `FileInputStream(String name)` | Open file for byte reading |
| `FileOutputStream` | `FileOutputStream(String name)` | Open file for byte writing |
| `FileReader` | `FileReader(String name)` | Open file for char reading |
| `FileWriter` | `FileWriter(String name)` | Open file for char writing |
| `BufferedReader` | `BufferedReader(Reader r)` | Wrap for efficient line reading |
| `PrintWriter` | `PrintWriter(Writer w)` | Wrap for `println()` support |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tracing `try-catch-finally` Execution Flow

### Problem Setup
Given the following Java program, **derive the exact output** produced and identify the return value.

```java
public class FinallyTrace {
    public static int compute() {
        try {
            System.out.println("Step 1: Try block");
            int result = 10 / 0;   // ArithmeticException
            return 100;
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Step 2: Catch block");
            return 200;
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Step 3: Finally block");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int val = compute();
        System.out.println("Returned: " + val);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Derivation

**Step 1** — JVM invokes `compute()`. Control enters the `try` block.

**Step 2** — `System.out.println("Step 1: Try block")` executes.
Output so far: `Step 1: Try block`

**Step 3** — The expression `10 / 0` is evaluated. Since 0 is an integer divisor, this triggers **`java.lang.ArithmeticException`** at runtime (integer division by zero).

**Step 4** — The exception propagates. The JVM searches for a matching `catch` block. `ArithmeticException` matches the declared `catch (ArithmeticException e)`. Control transfers to the catch.

**Step 5** — `System.out.println("Step 2: Catch block")` executes.
Output so far: `Step 1: Try block \n Step 2: Catch block`

**Step 6** — The `return 200;` statement is reached. **However**, before the method actually returns to the caller, the JVM **suspends** the return and **executes the `finally` block** first.

**Step 7** — `System.out.println("Step 3: Finally block")` executes.
Output so far:
```
Step 1: Try block
Step 2: Catch block
Step 3: Finally block
```

**Step 8** — After the `finally` block completes, the previously-suspended `return 200` resumes. The method returns the value **`200`** to `main()`.

**Step 9** — `main` prints `System.out.println("Returned: " + val)`.

### Final Output (Proven)
```
Step 1: Try block
Step 2: Catch block
Step 3: Finally block
Returned: 200
```

### Analytical Matrix — Return Value Behaviour

$$
\text{Returned Value} = \begin{cases} \text{return from try} & \text{if no exception} \wedge \text{finally has no return} \\ \text{return from catch} & \text{if caught exception} \wedge \text{finally has no return} \\ \text{return from finally} & \text{if finally contains a return} \end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: File Reading with `FileInputStream` — Byte Trace

### Problem
Read a file `data.txt` containing the ASCII bytes `H`, `e`, `l`, `l`, `o` (decimal values 72, 101, 108, 108, 111) using `FileInputStream`.

### Code
```java
import java.io.FileInputStream;
import java.io.IOException;

public class ByteRead {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("data.txt");
        int ch;
        while ((ch = fis.read()) != -1) {
            System.out.print((char) ch);
        }
        fis.close();
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace Table

| Iteration | `fis.read()` Returns | Decimal | `(char)` Cast | Loop Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Byte from file | 72 | `'H'` | `72 != -1` → true |
| 2 | Byte from file | 101 | `'e'` | `101 != -1` → true |
| 3 | Byte from file | 108 | `'l'` | `108 != -1` → true |
| 4 | Byte from file | 108 | `'l'` | `108 != -1` → true |
| 5 | Byte from file | 111 | `'o'` | `111 != -1` → true |
| 6 | End of file | **-1** | — | `-1 != -1` → false (exit) |

### Output
```
Hello
```

### The Mathematical Relation (Read Loop)

$$
\text{while}\bigl((ch = \text{read}()) \neq \text{EOF}\bigr) \quad \text{where} \quad \text{EOF} = -1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `int` and not `byte`?** A `byte` cannot represent `-1` distinctly from valid data (range −128 to 127). The `int` return type (0 to 255 for data, −1 for EOF) provides a unique sentinel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: BufferedReader Line-by-Line Read

### Problem
Read a file `notes.txt` with two lines: `KTU` and `2024`.

### Code
```java
import java.io.*;

public class LineRead {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("notes.txt"));
        String line;
        while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
            System.out.println(line);
        }
        br.close();
    }
}
```

### Trace Table

| Call | `br.readLine()` Returns | Loop Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `"KTU"` | `"KTU" != null` → true | Print `KTU` |
| 2 | `"2024"` | `"2024" != null` → true | Print `2024` |
| 3 | `null` (EOF) | `null != null` → false | Exit loop, then `close()` |

### Output
```
KTU
2024
```

> [!NOTE]
> **EOF distinction:** `BufferedReader.readLine()` returns `null` at EOF, **not** `-1`. This is a frequent exam pitfall distinguishing character streams from byte streams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Execution Flow of `try-catch-finally`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program enters try block] --> B{Exception thrown?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute remaining try code]
    C --> D[finally block executes]
    D --> E[Method returns normally]
    B -- Yes --> F{Does a matching catch exist?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Execute matching catch block]
    G --> H[finally block executes]
    H --> I[Method returns from catch]
    F -- No --> J[finally block executes]
    J --> K[Exception propagated to caller]
    K --> L[Caller handles or program terminates]
```

### Diagram Reading Guide
- **Diamond nodes** = decision points (boolean conditions).
- **Rectangle nodes** = actions/states.
- The `finally` block appears on **every successful return path** — illustrating its **guaranteed execution** property.

---

## 4.2 Diagram: Java I/O Stream Class Hierarchy (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BYTE["BYTE STREAMS 8-bit"]
        IS[InputStream abstract] --> FIS[FileInputStream]
        IS --> BIS[BufferedInputStream]
        IS --> DIS[DataInputStream]
        IS --> OIS[ObjectInputStream]
        OS[OutputStream abstract] --> FOS[FileOutputStream]
        OS --> BOS[BufferedOutputStream]
        OS --> DOS[DataOutputStream]
        OS --> PS[PrintStream]
        OS --> OOS[ObjectOutputStream]
    end
    subgraph CHAR["CHARACTER STREAMS 16-bit"]
        R[Reader abstract] --> ISR[InputStreamReader]
        ISR --> FR[FileReader]
        R --> BR[BufferedReader]
        W[Writer abstract] --> OSW[OutputStreamWriter]
        OSW --> FW[FileWriter]
        W --> BW[BufferedWriter]
        W --> PW[PrintWriter]
    end
    BR -.bridges.-> ISR
    BW -.bridges.-> OSW
```

### Diagram Reading Guide
- **Subgraph BYTE** = all 8-bit byte-oriented streams.
- **Subgraph CHAR** = all 16-bit Unicode character streams.
- **Dotted "bridges"** = the conversion point between byte and character worlds (`InputStreamReader` and `OutputStreamWriter` are the official *bridges*).
- `BufferedReader` is a *decorator* that wraps any `Reader` to add efficient line-reading capability.

---

## 4.3 Diagram: `throws` Declaration Propagation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[main method] -- calls --> B[methodA throws IOException]
    B -- calls --> C[methodB throws IOException]
    C -- calls --> D[readFile throws IOException]
    D -- reads from --> E[File System]
    E -.throws.-> D
    D -.propagates.-> C
    C -.propagates.-> B
    B -.propagates.-> A
    A -- must handle or declare --> A
```

### Diagram Reading Guide
- The **exception percolates upward** through the call stack if not caught.
- If `main` also declares `throws IOException`, the JVM's default uncaught-exception handler will print the stack trace and terminate.
- This is the **"ducking" pattern** — each method passes the responsibility to its caller.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Differentiate between `throw` and `throws` in Java. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `throw` | `throws` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Used to *explicitly throw* an exception object | Used to *declare* exceptions a method may propagate |
| **Location** | Inside the method body | In the method signature |
| **Number of exceptions** | Throws only **one** exception at a time | Can declare **multiple** exceptions separated by commas |
| **Syntax** | `throw new ArithmeticException("div by zero");` | `void read() throws IOException { ... }` |
| **Execution** | Runtime action (executes a throw) | Compile-time declaration (no execution) |
| **Applies to** | Both checked and unchecked exceptions | Mainly checked exceptions |

**Example:**
```java
void demo() throws IOException {       // throws — declaration
    if (error)
        throw new IOException("Bad");  // throw  — actual action
}
```

---

### Q2. Why does the `finally` block execute even after a `return` statement? Mention one real-world scenario where this is critical. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The `finally` block executes after `return` because the **JVM specification mandates** that before a method returns control to its caller, any pending `finally` blocks in the method's call frame must be completed. The `return` value is **temporarily saved**, the `finally` block runs, and then the saved return value is delivered to the caller.

**Real-world scenario:** **Database Connection Management.** Consider opening a database connection in `try` and executing a query. Whether the query succeeds, fails, or returns early, the connection **must be closed** in `finally` to prevent connection-pool exhaustion. If `finally` did not run after `return`, connections would leak and crash the application.

---

## Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Regulation:** Part B questions carry internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

#### (a) Explain the `throws` clause in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules for using `throws` with method overriding. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The `throws` clause is a Java keyword used in a method's signature to declare that the method may pass one or more exceptions to its caller. It does not handle the exception — it merely declares it.

**Syntax:**
```java
returnType methodName(params) throws ExceptionList { ... }
```

**Example Program:**
```java
import java.io.*;

class ThrowsDemo {
    // Declares it may throw IOException
    static void readFile(String name) throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(name);  // may throw FileNotFoundException
        fr.read();                              // may throw IOException
        fr.close();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            readFile("data.txt");
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Handled: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
1. `readFile()` declares `throws IOException` in its signature.
2. Inside, `FileReader` and `read()` may throw checked exceptions.
3. Since `readFile()` does not catch them, they propagate up.
4. `main()` is **forced** by the compiler to either catch `IOException` or re-declare it.

**Rules for `throws` with Method Overriding:**

1. **Same or narrower exceptions only:** An overriding method can throw the **same**, **subclass**, or **fewer** checked exceptions — never a broader or new unchecked one beyond parent's contract.
2. **Unchecked exceptions are free:** Overriding methods may throw any `RuntimeException` even if parent does not declare them.
3. **Error class is excluded:** Overriding methods cannot throw `java.lang.Error` or its subclasses.

**Example:**
```java
class Parent {
    void show() throws IOException { }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    @Override
    void show() throws FileNotFoundException { }  // OK — narrower
    // void show() throws Exception { }             // ERROR — broader
}
```

#### (b) Write a Java program that demonstrates the execution of the `finally` block in the following three scenarios: (i) when no exception occurs, (ii) when an exception is caught, and (iii) when an exception is not caught. Also explain the output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
public class FinallyScenarios {
    static void scenario1() {
        try {
            System.out.println("S1: Try block — no exception");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("S1: Catch block");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("S1: Finally block — ALWAYS runs");
        }
    }

    static void scenario2() {
        try {
            int x = 5 / 0;   // ArithmeticException
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("S2: Catch block — exception handled");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("S2: Finally block — ALWAYS runs");
        }
    }

    static void scenario3() {
        try {
            int x = 5 / 0;   // ArithmeticException
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {  // mismatch
            System.out.println("S2: Catch block");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("S3: Finally block — STILL runs");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        scenario1();
        System.out.println("---");
        scenario2();
        System.out.println("---");
        scenario3();
    }
}
```

**Output Explanation:**

```
S1: Try block — no exception
S1: Finally block — ALWAYS runs
---
S2: Catch block — exception handled
S2: Finally block — ALWAYS runs
---
S3: Finally block — STILL runs
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
        at FinallyScenarios.scenario3(...)
```

**Explanation:**
- **Scenario 1:** No exception → `try` completes → `finally` executes.
- **Scenario 2:** Exception caught → `catch` runs → `finally` executes.
- **Scenario 3:** Exception not caught → `finally` still executes → then exception propagates to `main` and aborts the program (JVM default handler).

**Key Inference:** The `finally` block is **agnostic** to whether the exception is caught — it runs in **all** cases.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

#### (a) Explain the Java I/O stream hierarchy. Differentiate between byte streams and character streams with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Java I/O Stream Hierarchy:**

Java organizes I/O into two parallel hierarchies rooted at four abstract classes in `java.io`:

1. **Byte Streams** — root: `InputStream` (read) and `OutputStream` (write). Handle data as raw 8-bit bytes. Suitable for binary data (images, audio, executables, serialized objects).

2. **Character Streams** — root: `Reader` (read) and `Writer` (write). Handle data as 16-bit Unicode characters. Suitable for text data (`.txt`, `.csv`, source code, JSON).

**Concrete Subclasses:**

| Hierarchy | Reading Class | Writing Class |
|---|---|---|
| File (byte) | `FileInputStream` | `FileOutputStream` |
| File (char) | `FileReader` | `FileWriter` |
| Buffered (byte) | `BufferedInputStream` | `BufferedOutputStream` |
| Buffered (char) | `BufferedReader` | `BufferedWriter` |
| Data (byte) | `DataInputStream` | `DataOutputStream` |
| Object | `ObjectInputStream` | `ObjectOutputStream` |
| Print | — | `PrintStream`, `PrintWriter` |

**Byte Stream Example (Copy a binary file):**
```java
import java.io.*;
public class ByteCopy {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        FileInputStream in = new FileInputStream("src.bin");
        FileOutputStream out = new FileOutputStream("dst.bin");
        int b;
        while ((b = in.read()) != -1) {
            out.write(b);
        }
        in.close();
        out.close();
    }
}
```

**Character Stream Example (Copy a text file line-by-line):**
```java
import java.io.*;
public class CharCopy {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("src.txt"));
        PrintWriter pw = new PrintWriter(new FileWriter("dst.txt"));
        String line;
        while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
            pw.println(line);
        }
        br.close();
        pw.close();
    }
}
```

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Byte Stream | Character Stream |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | 8-bit byte | 16-bit Unicode char |
| **Base classes** | `InputStream`, `OutputStream` | `Reader`, `Writer` |
| **EOF sentinel** | `-1` | `null` (for `readLine`) or `-1` (for `read()`) |
| **Data type** | Binary | Text |
| **Encoding aware** | No | Yes (uses default charset) |
| **Performance for text** | Slower (byte-to-char conversion needed) | Native |

#### (b) Write a Java program to read a text file `input.txt` using `FileInputStream` wrapped in a `BufferedInputStream`, and write the contents to `output.txt` using `FileOutputStream`. Handle exceptions appropriately. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.io.*;

public class FileCopyBuffered {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FileInputStream fis = null;
        BufferedInputStream bis = null;
        FileOutputStream fos = null;
        BufferedOutputStream bos = null;

        try {
            // Open streams
            fis = new FileInputStream("input.txt");
            bis = new BufferedInputStream(fis);
            fos = new FileOutputStream("output.txt");
            bos = new BufferedOutputStream(fos);

            int data;
            // Read byte-by-byte until EOF
            while ((data = bis.read()) != -1) {
                bos.write(data);
            }
            System.out.println("File copied successfully.");

        } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("File not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("I/O error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            // Close in REVERSE order of opening
            try { if (bos != null) bos.close(); } catch (IOException e) { }
            try { if (fos != null) fos.close(); } catch (IOException e) { }
            try { if (bis != null) bis.close(); } catch (IOException e) { }
            try { if (fis != null) fis.close(); } catch (IOException e) { }
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

1. **Stream Chaining (Decorator Pattern):** `BufferedInputStream` wraps `FileInputStream` to add buffering, drastically reducing disk I/O calls.
2. **EOF Check:** `data != -1` correctly detects the end of the file.
3. **Exception Handling:** Two distinct `catch` blocks handle `FileNotFoundException` (a subclass of `IOException`) and general I/O errors.
4. **Resource Cleanup:** `finally` block closes streams in **reverse order of opening** to prevent resource leaks. The `bos.close()` automatically flushes the buffer.

**Why use `BufferedInputStream`?** Direct `FileInputStream.read()` makes one disk I/O call per byte. `BufferedInputStream` reads a large block (default 8192 bytes) into memory once, then serves bytes from RAM — vastly faster.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Closing streams in `finally`:** Students often forget to handle `IOException` thrown by `close()` itself. The examiner awards **0.5 mark** for each `close()` wrapped in its own try-catch.
> 2. **Null check before `close()`:** Forgetting `if (fis != null)` causes `NullPointerException` if the file was never opened. This is a **favourite trick question**.
> 3. **`throws` vs try-catch in `main`:** Declaring `throws IOException` in `main` is valid but considered poor design in production code. Examiners prefer explicit try-catch.
> 4. **Confusing `readLine()` (returns `null`)** with `read()` (returns `-1`) is a **guaranteed mark-loss** point in I/O questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **`throws`** is a **declaration** placed in the method signature; it does **not** throw an exception. It is mandatory for **checked exceptions** and follows the **"declare or handle"** rule.
- ✅ **`throw`** is an **action statement** placed inside the method body; it **actually throws** an exception object using `throw new ...`.
- ✅ **`finally`** block **always executes** after `try`/`catch`, even if a `return` statement is encountered — except when `System.exit()` is called or the JVM crashes.
- ✅ A `finally` block that contains a `return` statement **overrides** any return from `try` or `catch` and **suppresses** propagating exceptions.
- ✅ **`try-finally`** is valid Java syntax even without `catch` — useful for cleanup when you want exceptions to propagate.
- ✅ **Byte streams** (`InputStream`/`OutputStream`) handle **8-bit binary data**; **character streams** (`Reader`/`Writer`) handle **16-bit Unicode text**.
- ✅ Byte stream `read()` returns **`int`** in range 0–255, with **-1** as EOF.
- ✅ `BufferedReader.readLine()` returns **`String`**, with **`null`** as EOF.
- ✅ The **bridge classes** between byte and character worlds are `InputStreamReader` and `OutputStreamWriter`.
- ✅ **`System.in`, `System.out`, `System.err`** are the three automatically available standard streams.
- ✅ **Always close I/O resources** in `finally` (or use try-with-resources in Java 7+) to prevent **resource leaks**.
- ✅ The **decorator pattern** is used heavily in `java.io`: `BufferedInputStream(FileInputStream)`, `BufferedReader(FileReader)`, etc.
- ✅ When **overriding** a method, the child can throw **same or narrower** checked exceptions only — never a broader checked exception.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, **always draw the I/O hierarchy diagram** and **trace the program output** step-by-step in your answer for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading Console Input
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reading Console Input

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Console Input** in Object-Oriented Programming (specifically Java) refers to the mechanism by which a running program receives data from the standard input stream (typically the keyboard) during runtime. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this is classified under **Stream-based I/O** and is implemented primarily using classes encapsulated within the `java.util` and `java.io` packages.

The three canonical approaches mandated by the syllabus are:

1. **`Scanner` class** (`java.util.Scanner`) — Token-based, high-level input parsing.
2. **`BufferedReader` class** (`java.io.BufferedReader`) — Character-stream based, line-based reading.
3. **`Console` class** (`java.io.Console`) — Secure console interaction (password masking, no echo).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Mandate:** The `Scanner` class is the *de facto* primary method tested in End Semester Examinations (ESE) for the **PEEIT741 - Object Oriented Programming** course, owing to its simplicity, type-safety, and direct alignment with the `java.util` package taught in Module 3.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting at a **restaurant counter** placing an order:

- **The Counter (System.in)** is the fixed input pipe — it never changes.
- **The Waiter (`Scanner` object)** is an intelligent intermediary. You tell the waiter *"give me the next integer"*, and the waiter reads from the counter, parses it, and hands you a clean `int` value. You don't worry about how it's typed, encoded, or buffered.
- **The Order Slip (`variable` like `int x`)** is what you ultimately receive — a perfectly typed value ready for processing.

If you used **`BufferedReader`**, the waiter would just hand you a *raw line of text* (a `String`), and *you* would have to parse it manually (e.g., `Integer.parseInt()`). It's more work, but you get the raw ingredients to cook as you wish.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** `Scanner` = *smart waiter who pre-processes*. `BufferedReader` = *raw ingredient delivery*. `Console` = *VIP secure channel for sensitive data*.

## 1.3 Core Technical Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Default Standard Input Stream:** `System.in` (of type `InputStream`).
- **Default Token Delimiter for `Scanner`:** Whitespace (space, tab, newline).
- **Buffer Size for `BufferedReader`:** **8192 characters** (default).
- **Scanner Resource:** Must be closed using `.close()` to prevent **resource leak** (penalized in ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the `Scanner` Class

The `Scanner` class (introduced in **Java 1.5**) is a *final* class located in `java.util`. It implements the `Iterator<String>` interface and uses **regular expressions** internally to parse primitive types and strings.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Decomposition)

1. **Import Statement** — The class lives in `java.util`, so the directive `import java.util.Scanner;` (or `import java.util.*;`) must precede the class declaration. Without it, the compiler raises *"cannot find symbol"*.
2. **Instantiation** — A `Scanner` object is constructed by passing `System.in` to its constructor: `Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);`. This binds the Scanner to the keyboard input stream.
3. **Tokenization** — When a read method (`nextInt()`, `next()`, etc.) is invoked, the Scanner internally reads bytes from `System.in`, decodes them using the platform's default charset, and buffers them.
4. **Pattern Matching** — A hidden `Pattern` object matches the buffered text against the expected token type (integer, float, etc.).
5. **Type Conversion** — The matched token is converted to the requested primitive type and returned to the caller.
6. **Exception Path** — If the token does not match the expected type, a `java.util.InputMismatchException` is thrown (an unchecked exception extending `RuntimeException`).
7. **Resource Cleanup** — `.close()` releases the underlying stream; failing to do so may cause a *resource leak warning* during compilation.

### 2.1.2 The Critical Distinction: `next()` vs `nextLine()`

This is the **single most frequently asked distinction** in KTU board exams.

| Method | Reads Until | Preserves Whitespace? | Leaves Delimiter in Buffer? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `next()` | Any whitespace (space, `\t`, `\n`) | No (token is trimmed) | Yes — the delimiter remains |
| `nextLine()` | Line terminator (`\n` or `\r\n`) | Yes | No — the `\n` is consumed and discarded |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Calling `nextInt()` followed by `nextLine()` causes the `nextLine()` to return an **empty string** because `nextInt()` leaves the trailing newline character in the input buffer. Always consume the leftover newline with an extra `sc.nextLine();` or use `sc.nextLine()` consistently and parse manually.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table (Cheat Sheet)

### 2.2.1 The `Scanner` Method Catalog

| Method Signature | Return Type | Reads | Throws on Failure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `nextInt()` | `int` | Next integer token | `InputMismatchException` |
| `nextLong()` | `long` | Next long integer token | `InputMismatchException` |
| `nextFloat()` | `float` | Next float token | `InputMismatchException` |
| `nextDouble()` | `double` | Next double token | `InputMismatchException` |
| `nextBoolean()` | `boolean` | `"true"` or `"false"` | `InputMismatchException` |
| `next()` | `String` | Next whitespace-delimited word | `NoSuchElementException` |
| `nextLine()` | `String` | Entire current line | `NoSuchElementException` |
| `next().charAt(0)` | `char` | First character of next word | `InputMismatchException` |
| `hasNextInt()` | `boolean` | Tests if next token is `int` | None |
| `close()` | `void` | Closes the Scanner | None |

### 2.2.2 Comparison of Three Input Classes

| Feature | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` | `Console` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Package | `java.util` | `java.io` | `java.io` |
| Thread-Safe | No | Yes (synchronized) | Yes (synchronized) |
| Parsing | Automatic (built-in) | Manual (`Integer.parseInt`) | Manual |
| Speed | **Slow** (regex overhead) | **Fast** (buffered) | **Fast** |
| Password Support | No | No | **Yes** (`readPassword()`) |
| Available in IDE | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** (null in Eclipse/IntelliJ) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **★★★★★ (Highest)** | **★★★** | **★ (Rare)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In production systems, `Scanner` is used for **CLI tools and prototypes**; `BufferedReader` is used for **high-throughput log/file readers**; `Console` is used for **security-sensitive applications** like password entry, SSH clients, and database admin tools.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough: The `Scanner` Mechanism

### 3.1.1 The Canonical "Read Three Integers" Program

```java
import java.util.Scanner;  // Step 1: Import the class from java.util package

public class SumOfThree {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 2: Instantiate Scanner bound to System.in (keyboard)
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Step 3: Prompt the user (output, not input — for UX)
        System.out.print("Enter three integers separated by space: ");

        // Step 4: Tokenize and parse three integers from the input buffer
        int a = sc.nextInt();   // reads token, parses to int
        int b = sc.nextInt();   // reads next token
        int c = sc.nextInt();   // reads next token

        // Step 5: Perform the computation
        int sum = a + b + c;

        // Step 6: Display the result
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);

        // Step 7: Release the resource (best practice)
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Line-by-Line Logical Deduction

- **Line `import java.util.Scanner;`:** This is a *single-type import declaration*. The Java compiler resolves the unqualified name `Scanner` in subsequent code to the fully qualified `java.util.Scanner`. Without this, the compiler reports `error: cannot find symbol`.
- **Line `Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);`:** `System.in` is a `public static final` field of type `java.io.InputStream` representing the standard input stream (connected to the keyboard by default in a console application). The `Scanner` constructor wraps this raw byte stream into a token-aware reader.
- **Line `sc.nextInt();`:** Internally performs the following chain of operations:
  1. Calls the private method `next()` to obtain the next whitespace-delimited token as a `String`.
  2. Invokes a `Pattern` matcher to verify the token conforms to the regex for signed decimal integers: `[\-+]?[0-9]+`.
  3. On match, calls `Integer.parseInt(token)` to convert the `String` to a primitive `int`.
  4. On mismatch, throws `InputMismatchException`.
- **Line `sc.close();`:** Closes the underlying `InputStream` (`System.in`). Once closed, the Scanner cannot be reused. In a long-running application, this prevents the JVM from holding an OS file descriptor indefinitely.

## 3.2 The `nextLine()` After `nextInt()` — Full Analytical Resolution

This is the **highest-value debugging scenario** in KTU 2024 Scheme labs.

### 3.2.1 The Problematic Code

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter your age: ");
        int age = sc.nextInt();        // User types: 25<Enter>
        System.out.print("Enter your full name: ");
        String name = sc.nextLine();   // ❌ BUG: returns empty string!
        System.out.println("Age: " + age + ", Name: '" + name + "'");
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Buffer Trace

Let the user input be: `25\nJohn Doe\n`

| Step | Action | Input Buffer State | Variable State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | Initial state | `25\nJohn Doe\n` | (empty) |
| 1 | `sc.nextInt()` invoked | Tokenizer reads `25`, converts to `int` | `age = 25` |
| 2 | `nextInt()` returns | Buffer now starts at `\nJohn Doe\n` | `age = 25` |
| 3 | `sc.nextLine()` invoked | Reads up to first `\n` → returns `""` | `name = ""` |
| 4 | `nextLine()` returns | Buffer now starts at `John Doe\n` | `name = ""` |
| 5 | Output rendered | — | Prints `Age: 25, Name: ''` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution Formula (The Golden Fix):** Insert a *consume-the-newline* call:
> ```java
> int age = sc.nextInt();
> sc.nextLine();              // consume the leftover newline
> String name = sc.nextLine(); // now reads "John Doe"
> ```
> This is worth **2 marks** in any KTU 2024 ESE question involving mixed input types.

## 3.3 Mathematical Modeling of the Tokenizer

The `Scanner` class uses a `java.util.regex.Pattern` under the hood. For `nextInt()`, the implicit pattern is:

$$
P_{\text{int}} = [\textcolor{red}{-}\textcolor{red}{+}\text{]}\,\text{?}\,\,[0-9]\,\text{+}
$$

For `nextDouble()`, the pattern is more complex:

$$
P_{\text{double}} = [\textcolor{red}{-}\textcolor{red}{+}\text{]}\,\text{?}\,\,[0-9]\,\text{*}\,\,[.]\,\text{?}\,[0-9]\,\text{+}\,[(\text{e}\,[\textcolor{red}{-}\textcolor{red}{+}]?[0-9]+)]\,\text{?}
$$

These regex patterns are **why `Scanner` is slower** than `BufferedReader` — every read involves pattern compilation and matching overhead.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Reading a `char` Input

Java's `Scanner` class has **no `nextChar()`** method. The standard idiom is:

```java
char ch = sc.next().charAt(0);
```

**Step-by-step deduction:**

1. `sc.next()` reads the next whitespace-delimited token as a `String` (e.g., `"Hello"`).
2. `.charAt(0)` invokes the `String.charAt(int index)` method, which returns the character at the specified index.
3. The `char` primitive is extracted and stored in `ch`.

**Boundary Condition:** If the user enters an empty token (e.g., presses Enter twice), `sc.next()` will throw `NoSuchElementException`. This is a common KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The `Scanner` Input Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Keyboard Hardware] --> B[Operating System STDIN Buffer]
    B --> C[System.in - java.io.InputStream]
    C --> D[Scanner Constructor Wraps System.in]
    D --> E[Internal CharBuffer - 1024 chars default]
    E --> F{nextInt / nextLine / nextDouble invoked?}
    F --> G[Token Delimiter Scanner reads whitespace]
    G --> H[Regex Pattern Matcher validates token type]
    H --> I{Token matches expected type?}
    I --> J[Yes - Return parsed primitive or String]
    I --> K[No - Throw InputMismatchException]
    J --> L[Variable receives value]
    L --> M[sc.close called?]
    M --> N[Yes - Release OS file descriptor]
    M --> O[No - Resource leak warning]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Three Input Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Scanner [Scanner java.util]
        S1[User types 25] --> S2[nextInt parses automatically]
        S2 --> S3[Returns int 25]
    end

    subgraph Buffered [BufferedReader java.io]
        B1[User types 25] --> B2[readLine returns String 25]
        B2 --> B3[Integer.parseInt manual conversion]
        B3 --> B4[Returns int 25]
    end

    subgraph ConsoleAPI [Console java.io]
        C1[User types 25] --> C2[readLine returns String 25]
        C2 --> C3[Manual parsing required]
        C3 --> C4[Returns int 25]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Object {
        <<java.lang.Object>>
    }
    class Scanner {
        <<java.util.Scanner>>
        +Scanner(InputStream source)
        +int nextInt()
        +double nextDouble()
        +String next()
        +String nextLine()
        +boolean hasNextInt()
        +void close()
    }
    class InputStream {
        <<java.io.InputStream>>
        +abstract int read()
    }
    class Pattern {
        <<java.util.regex.Pattern>>
        -String regex
        +static Pattern compile(String r)
    }
    Object <|-- Scanner
    Scanner --> InputStream : wraps
    Scanner --> Pattern : uses internally
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Scanner Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : new Scanner(System.in)
    Created --> Open : construction complete
    Open --> Reading : nextInt / nextLine called
    Reading --> Open : method returns value
    Reading --> Error : InputMismatchException thrown
    Error --> Open : exception caught and recovered
    Open --> Closed : sc.close called
    Closed --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between `next()` and `nextLine()` methods of the `Scanner` class in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**

| Aspect | `next()` | `nextLine()` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Delimiter (1 Mark)** | Reads until any whitespace | Reads until newline `\n` |
| **Whitespace handling (1 Mark)** | Truncates trailing whitespace, delimiter left in buffer | Consumes and discards the newline |
| **Use case (1 Mark)** | Used for single-word tokens | Used for full-line input including spaces |

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the role of `System.in` in Java? Why must a `Scanner` be closed after use?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **`System.in` (2 Marks):** `System.in` is a `public static final` object of type `java.io.InputStream` that represents the **standard input stream** of the JVM, typically connected to the keyboard. It provides a raw byte-oriented channel for reading input, which the `Scanner` class wraps to provide token-based parsing.

2. **Closing `Scanner` (1 Mark):** Calling `sc.close()` releases the underlying system resource (the file descriptor associated with `System.in`). Failing to close it may lead to a **resource leak**, triggering compiler warnings and exhausting OS resources in long-running applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3 Internal Choice Option A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program that reads the **name**, **roll number**, and **CGPA** of a student from the console using the `Scanner` class and displays the entered details in a formatted manner. Handle the case where the user might enter a non-numeric value for CGPA using exception handling.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the internal architecture of the `Scanner` class with a neat diagram. How does it differ from the `BufferedReader` class in terms of performance and parsing?

---

#### Part (a) — Complete Model Solution

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.InputMismatchException;

public class StudentDetails {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        System.out.print("Enter student name: ");
        String name = sc.nextLine();   // safe: no prior nextInt

        System.out.print("Enter roll number (integer): ");
        long roll = 0;
        boolean valid = false;
        while (!valid) {
            try {
                roll = sc.nextLong();
                valid = true;
            } catch (InputMismatchException e) {
                System.out.print("Invalid! Enter numeric roll number: ");
                sc.next();   // discard bad token
            }
        }

        System.out.print("Enter CGPA: ");
        double cgpa = 0.0;
        valid = false;
        while (!valid) {
            try {
                cgpa = sc.nextDouble();
                valid = true;
            } catch (InputMismatchException e) {
                System.out.print("Invalid! Enter numeric CGPA: ");
                sc.next();
            }
        }

        System.out.println("\n--- Student Record ---");
        System.out.printf("Name    : %s%n", name);
        System.out.printf("Roll No : %d%n", roll);
        System.out.printf("CGPA    : %.2f%n", cgpa);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation Breakdown (7 Marks):**
- Correct `import` statements and `Scanner` instantiation: **1 Mark**
- Reading `String`, `long`, `double` using appropriate methods: **2 Marks**
- Exception handling using `try-catch` with `InputMismatchException`: **2 Marks**
- Formatted output using `printf` and resource cleanup: **1 Mark**
- Logical correctness and compilation: **1 Mark**

---

#### Part (b) — Complete Model Solution

**Internal Architecture of `Scanner` Class (4 Marks):**

The `Scanner` class internally maintains the following fields:

- `private Pattern delimiter` — holds the delimiter pattern (default: whitespace).
- `private int horizon` — internal marker for lookahead position.
- `private CharSequence source` — reference to the source string being scanned.
- `private Matcher matcher` — used for pattern matching operations.
- A `BufferedReader`-like underlying stream reader for reading from `System.in`.

When `nextInt()` is invoked, the following sequence occurs:
1. The delimiter `Pattern` is consulted to locate the next token boundary.
2. The token is extracted as a substring.
3. The integer `Pattern` (`[+\-]?\d+`) is applied via a `Matcher`.
4. On success, `Integer.parseInt()` is called on the matched substring.
5. On failure, `InputMismatchException` is thrown.

**Comparison with `BufferedReader` (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Performance** | Slower (regex overhead, unbuffered reads) | Faster (character buffer of 8192 chars) |
| **Parsing** | Automatic — `nextInt()` returns `int` directly | Manual — `readLine()` returns `String`, conversion needed via `Integer.parseInt()` |
| **Thread Safety** | Not thread-safe | Thread-safe (methods are `synchronized`) |
| **Package** | `java.util` | `java.io` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Loss of Marks Hotspots:**
> 1. **Forgetting `sc.close()`** → Lose 0.5 to 1 mark for "best practice not followed".
> 2. **Using `nextInt()` followed by `nextLine()`** without the extra `nextLine()` to consume the newline → Bug in output (empty string). **Loses 2 marks** if not handled.
> 3. **Not importing `java.util.Scanner`** → Compilation error → **Zero marks** for the program.
> 4. **Confusing `next()` with `nextLine()`** in definitions → Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the package mention (`java.util`)** when defining the class → Lose 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Writing `nextChar()`** (which does not exist) → Lose 1 mark for "incorrect API usage".

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3 Internal Choice Option B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to read **two integers** and an **arithmetic operator** (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`) from the console and display the result of the operation. Use the `Scanner` class. Handle division by zero gracefully.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the `java.util` package in Java. List any **five** methods of the `Scanner` class with their return types and a one-line description each.

---

#### Part (a) — Complete Model Solution

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.InputMismatchException;

public class MiniCalculator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        try {
            System.out.print("Enter first integer: ");
            int a = sc.nextInt();

            System.out.print("Enter operator (+, -, *, /): ");
            char op = sc.next().charAt(0);

            System.out.print("Enter second integer: ");
            int b = sc.nextInt();

            double result = 0;
            boolean valid = true;

            switch (op) {
                case '+': result = a + b; break;
                case '-': result = a - b; break;
                case '*': result = a * b; break;
                case '/':
                    if (b == 0) {
                        System.out.println("Error: Division by zero!");
                        valid = false;
                    } else {
                        result = (double) a / b;
                    }
                    break;
                default:
                    System.out.println("Invalid operator!");
                    valid = false;
            }

            if (valid) {
                System.out.println("Result: " + result);
            }
        } catch (InputMismatchException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Non-integer input detected.");
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation Breakdown (7 Marks):**
- Correct input reading logic: **2 Marks**
- `switch`/`if-else` operator dispatch: **2 Marks**
