**Key Intuition:** The button does not handle the response itself. It *delegates* the responsibility to whoever has registered interest. This is exactly how Java's event handling works — a `JButton` does not know what happens when clicked; it merely informs the registered `ActionListener`.

### 1.3 Why Event Handling Matters in GUI Programming

Event-driven programming inverts the traditional top-down flow. Instead of the program *telling* the user what to do, the program *waits* and *reacts* to user actions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Hook:** Remember the acronym **"S-E-L-H"** — **S**ource, **E**vent, **L**istener, **H**andler. Every event-driven Java GUI program has these four pillars.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Delegation Event Model — Operational Mechanics

The Delegation Event Model operates through a strict three-step protocol:

**Step 1 — Component Declaration:**
A GUI component (e.g., `JButton btn = new JButton("Submit");`) is instantiated. Internally, it holds an `EventListenerList` — a vector of registered listeners.

**Step 2 — Listener Registration (Subscription):**
The source component exposes `addXxxListener()` methods (e.g., `addActionListener()`). The listener object (an instance of an interface implementing `ActionListener`) is registered via:

$$ \text{Source.addActionListener(ListenerObject)} $$

This is also called the **"wiring"** step — connecting the source to the handler.

**Step 3 — Event Dispatch (Notification):**
When the user interacts, the JVM constructs an `EventObject` subclass (e.g., `ActionEvent`) and invokes the appropriate callback method (`actionPerformed(e)`) on **every** registered listener, in **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core "Why":** Why delegation instead of inheritance? In the legacy Java 1.0 AWT, every component had to subclass `Component` and override `handleEvent()`. This created monolithic, hard-to-maintain classes. Delegation allows **separation of concerns** — the GUI definition class can be completely independent from the business logic class.

### 2.2 Event Class Hierarchy (Package: `java.awt` & `java.awt.event`)

The root class for all AWT events is `java.awt.AWTEvent`. The complete inheritance tree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{java.util.EventObject} \\
\quad \downarrow \\
\text{java.awt.AWTEvent} \\
\quad \downarrow \\
\text{java.awt.event.ActionEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.AdjustmentEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.ItemEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.TextEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.ComponentEvent} \\
\quad \downarrow \\
\text{java.awt.event.FocusEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.WindowEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.PaintEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.InputEvent} \\
\quad \downarrow \\
\text{java.awt.event.KeyEvent} \\
\text{java.awt.event.MouseEvent} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** `MouseEvent` is the **subclass** of `InputEvent`, which is the **subclass** of `ComponentEvent`. Memorize this chain — examiners commonly ask to trace the hierarchy.

### 2.3 Key Methods of `EventObject` and `AWTEvent`

The two root classes expose critical utility methods that KTU students **must** know:

| Class | Method | Return Type | Purpose |
|-------|--------|-------------|---------|
| `EventObject` | `getSource()` | `Object` | Returns the source object that fired the event |
| `EventObject` | `toString()` | `String` | Returns the event's string representation |
| `AWTEvent` | `getID()` | `int` | Returns the event type identifier (e.g., `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED`) |
| `AWTEvent` | `getSource()` | `Object` | Overridden — same as parent |
| `AWTEvent` | `paramString()` | `String` | Returns a string with event parameters |
| `AWTEvent` | `consume()` | `void` | Consumes the event so it is not propagated further |
| `AWTEvent` | `isConsumed()` | `boolean` | Returns whether the event has been consumed |

### 2.4 Listener Interfaces — The "Do-Lots-But-Simple" Pattern

A **Listener Interface** in Java is one that contains **one or more abstract methods**, ALL of which must be implemented by the handler class. Notable examples:

| Listener Interface | Methods | Used With |
|-------------------|---------|-----------|
| `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | Buttons, Menus, TextFields (Enter) |
| `MouseListener` | 5 methods (`mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`) | All Components |
| `MouseMotionListener` | 2 methods (`mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved`) | All Components |
| `KeyListener` | 3 methods (`keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped`) | All Components |
| `FocusListener` | 2 methods (`focusGained`, `focusLost`) | All Components |
| `WindowListener` | 7 methods (`windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`) | Windows |
| `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | Checkbox, Choice, List |
| `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent)` | TextField, TextArea |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** `MouseListener` has **5 methods** and `WindowListener` has **7 methods**. Implementing them inline forces you to write empty bodies for all — this is precisely why **Adapter Classes** were introduced (see Section 2.6).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | KTU Use-Case |
|--------------|------------|--------------|
| `e.getSource()` | Returns `Object` — the firing source | Identify which button fired the event |
| `e.getID()` | Returns `int` — event type ID | Programmatic event type checking |
| `addXxxListener(L)` | Registers a listener `L` with a source | Wiring GUI to handler |
| `removeXxxListener(L)` | Unregisters a listener | Memory cleanup |
| `processXxxEvent(AWTEvent)` | Default component dispatcher | Overridden for inheritance model |
| `EventQueue.invokeLater(R)` | Schedules a Runnable on AWT thread | Thread-safe GUI updates |
| `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED` | Constant = `1001` | Compare with `e.getID()` |
| `WindowEvent.WINDOW_CLOSING` | Constant = `201` | Window close detection |

### 2.6 Adapter Classes — The Elegant Shortcut

An **Adapter Class** is a default (no-op) implementation of a listener interface that contains *empty* method bodies. To use only some methods, subclass the adapter and override only what you need.

| Listener Interface | Corresponding Adapter Class |
|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` |
| `ComponentListener` | `ComponentAdapter` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no `ActionAdapter`?** Because `ActionListener` has only ONE method — there's nothing to "default." Adapters exist only for interfaces with multiple methods.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Industrial SCADA Systems:** Sensor data updates trigger `ActionEvent`-like notifications to HMI panels.
- **Embedded Java (Java ME):** Hardware interrupts map to event-driven AWT-style handling.
- **Network Monitoring Tools:** Real-time packet arrivals are dispatched to registered listeners.
- **ATM Machine GUIs:** Every button press is an `ActionEvent` handled by a bank-specific `ActionListener`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 End-to-End Flow of an Event — Analytical Walkthrough

Let us trace **what happens internally** when a user clicks a `JButton` named "Submit" inside a `JFrame`:

**Step 1: Object Instantiation**

A `JButton` object is created. Internally, the JDK's `AbstractButton` parent class initializes an empty `EventListenerList` called `listenerList`. This list is implemented as a custom two-dimensional vector — odd indices hold listener types, even indices hold listener instances.

$$
\text{JButton submit} = \text{new JButton}("Submit")
$$

The JVM allocates memory and the listener list is:

$$
\text{listenerList} = \varnothing \quad (\text{empty vector})
$$

**Step 2: Listener Registration**

The developer writes:

$$ \text{submit.addActionListener(handlerObject)} $$

The `addActionListener()` method (inherited from `AbstractButton`) executes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{listenerList.add}(\text{ActionListener.class}) \\
\text{listenerList.add}(\text{handlerObject}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

After this, the list becomes:

$$
\text{listenerList} = [\text{ActionListener.class}, \text{handlerObject}]
$$

**Step 3: User Interaction — Event Firing**

When the user clicks the button, the OS dispatches a platform-specific click. The AWT Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) intercepts it and invokes `JButton.processMouseEvent()` (inherited from `Component`).

The button then constructs an `ActionEvent` object:

$$
\text{ActionEvent e} = \text{new ActionEvent}(\text{submit}, \text{ActionEvent.ACTION\_PERFORMED}, "Submit")
$$

**Step 4: Event Dispatch**

The button then iterates over `listenerList`. For every registered `ActionListener`, it invokes `actionPerformed()`:

$$
\forall \, L \in \text{registeredListeners} \; : \; L.\text{actionPerformed}(e)
$$

If multiple listeners are registered, they execute in **FIFO order**.

**Step 5: Handler Execution**

The handler's `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method runs. Inside, the developer typically extracts source information:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object src} &= e.\text{getSource}() \\
\text{String label} &= ((JButton) \, \text{src}).\text{getText}() \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Methods Called in Sequence

| Phase | Method Called | Class | Purpose |
|-------|---------------|-------|---------|
| 1. Initialization | Constructor | `JButton` | Creates button + empty listener list |
| 2. Registration | `addActionListener()` | `AbstractButton` | Adds listener to internal vector |
| 3. OS Intercept | `processMouseEvent()` | `Component` (AWT) | Translates OS click to AWT event |
| 4. Event Creation | `new ActionEvent()` | `ActionEvent` constructor | Encapsulates event data |
| 5. Dispatch | `fireActionPerformed()` | `AbstractButton` (protected) | Iterates listeners and calls `actionPerformed()` |
| 6. Handling | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | User-defined | Executes business logic |

### 3.3 Multi-Listener Analytical Scenario

Suppose `JButton btn` has **three** listeners registered: `L1`, `L2`, `L3`. When clicked:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{listenerList} &= [ActionListener.class, L_1, ActionListener.class, L_2, ActionListener.class, L_3] \\
\text{Execution Order} &= L_1.\text{actionPerformed}() \rightarrow L_2.\text{actionPerformed}() \rightarrow L_3.\text{actionPerformed}() \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The dispatcher skips non-matching listener types (e.g., `MouseListener` entries) using the `instanceof` check:

$$
\text{if } (\text{listener instanceof ActionListener}) \; \text{then invoke}
$$

### 3.4 Event ID Constants — Derivation Table

Each event class defines `static final int` constants for its event types. These are used in `e.getID()` comparisons:

| Event Class | ID Constant | Numeric Value |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| `ActionEvent` | `ACTION_PERFORMED` | `1001` |
| `ActionEvent` | `ACTION_FIRST` / `ACTION_LAST` | `1001` / `1001` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_CLICKED` | `500` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_PRESSED` | `501` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_RELEASED` | `502` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_ENTERED` | `504` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_EXITED` | `505` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KEY_PRESSED` | `401` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KEY_RELEASED` | `402` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KEY_TYPED` | `400` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WINDOW_OPENED` | `200` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WINDOW_CLOSING` | `201` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"What value does `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED` return?"* — The answer is the constant **`1001`**, defined in `java.awt.event.ActionEvent`.

### 3.5 Using Anonymous Inner Class vs Lambda (Modern Java)

The handler can be implemented in **three ways**:

**Method 1 — Separate Class Implementing Interface:**

The class `MyHandler implements ActionListener` is defined separately and instantiated. Verbose but reusable.

**Method 2 — Anonymous Inner Class:**

An inline implementation registered directly:

$$ \text{btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener()}() \rightarrow \text{actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)} \rightarrow \{\ldots\}) $$

No class name, single use only.

**Method 3 — Lambda Expression (Java 8+):**

Since `ActionListener` is a *functional interface* (one abstract method), Java 8+ allows:

$$ \text{btn.addActionListener(e} \rightarrow \{\ldots\}) $$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** The NEP 2020-aligned syllabus now explicitly covers **lambda expressions in event handling**. Always mention the lambda version if asked for the *modern* approach.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Event Handling Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Interaction e.g. Mouse Click] --> B[OS Captures Hardware Event]
    B --> C[AWT Event Dispatch Thread EDT]
    C --> D[Component.processEvent AWTEvent]
    D --> E[processActionEvent AWTEvent]
    E --> F[Create ActionEvent Object]
    F --> G[Iterate EventListenerList]
    G --> H{Listener Registered?}
    H -- No --> Z[No Action Event Ignored]
    H -- Yes --> I[Invoke actionPerformed e]
    I --> J[User Defined Handler Code]
    J --> K[Update GUI / Business Logic]
    K --> L[Return to EDT Loop]
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 Delegation Event Model Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SourceLayer["GUI SOURCE LAYER"]
        S1[JButton]
        S2[JTextField]
        S3[JFrame Window]
        S4[JCheckBox]
    end

    subgraph EventLayer["EVENT OBJECT LAYER"]
        E1[ActionEvent]
        E2[KeyEvent]
        E3[WindowEvent]
        E4[ItemEvent]
    end

    subgraph ListenerLayer["LISTENER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        L1[ActionListener]
        L2[KeyListener]
        L3[WindowListener]
        L4[ItemListener]
    end

    subgraph HandlerLayer["USER HANDLER LAYER"]
        H1[actionPerformed]
        H2[keyPressed]
        H3[windowClosing]
        H4[itemStateChanged]
    end

    S1 -->|fires| E1 -->|delivered to| L1 -->|invokes| H1
    S2 -->|fires| E2 -->|delivered to| L2 -->|invokes| H2
    S3 -->|fires| E3 -->|delivered to| L3 -->|invokes| H3
    S4 -->|fires| E4 -->|delivered to| L4 -->|invokes| H4
```

### 4.3 Event Class Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[java.util.EventObject]
    R --> A[java.awt.AWTEvent]
    A --> AE[ActionEvent]
    A --> IE[ItemEvent]
    A --> TE[TextEvent]
    A --> CE[ComponentEvent]
    CE --> FE[FocusEvent]
    CE --> WE[WindowEvent]
    CE --> PE[PaintEvent]
    CE --> IE2[InputEvent]
    IE2 --> KE[KeyEvent]
    IE2 --> ME[MouseEvent]
    AE --> L1[ActionListener Interface]
    ME --> L2[MouseListener Interface]
    KE --> L3[KeyListener Interface]
    WE --> L4[WindowListener Interface]
```

### 4.4 Listener Registration Sequence (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant Btn as JButton Source
    participant LL as EventListenerList
    participant L as ActionListener Handler
    participant EDT as Event Dispatch Thread

    Note over L,Btn: Phase 1 Registration
    L->>Btn: addActionListener(this)
    Btn->>LL: add ActionListener.class
    Btn->>LL: add handler instance

    Note over U,EDT: Phase 2 Event Firing
    U->>Btn: Mouse Click
    Btn->>EDT: processMouseEvent
    EDT->>Btn: Create ActionEvent object
    Btn->>LL: Iterate listenerList
    LL->>L: actionPerformed e
    L->>L: Execute business logic
    L-->>Btn: Return from handler
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define **Event Source**, **Event Listener**, and **Event Object** in Java's Delegation Event Model. Explain how they interact.

**Model Answer:**

- **Event Source:** A GUI component (like `JButton`, `JFrame`, `JTextField`) that generates events when the user interacts with it. It exposes `addXxxListener()` methods to allow external objects to register interest.
- **Event Listener:** An interface (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`) that defines callback methods to be invoked when an event occurs.
- **Event Object:** A subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) that encapsulates all information about the event — its source, type ID, and event-specific data.

**Interaction:** When the source detects an interaction, it constructs an Event Object and invokes the corresponding callback method on all registered Listeners, passing the Event Object as an argument.

> **[Mark Distribution: 3 Marks]** — 1 mark each for correct definition + 0 bonus mark implied for interaction flow.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` What is the difference between the **Inheritance Event Model** and the **Delegation Event Model** in Java AWT?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Inheritance Event Model (Java 1.0) | Delegation Event Model (Java 1.1+) |
|--------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Mechanism | Override `handleEvent()` in subclass | Implement listener interface |
| Coupling | Tight coupling between GUI and logic | Loose coupling — separate handler classes |
| Method count | One big `handleEvent()` with `switch` | One small focused method per event type |
| Maintainability | Poor — large monolithic classes | Excellent — separation of concerns |
| Modern usage | Deprecated | Used everywhere |

> **[Mark Distribution: 3 Marks]** — Tabular comparison + clear conclusion.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.A(a)** Explain the **Delegation Event Model** in Java with a neat diagram. List any **four event classes** and their corresponding **listener interfaces** from `java.awt.event`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Delegation Event Model** is a modern event-handling approach introduced in Java 1.1 to overcome the limitations of the legacy inheritance-based model. In this approach:

1. **Event Source** generates the event (e.g., button click).
2. **Event Object** encapsulates event details.
3. **Event Listener** (an interface implementation) is registered with the source.
4. The source **delegates** event handling to the registered listener.

```
[ Diagram — Use the block diagram from Section 4.2 ]
```

**Four Event Classes and Corresponding Listeners:**

| Event Class | Listener Interface | Handler Method |
|-------------|-------------------|----------------|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked()`, `mousePressed()`, etc. |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed()`, `keyReleased()`, `keyTyped()` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing()`, `windowOpened()`, etc. |

> **[7 Marks: 4 marks for model explanation + 2 marks for diagram + 1 mark for table]**

---

**Q.A(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program to create a frame with a button labeled "**Click Me**". Each click on the button should display the **number of times** it has been clicked in a `Label`. Use a **separate class implementing `ActionListener`**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class ClickHandler implements ActionListener {
    private Label countLabel;
    private int count;

    public ClickHandler(Label lbl) {
        this.countLabel = lbl;
        this.count = 0;
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        count++;
        countLabel.setText("Clicked: " + count + " times");
    }
}

public class ClickCounterApp extends Frame {
    private Button btn;
    private Label lbl;
    private ClickHandler handler;

    public ClickCounterApp() {
        setTitle("Click Counter");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        btn = new Button("Click Me");
        lbl = new Label("Clicked: 0 times");

        handler = new ClickHandler(lbl);
        btn.addActionListener(handler);

        add(btn);
        add(lbl);

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ClickCounterApp();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**

| Code Line | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| `class ClickHandler implements ActionListener` | Separate handler class |
| `btn.addActionListener(handler)` | Registers listener with source |
| `count++` | Increments click counter |
| `lbl.setText(...)` | Updates label dynamically |
| `addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {...})` | Enables window close |

> **[7 Marks: 2 marks class structure + 2 marks event wiring + 2 marks handler logic + 1 mark window closing]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B(a)** Explain the concept of **Adapter Classes** in Java AWT. Why are they needed? Give an example using `MouseAdapter`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** An Adapter Class is a default (no-op) implementation of a listener interface that contains empty method bodies for all abstract methods. It allows developers to override **only the methods they need**, instead of implementing every method of the interface.

**Why Needed:** Many listener interfaces have multiple methods. For example:
- `MouseListener` has **5 methods**
- `WindowListener` has **7 methods**
- `KeyListener` has **3 methods**

If a developer wants to handle only `mouseClicked()`, they still must write empty bodies for the other 4 methods of `MouseListener`. Adapter classes solve this — just subclass `MouseAdapter` and override the required method.

**Example:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseDemo extends Frame {
    public MouseDemo() {
        setTitle("Mouse Adapter Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);

        addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Mouse clicked at: "
                    + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY());
            }
        });

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MouseDemo();
    }
}
```

**Available Adapter Classes:** `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `ComponentAdapter`, `MouseMotionAdapter`, `ContainerAdapter`.

> **[7 Marks: 3 marks concept + 2 marks need + 2 marks example]**

---

**Q.B(b)** Differentiate between `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener`. Write a Java program using **anonymous inner class** to handle both mouse click and mouse drag events on a `Frame`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `MouseListener` | `MouseMotionListener` |
|--------|----------------|----------------------|
| Methods | 5 (`clicked`, `pressed`, `released`, `entered`, `exited`) | 2 (`dragged`, `moved`) |
| Adapter | `MouseAdapter` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| Triggered When | Mouse button pressed/released or cursor enters/exits | Cursor moves over component |
| Package | `java.awt.event` | `java.awt.event` |

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CombinedMouseDemo extends Frame {
    public CombinedMouseDemo() {
        setTitle("Mouse Click and Drag");
        setSize(400, 300);

        // Register MouseListener using anonymous inner class
        addMouseListener(new MouseListener() {
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Clicked at: " + e.getPoint());
            }
            public void mousePressed(MouseEvent e) { }
            public void mouseReleased(MouseEvent e) { }
            public void mouseEntered(MouseEvent e) { }
            public void mouseExited(MouseEvent e) { }
        });

        // Register MouseMotionListener using anonymous inner class
        addMouseMotionListener(new MouseMotionListener() {
            public void mouseDragged(MouseEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Dragged to: " + e.getPoint());
            }
            public void mouseMoved(MouseEvent e) { }
        });

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CombinedMouseDemo();
    }
}
```

> **[7 Marks: 2 marks differentiation + 3 marks program + 2 marks execution/output flow]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `addWindowListener` for window close:** Examiners deduct **1 full mark** if the program cannot be closed properly. Always include a `WindowAdapter` with `windowClosing` for frame-based programs.
> 2. **Confusing `KeyEvent` IDs:** `KEY_PRESSED = 401`, `KEY_RELEASED = 402`, `KEY_TYPED = 400` — students often mix these up.
> 3. **Using `MouseListener` for drag events:** `mouseDragged` is in `MouseMotionListener`, NOT `MouseListener`. This is the **single most common error** in KTU answer sheets.
> 4. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`:** A program without this will compile and run but show nothing — examiners may deduct marks for incomplete output.
> 5. **Not importing `java.awt.event.*`:** Listener interfaces live here. Missing imports cause compile errors.
> 6. **Confusing `getSource()` (returns Object) with `getComponent()`:** `getComponent()` is specifically for `MouseEvent`, not all events.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Delegation Event Model** replaced Java 1.0's inheritance model — uses **listeners** instead of `handleEvent()` overrides.
- [x] Four pillars: **S**ource, **E**vent, **L**istener, **H**andler.
- [x] `java.awt.AWTEvent` is the superclass of all AWT event classes.
- [x] `EventObject.getSource()` returns the firing component as `Object` — cast as needed.
- [x] `AWTEvent.getID()` returns an `int` event-type constant.
- [x] **ActionEvent** has ID `1001` (`ACTION_PERFORMED`) — most-tested constant in KTU.
- [x] **`MouseListener` ≠ `MouseMotionListener`** — `mouseDragged` is in the latter.
- [x] **`KeyListener` has 3 methods** — `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped`.
- [x] **`WindowListener` has 7 methods** — use `WindowAdapter` to avoid empty stubs.
- [x] **Adapter Classes** provide no-op default implementations — override only what's needed.
- [x] **No `ActionAdapter`** because `ActionListener` has only one method.
- [x] Registration uses `addXxxListener(L)`; de-registration uses `removeXxxListener(L)`.
- [x] Multiple listeners execute in **FIFO order** of registration.
- [x] Java 8+ supports **lambda expressions** for functional listener interfaces (`ActionListener`, `ItemListener`).
- [x] Always include a **window-closing handler** in Frame-based programs — examiners expect it.
- [x] **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** is responsible for delivering events to listeners.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Delegation Event Model ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Delegation Event Model** is the modern, standard event-handling architecture introduced in **JDK 1.1** that fully replaced the older hierarchical (inheritance-based) event model of JDK 1.0. In this model, an event generated by a GUI component (the **event source**) is *not* handled by that component itself; instead, the responsibility of handling the event is **delegated** to one or more separate objects known as **event listeners** (or **event handlers**) that have registered their interest with the source. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for OOP, the model has **three primary actors**: | Actor | Role | |---|---| | **Event Source** | The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) that generates an event when the user interacts with it. | | **Event Object** | An object (subclass of `java.util.EventObject`) that encapsulates the state of the event (e.g., which key was pressed, mouse coordinates). | | **Event Listener** | An object that implements a `XxxListener` interface and contains the methods that will be executed when the event occurs. | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Delegation"?** The name comes from the principle that the source component *delegates* the work of event processing to another object, decoupling the GUI component from event-handling logic. This adheres to the **Single Responsibility Principle** of OOP design. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **newspaper office**: 1. The **printing press (Event Source)** produces a newspaper the moment an event happens (someone buys a paper). 2. The **newspaper itself (Event Object)** carries the information about the event (what was bought, when, by whom). 3. The **delivery boy (Event Listener)** has registered with the press to be notified, picks up the paper, and performs the action (delivers it to the subscriber). The printing press does **not** know how to deliver papers — it just *delegates* that work. If you replace the delivery boy with another, the press doesn't change at all. This loose coupling is the essence of the Delegation Event Model. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Delegation Event Model is the **only** event model tested in KTU exams. The old JDK 1.0 model is considered outdated and out of syllabus, though you may get a one-line comparison question. ### 1.3 Key Terminology in Bold - **Event Source** — Object that registers listeners and generates events. - **Event Listener** — Object whose methods are invoked in response to the event. - **Event Object** — Carries information from the source to the listener. - **Listener Interface** — Java interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) that the handler class must implement. - **Adapter Class** — Empty default-implementation class of a listener interface used to override only required methods. - **Registration** — The `addXxxListener()` call that binds a listener to a source. - **Multicast Listener** — Multiple listeners can register for the same event from one source. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Bulleted Logic Breakdown The Delegation Event Model operates through the following structured sequence: 1. **Listener Implementation**: A class is created that implements a specific listener interface (e.g., `implements ActionListener`). The interface methods (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`) are overridden to define the response logic. 2. **Listener Instantiation**: An object of the listener class is instantiated. This object contains the event-handling code. 3. **Registration with Source**: The listener object is registered with the event source using the appropriate `addXxxListener(...)` method. For example: - `button.addActionListener(listenerObj);` - `frame.addWindowListener(windowListenerObj);` 4. **Event Generation**: When the user interacts with the source component (e.g., clicks the button), the component creates an **Event Object** that encapsulates details of the interaction. 5. **Event Dispatch**: The source component internally maintains a list of registered listeners. It iterates over this list and invokes the appropriate callback method on each listener, passing the event object as the argument. 6. **Event Handling**: The listener's overridden method executes, processing the event and producing the desired response. > **Why this matters in OOP design:** This model demonstrates **loose coupling** (source doesn't know listener's concrete class), **open/closed principle** (you can add new listeners without modifying source), and **interface-based programming** (listeners communicate only through interfaces). --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table | Concept | Syntax / Type | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Event Object base class | `java.util.EventObject` | Superclass of all Java event classes | | AWT Event base class | `java.awt.AWTEvent` | Superclass of all AWT/Swing events | | Common Event Classes | `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `FocusEvent` | Encapsulate specific user interactions | | Listener Interfaces | `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`, `ItemListener` | Define callback methods | | Adapter Classes | `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `FocusAdapter` | Provide empty implementations; override only what you need | | Registration method | `source.addXxxListener(listenerObj)` | Binds a listener to a source | | Unregistration method | `source.removeXxxListener(listenerObj)` | Unbinds a listener (rare in exams) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** In the KTU 2024 OOP Module 4, you are expected to know the **hierarchy of event classes** and the **exact name of every listener interface** corresponding to each event type. Memorize the table above. ### 2.3 Event Class Hierarchy (Conceptual Tree) - `java.util.EventObject` - `java.awt.AWTEvent` - `java.awt.event.ActionEvent` - `java.awt.event.MouseEvent` - `java.awt.event.KeyEvent` - `java.awt.event.WindowEvent` - `java.awt.event.ItemEvent` - `java.awt.event.FocusEvent` - `java.awt.event.ComponentEvent` - `java.awt.event.ContainerEvent` - `java.awt.event.TextEvent` - `java.awt.event.AdjustmentEvent` ### 2.4 Listener Interfaces and Their Methods | Listener Interface | Abstract Methods | Adapter Class | |---|---|---| | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | None (single method) | | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | `MouseAdapter` | | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | `MouseMotionAdapter` | | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` | `KeyAdapter` | | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified` | `WindowAdapter` | | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | None (single method) | | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | `FocusAdapter` | ### 2.5 Why Adapters Exist Java's listener interfaces can have **multiple methods**. For instance, `WindowListener` has **7 methods**. If you only want to handle `windowClosing`, you would be forced to write empty bodies for the other **6 methods** — verbose and error-prone. **Adapter classes** solve this by providing empty default implementations of all methods. You extend the adapter and override only the methods you need. > **Real-world engineering utility:** The Delegation Event Model is the foundation of all modern GUI frameworks — not just Java AWT/Swing, but also the conceptual basis for event handling in JavaScript (DOM events), Android (View.OnClickListener), C# (.NET delegates and events), and Python's Tkinter (`command=` callbacks). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since this is an OOP (CS) course, **Java code is the natural language** for explanations. All examples below use standard AWT/Swing APIs that are part of the KTU 2024 syllabus. --- ### 3.1 Generic 5-Step Implementation Procedure These are the five canonical steps that the KTU examiner expects for *any* "write a program to handle X event" question: **Step 1 — Identify the Event Source Component** Decide which component will generate the event (e.g., a `JButton` for click events). **Step 2 — Choose the Appropriate Listener Interface** Map the desired event to its listener interface (e.g., click on `JButton` → `ActionListener`). **Step 3 — Implement the Listener Interface** Create a class (or inner/anonymous class) that `implements` the listener interface and overrides its method(s). **Step 4 — Register the Listener with the Source** Call `source.addXxxListener(listenerObject)` to bind them. **Step 5 — Handle the Event** Inside the overridden method, write the response code. --- ### 3.2 Implementation Style 1 — Separate Top-Level Class (Most Common in Exams) **Problem:** Create a simple AWT program with a button. When the button is clicked, display "Button Clicked" on the title bar of the frame. ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; class MyButtonHandler implements ActionListener { private Frame targetFrame; public MyButtonHandler(Frame f) { this.targetFrame = f; } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { targetFrame.setTitle("Button Clicked"); } } public class DelegationDemo1 { public static void main(String[] args) { Frame f = new Frame("Delegation Event Demo"); Button b = new Button("Click Me"); b.setBounds(100, 100, 80, 30); f.setSize(300, 250); f.setLayout(null); f.add(b); f.setVisible(true); // Step 4: Register the listener b.addActionListener(new MyButtonHandler(f)); } } ``` **Explanation of the flow:** - The `MyButtonHandler` class implements `ActionListener` and overrides `actionPerformed`. - An instance of this handler is passed to `b.addActionListener(...)` — this is the **delegation** step. - When the user clicks the button, AWT internally creates an `ActionEvent` object and calls `actionPerformed()` on the registered handler. - The handler modifies the frame title in response. --- ### 3.3 Implementation Style 2 — Using an Adapter Class (for Multi-Method Listeners) **Problem:** Handle only the *window-closing* event of a frame, so that the program terminates when the user clicks the close button. ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class AdapterDemo extends Frame { AdapterDemo() { setSize(300, 200); setTitle("Adapter Class Demo"); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setVisible(true); // Register an anonymous WindowAdapter addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.exit(0); } }); } public static void main(String[] args) { new AdapterDemo(); } } ``` **Why an adapter here?** `WindowListener` requires **7 methods**. Extending `WindowAdapter` lets us override only `windowClosing` and ignore the other 6. --- ### 3.4 Implementation Style 3 — Inner Class (Cleaner for Multi-Source GUIs) ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class InnerClassDemo extends Frame { InnerClassDemo() { setSize(300, 200); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); Button b1 = new Button("OK"); Button b2 = new Button("Cancel"); add(b1); add(b2); setVisible(true); b1.addActionListener(new ButtonHandler("OK clicked")); b2.addActionListener(new ButtonHandler("Cancel clicked")); } // Inner class — has access to outer class members class ButtonHandler implements ActionListener { String message; ButtonHandler(String msg) { this.message = msg; } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { System.out.println(message); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new InnerClassDemo(); } } ``` --- ### 3.5 Implementation Style 4 — Anonymous Inner Class (Compact Form) ```java b1.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { System.out.println("OK clicked"); } }); ``` **Analytical Comparison Matrix of Implementation Styles:** | Style | Verbosity | Use When | KTU Marks Weight | |---|---|---|---| | Separate class | High | Multiple components, reusable handler | Most common (8 marks) | | Inner class | Medium | Handler uses outer class fields | Sometimes asked | | Anonymous class | Low | Single-use, simple logic | Often expected in outputs | | Adapter class | Low | Multi-method listener, only 1–2 needed | Window/Key/Mouse events | --- ### 3.6 Generic Event-Dispatch Algorithm (Pseudocode) The following describes what happens *internally* inside the JDK when an event fires: $$ \text{When event occurs on source } S \text{:} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: } S \text{ creates an EventObject } E \text{ representing the event.} \\ &\text{Step 2: } S \text{ looks up its internal listener list } L = \{L_1, L_2, \dots, L_n\}. \\ &\text{Step 3: } \text{For each } L_i \in L, \text{ invoke } L_i.\text{handlerMethod}(E). \\ &\text{Step 4: } \text{If no listener is registered, the event is silently discarded.} \end{aligned} $$ > **Key takeaway for KTU:** Multiple listeners can be registered to the same source — the event is **multicast** (broadcast to all). The source does not know what the listener does, only that it conforms to the listener interface. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Delegation Event Model — Block-Level Flow Architecture The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete data flow of the Delegation Event Model, from user interaction to handler invocation. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are kept clean (no markdown formatting) to satisfy the diagram-safeguard rules. ```mermaid graph TD A[USER INTERACTION] --> B[EVENT SOURCE COMPONENT] B --> C{EVENT DETECTED} C -->|Create| D[EVENT OBJECT INSTANCE] D --> E[INTERNAL LISTENER LIST LOOKUP] E -->|Iterate| F[LISTENER 1 HANDLER METHOD] E -->|Iterate| G[LISTENER 2 HANDLER METHOD] E -->|Iterate| H[LISTENER N HANDLER METHOD] F --> I[RESPONSE ACTION EXECUTED] G --> I H --> I I --> J[GUI STATE UPDATED] ``` --- ### 4.2 Component Relationship Diagram This diagram shows the static relationships between the three actors in the model. ```mermaid graph LR A[EVENT SOURCE
JButton JTextField JFrame] -->|addXxxListener| B[REGISTRATION LINK] B --> C[EVENT LISTENER
implements ActionListener] A -->|fires| D[EVENT OBJECT
ActionEvent etc] D -->|passed as argument| C C -->|overrides| E[handlerMethod e] E -->|updates| A ``` --- ### 4.3 Listener Interface Hierarchy (Simplified Block View) ```mermaid graph TD A[java util EventObject] --> B[java awt AWTEvent] B --> C1[ActionEvent] B --> C2[MouseEvent] B --> C3[KeyEvent] B --> C4[WindowEvent] C1 --> D1[ActionListener interface] C2 --> D2[MouseListener interface] C3 --> D3[KeyListener interface] C4 --> D4[WindowListener interface] D2 --> E1[MouseAdapter class] D3 --> E2[KeyAdapter class] D4 --> E3[WindowAdapter class] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Every event class has a corresponding listener interface (right side), and every multi-method listener has a corresponding adapter class. AWT/Swing maintains this 1-to-1 mapping consistently. --- ### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Click on a Button ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as JButton Source participant E as ActionEvent Object participant L as ActionListener Handler U->>S: mouse click S->>E: new ActionEvent this id command S->>L: actionPerformed E L->>S: setTitle change state L-->>U: visual update ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visual Question Pattern:** Examiners often show such a flow diagram in Part A and ask: *"Identify the listener interface used."* or *"What object is passed to the handler method?"* Practice interpreting such diagrams quickly. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the Delegation Event Model. List any two advantages of using this model over the older JDK 1.0 event model.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Delegation Event Model** is an event-handling architecture in Java (introduced in JDK 1.1) in which the responsibility of processing an event generated by a component (the *event source*) is delegated to a separate object (the *event listener*) that implements a specific listener interface and has registered itself with the source. **Two advantages over the JDK 1.0 model:** 1. **Loose Coupling:** The event source has no knowledge of the concrete class of the listener — it only knows the listener interface. This makes components more reusable. 2. **No Inheritance Constraint:** In the old model, a class had to inherit from a component class to handle its events, leading to deep, restrictive class hierarchies. The delegation model allows any class (including inner and anonymous classes) to act as a listener. *(Marks split: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between an Event Source and an Event Listener. Give one example of each from AWT.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Event Source | Event Listener | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | A GUI component that generates (fires) events in response to user actions. | An object whose methods are invoked by the source in response to an event. | | **Role** | Maintains a list of registered listeners and dispatches events. | Implements a listener interface and contains the response logic. | | **Example** | `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JFrame` (AWT) | A class implementing `ActionListener`, `KeyListener`, etc. | *(Marks split: 1 mark each for definition and example, 1 mark for the contrasting point.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of the Delegation Event Model with a neat block diagram. List the key participants and explain the role of each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program to create a `Frame` containing a `TextField` and a `Button` labeled "Reverse". When the button is clicked, the text in the text field should be reversed and displayed back in the same text field. Use the Delegation Event Model with a **separate listener class**. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks **Architecture of the Delegation Event Model:** The Delegation Event Model comprises three participants working in coordination: **1. Event Source (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`)** *(1.5 marks)* - A GUI component on which the user performs some interaction (click, key press, etc.). - Maintains an internal list of registered listeners using `addXxxListener()` calls. - On user interaction, it constructs an `EventObject` and notifies all registered listeners by invoking their appropriate callback methods. **2. Event Object (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`)** *(1.5 marks)* - Subclass of `java.util.EventObject`. - Encapsulates all relevant information about the event: source component, ID, time stamp, modifiers, and event-specific data (e.g., mouse coordinates, key code). - Passed as the argument to the listener's callback method. **3. Event Listener (e.g., an `ActionListener` implementation)** *(2 marks)* - An object of a class that implements a specific `XxxListener` interface. - The source does not know the listener's concrete class — only that it conforms to the interface (loose coupling). - The interface methods (e.g., `actionPerformed`) are overridden to specify the response. **Mechanism of operation** *(2 marks)*: 1. The listener is registered with the source via `source.addXxxListener(listener)`. 2. When the user interacts, the source creates an `EventObject`. 3. The source iterates over its listener list and invokes the appropriate method on each listener, passing the event object. 4. Each listener's overridden method executes, performing its response. *(Refer to the flow diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes.)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; // ===== Separate Listener Class ===== class ReverseHandler implements ActionListener { private TextField targetField; public ReverseHandler(TextField tf) { this.targetField = tf; } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String original = targetField.getText(); String reversed = new StringBuilder(original).reverse().toString(); targetField.setText(reversed); } } // ===== Main Application Class ===== public class ReverseTextDemo extends Frame { ReverseTextDemo() { setTitle("Reverse Text Demo"); setSize(350, 120); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); TextField tf = new TextField(20); Button btn = new Button("Reverse"); add(tf); add(btn); setVisible(true); // Step: Register the listener btn.addActionListener(new ReverseHandler(tf)); } public static void main(String[] args) { new ReverseTextDemo(); } } ``` **Step-by-step explanation** *(for the 7 marks)*: - The `ReverseHandler` class implements `ActionListener` and overrides `actionPerformed`. *(1 mark)* - It stores a reference to the `TextField` so it can read/write text. *(1 mark)* - Inside `actionPerformed`, the text is retrieved with `getText()`, reversed using `StringBuilder.reverse()`, and written back with `setText()`. *(2 marks)* - The handler is registered to the button using `btn.addActionListener(new ReverseHandler(tf))` — this is the **delegation**. *(1 mark)* - The `Frame` is configured with `FlowLayout`, made visible, and the program is run from `main()`. *(1 mark)* - A closing remark on loose coupling: the `Button` has no idea what the handler does — it just fires the event. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** What are Adapter Classes in Java's Delegation Event Model? Why are they used? Explain with a suitable example of handling the *window-closing* event of a `Frame` using a `WindowAdapter`. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Java AWT program to handle the following events on a `Frame`: - When the mouse enters the frame, the background color changes to **yellow**. - When the mouse exits the frame, the background color changes back to the **default** (light gray). Use an **anonymous adapter class** for the listener. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks **Adapter Classes** *(2 marks)*: An adapter class in Java's AWT event package is a class that **provides empty (no-op) implementations** of all the methods declared in a corresponding multi-method listener interface. For example, `WindowAdapter` provides empty bodies for all 7 methods of `WindowListener`. **Why are they used?** *(2 marks)*: Some listener interfaces (e.g., `WindowListener` with 7 methods, `MouseListener` with 5 methods) require the implementing class to provide *all* the methods — even those it does not need. Writing empty method bodies for the unused ones is tedious and error-prone. Adapters solve this by giving us a default empty implementation; we simply extend the adapter and override only the methods we care about. **Example using `WindowAdapter`** *(3 marks)*: ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class AdapterExample extends Frame { AdapterExample() { setSize(300, 200); setTitle("WindowAdapter Demo"); setVisible(true); addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.out.println("Window is closing. Exiting program."); System.exit(0); } }); } public static void main(String[] args) { new AdapterExample(); } } ``` Here, by extending `WindowAdapter`, we override only `windowClosing`; the other 6 methods inherit their empty default implementations and are never invoked. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class MouseColorDemo extends Frame { MouseColorDemo() { setSize(400, 300); setTitle("Mouse Enter/Exit Color Demo"); setBackground(Color.LIGHT_GRAY); setVisible(true); // Anonymous adapter class for MouseListener addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() { public void mouseEntered(MouseEvent e) { setBackground(Color.YELLOW); } public void mouseExited(MouseEvent e) { setBackground(Color.LIGHT_GRAY); } }); } public static void main(String[] args) { new MouseColorDemo(); } } ``` **Explanation** *(for 7 marks)*: - The `Frame` is the event source, with initial background `Color.LIGHT_GRAY`. *(1 mark)* - A `MouseAdapter` is used because `MouseListener` has 5 methods; we need only 2 (`mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`). *(2 marks)* - An **anonymous class** is declared inline — the `new MouseAdapter() { ... }` block. *(1 mark)* - Inside `mouseEntered`, the background is set to `Color.YELLOW`; inside `mouseExited`, it is reset. *(2 marks)* - The listener is auto-registered to the `Frame` itself (since `Frame` is a `Component` and supports `addMouseListener`). *(1 mark)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:** > - Many students **forget to call `setVisible(true)`** *after* registering the listener. If listeners are added after the component is shown, some events (like the very first mouse enter) may be missed. Always add listeners **before** showing the component. > - A common mistake is writing `extends MouseListener` instead of `implements MouseListener`. **Interfaces are implemented, not extended.** Adapter classes are *extended*. > - Students confuse the order: `ActionListener` has *one* method → no adapter exists. Only multi-method listeners have adapters. Do not invent `ActionAdapter`. > - The argument type in the callback method **must match** the event. Forgetting `e.getSource()` and accidentally casting it to the wrong class is another classic mark-deduction error. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Delegation Event Model** - **Three actors:** Event Source, Event Object, Event Listener. Memorize the roles. - **Source knows only the listener interface**, not the concrete class — that is loose coupling. - **One source can have many listeners** (multicast). One listener can register with many sources. - **Event Object** is a subclass of `java.util.EventObject`; AWT events extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`. - **ActionListener** has only 1 method (`actionPerformed`) — **no adapter exists**. - **MouseListener** has 5 methods — adapter is `MouseAdapter`. - **KeyListener** has 3 methods — adapter is `KeyAdapter`. - **WindowListener** has 7 methods — adapter is `WindowAdapter`. - **ItemListener** and **ActionListener** have 1 method each — no adapters. - Registration: always use the `addXxxListener(listenerObj)` method. The `Xxx` must match the event type (e.g., `addActionListener`, `addMouseListener`). - **Adapter classes vs. interfaces:** Extend an adapter; implement an interface. Adapters are *classes*, not interfaces. - **Inner classes** give the handler access to outer-class members. - **Anonymous classes** are compact one-shot listeners — common in AWT/Swing exam code. - The Delegation Event Model replaced the JDK 1.0 model based on `handleEvent()` and inheritance from `Component`. - Events on a component that has **no registered listener** are silently discarded — no compilation or runtime error. - The model is **synchronous**: the event is handled on the same thread (the Event Dispatch Thread in Swing) that triggered it. - For KTU, you should be able to write a program using **any of the four implementation styles** (separate class, inner class, anonymous class, adapter class). - **The callback method signature is fixed** by the interface — you cannot rename `actionPerformed` and expect the source to call it. - If two listeners are registered, both will be invoked in the order they were added.

topics.title: Event Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) Object-Oriented Programming, an **Event Class** is a specialized class encapsulated within the `java.awt.event` package that encapsulates (wraps) the state generated when a user interacts with a Graphical User Interface (GUI) component. Every event in Java is represented as an **object** that is dispatched from an **Event Source** to a registered **Event Listener/Handler**.

The root of the entire event hierarchy is `java.util.EventObject`, which is extended by `java.awt.AWTEvent`. All concrete event classes (such as `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `TextEvent`, etc.) are subclasses of `AWTEvent`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The hierarchy you must memorize begins from `java.util.EventObject` → `java.awt.AWTEvent` → Specific event classes. The package is `java.awt.event`.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting at a restaurant table. You want to order food.

*   **The Event Source** is *you* (the customer). You are the one generating the action.
*   **The Event Class** is the *order slip*. The slip carries structured information: *what* you ordered, *when* you ordered it, *which* table you are at, and any *special modifiers* (extra spicy).
*   **The Event Listener** is the *waiter*. The waiter receives the slip and decides what to do (go to the kitchen, update the billing system, etc.).

Just as a restaurant might have different slips for different actions (a "Drink Order Slip", a "Complaint Slip", a "Bill Request Slip"), Java has different Event Classes for different user interactions:
*   **ActionEvent** → A "Perform Action" slip (button click, menu selection)
*   **MouseEvent** → A "Mouse Action" slip (clicked, pressed, released, entered, exited)
*   **KeyEvent** → A "Keyboard Action" slip (key typed, pressed, released)
*   **WindowEvent** → A "Window Action" slip (opened, closed, iconified)

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Important Constants (Bolded)

*   **Event ID** is an `int` that identifies the specific type of event. For example, `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED` has the integer value **1001**.
*   **Modifiers** refer to keyboard modifier keys (Shift, Ctrl, Alt, Meta) held during an event, represented as bitmasks.
*   **Event Object Identity**: Every event object carries an immutable reference to its **Source** (the component that generated it).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:** An Event Class is fundamentally a **data carrier object** created by the JVM/AWT when a user performs an interaction. Its job is purely to transport information from the source to the listener.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Event Class Hierarchy (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

Java's event handling uses a clear inheritance chain to allow polymorphic handling:

**Step 1: `java.util.EventObject`** (The Abstract Root)
*   This is the **ultimate parent** of all events in Java (not just AWT).
*   It contains a single field: `Object source`.
*   **Method:** `Object getSource()` — Returns the object on which the event initially occurred.

**Step 2: `java.awt.AWTEvent`** (The AWT Root)
*   Extends `EventObject`.
*   Adds an `int id` field to identify the specific subtype of event.
*   **Key Methods:**
    *   `int getID()` — Returns the event type identifier.
    *   `String paramString()` — Returns a string representing the event's state parameters.
    *   `void consume()` — Consumes the event so it is not processed by the parent container.
    *   `boolean isConsumed()` — Returns whether this event has been consumed.

**Step 3: Concrete Event Classes** (The Specific Slips)
These extend `AWTEvent` directly or indirectly (through `ComponentEvent` → `InputEvent`).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Critical Event Classes

### 2.2.1 `ActionEvent`
Generated when a button is clicked, a list item is double-clicked, or a menu item is selected.

*   **Constructor:** `ActionEvent(Object src, int id, String command)`
*   **Key Methods:**
    *   `String getActionCommand()` — Returns the command name associated with the action.
    *   `int getModifiers()` — Returns the modifier keys held down (Shift, Ctrl, Alt).
    *   `long getWhen()` — Returns the timestamp of when the event occurred.

### 2.2.2 `MouseEvent` (Extends `InputEvent` → `ComponentEvent`)
Generated for all mouse interactions.

*   **Key Methods:**
    *   `int getX()` / `int getY()` — Returns the x/y coordinate of the mouse pointer relative to the source component.
    *   `Point getPoint()` — Returns the coordinates as a `Point` object.
    *   `int getClickCount()` — Returns the number of consecutive mouse clicks.
    *   `int getButton()` — Returns which mouse button was changed (e.g., `BUTTON1`, `BUTTON2`, `BUTTON3`).

### 2.2.3 `KeyEvent` (Extends `InputEvent` → `ComponentEvent`)
Generated for keyboard interactions.

*   **Virtual Key Constants:** `VK_A` to `VK_Z`, `VK_0` to `VK_9`, `VK_F1` to `VK_F12`, `VK_ENTER`, `VK_ESCAPE`, `VK_SHIFT`, `VK_LEFT`, etc.
*   **Key Methods:**
    *   `char getKeyChar()` — Returns the character associated with the key.
    *   `int getKeyCode()` — Returns the integer virtual key code.
    *   `boolean isActionKey()` — Returns true if the key is an action key (e.g., F1, arrow keys).

### 2.2.4 `WindowEvent`
Generated when a Window is opened, closed, activated, deactivated, iconified, or deiconified.

*   **Key Methods:**
    *   `Window getWindow()` — Returns the Window object that triggered the event.
    *   `Window getOppositeWindow()` — Returns the other Window involved in a focus or activation event.

### 2.2.5 `ItemEvent`
Generated when an item is selected or deselected in an item-selectable component (Checkbox, CheckboxMenuItem, Choice, List).

*   **Constants:** `ItemEvent.SELECTED`, `ItemEvent.DESELECTED`.
*   **Key Methods:**
    *   `Object getItem()` — Returns the item affected by the event.
    *   `ItemSelectable getItemSelectable()` — Returns the originator of the event.
    *   `int getStateChange()` — Returns whether the item was selected or deselected.

### 2.2.6 `TextEvent`
Generated when the content of a text component changes.

*   **Key Method:** `TextComponent getTextComponent()` — Returns the source `TextComponent` (a "high-level" event with no ID).

### 2.2.7 `FocusEvent`
Generated when a component gains or loses keyboard focus.

*   **Key Method:** `boolean isTemporary()` — Returns true if the focus change is temporary.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Event Class | Source Components | Key Methods | ID Constant Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ActionEvent** | Button, MenuItem, List, TextField | `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()` | `ACTION_PERFORMED` (**1001**) |
| **MouseEvent** | All Components | `getX()`, `getY()`, `getClickCount()`, `getButton()` | `MOUSE_CLICKED`, `MOUSE_PRESSED` |
| **KeyEvent** | All Components | `getKeyChar()`, `getKeyCode()`, `isActionKey()` | `KEY_PRESSED`, `KEY_TYPED`, `KEY_RELEASED` |
| **WindowEvent** | Window, Frame, Dialog | `getWindow()`, `getOppositeWindow()` | `WINDOW_OPENED`, `WINDOW_CLOSED` |
| **ItemEvent** | Checkbox, Choice, List | `getItem()`, `getStateChange()` | `ITEM_STATE_CHANGED` (**701**) |
| **TextEvent** | TextField, TextArea | `getTextComponent()` | `TEXT_VALUE_CHANGED` (**900**) |
| **FocusEvent** | All Components | `isTemporary()`, `getOppositeComponent()` | `FOCUS_GAINED`, `FOCUS_LOST` |
| **AdjustmentEvent** | Scrollbar | `getValue()`, `getAdjustmentType()` | `ADJUSTMENT_VALUE_CHANGED` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tip:** You do **NOT** need to memorize the integer values (1001, 701, 900), but knowing the *names* of the constants is mandatory for KTU exams.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Event Class mechanism is the foundation of all GUI-based software engineering:
*   **Industrial SCADA Systems:** `MouseEvent` and `KeyEvent` capture operator inputs on control panels.
*   **Banking Software:** `ActionEvent` handles button clicks for "Transfer Funds" or "Submit".
*   **Gaming Development (Java 2D):** `KeyEvent` and `MouseEvent` drive player movement and combat interactions.
*   **IDE Plugins:** `WindowEvent` detects when developers close editor tabs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Programmatic Implementation

## 3.1 The Event Handling Flow (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

To deeply understand Event Classes, you must understand how they are created and consumed in Java:

1.  **User Interaction:** The user clicks a button on the GUI.
2.  **Event Source Detection:** The JVM detects the interaction on the specific component (e.g., `Button` instance).
3.  **Event Object Instantiation:** The AWT framework **instantiates** the appropriate Event Class object (e.g., `new ActionEvent(button, ACTION_PERFORMED, "OK")`).
4.  **Event Dispatching:** The event object is passed to every registered `Listener` object via its respective handler method (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`).
5.  **Data Extraction:** The listener method uses the **methods of the Event Class** to extract information (e.g., `e.getActionCommand()` to know which button was clicked).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Example: ActionEvent & WindowEvent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Java code demonstrates the complete lifecycle of an Event Class object.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EventClassDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener, WindowListener {

    Button btnSubmit;
    Label lblOutput;

    public EventClassDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        btnSubmit = new Button("Click Me");
        lblOutput = new Label("Status: Awaiting Input");

        add(btnSubmit);
        add(lblOutput);

        // Event Source Registration
        btnSubmit.addActionListener(this);
        addWindowListener(this);

        setSize(300, 200);
        setTitle("KTU Event Class Demonstration");
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Listener Method receives the ActionEvent object 'e'
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Using Event Class methods to extract data
        String command = e.getActionCommand();
        int modifiers = e.getModifiers();
        long timestamp = e.getWhen();
        Object source = e.getSource();

        lblOutput.setText("Action: " + command + " | Modifiers: " + modifiers);
    }

    // WindowEvent listener methods
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { 
        System.exit(0); 
    }
    public void windowOpened(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowClosed(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowIconified(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowDeiconified(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowActivated(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowDeactivated(WindowEvent e) {}

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new EventClassDemo();
    }
}
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Methods to User Actions

| User Action Triggered | Event Class Instantiated | Method Called to Extract Data | Return Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Clicks a "Save" Button | `ActionEvent` | `e.getActionCommand()` | `"Save"` |
| Holds Shift + Clicks a Button | `ActionEvent` | `e.getModifiers()` | `ActionEvent.SHIFT_MASK` (bitmask value) |
| Moves mouse over a Panel | `MouseEvent` | `e.getX()` / `e.getY()` | `int` coordinates (e.g., `150`, `75`) |
| Double-clicks an item in a List | `MouseEvent` | `e.getClickCount()` | `2` |
| Presses the 'A' key | `KeyEvent` | `e.getKeyChar()` | `'a'` (or `'A'` if shift is held) |
| Presses the F1 key | `KeyEvent` | `e.getKeyCode()` | `KeyEvent.VK_F1` |
| Selects a Checkbox | `ItemEvent` | `e.getStateChange()` | `ItemEvent.SELECTED` |
| Closes a Window | `WindowEvent` | `e.getWindow()` | Reference to the `Window` object closed |
| Focus enters a TextField | `FocusEvent` | `e.isTemporary()` | `boolean` (true/false) |
| Text changes in TextArea | `TextEvent` | `e.getTextComponent()` | The `TextComponent` object |

---

## 3.4 Constructor Signatures (Exam-Critical)

For board exams, you may be asked to write or identify constructors of event classes. Here are the **canonical signatures**:

$$ \text{ActionEvent(Object src, int id, String command)} $$

$$ \text{ActionEvent(Object src, int id, String command, int modifiers)} $$

$$ \text{ActionEvent(Object src, int id, String command, long when, int modifiers)} $$

$$ \text{MouseEvent(Component src, int id, long when, int modifiers, int x, int y, int clickCount, boolean popupTrigger, int button)} $$

$$ \text{KeyEvent(Component src, int id, long when, int modifiers, int keyCode, char keyChar)} $$

$$ \text{WindowEvent(Window src, int id, Window opposite, int oldState, int newState)} $$

$$ \text{ItemEvent(ItemSelectable src, int id, Object item, int stateChange)} $$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Event Class Hierarchy

```mermaid

    B --> C11["WindowEvent"]
    
    C3 --> D1["InputEvent"]
    C3 --> D2["FocusEvent"]

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Event Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Phase1 ["Phase 1: Event Generation"]
        U["User Interaction"] -->|"Click/Type/Move"| ES["Event Source Component"]
        ES -->|"Creates"| EObj["Event Class Object Instantiated"]
        EObj -->|"Carries Data"| Data["Source ID, Coords, Modifiers, Timestamp"]
    end
    
    subgraph Phase2 ["Phase 2: Event Dispatch"]
        Data -->|"Passed To"| List["Registered Listener Object"]
        List -->|"Invokes"| Handler["Handler Method e.g. actionPerformed"]
    end
    
    subgraph Phase3 ["Phase 3: Event Processing"]
        Handler -->|"Extracts Info"| Methods["Event Class Methods getActionCommand, getX, getKeyCode"]
        Methods -->|"Updates"| GUI["GUI State/Output"]
    end
    
    style U fill:#e1f5ff
    style ES fill:#fff4e1
    style EObj fill:#ffe1e1
    style List fill:#e1ffe1

```

## 4.3 Mapping Diagram: Event Class to Listener Interface

```mermaid

    subgraph HM ["Handler Methods"]
        H1["actionPerformed"]

        H3["mouseDragged mouseMoved"]

        H5["windowOpened windowClosing windowClosed windowIconified etc"]
        H6["itemStateChanged"]
    end
    
    AE --> AL
    ME --> ML
    ME --> MWL
    KE --> KL
    WE --> WL

    AL --> H1
    ML --> H2
    MWL --> H3
    KL --> H4
    WL --> H5
    IL --> H6
    
    style AE fill:#ffcc99
    style ME fill:#ffcc99
    style KE fill:#ffcc99

    style MWL fill:#99ccff
    style KL fill:#99ccff
    style WL fill:#99ccff
    style IL fill:#99ccff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Event Classes in Java. Name the root classes of the event class hierarchy.**

**Model Answer:**
Event Classes are classes in the `java.awt.event` package that encapsulate information about a user interaction (such as a button click or mouse movement) so that it can be passed from an event source to an event listener.

The root classes of the event class hierarchy are:
1.  **`java.util.EventObject`** — The ultimate superclass of all event classes.
2.  **`java.awt.AWTEvent`** — The immediate subclass of `EventObject`, which is the parent of all AWT-specific event classes.

**(3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for hierarchy roots)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any FOUR methods of `ActionEvent` class and state their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**
1.  **`String getActionCommand()`** → Returns the command string associated with this event (e.g., the label of the button clicked).
2.  **`int getModifiers()`** → Returns the modifier keys (Shift, Ctrl, Alt, Meta) that were held down during the event, as a bitmask.
3.  **`long getWhen()`** → Returns the timestamp (in milliseconds) at which the action occurred.
4.  **`Object getSource()`** → *(Inherited from EventObject)* Returns the object on which the event initially occurred.

**(3 Marks: 0.75 per method)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Event Class hierarchy in Java starting from `java.util.EventObject`. Describe at least 5 concrete event classes with their respective source components. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Event Class hierarchy in Java begins with `java.util.EventObject`, which is the abstract root class for all event objects. Every event in Java is an instance of a class that descends (directly or indirectly) from this root.

**Hierarchy Levels:**

**Level 1 — `java.util.EventObject`:** The ultimate base class. It stores a reference to the object that generated the event and provides the `getSource()` method.

**Level 2 — `java.awt.AWTEvent`:** Extends `EventObject` and adds an integer `id` to identify the specific subtype of event. It provides methods like `getID()`, `paramString()`, `consume()`, and `isConsumed()`.

**Level 3 — Concrete Event Classes:** These are the specific event types used in GUI programming.

**Five Concrete Event Classes:**

| # | Event Class | Parent | Source Components | Common Methods |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ActionEvent** | `AWTEvent` | `Button`, `MenuItem`, `TextField`, `List` | `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()` |
| 2 | **MouseEvent** | `InputEvent` → `ComponentEvent` | Any `Component` | `getX()`, `getY()`, `getClickCount()`, `getButton()` |
| 3 | **KeyEvent** | `InputEvent` → `ComponentEvent` | Any `Component` | `getKeyChar()`, `getKeyCode()`, `isActionKey()` |
| 4 | **WindowEvent** | `AWTEvent` | `Window`, `Frame`, `Dialog` | `getWindow()`, `getOppositeWindow()` |
| 5 | **ItemEvent** | `AWTEvent` | `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List` | `getItem()`, `getStateChange()`, `getItemSelectable()` |

**(7 Marks: 2 for hierarchy explanation + 5 for table of 5 classes × 1 mark each)**

---

**(b)** Write a Java program that creates a Frame containing a TextField and a Button. When the button is clicked, the text entered in the TextField should be displayed in a Label. Use the `ActionEvent` class methods to extract the data. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ActionEventDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    
    TextField tfInput;
    Button btnShow;
    Label lblOutput;
    
    public ActionEventDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        tfInput = new TextField(20);
        btnShow = new Button("Show Text");
        lblOutput = new Label("Output will appear here");
        
        add(tfInput);
        add(btnShow);
        add(lblOutput);
        
        // Register the event source with the listener
        btnShow.addActionListener(this);
        
        // Close window gracefully
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });
        
        setSize(350, 150);
        setTitle("ActionEvent Demonstration");
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Using ActionEvent class methods
        String command = e.getActionCommand();
        Object source = e.getSource();
        
        if (e.getSource() == btnShow) {
            String inputText = tfInput.getText();
            lblOutput.setText("Command: " + command + " | Text: " + inputText);
        }
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ActionEventDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
*   `implements ActionListener` → The class registers itself as a listener for ActionEvents.
*   `btnShow.addActionListener(this);` → The Button is the event source; it registers `this` as the listener.
*   `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` → The handler method receives the `ActionEvent` object.
*   `e.getActionCommand()` → Returns the button's label ("Show Text").
*   `e.getSource()` → Returns the source object (the Button itself).

**(7 Marks: 2 for program structure + 3 for correct listener registration + 2 for correct extraction of data)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain `MouseEvent` and `KeyEvent` classes in detail. List at least 6 methods in total from both classes and describe their return values. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**`MouseEvent` Class:**
`MouseEvent` is generated whenever the user interacts with the mouse over a component (e.g., click, press, release, enter, exit, move, drag). It is a subclass of `InputEvent`, which itself extends `ComponentEvent`.

**`KeyEvent` Class:**
`KeyEvent` is generated when the user types, presses, or releases a key on the keyboard while a component has focus. It is also a subclass of `InputEvent`.

**Methods of `MouseEvent`:**

1.  **`int getX()`** → Returns the horizontal x-coordinate of the event relative to the source component.
2.  **`int getY()`** → Returns the vertical y-coordinate of the event relative to the source component.
3.  **`int getClickCount()`** → Returns the number of consecutive mouse clicks (1 for single-click, 2 for double-click, etc.).
4.  **`int getButton()`** → Returns which mouse button was involved (e.g., `BUTTON1` for left, `BUTTON3` for right).

**Methods of `KeyEvent`:**

5.  **`char getKeyChar()`** → Returns the character associated with the key. For example, pressing 'A' returns `'a'` (or `'A'` if Shift is held).
6.  **`int getKeyCode()`** → Returns the integer code of the key using virtual key constants like `VK_A`, `VK_F1`, `VK_ENTER`, `VK_LEFT`.
7.  **`boolean isActionKey()`** → Returns `true` if the key is an action key (arrow keys, function keys F1–F12, Home, End, etc.), which do not produce a character.

**(7 Marks: 2 for class descriptions + 1 per method × 5 methods minimum = 7)**

---

**(b)** Write a Java program that handles a `WindowEvent` to perform specific actions when the window is opened, closed, and closing. The program should also handle a `KeyEvent` to display the key pressed in a TextField. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class WindowKeyEventDemo extends Frame implements WindowListener, KeyListener {
    
    TextField tfKeyDisplay;
    Label lblStatus;
    
    public WindowKeyEventDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        
        lblStatus = new Label("Window Status: ");
        tfKeyDisplay = new TextField(25);
        
        add(lblStatus);
        add(tfKeyDisplay);
        
        // Register listeners
        addWindowListener(this);
        tfKeyDisplay.addKeyListener(this);
        
        setSize(400, 150);
        setTitle("Window and Key Event Demo");
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    // WindowEvent handler methods
    public void windowOpened(WindowEvent e) {
        lblStatus.setText("Window Status: Opened");
        System.out.println("Window Opened. Window: " + e.getWindow().getTitle());
    }
    
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
        lblStatus.setText("Window Status: Closing...");
        System.out.println("Window Closing event received");
    }
    
    public void windowClosed(WindowEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Window Closed. Source: " + e.getSource());
        System.exit(0);
    }
    
    public void windowIconified(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowDeiconified(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowActivated(WindowEvent e) {}
    public void windowDeactivated(WindowEvent e) {}
    
    // KeyEvent handler methods
    public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) {
        int code = e.getKeyCode();
        System.out.println("Key Pressed Code: " + code);
    }
    
    public void keyReleased(KeyEvent e) {}
    
    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        char c = e.getKeyChar();
