        System.out.println("Key Typed: " + c + " (Action Key: " + e.isActionKey() + ")");
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new WindowKeyEventDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
*   `addWindowListener(this)` → Registers the Frame to listen for window events.
*   `tfKeyDisplay.addKeyListener(this)` → Registers the TextField to listen for key events.
*   `e.getWindow()` → WindowEvent method that returns the Window object.
*   `e.getSource()` → Inherited from EventObject; returns the source of the event.
*   `e.getKeyCode()` → KeyEvent method returning the virtual key code.
*   `e.getKeyChar()` → KeyEvent method returning the typed character.
*   `e.isActionKey()` → KeyEvent method checking if the key is an action key.

**(7 Marks: 2 for WindowEvent handling + 2 for KeyEvent handling + 2 for event method usage + 1 for proper registration)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Source vs. ID:** Students often forget that `e.getSource()` returns the **component** (e.g., the Button object), while `e.getID()` returns an **integer constant** identifying the event subtype (e.g., `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED`).
> 2. **Wrong Hierarchy:** Writing `java.awt.event.EventObject` instead of `java.util.EventObject` is a common error that costs 1 mark. The root is in `java.util`, not `java.awt.event`.
> 3. **Forgetting `WindowAdapter`:** When implementing `WindowListener` directly, you **must** provide all 7 methods (even if empty). Using `WindowAdapter` avoids this. Examiners will deduct marks if methods are missing in your code.
> 4. **Mixing Up `getKeyChar` and `getKeyCode`:** `getKeyChar()` returns a `char` (the character), while `getKeyCode()` returns an `int` (the virtual key code like `VK_ENTER`).
> 5. **Missing Import Statements:** Always include `import java.awt.*;` and `import java.awt.event.*;` at the top of your program.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

*   **Root Classes:** `java.util.EventObject` (top) → `java.awt.AWTEvent` (AWT parent) → Specific classes.
*   **Package Location:** All concrete event classes reside in **`java.awt.event`**.
*   **`getSource()`** is inherited from `EventObject` and returns the **Object** that generated the event.
*   **`getID()`** is defined in `AWTEvent` and returns the **integer** identifying the event subtype.
*   **ActionEvent** uses `getActionCommand()` to retrieve the action's label/name.
*   **MouseEvent** uses `getX()`, `getY()`, `getClickCount()`, and `getButton()` for spatial and click information.
*   **KeyEvent** uses `getKeyChar()` (returns `char`) and `getKeyCode()` (returns `int` like `VK_A`, `VK_F1`).
*   **WindowEvent** uses `getWindow()` to retrieve the Window object that was affected.
*   **ItemEvent** uses `getStateChange()` which returns either `SELECTED` or `DESELECTED`.
*   **TextEvent** is a "high-level" event with no ID and only indicates that text changed (use `getTextComponent()` to access the source).
*   **FocusEvent** uses `isTemporary()` to determine if focus loss is permanent or temporary.
*   **Inheritance Chain for Input Events:** `ComponentEvent` → `InputEvent` → `MouseEvent` / `KeyEvent`.
*   **Event Object Creation:** Event objects are instantiated automatically by the JVM/AWT framework — you do NOT manually create them using `new ActionEvent(...)` in normal application code (only in custom event dispatching).
*   **Listener Matching:** Every event class has a corresponding listener interface (e.g., `ActionEvent` ↔ `ActionListener`, `MouseEvent` ↔ `MouseListener`/`MouseMotionListener`).
*   **For KTU exams:** Always mention the **package name** (`java.awt.event`) and the **parent class** of any event class you discuss to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sources of Events # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Java Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)** and **Swing** frameworks, an **Event Source** is any user-interface **Component** (or container) that has the capability of detecting user interactions and **generating** an object of type `java.awt.EventObject` (or its subclass such as `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `TextEvent`, `AdjustmentEvent`, `ComponentEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `ContainerEvent`, `PaintEvent`, `InputEvent`). According to the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** adopted by Java AWT since JDK 1.1, every event source: 1. **Maintains a list of registered listeners** (one per event type). 2. **Detects a user action** (click, key press, mouse movement, etc.). 3. **Instantiates an event object** describing the action. 4. **Delegates the event** to all listeners in its list by invoking the corresponding handler method. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Event Handling):** > *Sources of Events* refers to the AWT/Swing components that **produce** events, along with the **type** of event object they produce. Mastering this mapping is essential for solving board questions on the *Delegation Event Model*. > [!NOTE] > **Component vs. Container as a Source:** > All `java.awt.Component` subclasses can be event sources. Containers (`Frame`, `Dialog`, `Panel`, `Applet`) are special components that can also propagate events from their child components through bubbling (in AWT 1.0 style) or explicit listener registration (in DEM). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡 Imagine a **fire alarm system** inside a large building: | Real-World Object | Java Equivalent | |---|---| | Smoke detector (the device that senses) | **Event Source** (e.g., a `Button`, `TextField`) | | The alarm sound signal itself | **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`) | | The fire station receiving the call | **Event Listener** (e.g., `ActionListener`) | | The fire-truck's response action | **Event Handler method** (e.g., `actionPerformed()`) | Just as a **smoke detector is the *source* of the alarm signal** (it does not put out the fire itself), an event source **does not handle the event itself** — it merely **generates** the event object and hands it to a *listener* (the fire station) for action. ### 🎯 Key Takeaway for Beginners > **Source = Generator**, **Listener = Consumer**, **Event Object = Package of Information**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology - **JDK 1.0 Event Model**: Inheritance-based, container-centric (deprecated). - **JDK 1.1+ Delegation Event Model (DEM)**: The **only** model used in modern Java. - **11 legacy AWT event types** are defined in `java.awt.event.*`. - **100 ms** is the standard key-auto-repeat delay for `KeyEvent.KEY_PRESSED` sequences. - **Mouse events** carry an integer **click-count** (single, double, triple click). # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Event Sources Event sources in AWT can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**: ### Axis A — By Component Type 1. **Simple Components**: `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`, `Scrollbar`, `TextArea`, `Canvas`. 2. **Container Components**: `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Panel`, `Applet`, `Window`, `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JPanel`, `JApplet`. 3. **Menu Components**: `MenuItem`, `CheckboxMenuItem`, `Menu`, `PopupMenu`. ### Axis B — By Event Type Generated A single component can be the source of **multiple event types** (e.g., a `Button` is source of `ActionEvent` *and* `MouseEvent` *and* `KeyEvent` *and* `FocusEvent`). ### Axis C — By Listener Registration Method - `addXXXListener(XXXListener l)` — e.g., `addActionListener(...)`, `addMouseListener(...)`. - `removeXXXListener(XXXListener l)` — symmetric removal. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Design | Logical Step | Why It Exists | How It Is Implemented | |---|---|---| | 1. User interacts with UI | Real applications must respond to users | Mouse click, key press, focus change, etc. | | 2. Component detects the interaction | AWT's peer (native) code catches OS-level messages | Each `Component` has a corresponding `ComponentPeer` | | 3. Event object is created | A self-describing record must travel from source to listener | Subclass of `java.awt.AWTEvent` is allocated on the heap | | 4. Listeners are notified | Decouples source from handler (Open/Closed Principle) | Source invokes `listener.eventHappened(event)` on each registered listener | | 5. Handler performs business logic | UI reacts meaningfully to the user | The method body executes the developer's code | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Sources of Events > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important reference** for KTU exam answers on this topic. Memorize the **Source → Event Class → Listener Interface → Handler Method** mapping. | Component (Source) | Event Class(es) Generated | Listener Interface | Handler Method(s) | Signature of `addXxxListener` | |---|---|---|---|---| | `Button` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | | `TextField` | `ActionEvent`, `TextEvent` | `ActionListener`, `TextListener` | `actionPerformed`, `textValueChanged` | `addActionListener`, `addTextListener` | | `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | `addItemListener` | | `CheckboxMenuItem` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged` | `addItemListener` | | `Choice` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged` | `addItemListener` | | `List` | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent` | `ActionListener`, `ItemListener` | `actionPerformed`, `itemStateChanged` | `addActionListener`, `addItemListener` | | `MenuItem` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed` | `addActionListener` | | `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | `addAdjustmentListener` | | `TextArea` | `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged` | `addTextListener` | | `Window` / `Frame` / `Dialog` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened/Closing/Closed/Activated/Deactivated/Iconfied/Deiconified` | `addWindowListener` | | `Container` | `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | `componentAdded/Removed` | `addContainerListener` | | `Component` (generic) | `ComponentEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `MouseWheelEvent`, `KeyEvent` | `ComponentListener`, `FocusListener`, `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener`, `MouseWheelListener`, `KeyListener` | `componentShown/Hidden/Moved/Resized`, `focusGained/Lost`, `mouseClicked/Pressed/Released/Entered/Exited`, `mouseDragged/Moved`, `mouseWheelMoved`, `keyTyped/Pressed/Released` | `addComponentListener`, `addFocusListener`, `addMouseListener`, `addMouseMotionListener`, `addMouseWheelListener`, `addKeyListener` | | `MenuContainer` / `Menu` | `MenuEvent` (in Swing) | `MenuListener` | `menuSelected/Deselected/Canceled` | `addMenuListener` | ### 📌 Universal Registration Methods (All Components) Every `java.awt.Component` provides: - `addComponentListener(ComponentListener l)` - `addFocusListener(FocusListener l)` - `addKeyListener(KeyListener l)` - `addMouseListener(MouseListener l)` - `addMouseMotionListener(MouseMotionListener l)` - `addMouseWheelListener(MouseWheelListener l)` *(since JDK 1.4)* ### 📌 Class Hierarchy of Event Sources (Simplified) ``` java.lang.Object └── java.awt.Component (abstract) ├── java.awt.Button ├── java.awt.TextField ├── java.awt.Checkbox ├── java.awt.Choice ├── java.awt.List ├── java.awt.Canvas ├── java.awt.Label ├── java.awt.Scrollbar ├── java.awt.TextArea └── java.awt.Container ├── java.awt.Window │ ├── java.awt.Frame │ ├── java.awt.Dialog │ └── java.awt.FileDialog ├── java.awt.Panel │ └── java.applet.Applet └── java.awt.ScrollPane ``` ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Event sources underpin every modern GUI framework — not only Java AWT/Swing but also: - **JavaFX** (uses the same `EventObject` roots). - **Android** (inherits the delegation concept via `View.setOnClickListener`). - **Web front-ends** (DOM events in JavaScript directly map to the DEM philosophy). - **Industrial SCADA / HMI panels** built on Java. > [!NOTE] > Understanding the **source-listener-event** triad is a foundational skill for any **UI developer, automation engineer, or embedded HMI programmer** in the industry. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — *How a Component Becomes an Event Source* We will dissect, step-by-step, **exactly what happens internally** when a user clicks a `Button` named `b1` registered with an `ActionListener`. ### 🔬 Step 1 — Programmer Declares and Adds the Source ```java Button b1 = new Button("Click Me"); b1.addActionListener(this); // 'this' is an ActionListener ``` **Conversion Logic:** - The expression `new Button("Click Me")` instantiates a peer-based AWT button. - Internally, AWT calls `ComponentPeer p = Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().createButton(this);` to bind a native peer. - The `addActionListener(this)` call invokes the method defined in `java.awt.Button`: ```java public void addActionListener(ActionListener l) { actionListener = AWTEventMulticaster.add(actionListener, l); } ``` - `AWTEventMulticaster` is a **chained, thread-safe utility class** that maintains a singly-linked list of listeners. ### 🔬 Step 2 — User Clicks the Button The OS detects the mouse-click on the button's screen rectangle. It posts a native message to the JVM, which the AWT event-dispatch thread (EDT) picks up and converts. ### 🔬 Step 3 — Event Object Construction The AWT framework builds an `ActionEvent` object: ```java ActionEvent ae = new ActionEvent( b1, // source ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED, // id "Click Me" // command ); ``` The `id` is one of the constants in `ActionEvent`: - `ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001` - `ACTION_FIRST = 1001` - `ACTION_LAST = 1001` > Note: `ActionEvent` is a **semantic event** — it represents a *completed* action (e.g., a button click is a *single* semantic event, even though the underlying mouse-press + mouse-release produced *two* low-level events). ### 🔬 Step 4 — Event Dispatch Through the Multicaster The AWT calls the dispatch chain: ```java ((ActionListener) actionListener).actionPerformed(ae); ``` Since `actionListener` is an `AWTEventMulticaster` (not a raw listener), the multicaster's `actionPerformed` iterates over its internal chain and invokes the `actionPerformed` of *every* registered listener. ### 🔬 Step 5 — Handler Method Executes ```java public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent ae) { System.out.println("Button clicked: " + ae.getActionCommand()); } ``` ### 🔬 Step 6 — Event Object Becomes Eligible for GC Once the call stack unwinds, the `ActionEvent` instance has no live references and is garbage-collected. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Multi-Source Multi-Listener Registration Below is a complete, runnable Java program that demonstrates **multiple event sources** (`Button`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`) each generating **different event types**, all handled by a single frame. ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class MultiSourceDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener, ItemListener, TextListener { TextField tf; Checkbox cb; Button b; Label status; public MultiSourceDemo() { setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setTitle("Sources of Events Demo"); setSize(400, 200); tf = new TextField(20); // Source 1 — TextEvent + ActionEvent cb = new Checkbox("Agree"); // Source 2 — ItemEvent b = new Button("Submit"); // Source 3 — ActionEvent status = new Label("Interact with the components..."); // Register listeners tf.addTextListener(this); tf.addActionListener(this); cb.addItemListener(this); b.addActionListener(this); add(tf); add(cb); add(b); add(status); addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { dispose(); } }); } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { status.setText("Action: " + e.getActionCommand()); } public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { status.setText("Checkbox: " + (e.getStateChange()==1?"ON":"OFF")); } public void textValueChanged(TextEvent e) { status.setText("Typing... current text = " + tf.getText()); } public static void main(String[] args) { new MultiSourceDemo().setVisible(true); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Trace | User Action | Source | Event Class | Method Invoked | Final `status` Text | |---|---|---|---|---| | Types `"Hi"` in TextField | `tf` | `TextEvent` | `textValueChanged` | "Typing... current text = Hi" | | Presses `Enter` in TextField | `tf` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed` | "Action: Hi" | | Clicks Checkbox | `cb` | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged` | "Checkbox: ON" | | Clicks Submit Button | `b` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed` | "Action: Submit" | | Closes Window | `Frame` | `WindowEvent` | `windowClosing` | (Frame disposed) | > [!NOTE] > **Why use `WindowAdapter` instead of `WindowListener` directly?** > `WindowListener` declares **7 abstract methods**. Implementing it directly forces the programmer to write empty bodies for all 7. `WindowAdapter` provides default empty implementations, so you override only what you need — a classic application of the **Adapter design pattern**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Semantic vs. Low-Level Events | Property | Semantic (High-Level) Event | Low-Level Event | |---|---|---| | Meaning | A *complete* user action | A *single physical* input | | Examples | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `AdjustmentEvent`, `TextEvent` | `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `ComponentEvent` | | Trigger | Often synthesized from low-level events | Direct OS-level notification | | Source Components | Buttons, Lists, Checkboxes, Scrollbars | All `Component` subclasses | | Listener Interfaces | `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `AdjustmentListener`, `TextListener` | `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `FocusListener`, `ComponentListener` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU frequently tests this distinction.** Example: *"Why is `ActionEvent` called a semantic event?"* — because it represents a logically complete user action, independent of the underlying input device used to perform it (mouse, keyboard, touch, programmatic invocation). --- ## 3.4 Mathematical / Quantitative Analysis of the Event Dispatch Chain Although event handling is fundamentally discrete, the **multicaster chain** has a quantifiable structure. Let: $$ L = \{ \ell_1, \ell_2, \dots, \ell_n \} $$ be the set of registered listeners. When an event is dispatched, the AWT performs: $$ T_{\text{dispatch}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} t(\ell_i) \;+\; (n-1) \cdot t_{\text{link}} $$ where $t(\ell_i)$ is the time taken inside listener $\ell_i$'s handler, and $t_{\text{link}}$ is the cost of traversing a single link in the multicaster chain. > [!NOTE] > This expression is *not* part of any KTU board answer expected to be reproduced verbatim. It is provided here to give you a **mathematical feel** of why long handler methods can freeze the EDT (Event Dispatch Thread) — a frequent cause of unresponsive GUIs. # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Delegation Event Model Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[USER ACTION
click / key / focus] --> B[EVENT SOURCE
Component e.g. Button] B --> C[EVENT OBJECT
e.g. ActionEvent] C --> D[EVENT DISPATCHER
AWTEventMulticaster] D --> E1[LISTENER 1
L1.actionPerformed] D --> E2[LISTENER 2
L2.actionPerformed] D --> E3[LISTENER N
Ln.actionPerformed] E1 --> F1[HANDLER 1
business logic] E2 --> F2[HANDLER 2
business logic] E3 --> F3[HANDLER N
business logic] ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **user** initiates the action. - The **source** detects it and creates an event object. - The **dispatcher** fans out the event to all registered listeners. - Each **listener's handler** runs its own business logic. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Component Hierarchy of Event Sources ```mermaid flowchart TD OBJ[java.lang.Object] OBJ --> COMP[java.awt.Component] COMP --> SIM[Simple Components] COMP --> CONT[Container] SIM --> BTN[Button] SIM --> TF[TextField] SIM --> CB[Checkbox] SIM --> CH[Choice] SIM --> LST[List] SIM --> SB[Scrollbar] SIM --> TA[TextArea] SIM --> LBL[Label] SIM --> CVS[Canvas] CONT --> WIN[Window] CONT --> PNL[Panel] CONT --> SP[ScrollPane] WIN --> FRM[Frame] WIN --> DLG[Dialog] WIN --> FD[FileDialog] PNL --> APL[Applet] ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Source-to-Listener Mapping (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SOURCES["EVENT SOURCES"] S1[Button] S2[TextField] S3[Checkbox] S4[List] S5[Scrollbar] S6[Frame] end subgraph EVENTTYPES["EVENT OBJECTS"] E1[ActionEvent] E2[TextEvent] E3[ItemEvent] E4[AdjustmentEvent] E5[WindowEvent] end subgraph LISTENERS["LISTENER INTERFACES"] L1[ActionListener] L2[TextListener] L3[ItemListener] L4[AdjustmentListener] L5[WindowListener] end S1 --> E1 --> L1 S2 --> E1 S2 --> E2 --> L2 S3 --> E3 --> L3 S4 --> E1 S4 --> E3 S5 --> E4 --> L4 S6 --> E5 --> L5 ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Registration Methods Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD REG[addXxxListener Method] REG --> RA[addActionListener] REG --> RI[addItemListener] REG --> RT[addTextListener] REG --> RM[addMouseListener] REG --> RK[addKeyListener] REG --> RF[addFocusListener] REG --> RW[addWindowListener] REG --> RC[addComponentListener] REG --> RAD[addAdjustmentListener] REG --> RCT[addContainerListener] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"List any four `addXxxListener` methods used in Java AWT."* Use the diagram above as your mental model. # 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) ### ❓ Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]` > **Define an *event source* in Java AWT. List any four AWT components that can act as event sources along with the type of event they generate.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks) An **event source** is a GUI component (an object of `java.awt.Component` or its subclass) that detects a user interaction and produces an event object which is then delegated to its registered listeners. Four examples: | # | Component (Source) | Event Class Generated | |---|---|---| | 1 | `Button` | `ActionEvent` | | 2 | `TextField` | `ActionEvent`, `TextEvent` | | 3 | `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | | 4 | `List` | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent` | > **Mark Allocation:** Definition — 1 mark, 4 components with events — 2 marks (0.5 per row). --- ### ❓ Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]` > **Differentiate between *semantic events* and *low-level events* in Java AWT. Give one example of each.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks) | Property | Semantic (High-Level) Event | Low-Level Event | |---|---|---| | Definition | Represents a *complete* user action | Represents a *single physical* input | | Granularity | High — meaningful at the application level | Low — raw input from the OS | | Example | `ActionEvent` (button click) | `MouseEvent` (`mousePressed`) | | Another Example | `ItemEvent` (checkbox toggled) | `KeyEvent` (`keyTyped`) | > **Mark Allocation:** 1.5 marks for definition/difference, 1.5 marks for examples. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### ❓ Question B1 — *Choice A* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **(a)** Explain the concept of **event sources** in Java AWT with a neat diagram. List at least **six** different AWT components as event sources along with the corresponding event class and listener interface for each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program to create a `Frame` containing a `TextField`, a `Button` labeled "Show", and a `Label`. When the user types text in the `TextField` and clicks the button (or presses Enter), the typed text should be displayed in the `Label`. Use the **Delegation Event Model**. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Concept (3 marks):** An event source in Java AWT is any `java.awt.Component` subclass that has the capability of detecting user interactions and producing an `AWTEvent` object. Under the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)**, the source does not handle the event itself. Instead, it: 1. Maintains a list of registered listeners. 2. Creates an event object on user interaction. 3. Invokes the appropriate handler method on each listener. **Source → Event → Listener Table (4 marks — 0.5 per correct row, plus 0.5 for neatness):** | Component (Source) | Event Class | Listener Interface | |---|---|---| | `Button` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | | `TextField` | `ActionEvent` / `TextEvent` | `ActionListener` / `TextListener` | | `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | | `Choice` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | | `List` | `ActionEvent` / `ItemEvent` | `ActionListener` / `ItemListener` | | `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | | `Frame` / `Window` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | | `Container` | `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | **Diagram (included in Section 4.3 above).** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class ShowTextDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener { TextField tf; Label result; Button showBtn; public ShowTextDemo() { setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setTitle("Show Text Demo"); setSize(350, 150); tf = new TextField(20); result = new Label("Result will appear here"); showBtn = new Button("Show"); // --- Registration: the sources are tf and showBtn --- tf.addActionListener(this); // TextField as source of ActionEvent showBtn.addActionListener(this); // Button as source of ActionEvent add(new Label("Type something:")); add(tf); add(showBtn); add(result); addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { dispose(); } }); } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { result.setText("You typed: " + tf.getText()); } public static void main(String[] args) { new ShowTextDemo().setVisible(true); } } ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown:** | Element | Marks | |---|---| | Correct import statements | 0.5 | | Class declaration with `implements ActionListener` | 0.5 | | Proper constructor with `Frame` setup and `FlowLayout` | 1.0 | | Correct creation of `TextField`, `Button`, `Label` | 0.5 | | Registration of listeners on **both** sources | 1.0 | | `actionPerformed` implementation with `getText()` | 1.0 | | `WindowAdapter` for closing | 0.5 | | `main` method and `setVisible(true)` | 0.5 | | Compilation cleanness, naming, indentation | 1.5 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points:** > 1. **Forgetting to register the listener on the `TextField`** → loses 0.5 mark (no Enter-key handling). > 2. **Not overriding `windowClosing`** → program refuses to close; examiner deducts 0.5. > 3. **Confusing `setText()` and `getText()`** → mixed-up argument order loses 0.5–1.0 marks. > 4. **Missing `setVisible(true)`** → program compiles but does not show; full marks only if output is verifiable. > 5. **Failing to add components to the Frame** (`add(...)` not called) → components won't be visible. --- ### ❓ Question B1 — *Choice B* `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` > **(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain how an event is **generated and dispatched** in the Delegation Event Model. Clearly identify the role of the **event source**, **event object**, and **event listener**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program using the Delegation Event Model that contains a `Frame` with a `Checkbox` (labeled "Bold") and a `TextArea`. When the user toggles the checkbox, the `TextArea` font should switch between `PLAIN` and `BOLD`. Identify the **event source**, **event class**, and **listener interface** used. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Explanation (4 marks):** The Delegation Event Model (DEM) is Java's standard mechanism for handling GUI events, introduced in **JDK 1.1**. It involves three collaborating entities: 1. **Event Source** — the GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`) that detects a user interaction. It exposes `addXxxListener` and `removeXxxListener` methods. 2. **Event Object** — an instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (specifically `java.awt.AWTEvent` in GUI) that encapsulates details: source, id, timestamp, modifiers, etc. 3. **Event Listener** — an object that implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`). It is registered with the source and is invoked when the event occurs. **Dispatch Flow (3 marks):** ``` User → Source detects action → Source creates Event object → Source invokes registered listener's handler → Handler performs the business logic ``` Use **Diagram 4.1** from Section 4 above for the supporting figure. #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Identification (1 mark):** - **Event Source:** `Checkbox` (named `boldCheck`) - **Event Class:** `ItemEvent` - **Listener Interface:** `ItemListener` ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class BoldToggle extends Frame implements ItemListener { Checkbox boldCheck; TextArea ta; public BoldToggle() { setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setTitle("Bold Toggle Demo"); setSize(400, 250); boldCheck = new Checkbox("Bold"); ta = new TextArea("Type here...", 5, 30); boldCheck.addItemListener(this); // SOURCE -> LISTENER registration add(boldCheck); add(ta); addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { dispose(); } }); } public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { int style = (boldCheck.getState()) ? Font.BOLD : Font.PLAIN; ta.setFont(new Font("Serif", style, 16)); } public static void main(String[] args) { new BoldToggle().setVisible(true); } } ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown:** | Element | Marks | |---|---| | Correct source/event/listener identification (1 line each) | 1.0 | | Imports + class declaration | 0.5 | | `Checkbox` and `TextArea` instantiation | 0.5 | | `addItemListener(this)` registration | 1.0 | | `itemStateChanged` correctly implemented | 1.5 | | Use of `Font.BOLD` and `Font.PLAIN` constants | 0.5 | | Window closing handler | 0.5 | | `main` + `setVisible(true)` | 0.5 | | Code neatness, indentation, comments | 1.0 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Specific to This Question:** > 1. **Using `e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED`** is more *semantically correct* than `e.getStateChange() == 1` (the literal `1` is the same value, but the constant is self-documenting). Using the constant earns an extra 0.5 mark for *good practice*. > 2. **Forgetting to call `setFont(new Font(...))` with a new `Font` object** — you cannot just toggle a bit; you must create a new `Font` instance. > 3. **Not invoking `boldCheck.getState()`** — many students mistakenly use `e.getItem()` (which returns the `Checkbox` itself) instead of querying the source. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sources of Events** - [x] **Event Source** = the component that *generates* the event. Every AWT `Component` is a potential source. - [x] **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** = JDK 1.1 standard, replaces the JDK 1.0 inheritance model. - [x] The three pillars of DEM: **Source** → **Event Object** → **Listener**. - [x] Every event class is a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` → `java.awt.AWTEvent`. - [x] All event sources implement the `java.awt.MenuContainer` interface. - [x] **Adapter classes** (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`) provide empty default implementations of multi-method listener interfaces. - [x] **Semantic events** = `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `AdjustmentEvent`, `TextEvent` — represent *completed* user actions. - [x] **Low-level events** = `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `ComponentEvent`, `ContainerEvent` — represent *single physical* inputs. - [x] Registration syntax: `component.addXxxListener(listenerObject);` (no `new` keyword for the listener if reusing `this` or an existing object). - [x] Removal syntax: `component.removeXxxListener(listenerObject);` — symmetric to registration. - [x] Multiple listeners can be registered to the **same source** for the **same event** (handled by `AWTEventMulticaster`). - [x] The **same listener** can be registered to **multiple sources** — the source can be recovered via `event.getSource()`. - [x] `EventObject.getSource()` returns `Object` — must be downcast to the actual component type. - [x] All event handling runs on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** — never perform long I/O inside a listener. - [x] AWT events are **untyped** at the source level (a `Button` can also receive `MouseEvent`s and `KeyEvent`s), but typed at the **listener** level. ### 🔑 Most-Asked Source-to-Listener Pairs in KTU Exams | Priority | Must Memorize | |---|---| | 🥇 Top | `Button` → `ActionEvent` → `ActionListener` | | 🥈 High | `TextField` → `ActionEvent` / `TextEvent` | | 🥉 Medium | `Checkbox` → `ItemEvent` → `ItemListener` | | 🥉 Medium | `List` / `Choice` → `ItemEvent` | | 4️⃣ | `Frame` → `WindowEvent` → `WindowListener` | | 5️⃣ | `Scrollbar` → `AdjustmentEvent` → `AdjustmentListener` | > **Final Exam Strategy:** When asked *"Give an example of an event source"*, immediately answer with the **complete triad** — Source + Event + Listener + Handler. Examiners reward completeness; a one-line answer is *not* enough for full marks.

topics.title: Event Listener Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Listener Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Event Listener Interfaces — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Event Listener Interface** in Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) is a programming contract (a pure abstract type) that defines one or more abstract methods, known as **event-handler methods**. These methods are automatically invoked by the JVM's event dispatching thread when a specific type of event (e.g., a mouse click, key press, button action, window close) occurs on a registered GUI component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Object Oriented Programming (PEEIT741)*, the **delegation event model** forms the backbone of all GUI-based Java programs. The three principal participants of this model are:
> 1. **Event Source** — the GUI component on which the event originates.
> 2. **Event Object** — encapsulates the data/state of the event.
> 3. **Event Listener Interface** — the receiver that handles the event.

> [!NOTE]
> All Java AWT event listener interfaces are declared inside the `java.awt.event` package, and they all extend the base tagging interface `java.util.EventListener`. This tagging interface contains **no methods** — it merely marks a class as being a valid listener.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Subscription Model

Imagine you subscribe to a daily newspaper. The **publisher** is the *event source*, the **newspaper delivery** is the *event object*, and **you (the subscriber)** are the *event listener*.

- You don't have to go to the press every day. The publisher **delegates** the responsibility of informing you to a delivery system.
- You simply declare "I am interested in receiving this newspaper" (i.e., you **register** as a listener).
- When an event happens (the press prints a paper), the delivery system calls you, and you **read** (handle) the paper.

This is exactly how the **delegation event model** works in Java:

| Newspaper Analogy | Java AWT Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Newspaper Publisher | Event Source (e.g., `JButton`, `JFrame`) |
| Newspaper Itself | Event Object (e.g., `ActionEvent`) |
| Subscriber | Event Listener (e.g., `ActionListener` object) |
| Subscription Form | `component.addListener(listenerObj)` call |
| Reading the News | Overridden handler method (e.g., `actionPerformed()`) |

## 1.3 Why Interfaces Are Used (Not Concrete Classes)

Java cannot anticipate which specific action *your* program needs to take when a button is clicked. Should it save a file? Compute taxes? Draw a circle? Therefore, Java defines **what** must happen (the method signature) and leaves **how** it happens to the programmer — this is the **pure abstraction principle**.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric for KTU**: A class is recognized as a *listener* only if it **implements** a listener interface and **overrides all its abstract methods**. The KTU valuation key explicitly checks for this `implements` keyword in your code.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Delegation Event Model

The flow of an event in a Java AWT/Swing application follows a strict 5-stage lifecycle:

1. **The User performs an action** (e.g., clicks a button). This action is detected by the underlying operating system.
2. **The JVM creates an Event Object** that encapsulates details such as the source component, time, coordinates, and modifiers (e.g., Shift, Ctrl).
3. **The Event Source dispatches the event** by iterating through its internal list of registered listeners and invoking the corresponding handler method on each listener.
4. **The Event Listener's method executes** — this is the user-defined code that reacts to the event.
5. **Control returns to the event dispatch thread**, allowing the GUI to continue processing further events.

## 2.2 Categories of Event Listener Interfaces

KTU 2024 emphasizes the following major listener interfaces (grouped by event class). Each row below is **high-yield** for board exams:

| Listener Interface | Event Class | Key Abstract Method(s) | Triggered By |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | Button click, Menu item select, TextField Enter |
| `MouseListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | Mouse interactions |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | Mouse movement |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyEvent` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` | Keyboard input |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowEvent` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` | Frame/window state changes |
| `ItemListener` | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` | Checkbox toggle, Choice selection |
| `TextListener` | `TextEvent` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` | TextField/TextArea change |
| `AdjustmentListener` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | Scrollbar movement |
| `ComponentListener` | `ComponentEvent` | `componentResized`, `componentMoved`, `componentShown`, `componentHidden` | Component visibility/resize |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusEvent` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | Focus shift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to know the relationship between the **7 methods of `WindowListener`** and the **5 methods of `MouseListener`**. Memorize them — they appear in nearly every Part B question paper.

## 2.3 The Inheritance Hierarchy

All listener interfaces are children of the marker interface `java.util.EventListener`:

```
java.util.EventListener  (marker — no methods)
        |
        +-- java.awt.event.ActionListener
        +-- java.awt.event.MouseListener
        +-- java.awt.event.KeyListener
        +-- java.awt.event.WindowListener
        +-- java.awt.event.ItemListener
        +-- ... (and so on)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `EventListener` marker interface is a **design pattern** known as the *tagging interface*. Its presence allows the JVM (and the `addXxxListener` methods) to perform `instanceof` checks at runtime to confirm the registered object is indeed a listener.

## 2.4 Adapter Classes — The "Shortcut" for Empty Methods

Implementing an interface with 7 abstract methods (like `WindowListener`) is tedious if you only need 1 or 2. Java provides **Adapter classes** in `java.awt.event` that implement the interface with **empty (no-op) method bodies**. You then extend the adapter and override only what you need.

| Listener Interface | Corresponding Adapter Class |
|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` |
| `ComponentListener` | `ComponentAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` |

> [!WARNING]
> Note that `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, and `AdjustmentListener` have **only ONE method** each, so they have **no corresponding adapter class**. You must implement them directly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event listener interfaces are not just academic — they power:

- **Industrial SCADA interfaces** (tank-level alarms triggered by `ActionListener`).
- **Banking ATM screens** (keypad input via `KeyListener`).
- **Medical monitoring dashboards** (mouse-drag-to-zoom on ECG/EKG charts via `MouseMotionListener`).
- **Aerospace cockpit HUDs** (real-time `ComponentListener` resizing on multi-monitor rigs).

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula |
|---|---|
| Listener Registration | `Component source = new Button("OK"); ActionListener l = new MyHandler(); source.addActionListener(l);` |
| Unregistering | `source.removeActionListener(l);` |
| Multiple Listeners | A single source can call **N listeners** in the order they were added. |
| Single Method Count | `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, `AdjustmentListener` $\Rightarrow$ **1 method** |
| Five Method Count | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` $\Rightarrow$ **5 + 2 methods** |
| Seven Method Count | `WindowListener` $\Rightarrow$ **7 methods** |
| Three Method Count | `KeyListener`, `ComponentListener`, `FocusListener` $\Rightarrow$ **3 methods** |
| Package | `java.awt.event` |
| Marker Superinterface | `java.util.EventListener` |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Java Source Code Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following code blocks are presented in the **Java language** because this topic is from a CS course (`PEEIT741 - Object Oriented Programming`). Per the KTU 2024 scheme, Java is the prescribed implementation language for this module.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — `ActionListener` (Most Frequently Asked)

**Question:** *Write a Java AWT program that displays a button labeled "Click Me". When the user clicks the button, the background color of the frame should change to red.*

### Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough

1. **Import** all classes from `java.awt` and `java.awt.event` packages.
2. **Create a class** that extends `Frame` so that the program can act as the main window.
3. **Implement the `ActionListener` interface** in the same class to handle button clicks.
4. **In the constructor**, set the frame size, layout, create a `Button`, and register the listener using `addActionListener(this)`.
5. **Override `actionPerformed()`** — check the command string (or source object) and call `setBackground(Color.red)` on the frame.
6. **In `main()`**, instantiate the class to launch the GUI.

### Complete Java Code

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ColorChanger extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Button b;

    ColorChanger() {
        setTitle("KTU ActionListener Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        b = new Button("Click Me");
        b.addActionListener(this);   // Step 4: Register the listener
        add(b);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Step 5: Override the abstract method
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == b) {
            setBackground(Color.RED);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ColorChanger();
    }
}
```

**Code Logic Row-by-Row:**

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `import java.awt.*;` | Imports `Frame`, `Button`, `FlowLayout`, `Color`. |
| `import java.awt.event.*;` | Imports `ActionListener`, `ActionEvent`. |
| `implements ActionListener` | The class pledges to provide `actionPerformed()`. |
| `b.addActionListener(this);` | **Critical registration step** — the button now knows to call back into *this* object. |
| `setBackground(Color.RED);` | The actual "reaction" to the event. |

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `WindowListener` Using an Adapter Class

**Question:** *Write a Java program to display a window that prints "Window is closing" on the console when the user attempts to close it, and then terminates the program.*

### Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough

1. Since `WindowListener` has 7 methods, we will extend `WindowAdapter` instead of implementing the interface directly.
2. The `WindowAdapter` provides empty bodies for all 7 methods, so we only override `windowClosing()`.
3. We create the frame, register the adapter object, and add a `Label` to make the window non-empty (so the close button is visible).

### Complete Java Code

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class WindowDemo extends Frame {
    WindowDemo() {
        setTitle("KTU WindowListener Demo");
        setSize(300, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        add(new Label("Try closing this window"));

        // Register a WindowAdapter (not the full WindowListener)
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Window is closing");
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new WindowDemo();
    }
}
```

**Code Logic Row-by-Row:**

| Line | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { ... })` | Uses an **anonymous inner class** to extend the adapter. |
| `windowClosing(WindowEvent e)` | Triggered when the user clicks the X-button on the frame. |
| `System.exit(0);` | Terminates the JVM. |

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — `KeyListener` to Display Typed Character

### Complete Java Code

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class KeyDemo extends Frame implements KeyListener {
    TextField tf;
    Label result;

    KeyDemo() {
        setTitle("KTU KeyListener Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        tf = new TextField(20);
        tf.addKeyListener(this);
        result = new Label("Last key: ");
        add(tf);
        add(result);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        result.setText("Last key typed: " + e.getKeyChar());
    }

    public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) {}
    public void keyReleased(KeyEvent e) {}

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new KeyDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU valuation key will deduct marks if the **two empty methods** (`keyPressed`, `keyReleased`) are not provided — the listener interface has 3 abstract methods and **all** must be overridden.

## 3.4 Derivation: Why an Event Source Holds a List of Listeners

The Java compiler internally translates:

$$
\text{source.addActionListener}(L) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{listeners.add}(L)
$$

where `listeners` is an internal `ArrayList<ActionListener>` (or `EventListener` accumulator) inside every `Component`. When the event fires:

$$
\forall \, L \in \text{listeners} : L.\text{actionPerformed}(e)
$$

This means **the order in which `addXxxListener` is called determines the order in which the listeners are notified** — a KTU-favorite viva question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Delegation Event Model — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Action e.g. mouse click] --> osB[Operating System Detects Event]
    osB --> jvmC[JVM Creates Event Object e.g. ActionEvent]
    jvmC --> srcD[Event Source e.g. Button]
    srcD --> regE[Registered Listener e.g. ActionListener]
    regE --> handlerF[Handler Method actionPerformed e]
    handlerF --> reactG[Custom Reaction Code setBackground etc]
    reactG --> retH[Control Returns to Event Dispatch Thread]
    retH --> nextI[Next User Action or Idle]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrow** indicates the direction of event flow.
- Note the **bifurcation** at the Event Source: if *no listener is registered*, the event is silently dropped — this is a classic KTU viva question ("What happens if a button has no listener?").

## 4.2 Listener Interface Inheritance Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[java util EventListener]
    root --> act[ActionListener 1 method]
    root --> item[ItemListener 1 method]
    root --> textL[TextListener 1 method]
    root --> adj[AdjustmentListener 1 method]
    root --> mouse[MouseListener 5 methods]
    root --> mm[MotionListener 2 methods]
    root --> keyL[KeyListener 3 methods]
    root --> focus[FocusListener 2 methods]
    root --> comp[ComponentListener 4 methods]
    root --> win[WindowListener 7 methods]

    mouse --> mouseA[MouseAdapter]
    mm --> mouseMA[MotionAdapter]
    keyL --> keyA[KeyAdapter]
    focus --> focusA[FocusAdapter]
    comp --> compA[ComponentAdapter]
    win --> winA[WindowAdapter]
```

## 4.3 Registration & Event Dispatch Sequence (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant SRC as Button Event Source
    participant JVM as JVM Event Dispatcher
    participant LIS as ActionListener Handler

    U->>SRC: Click the button
    SRC->>JVM: OS notifies JVM
    JVM->>JVM: Construct ActionEvent object
    JVM->>LIS: getListeners list iteration
    LIS->>LIS: actionPerformed e executes
    LIS-->>JVM: Return after user code completes
    JVM-->>SRC: Ready for next event
    SRC-->>U: GUI reflects new state e.g. red background
```

## 4.4 Adapter vs Direct Implementation — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[Need to handle a multi method listener?]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2[Do you need all methods?]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3[Implement the interface directly]
    q2 -- No --> q4[Extend the corresponding Adapter class]
    q1 -- No single method listener --> q3
    q4 --> over[Override only the methods you need]
    q3 --> over2[Override ALL abstract methods]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** What is an event listener in Java AWT? Name any two listener interfaces from the `java.awt.event` package.

**Model Answer:**

An *event listener* is an object that receives and processes notifications when a specific type of event occurs on a GUI component. It is the receiver in the **delegation event model**. The listener must implement a listener interface that defines one or more event-handler methods.

Two listener interfaces from `java.awt.event`:
1. `ActionListener` — handles button clicks and menu item selections.
2. `MouseListener` — handles mouse events such as click, press, release, enter, and exit.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning the **package name** (`java.awt.event`) and the **marker super-interface** (`java.util.EventListener`) earns the third mark.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between a listener interface and an adapter class in Java AWT.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Listener Interface | Adapter Class |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Pure abstract type (can have 1+ abstract methods) | Concrete class providing empty implementations |
| Methods | All methods are abstract | All methods are no-op (empty body) |
| Usage | `class X implements ActionListener` | `class Y extends WindowAdapter` |
| Methods to override | **All** methods (mandatory) | **Only the ones you need** |
| Example | `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` |

## 5.2 Part B Question Bank (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `ActionListener` + `WindowListener`

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the delegation event model in Java AWT with a neat diagram. List the components of the model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java AWT program that creates a frame with a button labeled "Greet". When the button is clicked, a message dialog should display "Welcome to KTU". Implement the appropriate event listener.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**The Delegation Event Model** has three principal components:

1. **Event Source** — the GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`, `Frame`) on which the event occurs.
2. **Event Object** — encapsulates event data (source, type, time, coordinates). All event classes extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
3. **Event Listener Interface** — defines the handler method(s) the source will call when the event fires.

**Flow:**
- A *listener object* is **registered** with the source via `addXxxListener(...)`.
- When the user triggers the event, the source **delegates** the handling by calling the listener's method.
- The listener **processes** the event, and control returns to the GUI thread.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual flow.)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreetApp extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Button b;

    GreetApp() {
        setTitle("KTU Greet App");
        setSize(300, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        b = new Button("Greet");
        b.addActionListener(this);
        add(b);

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == b) {
            Dialog d = new Dialog(this, "Greeting", true);
            d.setSize(200, 100);
            d.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
            d.add(new Label("Welcome to KTU"));
            d.setVisible(true);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new GreetApp();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of the code:**

| Block | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `implements ActionListener` | Registers the class as an action listener. |
| `b.addActionListener(this);` | Adds the current object to the button's listener list. |
| `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | Contains the custom reaction (display dialog). |
| `addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {...})` | Handles window close to terminate the program. |

### Question B (Choice 2) — `KeyListener` + Adapter Pattern

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** List any **five** listener interfaces from the `java.awt.event` package, the event class they handle, and the method they declare.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java AWT program using `KeyListener` to capture the character typed in a `TextField` and display it in a `Label` below the text field. Use a separate handler class to implement the listener.

#### Model Solution to (a)

| S.No. | Listener Interface | Event Class | Method(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ActionListener` | `ActionEvent` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| 2 | `MouseListener` | `MouseEvent` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| 3 | `KeyListener` | `KeyEvent` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` |
| 4 | `WindowListener` | `WindowEvent` | `windowClosing`, `windowOpened`, `windowClosed`, etc. |
| 5 | `ItemListener` | `ItemEvent` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` |

#### Model Solution to (b)

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class KeyHandler implements KeyListener {
    Label lbl;
    KeyHandler(Label lbl) { this.lbl = lbl; }

    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        lbl.setText("Typed: " + e.getKeyChar());
    }
    public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) {}
    public void keyReleased(KeyEvent e) {}
}

public class KeyApp extends Frame {
    KeyApp() {
        setTitle("KTU KeyListener — Separate Handler");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        TextField tf = new TextField(20);
        Label lbl = new Label("Typed char will appear here");
        add(tf);
        add(lbl);

        tf.addKeyListener(new KeyHandler(lbl));

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.exit(0); }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new KeyApp();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of the code:**

- A **separate class `KeyHandler`** implements `KeyListener`, demonstrating the proper separation of concerns.
- The `KeyHandler` constructor takes a `Label` reference so it can update the GUI from outside the `KeyApp` class.
- The two unused methods (`keyPressed`, `keyReleased`) are **mandatorily provided** as no-ops to satisfy the interface contract.
- A `WindowAdapter` (anonymous inner class) is used to gracefully terminate the program on window close.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `implements` keyword** when defining the listener class. Without it, the class is *not* a listener — full 7 marks can be lost.
> 2. **Not providing all abstract methods** for single-class implementations (e.g., omitting `keyPressed`/`keyReleased` in `KeyListener`). Compilation fails.
> 3. **Confusing `ActionEvent`'s `getSource()`** with `getActionCommand()`. Use `getSource()` for object comparison, `getActionCommand()` for label comparison.
> 4. **Forgetting to register the listener** via `addXxxListener(...)`. The compiler will not catch this — the button will simply do nothing.
> 5. **Spelling mistake: `actionPerfomed`** instead of `actionPerformed` — this is a common KTU answer-script slip that loses the full 7 marks for that sub-question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Package**: All listener interfaces live in `java.awt.event`.
- [x] **Marker super-interface**: `java.util.EventListener` (no methods).
- [x] **Three pillars of the delegation event model**: Source $\rightarrow$ Event Object $\rightarrow$ Listener.
- [x] **Single-method listeners**: `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, `AdjustmentListener` — implement them directly; **no adapters exist**.
- [x] **Multi-method listeners**: `MouseListener` (5), `WindowListener` (7), `KeyListener` (3), etc. — use corresponding Adapter classes to avoid boilerplate.
- [x] **Registration syntax**: `component.addXxxListener(listenerObject);`
- [x] **Unregistration syntax**: `component.removeXxxListener(listenerObject);`
- [x] **Multiple listeners allowed**: A single source can have N listeners, called in registration order.
- [x] **Event dispatching is single-threaded** (the Event Dispatch Thread) — long tasks in handlers will freeze the GUI.
- [x] **An anonymous inner class** (`new ActionListener() { ... }`) is a common KTU-friendly way to define a listener inline.
- [x] **`getSource()`** returns the originating component; **`getActionCommand()`** returns the button's label string.
- [x] **Compulsory `main()` method** inside the frame class to launch the GUI.
- [x] **Window close** must be handled explicitly with a `WindowAdapter` — otherwise the window hides but the JVM does not exit.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mantra**: *"Implement or Extend, but always Override."* Implement a single-method listener; extend an adapter for multi-method listeners. Always override **all** abstract methods, and always remember to **register** the listener using `addXxxListener(...)` — otherwise your GUI is silent forever.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using the Delegation Model, Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using the Delegation Model, Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Event Delegation Model

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Event Delegation Model (EDM)** is a design architecture introduced in JDK 1.1 in which the responsibility of handling an event generated by a GUI component is *delegated* (handed over) from the component itself to a separate object known as an **Event Listener** (or **Delegate**). The source component generates the event object and forwards it to one or more registered listeners via method invocation — the listener contains the actual business logic for handling that event.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Subscription Model
Imagine you live in a house. Instead of the newspaper company sending reporters to your house every time news breaks (the old AWT 1.0 **inheritance model**), you simply **register your address with the newspaper office (the event source)**. When an event (news) occurs, the office automatically dispatches the paper to all registered subscribers (listeners). You — the subscriber — decide what to do with it: read it, file it, or recycle it. The newspaper office does not know or care how you handle it. This *decoupled* publication–subscription flow is exactly the Event Delegation Model.

### Key Vocabulary (KTU Board Favourites)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Event Source** | The GUI object that originates the event (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`) |
| **Event Object** | An instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` describing what happened |
| **Event Listener** | An object whose class implements a `XxxListener` interface — the receiver of the event |
| **Event Delegation** | The act of the source *passing* the event object to the listener via a callback method |
| **Callback Method** | The method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) automatically invoked by the source on the listener |

---

## 1.2 The Java Thread Model

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Thread** in Java is the smallest unit of execution *within a process*. Java's **Thread Model** is the framework that defines how threads are created, scheduled, synchronized, and terminated. It is built on the philosophy *"one program, one or more threads of execution"*, where each thread shares the process's heap memory but maintains its own **program counter**, **stack**, and **local variables**. Java was the *first mainstream language* to **build thread support into the language itself** via the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` interface.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen
Think of a Java program as a busy restaurant kitchen.
- The **Restaurant (Process)** has one shared pantry (Heap Memory) and one set of recipes (Bytecode).
- Each **Chef (Thread)** has their *own* cutting board, knife, and station (Stack + PC).
- The **Head Chef (Main Thread)** starts the service, but he can *hire* more chefs (spawn threads) to chop, fry, and plate simultaneously.
- All chefs draw ingredients from the **same pantry**, so they must be careful not to grab the same tomato at the same time — this is where **synchronization** (the kitchen rulebook) comes in.

### Why Multithreading? — KTU Board Answer Skeleton
> [!NOTE] **Why we need threads in Java (4-mark favourite)**
> 1. **Responsiveness** — A GUI thread keeps the interface alive while a worker thread does heavy computation.
> 2. **Resource Economy** — Threads share the process memory, no need to spin up a new process (which is costly in RAM and startup time).
> 3. **Parallelism on Multi-Core CPUs** — Multiple threads run in *true parallel* on different cores.
> 4. **Asynchronous Behaviour** — Networking, animation, file I/O can occur without freezing the main flow.

### The Two Strategic Models of Multithreading

| Model | Description | KTU 2024 Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Green Thread Model** | Threads managed *entirely by the JVM*, not the OS. Used in early Sun JDK on Solaris. | **Deprecated** in modern Java; historical reference only |
| **Native Thread Model** | Threads are mapped **1-to-1** to OS-level (kernel) threads managed by the underlying operating system. | **Current Standard** — used by all modern JVMs on Linux, Windows, macOS |

> [!IMPORTANT] Modern Java uses a **Native Thread Model** where `java.lang.Thread` is a thin wrapper over the OS thread. The JVM, however, still owns the **thread scheduler** and decides *when* each kernel thread gets CPU time using a **preemptive, priority-based** algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Event Delegation Model

The Delegation Model has **four essential participants**. The KTU board *loves* asking you to list them — memorize this.

### Step 1 — The Event Source Registers Itself
A GUI component (e.g., `JButton btn = new JButton("Click");`) is created. Internally, the JDK provides methods like `addActionListener(...)` so external objects can register their interest.

### Step 2 — The Listener is Bound
A class implements a listener interface. The source component holds a `List<MyListener>` internally and stores the reference. Multiple listeners may be registered for one event — this is **broadcast delegation**.

### Step 3 — The Event Occurs
When the user clicks the button, the AWT/Swing Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) constructs an `ActionEvent` object containing:
- The **source** (`btn`)
- The **ID** (`ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED`)
- The **timestamp** and any command string.

### Step 4 — Delegation Call-back
The source iterates through its listener list and invokes `listener.actionPerformed(e)` on every registered listener. The **source does nothing else** — pure delegation.

### Advantages Over the Old Inheritance Model

| Feature | Old (JDK 1.0 Inheritance) | New (Delegation Model) |
|---|---|---|
| Reusability | Limited — each class pre-defined which events it handles | **Any** class can become a listener for **any** source |
| Separation of Concerns | UI & logic mixed | UI in `JFrame`, logic in separate `Controller` |
| Extensibility | Hard to add new event types | Trivial — define a new `XxxEvent` and `XxxListener` |
| Event Propagation | None | Events can be **consumed** by listeners |
| Multi-listener | Not supported | **One source → many listeners** natively supported |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Pitfall Avoidance** — Do not confuse *Event Delegation* with *Delegation Event Model*. They are the same thing; the official JDK 1.1 documentation uses both phrasings.

---

## 2.2 Java Thread Class — Internal Architecture

### The `java.lang.Thread` Class — Key Fields

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `name` | `String` | Human-readable thread name (default: `Thread-N`) |
| `priority` | `int` | Hint to scheduler; range **1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX)**, default **5 (NORM)** |
| `daemon` | `boolean` | If `true`, thread dies when only daemons remain |
| `target` | `Runnable` | The "work object" whose `run()` will be invoked |
| `threadStatus` | `int` | JVM-private state (NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED) |
| `threadId` | `long` | Unique positive ID assigned at creation |
| `threadGroup` | `ThreadGroup` | Security/management group this thread belongs to |

### Java's Thread Creation — Two Mandatory Ways
Java provides exactly **two** language-level ways to create a thread. The KTU board almost always asks: *"Explain the two ways of creating a thread in Java."*

#### Path A — Extend the `Thread` Class
```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        // Code executed on the new thread
        System.out.println("Running in: " + getName());
    }
}
// Usage:
MyThread t = new MyThread();
t.start();   // creates new call stack, calls run() on it
```

#### Path B — Implement the `Runnable` Interface (Delegation Pattern in Action!)
```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Task running on: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}
// Usage:
Thread t = new Thread(new MyTask());
t.start();
```

### Why Path B is the "Right" Way — Interface vs Inheritance
> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Insight**
> Since Java does not support **multiple class inheritance**, extending `Thread` locks your class out of inheriting any other useful base class. Implementing `Runnable` is preferred because:
> 1. Your task class is **not tied to a thread type** — better OOP.
> 2. The *task* and the *thread* are separated (pure **delegation** — the `Thread` object delegates the `run()` call to its `Runnable` target).
> 3. The same `Runnable` instance can be **shared by multiple threads**, allowing data sharing without extra effort.

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Mistake** — Calling `t.run()` instead of `t.start()`. `run()` executes in the *current* thread's stack; `start()` is the only way to create a **new** call stack and invoke `run()` concurrently.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Thread State Diagram Symbols

| Constant in `Thread.State` | Meaning | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **NEW** | Thread object created, `start()` not yet called | `new Thread(...)` |
| **RUNNABLE** | Eligible to run; may be actually running on CPU | `start()` called |
| **BLOCKED** | Waiting to acquire a monitor lock (synchronization) | Entered `synchronized` block held by another thread |
| **WAITING** | Waiting indefinitely until another thread notifies | `Object.wait()`, `Thread.join()` (no timeout), `LockSupport.park()` |
| **TIMED_WAITING** | Waiting for a specified time | `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` |
| **TERMINATED** | `run()` has finished (normally or via exception) | `run()` returns / uncaught exception |

### Key Methods on `Thread` (Board Favourites)

| Method | Signature | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `start()` | `void start()` | Spawns new call stack, calls `run()` once |
| `run()` | `void run()` | Entry-point executed by the new thread |
| `sleep(long ms)` | `static void sleep(long)` | Pauses *current* thread; **does NOT release locks** |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints scheduler to give other threads a chance |
| `join()` | `void join()` | Calling thread blocks until target thread finishes |
| `interrupt()` | `void interrupt()` | Sets interrupt flag; many methods throw `InterruptedException` |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean isAlive()` | True if thread has started and not yet died |
| `setPriority(int)` | `void setPriority(int)` | Range 1–10; `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY`, `MAX_PRIORITY`, `NORM_PRIORITY` |
| `setDaemon(boolean)` | `void setDaemon(boolean)` | Must be called *before* `start()` |

### KTU 2024 Mandatory Distinction Table

| Method | Static? | Throws `InterruptedException`? | Releases Lock? | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sleep()` | Yes | Yes | **No** | milliseconds / nanoseconds |
| `wait()` (on Object) | No | Yes | **Yes** | milliseconds (with overload) |
| `yield()` | Yes | No | N/A | N/A |
| `join()` | No | Yes | N/A | milliseconds |
| `notify()` (on Object) | No | No | N/A | N/A |
| `notifyAll()` (on Object) | No | No | N/A | N/A |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Event Delegation Model in Action

**Problem (KTU Model Style):** Write a Java program that creates a window with two buttons. When the first button is clicked, the background should turn red. When the second button is clicked, the background should turn blue. Use the **Event Delegation Model**.

### Exhaustive Java Solution
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ColorChanger extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    private JButton btnRed, btnBlue;
    private Container cp;

    // ---- Constructor ----
    public ColorChanger() {
        setTitle("Delegation Model Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        cp = getContentPane();
        cp.setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        btnRed  = new JButton("RED");
        btnBlue = new JButton("BLUE");

        // === STEP 1: SOURCE REGISTERS LISTENER (DELEGATION) ===
        btnRed.addActionListener(this);
        btnBlue.addActionListener(this);

        cp.add(btnRed);
        cp.add(btnBlue);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    // ---- STEP 2: CALLBACK METHOD IMPLEMENTED BY LISTENER ----
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // e.getSource() returns the component that fired the event
        if (e.getSource() == btnRed) {
            cp.setBackground(Color.RED);
        } else if (e.getSource() == btnBlue) {
            cp.setBackground(Color.BLUE);
        }
    }

    // ---- Main thread (UI thread / EDT) ----
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // EDT-safe launch (recommended for Swing)
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ColorChanger());
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow
1. `JButton btnRed` and `btnBlue` are created — these are the **event sources**.
2. `btnRed.addActionListener(this)` — `this` (the `ColorChanger` object) **registers** itself as a listener. Internally, the button stores `this` in an `EventListener` list.
3. User clicks `btnRed` → Swing's EDT constructs an `ActionEvent` object `e` with `e.getSource() == btnRed`.
4. The button iterates its listener list and calls `this.actionPerformed(e)` — **this is delegation**.
5. The listener (`this`) decides what to do (change background to RED).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Two Ways of Creating a Thread

**Problem (KTU Model Style, 14 marks):** Explain the two ways of creating a thread in Java with suitable programs. State the advantages of the `Runnable` interface over the `Thread` class.

### Path A — Extending `Thread`

```java
class HelloThread extends Thread {
    public HelloThread(String name) {
        super(name);   // sets thread name
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " - Count: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        HelloThread t1 = new HelloThread("Worker-A");
        HelloThread t2 = new HelloThread("Worker-B");
        t1.start();   // t1 begins executing run() on new stack
        t2.start();   // t2 begins executing run() on a second new stack
        System.out.println("Main thread done.");
    }
}
```

### Path B — Implementing `Runnable` (the Delegation Pattern)
```java
class HelloTask implements Runnable {
    private final String tag;

    public HelloTask(String tag) { this.tag = tag; }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(tag + " - Count: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new HelloTask("Worker-A"), "T1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new HelloTask("Worker-B"), "T2");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        System.out.println("Main thread done.");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace
1. `main()` starts on the **Main thread**.
2. `new Thread(new HelloTask("Worker-A"), "T1")` constructs a `Thread` object whose `target` field points to the `HelloTask` instance.
3. `t1.start()` does **two** things:
   - Allocates a fresh **call stack** in JVM memory.
   - Invokes the new stack's first frame: `target.run()`.
4. Both threads (Main + T1 + T2) compete for CPU time according to the scheduler.
5. Output is **non-deterministic** — order of "Worker-A" vs "Worker-B" lines varies between runs. This is a classic KTU question: *"Why is the output not fixed?"*

### Analytical Matrix — Path A vs Path B

| Criterion | `extends Thread` | `implements Runnable` |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance flexibility | Wastes the single-inheritance slot | **Inherits freely from any class** |
| Code–Thread separation | Task & thread merged | Task & thread **decoupled** (true OOP) |
| Shareable among threads | Each thread is its own subclass instance | **Same `Runnable` can be passed to many `Thread`s** |
| Implements **Delegation** principle | No | **Yes — Thread delegates `run()` to `Runnable`** |
| KTU recommendation | Avoid in production | **Preferred** |

### Expected Sample Output (Interleaved)
```
Worker-A - Count: 1
Main thread done.
Worker-B - Count: 1
Worker-A - Count: 2
Worker-B - Count: 2
...
```

---

## 3.3 Thread Lifecycle — Step-by-Step State Transitions

The Java thread moves through **6 states** defined in `Thread.State` enum. Let us derive each transition.

### State Diagram Equations
Let $S$ be the current state of a thread. A thread's life is governed by:

$$
S(t) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{NEW}, & \text{when } t < t_{\text{start}} \\
\text{RUNNABLE}, & \text{after } \texttt{start()} \text{ is called} \\
\text{BLOCKED}, & \text{waiting for monitor lock} \\
\text{WAITING}, & \text{after } \texttt{wait()}\text{, }\texttt{join()}\text{, }\texttt{park()} \\
\text{TIMED\_WAITING}, & \text{after } \texttt{sleep(ms)}\text{, }\texttt{wait(ms)}\text{, }\texttt{join(ms)} \\
\text{TERMINATED}, & \text{after } \texttt{run()} \text{ returns}
\end{cases}
$$

### Transition Table

| From State | Method Triggered | To State |
|---|---|---|
| NEW | `start()` | RUNNABLE |
| RUNNABLE | `Object.wait()` | WAITING |
| RUNNABLE | `Object.wait(ms)` | TIMED_WAITING |
| RUNNABLE | Enters `synchronized` block held by another thread | BLOCKED |
| RUNNABLE | `Thread.sleep(ms)` | TIMED_WAITING |
| RUNNABLE | `Thread.join()` (caller side) | WAITING |
| BLOCKED | Lock released by holder | RUNNABLE |
| WAITING | `Object.notify()` / `notifyAll()` | BLOCKED → RUNNABLE |
| TIMED_WAITING | Timeout elapsed or `notify()` | RUNNABLE |
| RUNNABLE | `run()` returns (or uncaught exception) | TERMINATED |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Pitfall** — `sleep()` does **NOT** release the lock, but `wait()` **DOES**. This is the most-marks-losing distinction in multithreading questions.

---

## 3.4 Priorities — How They Actually Work

A Java thread priority is an *integer in [1, 10]*. The thread scheduler on every OS behaves differently, so Java defines priorities only as **hints**.

$$
\text{Priority}(t) \in \{\text{MIN}=1,\; \text{NORM}=5,\; \text{MAX}=10\}
$$

> [!NOTE] **The Formula**
> $$\text{Effective Priority} = \text{Thread's } setPriority() \text{ value, mapped to OS native priority}$$
> On Linux, Java's 1–10 maps to `nice` values via `pthread_setschedparam`. On Windows, it maps to `SetThreadPriority` levels. The JVM does **not** guarantee that a higher-priority thread preempts a lower-priority one — it is a *hint*, not a contract.

### Daemon Threads — The Background Workers
A **daemon** thread is a low-priority service provider (e.g., the Garbage Collector, Finalizer thread). The JVM does not wait for daemon threads when shutting down.

$$
\text{JVM exits when the last non-daemon thread terminates.}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Event Delegation Model — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeU[USER ACTION] --> nodeS[EVENT SOURCE]
    nodeS[EG JButton] --> nodeEO[EVENT OBJECT]
    nodeEO -- dispatched to --> nodeL[EVENT LISTENER]
    nodeL -- implements --> nodeLI[ActionListener interface]
    nodeL -- callback method --> nodeAM[actionPerformed]
    nodeAM --> nodeH[HANDLER LOGIC]
    nodeH --> nodeU

    nodeM[LISTENER REGISTRY] -.-> nodeS
    nodeL -. registered in .-> nodeM

    classDef src fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
