```mermaid
    classDef lst fill:#d6e7ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef evt fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#a68500,color:#000

    class nodeS,nodeM src
    class nodeL,nodeLI,nodeAM,nodeH lst
    class nodeEO,nodeU evt
```

### Reading the Diagram
- The **solid arrows** show the runtime flow: user action → source → event object → listener → callback → handler.
- The **dashed arrows** show the registration phase: the listener registers itself with the source *before* the event happens.

---

## 4.2 Java Thread Lifecycle — Mermaid State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start called
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : waiting for monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait join park
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify notifyAll
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep wait ms join ms
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout elapsed or notify
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run returns or uncaught exception
    TERMINATED --> [*]

    note right of NEW : Thread object created, start not called
    note right of RUNNABLE : Eligible for CPU, may be running
    note right of BLOCKED : Waiting for synchronized lock
    note right of WAITING : Indefinite wait
    note right of TIMED_WAITING : Bounded wait
    note right of TERMINATED : run method exited
```

---

## 4.3 Two Thread Creation Strategies — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Path A: extends Thread"
        A1[Subclass of Thread] --> A2[Overrides run method]
        A2 --> A3[new MyThread.start]
        A3 --> A4[JVM creates new stack]
        A4 --> A5[run executed on new stack]
    end

    subgraph "Path B: implements Runnable"
        B1[Task class implements Runnable] --> B2[Provides run method]
        B2 --> B3[new Thread new MyTask]
        B3 --> B4[Thread holds target reference]
        B4 --> B5[start triggers target.run]
    end

    A5 --- SHARED[SHARED HEAP MEMORY]
    B5 --- SHARED

    SHARED --> COUNTER[shared data structures]

    classDef pathA fill:#ffe1c2,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef pathB fill:#c2e5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef shared fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 pathA
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 pathB
    class SHARED,COUNTER shared
```

---

## 4.4 Memory Model — Stack vs Heap for Threads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC["PROCESS - JVM"]
        subgraph T1["Thread 1 Stack"]
            T1PC[PC]
            T1LV[Local Vars]
            T1FR[Frames]
        end
        subgraph T2["Thread 2 Stack"]
            T2PC[PC]
            T2LV[Local Vars]
            T2FR[Frames]
        end
        subgraph T3["Main Thread Stack"]
            T3PC[PC]
            T3LV[Local Vars]
        end
        HEAP["SHARED HEAP - Objects, Static Fields, Class Metadata"]
    end

    T1FR -. accesses .-> HEAP
    T2FR -. accesses .-> HEAP
    T3LV -. accesses .-> HEAP

    classDef stack fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef heap fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#00802b,color:#000

    class T1,T2,T3,T1PC,T1LV,T1FR,T2PC,T2LV,T2FR,T3PC,T3LV stack
    class HEAP heap
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight** — Because all threads share the same heap, **object references are shared but local primitives on the stack are not**. This is the root cause of nearly every multithreading bug (race conditions, visibility issues) and motivates the need for synchronization.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Event Delegation Model? List its key participants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Event Delegation Model** is an event-handling architecture in which the responsibility of processing an event generated by a GUI component is **delegated** to a separate object called the *event listener*. The component (event source) does not handle the event itself; it simply notifies all registered listeners by invoking a callback method on them. *(1 mark)*

The four key participants are:
1. **Event Source** — the component that generates the event (e.g., `JButton`). *(1 mark)*
2. **Event Object** — instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` describing the event. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Event Listener** — object implementing a `XxxListener` interface that receives the event. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Event Delegation / Callback** — the source invokes the listener's callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) to handle the event. *(marginal credit)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `start()` | `run()` |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Spawns a **new call stack** and invokes `run()` on it | Just a normal method call |
| Thread created? | **Yes**, a new thread of execution begins | **No**, executes in the caller's thread |
| Invocation | Called **once** per thread instance | Can be called any number of times |
| Multi-threading | Enables concurrent execution | Does **not** enable concurrency |

If a programmer calls `t.run()` instead of `t.start()`, the code runs sequentially in the *current* thread — defeating the purpose of multithreading. *(1 mark for the pitfall)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Event Delegation Model in Java with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages over the inheritance-based event handling model of JDK 1.0. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program using the Event Delegation Model where clicking a button displays "Hello KTU" in a text field. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Event Delegation Model — Explanation (7 marks)**

The **Event Delegation Model** (introduced in JDK 1.1) is a design in which event handling responsibility is *delegated* from the GUI component (event source) to one or more external objects called **event listeners**. The steps are:

1. **Listener Registration** — A class implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) and registers itself with the source using `source.addXxxListener(listener)`. *(1 mark)*
2. **Event Generation** — When the user interacts with the source, the AWT/Swing EDT creates an `EventObject` subclass instance (e.g., `ActionEvent`) carrying source info, ID, and timestamp. *(1 mark)*
3. **Delegation** — The source iterates through its internal listener list and invokes the appropriate callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`) on each listener. *(2 marks)*
4. **Handling** — The listener executes the application-specific logic and may also call `e.consume()` to stop propagation. *(1 mark)*

**Advantages over JDK 1.0 Inheritance Model (2 marks):**
- **Decoupling** — UI code and event-handling code are in separate classes (better OOP design).
- **Reusability** — A single listener class can be reused for many sources; a single source can have many listeners.
- **Extensibility** — New event types and listener interfaces are easy to add.
- **Event propagation control** via the `EventObject.consume()` method.

**Neat Diagram** (as in Section 4.1 above) — *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Java Program — Clicking a Button Displays "Hello KTU" (7 marks)**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class HelloKTU extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    private JTextField txt;
    private JButton btn;

    public HelloKTU() {
        setTitle("Hello KTU");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        txt = new JTextField(15);
        btn = new JButton("Greet");

        // SOURCE REGISTERS LISTENER (DELEGATION)
        btn.addActionListener(this);

        add(txt);
        add(btn);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // CALLBACK METHOD - the actual delegation target
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        txt.setText("Hello KTU");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new HelloKTU());
    }
}
```

**Mark Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Correct class extending `JFrame` and implementing `ActionListener` — *1 mark*
- GUI components instantiated (`JTextField`, `JButton`) — *1 mark*
- Listener registered via `addActionListener(this)` — *1 mark*
- `actionPerformed` overridden and `txt.setText("Hello KTU")` written — *2 marks*
- `main` method and `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` for EDT safety — *1 mark*
- Code compiles and runs correctly — *1 mark*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Java Thread Model. Discuss the two ways of creating a thread in Java with suitable code snippets. State the advantages of `Runnable` over the `Thread` class. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that creates three threads, each printing numbers 1 to 5 with a 300 ms delay. Use the `Runnable` interface. Predict and justify the order of outputs. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Java Thread Model + Two Thread Creation Ways (7 marks)**

The **Java Thread Model** defines how Java programs use multiple threads of execution. A *thread* is a single sequential flow of control within a process. Java supports multithreading at the **language level** (not just the OS level) through:
- The `java.lang.Thread` class
- The `java.lang.Runnable` functional interface
- Keywords `synchronized`, `volatile`
- Methods `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` inherited from `Object`

**Thread States:** NEW → RUNNABLE → (BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING) → TERMINATED *(1 mark)*

**Two Ways to Create a Thread:**

**Way 1 — Extending `Thread` class:**
```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running: " + getName());
    }
}
MyThread t = new MyThread();
t.start();
```

**Way 2 — Implementing `Runnable` interface:** *(code as in Section 3.2 Path B)*

**Advantages of `Runnable` over `Thread`:** *(2 marks)*
- Java does not support multiple inheritance — extending `Thread` blocks extending other useful classes.
- The task (`Runnable`) and the thread (`Thread`) are decoupled, applying the **Delegation** principle: the thread delegates execution to its `Runnable` target.
- The same `Runnable` instance can be passed to multiple `Thread` objects, enabling **shared data** among threads naturally.
- Better adherence to OOP — a `Runnable` is a *task*, not a *thread*, separating concerns.

---

**(b) Three Threads Printing Numbers (7 marks)**

```java
class NumberPrinter implements Runnable {
    private final String label;

    public NumberPrinter(String label) {
        this.label = label;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(label + " : " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(300);   // delay in ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(label + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new NumberPrinter("T1"));
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new NumberPrinter("T2"));
        Thread t3 = new Thread(new NumberPrinter("T3"));

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t3.start();
    }
}
```

**Predicted Output — Justification (2 marks):**
The output is **non-deterministic**. All three threads begin almost simultaneously, and the **thread scheduler** decides the execution order. Possible interleaving:
```
T1 : 1
T3 : 1
T2 : 1
T1 : 2
T2 : 2
T3 : 2
...
```

The order *varies* between runs because:
1. The OS scheduler uses a **preemptive, priority-based** algorithm with quantum time-slicing.
2. Even though all threads have the same default priority (5), the underlying OS may map them to different native priorities.
3. `sleep(300)` only pauses for *at least* 300 ms — the next run can be much later if the CPU is busy.
4. The JVM does not guarantee any order — this is **a feature**, not a bug, of true concurrency.

**Mark Breakdown (7 marks):**
- `NumberPrinter implements Runnable` correctly — *1 mark*
- `run()` with loop 1 to 5 and `Thread.sleep(300)` — *2 marks*
- Three `Thread` objects instantiated in `main` — *1 mark*
- `start()` called on all three — *1 mark*
- Correctly identifying that output is **non-deterministic** with reason — *2 marks*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (3 most common mistakes):**
> 1. **Confusing `start()` with `run()`** — Calling `run()` directly. Result: no multithreading happens, and the examiner will deduct **at least 2 marks** for failing to demonstrate concurrency.
> 2. **Forgetting to register the listener** — Writing `btn.addActionListener(this);` incorrectly or omitting it. The button does nothing, and the program silently fails at runtime. Always double-check the listener registration line.
> 3. **Writing `extends Thread` AND trying to extend another class** — Java does not support multiple inheritance. Use `implements Runnable` if your class needs to inherit from another class (e.g., `JFrame`).
> 4. **Believing `sleep()` releases locks** — It does **not**. Only `wait()` releases the monitor lock. Conflating these is the #1 marks-killer in synchronization questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize**

- **Event Delegation Model (EDM)** = Source + Event Object + Listener + Callback.
- Four participants: **Source, Event, Listener, Delegate (callback)**.
- Source registers listener via `addXxxListener(...)`.
- Source invokes listener's callback method when the event occurs.
- **JDK 1.0** used inheritance-based event handling — replaced by EDM in **JDK 1.1**.
- A **Thread** = lightweight sub-process. Java uses the **Native Thread Model** (1-to-1 with OS threads).
- A **Process** has at least **one** thread (the Main thread).
- Two ways to create a thread: `extends Thread` OR `implements Runnable` (preferred).
- `start()` creates a new call stack and calls `run()`; `run()` is just a method.
- **Always prefer `Runnable`** — it honours OOP delegation and avoids the single-inheritance problem.
- Thread states: **NEW → RUNNABLE → (BLOCKED | WAITING | TIMED_WAITING) → TERMINATED**.
- Thread priorities range from **1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX)**, default **5 (NORM)**.
- `sleep()` — does **not** release locks; `wait()` — **does** release locks.
- `yield()` — static hint to scheduler; `join()` — caller waits for target to finish.
- **Daemon threads** die when only daemons remain (e.g., Garbage Collector).
- Output of multithreaded programs is **non-deterministic** — never write answers claiming a fixed order.
- **Synchronization** is the next topic — uses `synchronized` keyword or `Lock` interface to prevent race conditions on shared heap data.
- Each thread has its **own stack and PC**, but **shares the heap** with other threads in the same process.
- `Thread.currentThread()` returns a reference to the currently executing thread object — useful inside `run()`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Main Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Main Thread

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, **the Main Thread** is the primary, non-daemon thread that is automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) when a program begins execution. It is the thread responsible for executing the entry-point method `public static void main(String[] args)` of the program. Conceptually, every standalone Java application possesses exactly **one main thread** upon startup, even if the program does not explicitly create any additional threads.

From a formal Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) and concurrent programming standpoint, the main thread is an instance of the `java.lang.Thread` class (or, more accurately, an instance of a class implementing `java.lang.Runnable` whose `run()` method is invoked internally by the JVM bootstrap sequence). The main thread is identified by the name **"main"** when retrieved via the `Thread.currentThread().getName()` method. Its initial priority is set to **Thread.NORM_PRIORITY**, which corresponds to the integer value **5** on a scale of 1 to 10.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The main thread is fundamental to understanding thread lifecycle, daemon threads, and exception propagation. Any uncaught exception thrown in the `main` method that is not handled propagates to the main thread's default uncaught exception handler, which terminates the JVM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *"The Main Thread is the first user-thread spawned by the JVM when a Java application starts. It begins execution at the `main()` method and acts as the parent from which all subsequent threads (daemon or non-daemon) are typically created."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are directing a large theatrical play.

- **The Director (Main Thread):** The play begins the moment the director walks onto the stage and says, "Action!". The director is the first person to begin working, manages the opening scene, and from his cue, all the actors (other threads) come out one by one to perform their roles.
- **The Stagehands (Daemon Threads):** Background workers like the lighting crew, sound operators, and the cleaner who mops up between scenes. They work silently in the background. When the director leaves (the main thread finishes), they automatically stop — even if they were in the middle of polishing the floor.
- **The Audience (JVM):** The audience will only leave when the director and all the actors have finished the play. If the main thread (director) ends but some actors are still on stage, the show continues. But if only stagehands remain, the audience goes home (JVM exits).

This analogy makes it clear: **The main thread is the orchestrator** — it is the primary user-controlled thread whose lifecycle directly influences when the JVM terminates.

## 1.3 Properties of the Main Thread

| Property | Default Value | Description |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Name** | `"main"` | Retrieved via `Thread.currentThread().getName()` |
| **Priority** | `5` (Thread.NORM_PRIORITY) | Default priority, range is 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX) |
| **Daemon Status** | `false` (Non-daemon) | It is a *user thread*, so JVM waits for it to finish |
| **Thread Group** | `main` | Belongs to the thread group named "main" |
| **Stack Size** | JVM-dependent | Can sometimes be configured via JVM flags |
| **Parent** | None (JVM-spawned) | Created by the JVM bootstrap process |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** Even though we say "the main thread", under the hood, the JVM creates a `Thread` object, instantiates it, and invokes its `start()` mechanism (which then calls `run()` containing the user's `main()` method body). The main thread *is* a thread object, just like any other.

## 1.4 Why Study the Main Thread?

Studying the main thread is crucial because:

1. **Lifecycle Management:** It determines when the program logically "ends".
2. **Exception Handling:** Unhandled exceptions in the main thread cause JVM termination.
3. **Daemon vs. User Threads:** The main thread is a *user thread*, so the JVM does not exit until it completes.
4. **Thread Control Reference:** It serves as the reference point from which child threads are spawned.
5. **Concurrency Foundation:** Understanding the main thread is the gateway to understanding multithreading, race conditions, and synchronization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Main Thread

The execution flow of a Java program with respect to the main thread can be broken down into the following logical stages:

### Stage 1 — JVM Bootstrap
- The Java Virtual Machine is loaded into memory by the operating system.
- The JVM initializes its internal data structures: heap, method area, stack, and PC registers.

### Stage 2 — Main Thread Creation
- The JVM creates a new `Thread` object.
- This thread is assigned the name **"main"**.
- It belongs to a `ThreadGroup` also named **"main"**.
- Its priority is set to **Thread.NORM_PRIORITY** = **5**.
- Its daemon status is set to **false** (a user thread).

### Stage 3 — Execution of `main(String[] args)`
- The newly created main thread begins executing the body of the user's `main()` method.
- From this method, statements are executed sequentially (unless concurrency is introduced).
- Other threads may be created using `Thread t = new Thread(...); t.start();`.

### Stage 4 — Thread Termination Logic
- When `main()` returns, the main thread enters the dying state.
- However, the JVM does **not** terminate immediately if any other user threads are still alive.
- The JVM exits only when **all user threads have terminated** (and any shutdown hooks have run).

### Stage 5 — JVM Shutdown
- If only daemon threads remain when the main thread finishes, the JVM terminates them abruptly.
- `System.exit(0)` can be invoked to force termination, but normal program flow ends when the main thread completes.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why a separate thread?** The JVM design treats `main()` like any other thread's `run()` method for uniformity. This unified design simplifies thread management.
- **How is priority set?** The constructor of the main thread internally calls `setPriority(NORM_PRIORITY)`.
- **How does the JVM know the main thread has ended?** It tracks thread state via the `Thread.State` enum (`NEW`, `RUNNABLE`, `BLOCKED`, `WAITING`, `TIMED_WAITING`, `TERMINATED`).
- **Why is it non-daemon?** Because the main thread represents user-level work; the JVM must wait for it to complete meaningful application logic.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / API | Value / Output | Notes |
|---------|--------------|----------------|-------|
| Main Thread Name | `Thread.currentThread().getName()` | `"main"` | Default string identifier |
| Main Thread Priority | `Thread.currentThread().getPriority()` | `5` | Range: 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX) |
| Main Thread Daemon | `Thread.currentThread().isDaemon()` | `false` | User thread |
| Main Thread Group | `Thread.currentThread().getThreadGroup().getName()` | `"main"` | Top-level group |
| Main Thread State (running) | `Thread.currentThread().getState()` | `RUNNABLE` | When actively executing |
| Main Thread ID | `Thread.currentThread().getId()` | JVM-assigned | Unique long value |
| Min Priority Constant | `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY` | `1` | Static final int |
| Normal Priority Constant | `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` | `5` | Static final int |
| Max Priority Constant | `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY` | `10` | Static final int |
| JVM Exit Condition | All user threads terminated | Boolean | Daemons don't block exit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world enterprise systems (banking, telecom, IoT), the main thread is often a *bootstrap coordinator*. Frameworks like Spring Boot use the main thread to initialize the application context before delegating work to a thread pool (e.g., Tomcat's connector threads). Understanding main-thread behavior is essential for graceful shutdown and resource cleanup.

## 2.4 Relationship Between Main Thread and Daemon Threads

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │           JVM Lifecycle Control          │
        └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
        ┌─────────────────┼────────────────────┐
        ▼                 ▼                    ▼
   User Threads      User Threads         Daemon Threads
   (e.g., main)      (custom threads)     (GC, finalizer)
        │                 │                    │
        └────► JVM WAITS for ALL ◄─────────────┘
                  before exiting
```

- A **daemon thread** is a low-priority background thread that provides services to user threads (e.g., garbage collection).
- The JVM **does NOT wait** for daemon threads to complete before exiting.
- The main thread can explicitly convert a thread to daemon via `t.setDaemon(true)` — but this **must** be called *before* `t.start()`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Daemon Inheritance Rule:** When a thread is created, it inherits the daemon status of the thread that created it. Since the main thread is non-daemon, any thread it creates (without explicit `setDaemon(true)`) will also be a non-daemon (user) thread.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How the JVM Computes Program Termination

Let us formally derive the rule for JVM termination based on thread states.

### Step 1 — Define the Set of Active Threads

Let $T$ be the set of all threads currently alive in the JVM at any given instant:

$$T = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_n\}$$

### Step 2 — Partition into User and Daemon Threads

We partition $T$ into two disjoint subsets:

$$T = U \cup D, \quad \text{where } U \cap D = \emptyset$$

Here, $U$ is the set of user threads and $D$ is the set of daemon threads.

### Step 3 — Define the Termination Predicate

Let $L(t)$ denote the liveness predicate of thread $t$. $L(t) = 1$ if thread $t$ is alive (not yet in TERMINATED state), and $L(t) = 0$ otherwise.

The JVM continues to run if and only if:

$$\exists \, t \in U : L(t) = 1$$

That is, **at least one user thread is still alive**.

### Step 4 — Apply to the Main Thread

Let the main thread be denoted $m \in U$. When the `main()` method returns, we have $L(m) \rightarrow 0$. The JVM then evaluates:

$$\text{JVM Exit} = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } \forall t \in U, L(t) = 0 \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 5 — Consequence for Daemon Threads

If $D \neq \emptyset$ but $U = \emptyset$ (all user threads finished), the JVM forcibly terminates all daemon threads:

$$\forall t \in D : \text{interrupt}(t) \Rightarrow L(t) \rightarrow 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is why long-running daemon threads (like a poorly-written background logger) cannot prevent the JVM from shutting down. Only user threads have "veto power" over JVM termination.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Analytical Trace

Consider the following program:

```java
public class MainThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        System.out.println("Name: " + t.getName());
        System.out.println("Priority: " + t.getPriority());
        System.out.println("Daemon: " + t.isDaemon());
        System.out.println("Thread Group: " + t.getThreadGroup().getName());
    }
}
```

**Trace:**

| Step | Statement Executed | Output / State |
|------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | JVM creates main thread | $t_{\text{main}}$ created, state = NEW |
| 2 | `main()` invoked | $t_{\text{main}}$ state = RUNNABLE |
| 3 | `t = Thread.currentThread()` | $t$ refers to main thread |
| 4 | `t.getName()` | Prints `"main"` |
| 5 | `t.getPriority()` | Prints `5` |
| 6 | `t.isDaemon()` | Prints `false` |
| 7 | `t.getThreadGroup().getName()` | Prints `"main"` |
| 8 | `main()` returns | $t_{\text{main}}$ state = TERMINATED |
| 9 | No other user threads | JVM exits |

## 3.3 Mathematical Model: Priority and Scheduling

While Java thread priorities are not strictly enforced by all JVMs, the conceptual formula for thread scheduling preference is:

$$P_{\text{sched}}(t) = \alpha \cdot \text{Priority}(t) + \beta \cdot \text{CPU}_\text{burst}(t)^{-1} + \gamma \cdot \text{Age}(t)$$

Where:
- $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ are JVM-implementation constants.
- $\text{Priority}(t) \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$.
- $\text{CPU}_\text{burst}(t)^{-1}$ favors I/O-bound threads.
- $\text{Age}(t)$ prevents starvation.

Since the main thread has $\text{Priority}(t) = 5$, it sits at the **median** of the scheduling spectrum — neither favored nor starved under typical JVM implementations.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Main Thread vs. Other Threads

| Aspect | Main Thread | Custom User Thread | Daemon Thread |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Created by** | JVM | Programmer | Programmer |
| **Default priority** | 5 | 5 (inherited) | 5 (inherited) |
| **Daemon status** | `false` | `false` (inherited) | `true` (explicit) |
| **JVM waits for it?** | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Can change priority?** | Yes (but discouraged) | Yes | Yes |
| **Used for** | Entry point, orchestration | Concurrent tasks | Background services |
| **Example** | `main()` execution | Worker thread in pool | Garbage Collector |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JVM Thread Bootstrapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[JVM Start] --> B[Load System Classes]
    B --> C[Create Main Thread Object]
    C --> D[Set Thread Name = main]
    D --> E[Set Priority = 5]
    E --> F[Set Daemon = false]
    F --> G[Assign to main ThreadGroup]
    G --> H[Invoke main String args]
    H --> I{Is main returning normally?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Mark Main Thread as TERMINATED]
    I -- Exception Thrown --> K[Uncaught Exception Handler]
    K --> L[Stack Trace Printed]
    L --> M[JVM Terminates]
    J --> N{Any User Threads Alive?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Wait for User Threads]
    N -- No --> P{Terminate Daemons}
    O --> N
    P --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Main Thread & Subordinate Threads

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph JVM_Runtime
        direction TB
        MainGrp[Thread Group: main]
        MainTh[Main Thread priority 5 daemon false]
        ChildA[Worker Thread A priority 5 daemon false]
        ChildB[Worker Thread B priority 5 daemon false]
        DaemonX[Logger Daemon priority 5 daemon true]
        DaemonY[GC Daemon priority 5 daemon true]
    end

    MainGrp --> MainTh
    MainTh -.spawns.-> ChildA
    MainTh -.spawns.-> ChildB
    MainTh -.spawns.-> DaemonX
    JVM_Runtime -. internal .-> DaemonY

    MainTh --> EntryPoint[Executes main String args]
    EntryPoint --> ChildA
    EntryPoint --> ChildB
```

## 4.3 Lifecycle State Diagram of the Main Thread

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : JVM creates main thread
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start mechanism invoked
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : waiting for monitor lock
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : Object.wait or join
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep or timed wait
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notified or join completes
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout elapsed
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : main method returns
    TERMINATED --> [*] : thread object GC eligible
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The main thread typically transitions through NEW → RUNNABLE → TERMINATED in simple programs. The intermediate states (BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING) only appear when synchronization or sleep/wait mechanisms are invoked inside `main()`.

## 4.4 Daemon Inheritance Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    JVM[JVM] --> MT[Main Thread - Non Daemon]
    MT --> CT1[Child Thread 1 - Non Daemon inherited]
    MT --> CT2[Child Thread 2 - Non Daemon inherited]
    MT -.setDaemon true.-> DT1[Daemon Thread - True]
    MT -.setDaemon true.-> DT2[Daemon Thread - True]

    style MT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CT1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style CT2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style DT1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style DT2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Legend:**
- Yellow node = Main Thread (User Thread, JVM-spawned)
- Green nodes = Inherited user threads (non-daemon by default)
- Pink nodes = Explicitly set daemon threads

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the main thread in Java? List its default properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The main thread is the first user thread created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) when a Java program begins execution. It is responsible for executing the entry-point method `public static void main(String[] args)`.

**Default Properties:**

1. **Name:** `"main"` (retrieved via `Thread.currentThread().getName()`)
2. **Priority:** `5` (`Thread.NORM_PRIORITY`)
3. **Daemon Status:** `false` (it is a user thread)
4. **Thread Group:** `"main"`

*(Mark distribution: Definition = 1 mark, Properties listing = 2 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a daemon thread and the main thread.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Main Thread | Daemon Thread |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Created by | JVM | Programmer |
| Purpose | Entry point of program | Background services (e.g., GC) |
| JVM waits for termination | Yes | No |
| Default daemon status | `false` | `true` (must be set before start) |
| Inherits daemon status from | N/A (root thread) | Creating thread |

*(Mark distribution: Tabular distinction with at least 3 valid differences = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose marks by: (1) forgetting that the main thread's daemon status is `false` by default and *cannot* be changed to `true` after the program starts running, (2) confusing daemon inheritance rules, and (3) writing only the *program* without explaining the *output*, which is mandatory for full marks. Always explain **why** the output occurs, not just reproduce it.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate how to obtain and display the properties (name, priority, daemon status, thread group) of the main thread. Explain each line of output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What happens when the main thread terminates while other user threads and daemon threads are still running? Justify your answer with an example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

```java
public class MainThreadProps {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        System.out.println("Thread Name       : " + t.getName());
        System.out.println("Thread Priority   : " + t.getPriority());
        System.out.println("Is Daemon Thread? : " + t.isDaemon());
        System.out.println("Thread Group Name : " + t.getThreadGroup().getName());
        System.out.println("Thread ID         : " + t.getId());
        System.out.println("Thread State      : " + t.getState());
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Thread Name       : main
Thread Priority   : 5
Is Daemon Thread? : false
Thread Group Name : main
Thread ID         : 1
Thread State      : RUNNABLE
```

**Explanation of Each Line:**

1. **`Thread t = Thread.currentThread();`** — This static method returns a reference to the currently executing thread object. Inside `main()`, this returns the main thread. *(1 mark)*

2. **`t.getName()`** — Returns the name assigned to the thread by the JVM, which is `"main"` for the entry-point thread. *(1 mark)*

3. **`t.getPriority()`** — Returns the priority of the main thread. The JVM sets this to `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` (= 5). The valid range is 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX). *(1 mark)*

4. **`t.isDaemon()`** — Returns `false` because the main thread is a **user thread**, not a daemon. The JVM will wait for it to finish before terminating. *(1 mark)*

5. **`t.getThreadGroup().getName()`** — Returns the name of the thread group. The main thread belongs to the top-level group also named `"main"`. *(1 mark)*

6. **`t.getId()`** — Returns a unique positive `long` identifier assigned by the JVM. Typically `1` for the first thread. *(1 mark)*

7. **`t.getState()`** — Returns the current state from the `Thread.State` enum. While executing, the state is `RUNNABLE`. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

When the main thread terminates, the behavior depends on whether other **user threads** are still alive:

**Case 1: If other user threads are running**

The JVM does **NOT** terminate. It waits for all user threads to complete.

**Case 2: If only daemon threads are running**

The JVM **terminates immediately**, killing the daemon threads.

**Example Program:**

```java
class DaemonDemo extends Thread {
    public DaemonDemo(String name) {
        super(name);
    }
    public void run() {
        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " : " + i);
                Thread.sleep(500);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " exiting.");
    }
}

public class MainExitDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        DaemonDemo userThread = new DaemonDemo("User-Worker");
        DaemonDemo daemonThread = new DaemonDemo("Background-Daemon");
        daemonThread.setDaemon(true);
        
        userThread.start();
        daemonThread.start();
        
        System.out.println("Main thread finishing...");
        // main() returns here
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Main thread finishing...
User-Worker : 1
Background-Daemon : 1
User-Worker : 2
Background-Daemon : 2
...
User-Worker exiting.
[Daemon may be killed abruptly mid-loop or may finish]
```

**Justification:**

- The main thread executes `System.out.println("Main thread finishing...")` and then `main()` returns.
- The JVM checks if any **user threads** are alive. `User-Worker` is still running.
- Therefore, the JVM **waits** for `User-Worker` to complete.
- However, the daemon thread `Background-Daemon` may or may not complete — once `User-Worker` finishes and there are no remaining user threads, the JVM exits, **abruptly terminating** the daemon thread. *(3 marks for explanation + 4 marks for example & justification)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of daemon threads in Java. How are they related to the main thread? Write a Java program to illustrate that the JVM does not wait for daemon threads to finish. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the lifecycle states of the main thread with the help of a state diagram. Explain each transition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Concept of Daemon Threads:**

Daemon threads are low-priority background threads that provide services to user threads. The most common example is the **Garbage Collector** thread. The JVM does **not** wait for daemon threads to complete before shutting down.

**Relationship with Main Thread:**

1. The main thread is a **non-daemon (user)** thread.
2. By default, any thread created by the main thread **inherits** its non-daemon status.
3. The main thread can explicitly create daemon threads using `setDaemon(true)` **before** calling `start()`.
4. Once the main thread (and all other user threads) finish, the JVM exits, killing any remaining daemon threads.

**Java Program:**

```java
public class DaemonIllustration {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread daemon = new Thread(() -> {
            try {
                while (true) {
                    System.out.println("Daemon thread running...");
                    Thread.sleep(300);
                }
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Daemon interrupted.");
            }
        });
        
        daemon.setDaemon(true); // Must be called BEFORE start()
        daemon.start();
        
        System.out.println("Main thread sleeping for 1 second...");
        try {
            Thread.sleep(1000);
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }
        System.out.println("Main thread exiting.");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Main thread sleeping for 1 second...
Daemon thread running...
Daemon thread running...
Daemon thread running...
Main thread exiting.
[Daemon thread is killed; program ends even though its while loop was infinite]
```

**Explanation:**

- The main thread sleeps for 1 second and then exits.
- The daemon thread has an infinite `while(true)` loop.
- Despite the daemon thread's infinite loop, the JVM **terminates** once the main thread finishes because no other user threads exist.
- This proves that the JVM does not wait for daemon threads. *(7 marks: 2 for concept + 2 for relationship + 3 for program & explanation)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Lifecycle States of the Main Thread:**

The main thread, like all Java threads, can transition through six states defined in the `Thread.State` enum.

**State Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start method called by JVM
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : waiting to acquire monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait join or park invoked
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify notifyAll or joiner completes
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep or timed wait
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout elapses
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : main method returns normally or exception
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

**Explanation of Each State:**

1. **NEW:** The main thread object has been created by the JVM but `start()` has not yet been called. *(1 mark)*

2. **RUNNABLE:** The thread is executing or is ready to execute. The JVM's thread scheduler decides when to give it CPU time. *(1 mark)*

3. **BLOCKED:** The thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock (e.g., entering a `synchronized` block held by another thread). *(1 mark)*

4. **WAITING:** The thread is waiting indefinitely for another thread to perform a specific action (e.g., `Object.wait()`, `Thread.join()` without timeout). *(1 mark)*

5. **TIMED_WAITING:** The thread is waiting for a specified period (e.g., `Thread.sleep(milliseconds)`, `wait(timeout)`, `join(timeout)`). *(1 mark)*

6. **TERMINATED:** The thread has completed execution. The `main()` method has returned (either normally or via an uncaught exception). Once in this state, the thread cannot be restarted. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Main Thread**

- ✅ The main thread is created **automatically by the JVM** when a Java program starts.
- ✅ It executes the `public static void main(String[] args)` method.
- ✅ Its **default name** is `"main"`.
- ✅ Its **default priority** is `5` (`Thread.NORM_PRIORITY`).
- ✅ It is a **user thread** (daemon status = `false`); the JVM waits for it to finish.
- ✅ The JVM **exits only when all user threads** (including main) have terminated.
- ✅ **Daemon threads are terminated forcibly** by the JVM when all user threads finish.
- ✅ A new thread **inherits** the daemon status of the thread that created it (default: non-daemon from main).
- ✅ `setDaemon(true)` must be called **before** `start()`; otherwise, `IllegalThreadStateException` is thrown.
- ✅ Uncaught exceptions in the main thread **propagate to the JVM's default handler**, terminating the program.
- ✅ The main thread's lifecycle states: NEW → RUNNABLE → (BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING)* → TERMINATED.
- ✅ Use `Thread.currentThread()` inside `main()` to obtain a reference to the main thread object.
- ✅ Real-world use: main thread acts as a **bootstrap coordinator**; frameworks like Spring Boot delegate actual work to thread pools.
- ✅ Common exam pitfall: confusing **priority** (1–10) with **daemon status** (boolean); they are independent properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:** *"Main thread = Name 'main', Priority 5, Non-Daemon, Group 'main', Owner of JVM's patience."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating Thread

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread (Definition as per Java/KTU 2024 OOP Module 4):**
> A **Thread** is the smallest independent unit of execution within a process. In Java, a thread is represented by an object of the class `java.lang.Thread` (or any subclass thereof). Every Java program has at least one thread — the **main thread** — automatically spawned by the JVM to execute the `main()` method. Additional threads are explicitly created by the programmer to perform concurrent (parallel-style) tasks within the same program.

In the context of Object-Oriented Programming under the KTU 2024 scheme, "Creating a Thread" refers to the act of **instantiating a thread object and starting its execution lifecycle** so that the JVM allocates it a separate stack and schedules it independently of the main thread.

## 1.2 Two Standard Ways to Create a Thread (KTU High-Yield)

The Java API officially supports **two** methods for creating a thread:

| # | Method | Mechanism | Inheritance Cost |
|---|--------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Extending the `java.lang.Thread` class** | Subclass `Thread` and override `public void run()` | Wastes Java's single inheritance slot |
| 2 | **Implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` interface** | Implement `Runnable` and pass it to a `Thread` constructor | Preserves inheritance, more flexible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key API Insight (Frequently asked in KTU):** Both approaches ultimately require the programmer to define the `run()` method, because `Thread` itself implements `Runnable` and its default `run()` does nothing. The actual *concurrency* is triggered only when `start()` is invoked — calling `run()` directly runs it as a normal method, **not** as a thread.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Restaurant Analogy — "Threads as Chefs in a Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen. The **kitchen itself** is your *process* (the running Java program), and each **chef** working inside the kitchen is a *thread*. The kitchen has shared resources — burners, ovens, refrigerators (these correspond to *shared objects / heap memory*). The head chef (the **main thread**) starts the first dish (the `main()` method), but the moment an order comes in, the head chef can say: *"Hey, you, new chef — go chop vegetables for table 5!"* That moment is exactly the **`new Thread(...).start()`** call.
>
> - **Creating a thread** = hiring a new chef and giving them a job card (the `Runnable`).
> - **`start()`** = the head chef telling the new chef, *"Begin now!"*
> - **`run()`** = the actual chopping/cooking work written on the job card.
> - **Calling `run()` directly** = the head chef reading the job card aloud instead of handing it to the new chef — no new chef ever appears, no parallel work happens!

This analogy makes it instantly clear why `start()` and `run()` are not interchangeable.

## 1.4 The Main Thread (Default Thread)

Every standalone Java application begins execution with one implicit thread provided by the JVM:

```java
public class MainThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();   // captures the main thread
        System.out.println("Current Thread : " + t.getName());
        t.setName("KTU-Main");
        System.out.println("After rename   : " + t.getName());
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Current Thread : main
After rename   : KTU-Main
```

This baseline understanding is essential before exploring user-defined thread creation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Extending the `Thread` Class

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. Declare a class that **extends `java.lang.Thread`**.
2. **Override** the `public void run()` method — this method contains the code that will execute inside the new thread.
3. In the caller (typically `main()`), create an object of the subclass: `MyThread t = new MyThread();`
4. Invoke `t.start();` — this allocates a new call stack and invokes `run()` on it.
5. The JVM's thread scheduler decides exactly *when* the new thread runs (co-operative / pre-emptive depending on OS).

### 2.1.2 Reference Implementation

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    private String name;

    public MyThread(String name) {
        this.name = name;   // super(name) could also be called
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(name + " - Count: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(400); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
        System.out.println(name + " finished.");
    }
}

public class ExtendsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B");
        t1.start();        // creates new call stack -> run()
        t2.start();        // creates new call stack -> run()
        System.out.println("Main thread done.");
    }
}
```

### 2.1.3 Why "Why" and "How" (Reasoning Behind Each Step)

- **Why override `run()`?** Because `Thread`'s default `run()` is empty; it's a *hook* the JVM calls on the new stack.
- **Why call `start()` and not `run()`?** `start()` performs three low-level JVM actions: (a) checks the thread is not already started, (b) registers the thread with the native scheduler, (c) invokes `run()` on the **new** stack. Calling `run()` directly bypasses all of this.
- **Why `Thread.sleep()` in the loop?** Without a pause, one thread may finish before the other even starts, hiding the concurrency behaviour — a common KTU demonstration pitfall.

## 2.2 Method 2 — Implementing the `Runnable` Interface

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

1. Declare a class that **implements `java.lang.Runnable`**.
2. Provide a **public void run()** method (interface contract).
3. Create a `Thread` object, passing the `Runnable` instance to its constructor.
4. Call `start()` on the `Thread` object.

### 2.2.2 Reference Implementation

```java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    private String name;

    public MyRunnable(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(name + " - Count: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(400); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}

public class RunnableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyRunnable r1 = new MyRunnable("Worker-A");
        MyRunnable r2 = new MyRunnable("Worker-B");
        Thread t1 = new Thread(r1);
        Thread t2 = new Thread(r2);
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

### 2.2.3 Why "Why" and "How"

- **Why prefer `Runnable` over extending `Thread`?** Because Java classes can extend only **one** class. If your class needs to extend another (e.g., `JFrame` for a GUI), `Runnable` saves the inheritance slot.
- **Why the "Task–Worker" separation?** `Runnable` represents the *task* (the work to be done), while `Thread` represents the *worker* (the execution mechanism). Decoupling them allows the **same** `Runnable` task to be executed by **multiple** threads, or by a thread pool, or by an executor — a powerful OOP design choice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Question:** *"Why is implementing `Runnable` preferred over extending `Thread`?"*
> **Model Answer (4-point rubric):**
> 1. Java does not support multiple inheritance — `Runnable` preserves the option to extend another class.
> 2. The same `Runnable` instance can be passed to multiple `Thread` objects, enabling code reuse.
> 3. It separates the **task** from the **execution mechanism** (better OOP design).
> 4. It is compatible with **thread pools and executors** which accept only `Runnable`/`Callable` tasks.

## 2.3 Key `Thread` Constructors (High-Yield for KTU)

| Constructor | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Thread()` | Creates a thread with a default name (`Thread-N`). |
| `Thread(String name)` | Creates a named thread. |
| `Thread(Runnable target)` | Wraps a `Runnable` task. |
| `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` | Wraps a `Runnable` with a name. |
| `Thread(Runnable target, String name, long stackSize)` | Hints a custom stack size. |
| `Thread(ThreadGroup group, Runnable target, String name)` | Belongs to a `ThreadGroup`. |

## 2.4 Key `Thread` Methods (Formula Sheet)

| Method | Return Type | Behaviour | Static? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `start()` | `void` | Allocates new stack, invokes `run()`; throws `IllegalThreadStateException` if already started. | No |
| `run()` | `void` | Entry point of the new thread. | No |
| `sleep(long ms)` | `void` | Pauses current thread for *ms* milliseconds; **does not release the lock**. | **Yes** |
| `yield()` | `void` | Hints the scheduler to give other threads a chance; lock NOT released. | **Yes** |
| `join()` | `void` | Calling thread waits until this thread dies. | No |
| `interrupt()` | `void` | Sends an interrupt signal; sets the interrupted flag. | No |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean` | Returns `true` if the thread has started and not yet died. | No |
| `setName(String)` | `void` | Renames the thread. | No |
| `getName()` | `String` | Returns the thread's name. | No |
| `setPriority(int)` | `void` | Sets priority (1 to 10); range: `MIN_PRIORITY=1`, `NORM_PRIORITY=5`, `MAX_PRIORITY=10`. | No |
| `currentThread()` | `Thread` | Returns reference to the currently executing thread. | **Yes** |
| `getId()` | `long` | Returns the unique thread ID assigned by JVM. | No |
| `getState()` | `Thread.State` | Returns one of `NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED`. | No |

## 2.5 Thread Lifecycle States (Mandatory Diagram Concept)

A thread transitions through **six** states defined in `java.lang.Thread.State`:

| State | Description | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **NEW** | Thread object created, `start()` not yet called. | `new Thread()` |
| **RUNNABLE** | Ready-to-run; waiting for CPU time. | `start()` called |
| **BLOCKED** | Waiting to acquire a monitor lock. | Entering `synchronized` block held by another thread |
| **WAITING** | Waiting indefinitely for another thread's action (`join()` w/o timeout, `wait()`). | `wait()`, `join()` |
| **TIMED_WAITING** | Waiting for a specified time. | `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` |
| **TERMINATED** | `run()` has completed execution. | `run()` returns or throws uncaught exception |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web servers** (Tomcat, Jetty): one thread per incoming HTTP request.
- **GUI applications**: a separate **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** handles UI events so the interface does not freeze.
- **Database connection pools**: a pool of worker threads processes queued queries.
- **Real-time systems & games**: rendering, physics, AI, and audio all run on parallel threads.
- **Compilers & IDEs**: separate threads for syntax highlighting, autocompletion, and background compilation.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every modern multi-core processor is designed to run **multiple threads in true parallel** (e.g., an 8-core CPU can execute 8 threads simultaneously via SMT/Hyper-Threading). Java's thread abstraction is the language-level gateway to exploit this hardware parallelism — which is why KTU places "Creating Thread" in the OOP module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Through

## 3.1 Analytical Trace: `start()` vs `run()`

Let us mathematically and logically trace what happens when we execute the following two scenarios, as this is a classic KTU 14-marker.

### 3.1.1 Scenario A — Calling `run()` directly

```java
class T extends Thread {
    public void run() { System.out.println("run() executed by: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); }
}
public class DemoA {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        T t = new T();
        t.run();          // <-- direct call
        t.run();          // <-- direct call again
    }
}
```

**Execution trace (exhaustive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{JVM loads } \texttt{DemoA}, \text{ main thread } (M) \text{ starts.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & M: \texttt{T t = new T();} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Object created in heap, thread state} = \text{NEW}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & M: \texttt{t.run();} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Method dispatched on } M\text{'s stack, no new stack created.} \\
                      & \text{Output: "run() executed by: main"} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & M: \texttt{t.run();} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Same as Step 3, no exception, no new stack.} \\
                      & \text{Output: "run() executed by: main"} \\
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & \text{Sequential execution. Output produced twice on the same thread.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Scenario B — Calling `start()`

```java
public class DemoB {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        T t1 = new T();
        T t2 = new T();
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Execution trace (exhaustive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & M \text{ creates object } t1 \text{ (state = NEW).} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & M \text{ creates object } t2 \text{ (state = NEW).} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & M: \texttt{t1.start();} \\
                      & \quad \rightarrow \text{JVM verifies state is NEW (else } \texttt{IllegalThreadStateException}\text{).} \\
                      & \quad \rightarrow \text{JVM registers } t1 \text{ with native scheduler.} \\
                      & \quad \rightarrow \text{JVM allocates a new stack and invokes } t1.run() \text{ on it.} \\
                      & \quad t1 \text{ transitions: NEW} \to \text{RUNNABLE} \to \text{ (may run, may wait for CPU).} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & M: \texttt{t2.start();} \\
                      & \quad \rightarrow \text{Same as Step 3 for } t2. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Scheduler interleaves } M,\; t1,\; t2. \text{ Output order is non-deterministic.} \\
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & \text{True concurrent execution across three stacks (M, t1, t2).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule:** Once a thread's `start()` is called, calling `start()` again on the **same** thread object throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. This is a guaranteed KTU sub-question: *"What happens if `start()` is invoked twice on the same Thread object?"* — Answer: Compile succeeds, but at runtime the second `start()` throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.

## 3.2 Analytical Trace: Multiple Threads Sharing a `Runnable`

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " : " + i);
        }
    }
}
public class ShareDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyTask task = new MyTask();          // single Runnable object
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "T1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "T2");
        Thread t3 = new Thread(task, "T3");
        t1.start(); t2.start(); t3.start();
    }
}
```

**Logical derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Objects in heap:} \quad & \text{1 instance of } \texttt{MyTask} \text{ (the task),} \\
                              & \text{3 instances of } \texttt{Thread} \text{ (the workers).} \\
\text{Number of stacks:} \quad & \text{4 (main + 3 workers).} \\
\text{Number of concurrent } \texttt{run()} \text{ calls:} \quad & 3 \text{ (all invoking the SAME } \texttt{run()} \text{ on independent stacks).} \\
\text{Key insight:} \quad & \text{The same } \texttt{MyTask} \text{ object can serve as the job card for} \\
                            & \text{any number of worker threads — OOP reusability.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Priority Values (Verifiable)

```java
public class PriorityDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        System.out.println("MIN_PRIORITY  = " + Thread.MIN_PRIORITY);   // 1
        System.out.println("NORM_PRIORITY = " + Thread.NORM_PRIORITY);  // 5
        System.out.println("MAX_PRIORITY  = " + Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);   // 10
        System.out.println("Current priority of main = " + t.getPriority()); // 5
    }
}
```

**Output (deterministic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{MIN\_PRIORITY}  &= 1 \\
\texttt{NORM\_PRIORITY} &= 5 \\
\texttt{MAX\_PRIORITY}  &= 10 \\
\texttt{Current priority of main} &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Higher priority only *suggests* the scheduler run a thread more often — it is **not** a guarantee. On most JVMs the OS scheduler ultimately decides. Setting `MAX_PRIORITY` does not "lock out" lower-priority threads permanently; starvation-prevention mechanisms exist.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy of `Thread` and `Runnable`

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Object] --> B[Thread]
    I[Interface Runnable] --> B[Thread]
    B[Thread] --> C[MyThread subclass option 1]
    D[MyClass] -->|implements| I
    D[MyClass] --> E[Thread object wrapping]
    C[MyThread] --> F[start method invoked]
    E[Thread object wrapping] --> F[start method invoked]
    F[start method invoked] --> G[JVM allocates new call stack]
    G[JVM allocates new call stack] --> H[run method executes on new stack]
```

## 4.2 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start called
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : waiting for monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait or join without timeout
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notified or joined thread dies
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep or wait or join with timeout
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires or notified
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run completes or uncaught exception
    BLOCKED --> TERMINATED : stop deprecated or interrupt while blocked
    WAITING --> TERMINATED : interrupt or stop
    TIMED_WAITING --> TERMINATED : interrupt or stop
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Two Creation Methods Compared

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph METHOD1[Method 1 Extends Thread]
        A1[Declare class MyThread] --> A2[Override run method]
        A2 --> A3[Instantiate MyThread]
        A3 --> A4[Call start method]
        A4 --> A5[JVM runs run on new stack]
    end
    subgraph METHOD2[Method 2 Implements Runnable]
        B1[Declare class MyTask] --> B2[Implement run method]
        B2 --> B3[Instantiate MyTask task]
        B3 --> B4[Pass task to new Thread]
        B4 --> B5[Call start on Thread]
        B5 --> B6[JVM runs run on new stack]
    end
    A5 --> RESULT[Concurrent Execution Achieved]
    B6 --> RESULT
```

## 4.4 Stack Model — main vs worker thread

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PROC[Java Process]
        subgraph HEAP[Shared Heap Memory]
            OBJ1[MyTask object]
            OBJ2[Thread t1]
            OBJ3[Thread t2]
        end
        subgraph STACK1[Main Thread Stack]
            M1[main frame]
        end
        subgraph STACK2[t1 Stack]
            R1[run frame of MyTask]
        end
        subgraph STACK3[t2 Stack]
            R2[run frame of MyTask]
        end
    end
    M1 -->|invokes start| OBJ2
    M1 -->|invokes start| OBJ3
    OBJ2 -->|schedules| R1
    OBJ3 -->|schedules| R2
    R1 -->|reads| OBJ1
    R2 -->|reads| OBJ1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** All three stacks share the **same heap** (objects), but each thread has its **own private stack** for local variables and method frames. This is the architectural foundation for understanding race conditions, synchronization, and memory visibility in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define a thread. List the two ways of creating a thread in Java.**

**Model Answer (3-mark rubric):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A thread is a lightweight subprocess and the smallest unit of execution within a Java program. It shares the process's heap memory but has its own stack.
- **Two ways (2 marks):**
  1. By **extending** the `java.lang.Thread` class and overriding its `run()` method.
  2. By **implementing** the `java.lang.Runnable` interface and passing the object to a `Thread` constructor.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is the difference between calling `start()` and `run()` methods of a `Thread` object?**

**Model Answer (3-mark rubric):**

| Aspect | `start()` | `run()` |
|---|---|---|
| Stack | Creates a **new** call stack | Uses the **caller's** stack |
| Concurrency | Achieves true concurrent execution | Runs as a normal method call |
| Calls allowed | Only **once** per thread object | Can be called any number of times |
| Internal action | Registers with scheduler, then calls `run()` | Just executes the method body |

If `run()` is called directly, no new thread is created — this is a very common KTU pitfall.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the two ways of creating threads in Java with suitable code examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that creates two threads — one printing even numbers from 1 to 20 and the other printing odd numbers from 1 to 20. Use the `Runnable` interface approach. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Method 1: Extending `Thread` class (3.5 marks)**

```java
class Worker extends Thread {
    public Worker(String name) { super(name); }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(300); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}
public class Test1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Worker w1 = new Worker("Thread-A");
        Worker w2 = new Worker("Thread-B");
        w1.start();    // creates new stack for w1
        w2.start();    // creates new stack for w2
    }
}
```

**Method 2: Implementing `Runnable` interface (3.5 marks)**

```java
class Task implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(300); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}
public class Test2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Task task = new Task();
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "Thread-A");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "Thread-B");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Comparison Statement (1 mark, optional but improves scoring):**
The `Runnable` approach is preferred because it (i) does not waste the single-inheritance slot, (ii) allows the same task to be reused across multiple threads, and (iii) is the contract expected by modern `Executor` frameworks.

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

```java
class EvenPrinter implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 2; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Even  : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}
class OddPrinter implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Odd   : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}
public class OddEvenApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread evenT = new Thread(new EvenPrinter(), "EvenThread");
        Thread oddT  = new Thread(new OddPrinter(),  "OddThread");
        evenT.start();
        oddT.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of marks distribution:**
- 2 marks — Correct class definitions implementing `Runnable` with `run()`.
- 1 mark — Correct loop bounds (evens 2–20, odds 1–19).
- 1 mark — Passing the runnable to `Thread` constructor with names.
- 1 mark — Calling `start()` (not `run()`) on both threads.
- 1 mark — Proper exception handling for `InterruptedException`.
- 1 mark — Output explanation (non-deterministic interleaving because of concurrent execution).

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the lifecycle of a thread in Java with a neat state-transition diagram. List any four constructors of the `Thread` class. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is the significance of the `start()` method? Why is it illegal to call `start()` twice on the same `Thread` object? Demonstrate with a small code snippet. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Thread Lifecycle States (4 marks):**

1. **New** — Thread object is created but `start()` not yet invoked.
2. **Runnable** — `start()` has been called; thread is waiting for CPU time.
3. **Blocked / Waiting / Timed-Waiting** — Thread is suspended (e.g., waiting for a monitor lock, `wait()`, `join()`, `sleep(ms)`).
4. **Terminated (Dead)** — `run()` returns or an uncaught exception terminates it. A terminated thread cannot be restarted.

Transitions: `New → Runnable` (via `start()`), `Runnable ↔ Blocked/Waiting` (via locks / `wait` / `notify`), `Runnable → Terminated` (via `run()` completion). *(Refer to Section 4.2 of this note for the diagram.)*

**Four `Thread` Constructors (3 marks):**

1. `Thread()` — default constructor; thread name auto-assigned as `Thread-N`.
2. `Thread(String name)` — creates a thread with the supplied name.
3. `Thread(Runnable target)` — wraps a `Runnable` task.
4. `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` — wraps a `Runnable` and gives the thread a name.
   *(Valid alternates: `Thread(ThreadGroup g, Runnable r, String name)` and `Thread(Runnable r, String name, long stackSize)`. Any 4 correct ones score full marks.)*

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Significance of `start()` (3 marks):**
The `start()` method is the entry point that **actually creates a new thread of execution**. Internally it performs three critical jobs: (1) checks the thread is in the `NEW` state, (2) registers the thread with the native thread scheduler, (3) invokes the `run()` method on a freshly allocated call stack. Without `start()`, no concurrency occurs — calling `run()` directly just executes it on the current thread's stack.

**Why `start()` cannot be called twice (2 marks):**
The internal state of a `Thread` object is changed to non-`NEW` after the first `start()`. A second invocation triggers a guard check inside `start()` that throws `java.lang.IllegalThreadStateException`. This safeguard prevents the JVM from allocating two separate stacks for the same logical thread, which would corrupt the threading model.

**Code Demonstration (2 marks):**

```java
public class StartTwiceDemo extends Thread {
    public void run() { System.out.println("Running in: " + getName()); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        StartTwiceDemo t = new StartTwiceDemo();
        t.start();   // OK -> allocates new stack, prints message
        try { t.join(); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        t.start();   // THROWS IllegalThreadStateException at runtime
    }
}
```

**Output trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{Running in: Thread-0} \\
&\texttt{Exception in thread "main" java.lang.IllegalThreadStateException} \\
&\quad \texttt{at java.base/java.lang.Thread.start(Thread.java:...)} \\
&\quad \texttt{at StartTwiceDemo.main(StartTwiceDemo.java:7)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (and how to avoid it):**
>
> 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()` (–2 to –4 marks).** If the examiner's expected output shows two threads interleaving and your program calls `run()`, the output will be deterministic and sequential — the examiner will mark it *correct logic for a single-thread program* but deduct heavily for missing concurrency.
> 2. **Forgetting to handle `InterruptedException` (–1 mark).** `Thread.sleep()` declares a *checked* exception; omitting the `try-catch` causes a compile error, losing you at least 1 mark on code compilation.
> 3. **Calling `start()` twice (–2 marks).** The first call works, the second throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. Students often wrongly assume two `start()` calls create two threads — they do **not**; you must `new Thread()` again.
> 4. **Confusing `Thread.sleep()` (static) with instance `sleep()` (non-existent).** KTU students frequently write `t.sleep(500)` thinking it's an instance method — losing marks for a conceptual error. It is **`Thread.sleep(500)`**, a static method that pauses the *currently executing* thread.
> 5. **Writing `extends Runnable` (compile error).** `Runnable` is an **interface** — you must write `implements Runnable`, not `extends`.
> 6. **Omitting `@Override` annotation (not a mark loss, but examiner-friendly).** Always annotate `run()` overrides; it signals intent and protects against signature typos.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision points for KTU Module 4 — Creating Thread:**
>
> - A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution; every Java program begins with the **main thread**.
> - **Two creation methods:** (1) `extends Thread` + override `run()`, (2) `implements Runnable` + pass to `new Thread(runnable)`.
> - **`start()` ≠ `run()`.** `start()` creates a new stack and triggers concurrency; `run()` is just a normal method call.
> - **`start()` can be called only ONCE per `Thread` object**; a second call throws `IllegalThreadStateException` at runtime.
> - **`Runnable` is preferred** because: preserves inheritance, allows task reuse across threads, and is the contract used by modern Executors.
> - **Thread states** (6 in `Thread.State`): `NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED`.
> - **`Thread.sleep(ms)` is static** and pauses the *current* thread; it does NOT release any monitor lock.
> - **Priority constants:** `MIN_PRIORITY = 1`, `NORM_PRIORITY = 5`, `MAX_PRIORITY = 10` (suggestion to scheduler, not a guarantee).
> - **`currentThread()`** is a static method returning a reference to the currently executing thread.
> - **`isAlive()`** returns `true` if the thread has been started and has not yet terminated.
> - **Stack per thread, heap shared** — this distinction is foundational for later topics like synchronization and race conditions.
> - **Common interview/KTU traps:** `t.run()` vs `t.start()`, double `start()`, `extends Runnable` typo, unchecked `InterruptedException`.
> - **Modern best practice:** Use the `Runnable` interface and hand it to an `ExecutorService` (advanced topic, but KTU may mention in part B).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
