topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronization** in Object-Oriented Programming (Java) is the mechanism that ensures **mutual exclusion** and **coordinated access** to shared resources when multiple threads execute concurrently within a process. It guarantees that only one thread can access a *critical section* (shared mutable state) at a time, preserving **thread-safety** and the *happens-before* memory consistency guarantees defined by the Java Language Specification (JLS).

In Java, synchronization is built upon the concept of **monitors** (intrinsic locks) — every object in Java has an implicit lock associated with it. When a thread acquires this lock, all other threads attempting to acquire the same lock are blocked until the lock is released.

### Two Fundamental Problems Solved by Synchronization

| # | Problem | Synchronization's Solution |
|---|---------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Race Condition** — outcome depends on unpredictable thread interleaving | Mutual exclusion via `synchronized` blocks/methods |
| 2 | **Memory Inconsistency** — thread-local caches disagree with main memory | Happens-before guarantee via lock release/acquire |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Single-Stall Restroom**
>
> Imagine a public restroom with **only one stall** but **many people (threads)** wanting to use it. There is a **single key (the lock)** hanging on the door.
>
> - When Person A (Thread T1) enters, they take the key. No one else can enter.
> - Persons B, C, D (Threads T2, T3, T4) must wait outside.
> - When Person A finishes and exits, they **return the key** and the next waiting person can enter.
>
> The **stall = critical section**, the **key = monitor/intrinsic lock**, **people = threads**, **the act of returning the key = lock release**.
>
> Without this rule, two people would walk in simultaneously — chaos (a **race condition**).

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Weighted Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Terms (High Weightage in ESE & Series Exams):**
> - **Race Condition** — Concurrent access to shared data with non-deterministic outcome.
> - **Critical Section** — Code segment accessing shared resources.
> - **Monitor (Intrinsic Lock)** — Per-object implicit lock in Java.
> - **Mutual Exclusion** — Property ensuring one thread at a time in a critical section.
> - **Deadlock** — Two or more threads blocked forever, each waiting on the other.
> - **Inter-Thread Communication** — `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` for coordinated execution.
> - **Producer-Consumer Problem** — Classical synchronization problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for Synchronization — Why It Exists

Without synchronization, concurrent threads may:

1. **Interleave reads/writes** of shared variables → corrupted state.
2. **See stale values** due to CPU caching (each thread may have its own copy in registers/cache).
3. **Suffer from instruction reordering** by the compiler or CPU.

### 2.1.1 Race Condition — Worked Example

```java
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    public void increment() {
        count = count + 1;   // READ -> ADD -> WRITE (3 steps, NOT atomic)
    }
}
```

If two threads T1 and T2 invoke `increment()` simultaneously:

| Step | T1 | T2 | Shared `count` |
|------|----|----|----------------|
| 1 | READ `count = 0` | — | 0 |
| 2 | — | READ `count = 0` | 0 |
| 3 | WRITE `count = 1` | — | 1 |
| 4 | — | WRITE `count = 1` | **1 (LOST UPDATE!)** |

Expected: `count = 2`. Actual: `count = 1`. This is a **race condition**.

## 2.2 The `synchronized` Keyword — Two Forms

### Form 1: Synchronized Method

```java
class Counter {
    private int count = 0;
    public synchronized void increment() {   // lock = 'this' object
        count++;
    }
}
```

- The **intrinsic lock** of the object (`this`) is acquired on entry.
- Released automatically when the method returns (or throws an exception).

### Form 2: Synchronized Block (Fine-Grained)

```java
class Counter {
    private int count = 0;
    private final Object lock = new Object();

    public void increment() {
        synchronized (lock) {        // explicit lock object
            count++;
        }
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** Synchronized block is **preferred** when only a small portion of the method needs protection — improves concurrency by reducing lock-holding time.

## 2.3 Levels of Lock Granularity

| Lock Level | Syntax | Lock On | Best Use |
|-----------|--------|---------|----------|
| **Class Level** | `static synchronized` | `Class<>.class` object | Static shared resources |
| **Instance Level** | `synchronized method` | `this` object | Instance shared resources |
| **Block Level** | `synchronized(obj)` | any explicit object | Fine-grained critical sections |

## 2.4 Inter-Thread Communication — `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These methods **MUST** be called from within a `synchronized` context (otherwise `IllegalMonitorStateException` is thrown).

| Method | Effect on Calling Thread | Effect on Others |
|--------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| `wait()` | Releases lock & blocks until notified | — |
| `wait(long ms)` | Same, with timeout | — |
| `notify()` | Continues | Wakes **one** waiting thread (arbitrary) |
| `notifyAll()` | Continues | Wakes **all** waiting threads |

### Java Memory Model — Happens-Before Guarantees

The Java Language Specification defines these synchronization-based ordering rules:

1. **Unlock → Lock**: All actions before `unlock` are visible to a thread that subsequently acquires the same lock.
2. **Thread.start()**: Actions before `start()` are visible to the new thread.
3. **Thread.join()**: Actions in a thread are visible to any thread that successfully `join()`s it.

## 2.5 Deadlock — The Four Coffman Conditions

A deadlock occurs **only if ALL four** conditions hold simultaneously:

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Resource is non-shareable.
> 2. **Hold and Wait** — Thread holds one resource while waiting for another.
> 3. **No Preemption** — Locks cannot be forcibly taken.
> 4. **Circular Wait** — A cycle exists in the wait-for graph: T1 → T2 → T3 → T1.

**Breaking ANY ONE** of these prevents deadlock.

### Classic Deadlock Example

```java
class A { synchronized void foo(B b) { /* ... */ b.last(); } }
class B { synchronized void bar(A a) { /* ... */ a.last(); } }
```

## 2.6 The `volatile` Keyword (Lightweight Synchronization)

> [!NOTE]
> `volatile` provides **visibility** guarantee (not atomicity). Every read goes to main memory; every write goes immediately to main memory. It is cheaper than `synchronized`.

**Use when:** A single variable is written by one thread and read by others (no compound operations).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Syntax | Key Property | Common Pitfall |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|----------------|
| Intrinsic Lock | `synchronized(obj)` | Reentrant (same thread can re-acquire) | Calling `wait()` outside `synchronized` |
| Mutual Exclusion | `synchronized` | Only ONE thread in critical section | Deadlock if nested locks acquired in reverse order |
| `wait()` | `obj.wait()` | Releases lock + blocks | Must be in loop checking condition |
| `notify()` | `obj.notify()` | Wakes one waiting thread (arbitrary) | Signal loss possible — prefer `notifyAll()` |
| `notifyAll()` | `obj.notifyAll()` | Wakes all waiting threads | **Always safe** choice |
| `volatile` | `volatile int x;` | Visibility only | Does NOT ensure atomicity of `x++` |
| Deadlock prevention | Lock ordering | Break circular wait | Acquire locks in fixed global order |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why `count++` Is Not Atomic

The expression `count++` in Java bytecode is decomposed into **four distinct JVM instructions**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad &\texttt{aload\_0} &&\text{// load 'this' onto operand stack} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad &\texttt{dup} &&\text{// duplicate top of stack} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad &\texttt{getfield \#2} &&\text{// READ count from heap into stack} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad &\texttt{iconst\_1} &&\text{// push constant 1 onto stack} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad &\texttt{iadd} &&\text{// ADD top two stack values} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad &\texttt{putfield \#2} &&\text{// WRITE result back to heap} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Transition Logic:** Between Step 3 (READ) and Step 6 (WRITE), the thread can be **preempted** by the OS scheduler. Another thread can interleave its own READ-MODIFY-WRITE sequence, producing the **lost-update anomaly** documented in Section 2.1.1.

**Synchronization eliminates this race** by ensuring Steps 3–6 execute as an **indivisible unit** relative to all other threads attempting the same lock.

## 3.2 Detailed Producer-Consumer Problem (Step-by-Step Solution)

The Producer-Consumer problem demonstrates **cooperation** between threads, not just mutual exclusion.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

- A **shared bounded buffer** of size `N` (here `N = 1` for simplicity).
- **Producer** thread generates items and places them into the buffer.
- **Consumer** thread removes items and processes them.
- **Constraints:**
  - Producer must **wait** if buffer is **full**.
  - Consumer must **wait** if buffer is **empty**.

### 3.2.2 Exhaustive Java Implementation

```java
class Buffer {
    private int data;
    private boolean available = false;

    // Producer calls produce()
    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        // STEP 1: Check buffer-full condition (MUST be in 'while', not 'if')
        while (available) {
            System.out.println("Producer waiting (buffer full)...");
            wait();   // releases intrinsic lock & suspends this thread
        }

        // STEP 2: Critical section — modify shared state
        data = value;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + value);
        available = true;

        // STEP 3: Wake up one waiting consumer
        notifyAll();
    }

    // Consumer calls consume()
    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        // STEP 1: Check buffer-empty condition (MUST be in 'while')
        while (!available) {
            System.out.println("Consumer waiting (buffer empty)...");
            wait();
        }

        // STEP 2: Critical section
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + data);
        available = false;

        // STEP 3: Wake up producer
        notifyAll();
        return data;
    }
}
```

### 3.2.3 Driver Class

```java
public class ProdConsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Buffer buffer = new Buffer();

        // Producer Thread
        Thread producer = new Thread(() -> {
            try {
                for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buffer.produce(i);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        }, "Producer");

        // Consumer Thread
        Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> {
            try {
                for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buffer.consume();
            } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        }, "Consumer");

        producer.start();
        consumer.start();
    }
}
```

### 3.2.4 Trace Table — Why `while` and NOT `if`?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spurious wakeups** + **multiple waiters** make `if` unsafe. Consider:
>
> - Consumer C1 calls `consume()`, buffer is empty → C1 calls `wait()`, releases lock.
> - Consumer C2 also calls `consume()`, buffer still empty → C2 calls `wait()`.
> - Producer P puts item, calls `notifyAll()` → both C1 and C2 wake.
> - C1 consumes the item, sets `available = false`.
> - **If C2 used `if`**, it would proceed to consume again → **IndexOutOfBounds / wrong value**.
> - **With `while`**, C2 rechecks `available`, finds it `false`, and goes back to `wait()`.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Synchronization Mechanisms Comparison

| Feature | `synchronized` method | `synchronized` block | `volatile` | `ReentrantLock` (java.util.concurrent) |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Lock Type** | Intrinsic | Intrinsic | None (visibility only) | Explicit |
| **Reentrant** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | N/A | ✅ Yes |
| **Try-lock (timeout)** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ `tryLock()` |
| **Fairness** | ❌ Unfair | ❌ Unfair | N/A | ✅ Optional |
| **Multiple Conditions** | ❌ No (one per object) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ `newCondition()` |
| **Performance** | Good (biased locking in JVM) | Good | Best (cheapest) | Comparable |
| **Usage Simplicity** | High | Medium | High | Lower |
| **KTU Exam Relevance** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ (mentioned) |

## 3.4 Deadlock Avoidance — Banker's Algorithm (Conceptual Mapping)

For KTU, deadlock avoidance strategies (often asked for 7-mark sub-parts) include:

| Strategy | Mechanism | Implementation Hint |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Lock Ordering** | Always acquire locks in a fixed global order | Use `System.identityHashCode` to order two locks |
| **Lock Timeout** | Use `ReentrantLock.tryLock(timeout)` | Avoids indefinite blocking |
| **Deadlock Detection** | Use `ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads()` | Useful for debugging |
| **Open Calls** | Avoid holding a lock while calling external methods | Reduces contention |

### Lock Ordering Code Pattern

```java
public void transfer(Account from, Account to, int amount) {
    Account first  = (System.identityHashCode(from) < System.identityHashCode(to)) ? from : to;
    Account second = (first == from) ? to : from;

    synchronized (first) {
        synchronized (second) {
            from.debit(amount);
            to.credit(amount);
        }
    }
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Synchronization Lifecycle of a Thread

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Runnable : start()
    Runnable --> Blocked : acquire intrinsic lock (synchronized entry)
    Blocked --> Runnable : lock acquired
    Runnable --> Waiting : obj.wait()
    Waiting --> Blocked : obj.notify() or obj.notifyAll()
    Runnable --> Timed_Waiting : wait(ms) / sleep(ms)
    Timed_Waiting --> Runnable : timeout elapsed
    Runnable --> Terminated : run() returns
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Producer-Consumer Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Producer Thread Start] --> B{Buffer Full?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Call wait - release lock]
    C --> D[Suspended in wait set]
    D --> E[notifyAll wakes producer]
    E --> B
    B -- No --> F[Critical Section - Write Data]
    F --> G[Set available = true]
    G --> H[Call notifyAll]
    H --> I[Producer Loop Next Item]

    J[Consumer Thread Start] --> K{Buffer Empty?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Call wait - release lock]
    L --> M[Suspended in wait set]
    M --> N[notifyAll wakes consumer]
    N --> K
    K -- No --> O[Critical Section - Read Data]
    O --> P[Set available = false]
    P --> Q[Call notifyAll]
    Q --> R[Consumer Loop Next Item]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Deadlock — Circular Wait Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Thread T1] -- holds Lock A, waits for Lock B --> T2[Thread T2]
    T2 -- holds Lock B, waits for Lock A --> T1
    style T1 fill:#ffcccc
    style T2 fill:#ffcccc
```

> [!WARNING]
> The red-filled nodes above represent threads in a **deadlocked state**. Each holds one lock while waiting for the other, forming a cycle that breaks Coffman Condition 4 (Circular Wait) → **DEADLOCK**.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Java Memory Model & Synchronization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MainMemory[Main Memory Heap]
        X[Shared Variable X]
    end

    subgraph ThreadA[Thread A - CPU Cache]
        CacheA[Local Cache A]
        RegA[Registers A]
    end

    subgraph ThreadB[Thread B - CPU Cache]
        CacheB[Local Cache B]
        RegB[Registers B]
    end

    CacheA <--> X
    CacheB <--> X
    RegA --> CacheA
    RegB --> CacheB

    SyncGate{synchronized Block or volatile}

    X -.->|lock release flush| SyncGate
    SyncGate -.->|lock acquire invalidate| CacheA
    SyncGate -.->|lock acquire invalidate| CacheB
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: wait/notify Mechanism (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ObjectLock[Object with Intrinsic Lock]
        Owner[Current Owner Thread]
        EntrySet[Entry Set - BLOCKED Threads]
        WaitSet[Wait Set - WAITING Threads]
    end

    T1[Thread T1] -->|acquire lock| Owner
    T2[Thread T2] -->|contended| EntrySet
    T3[Thread T3] -->|contended| EntrySet
    Owner -->|release lock| Move1((Next owner from Entry Set))
    Owner -->|obj.wait call| WaitSet
    WaitSet -.->|obj.notify wakes| EntrySet
    EntrySet -.->|lock available| Move1
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define a *race condition* in multithreaded programming. Give a Java code example demonstrating the same.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **race condition** occurs when two or more threads access shared data concurrently, and the final outcome depends on the unpredictable interleaving of their execution.

**Example:**

```java
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    public void increment() { count++; }   // not thread-safe
}
```

When two threads invoke `increment()` simultaneously, the `count++` operation (read-modify-write) can interleave such that one update is lost. The expected value is 2 but actual may be 1 due to the race. **Solution:** Use `synchronized` to ensure atomicity of the critical section.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to **state the solution** (synchronization). For full 3 marks, you must **(1)** define, **(2)** give code, and **(3)** explain the bug.

---

### **Q2. [KTU Series Exam — Sept 2024]** Differentiate between `wait()` and `sleep()` in Java.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `wait()` | `sleep()` |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| **Class** | Defined in `Object` | Defined in `Thread` |
| **Lock release?** | ✅ Yes, releases intrinsic lock | ❌ No, holds the lock |
| **Must be in synchronized?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Wake-up mechanism** | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` | Timeout expires |
| **Purpose** | Inter-thread communication | Pausing execution |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **synchronization in Java**. Discuss synchronized methods and synchronized blocks with examples.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Synchronization is the mechanism in Java that controls the access of multiple threads to shared resources. It is based on the concept of an **intrinsic lock (monitor)** associated with every object. When a thread enters a synchronized context, it acquires the lock; all other threads attempting to acquire the same lock are blocked until it is released.

**Synchronized Method (2.5 marks):**
When a method is declared `synchronized`, the thread acquires the intrinsic lock of the object (`this`) before executing the method body.

```java
class Table {
    synchronized void printTable(int n) {   // synchronized method
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n * i));
            try { Thread.sleep(400); } catch (Exception e) {}
        }
    }
}
```

**Synchronized Block (2.5 marks):**
A synchronized block provides finer-grained control. Only a specific portion of code is protected, reducing lock-holding time and improving performance.

```java
class Table {
    void printTable(int n) {
        synchronized (this) {               // synchronized block
            for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
                System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n * i));
            }
        }
    }
}
```

> **Key Difference:** Synchronized methods lock the entire object (`this`), whereas synchronized blocks can lock on any object reference and protect only the enclosed code.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **deadlock**? Explain the **four necessary conditions** for deadlock with a Java example. How can deadlock be prevented?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A deadlock is a situation where two or more threads are permanently blocked, each waiting for a lock held by another thread in the cycle.

**Four Coffman Conditions (2 marks):**
1. **Mutual Exclusion** — At least one resource is held in a non-shareable mode.
2. **Hold and Wait** — A thread holds at least one resource while waiting for another.
3. **No Preemption** — Resources cannot be forcibly removed from a thread.
4. **Circular Wait** — A circular chain of threads exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next.

**Java Example (2.5 marks):**

```java
public class DeadlockDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final Object lockA = new Object();
        final Object lockB = new Object();

        Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {
            synchronized (lockA) {
                System.out.println("T1: holds LockA, waiting for LockB");
                synchronized (lockB) { System.out.println("T1: acquired LockB"); }
            }
        });

        Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> {
            synchronized (lockB) {
                System.out.println("T2: holds LockB, waiting for LockA");
                synchronized (lockA) { System.out.println("T2: acquired LockA"); }
            }
        });

        t1.start(); t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Prevention Strategies (1 mark):**
- **Lock ordering** — Always acquire locks in a fixed global order (e.g., based on `System.identityHashCode`).
- **Lock timeout** — Use `ReentrantLock.tryLock(timeout)`.
- **Avoid nested locks** when possible.
- **Use higher-level concurrency utilities** from `java.util.concurrent`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students name the four conditions but fail to **map each to a specific prevention technique**. Linking **Condition 4 (Circular Wait) → Lock Ordering** is the most-mapped, board-preferred answer.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **inter-thread communication** in Java using `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()`. Write a Java program to solve the **Producer-Consumer problem** using these methods.

**Model Answer:**

**Theory (3 marks):**
Inter-thread communication allows synchronized threads to coordinate their activities. Java provides three methods (all defined in `Object` class, all must be called from a synchronized context):

- `wait()` — Causes the current thread to release the intrinsic lock and wait until another thread invokes `notify()` or `notifyAll()` on the same object.
- `notify()` — Wakes up a **single** waiting thread on the same object's monitor (choice is arbitrary but implementation-defined).
- `notifyAll()` — Wakes up **all** waiting threads on the same object's monitor. **Preferred in most scenarios** to avoid signal loss.

These methods belong to `Object` (not `Thread`) because the lock is associated with the object, not the thread.

**Producer-Consumer Program (4 marks):**

```java
class SharedBuffer {
    private int item;
    private boolean available = false;

    synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (available) wait();
        item = value;
        System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " Produced: " + value);
        available = true;
        notifyAll();
    }

    synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!available) wait();
        System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " Consumed: " + item);
        available = false;
        notifyAll();
        return item;
    }
}

public class ProducerConsumer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SharedBuffer buffer = new SharedBuffer();

        Thread producer = new Thread(() -> {
            try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buffer.produce(i); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        }, "Producer");

        Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> {
            try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buffer.consume(); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        }, "Consumer");

        producer.start();
        consumer.start();
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1:** Using `if` instead of `while` for the wait condition. **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to call `notifyAll()` (producer doesn't wake consumer or vice versa → permanent wait). **Pitfall #3:** Calling `wait()` outside `synchronized` → `IllegalMonitorStateException`.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Producer-Consumer problem** in detail. Describe the role of `synchronized`, `wait()`, and `notifyAll()` in solving it. What happens if we use `notify()` instead of `notifyAll()`?

**Model Answer:**

**Problem Statement (2 marks):**
The Producer-Consumer problem (also called the *Bounded Buffer problem*) is a classic synchronization problem:
- One or more **producer** threads generate data items and place them into a shared buffer of finite size.
- One or more **consumer** threads remove items and process them.
- **Constraints:**
  - Producer must **block** if the buffer is **full**.
  - Consumer must **block** if the buffer is **empty**.
  - Access to the buffer must be **mutually exclusive**.

**Role of Synchronization Mechanisms (3 marks):**

| Mechanism | Role |
|-----------|------|
| `synchronized` | Provides **mutual exclusion** — only one thread (producer OR consumer) can access the buffer at a time. Prevents race conditions on `item` and `available`. |
| `wait()` | Releases the lock and suspends the thread when the buffer is in the wrong state (full for producer / empty for consumer). Avoids busy-waiting, saving CPU. |
| `notifyAll()` | Wakes all waiting threads after a state change so they can re-check the condition and proceed if applicable. |

**`notify()` vs `notifyAll()` — Why `notifyAll()` is Safer (2 marks):**
Using `notify()` may wake **the wrong type** of thread. Example:
- Producer puts item, calls `notify()` → consumer wakes (good).
- But if there are **two producers** and **one consumer**, calling `notify()` from a producer may wake the **other producer**, who finds the buffer full and goes back to `wait()`. The consumer remains blocked → **lost signal → potential starvation**.

`notifyAll()` wakes everyone; each thread rechecks the condition in its `while` loop; only the correct ones proceed. **Hence `notifyAll()` is the safer default.**

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Synchronization**

- **Synchronization = mutual exclusion + memory visibility** guarantee.
- Every Java object has an **intrinsic lock (monitor)**; threads acquire it via `synchronized`.
- **Intrinsic locks are reentrant** — same thread can re-enter without deadlocking itself.
- `synchronized` method locks `this`; `synchronized(this) { }` is equivalent.
- `synchronized static` method locks `Class<>.class` object.
- **Synchronized block > synchronized method** for fine-grained performance.
- `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` belong to **`Object`**, NOT `Thread`.
- They **must** be called inside a `synchronized` block on the **same object** used as the lock.
- Always use **`while` condition** (not `if`) around `wait()` to guard against spurious wakeups.
- `notify()` wakes **one** (arbitrary) waiter; `notifyAll()` wakes **all** — **prefer `notifyAll()`**.
- `volatile` provides **visibility only** — NOT atomicity. Do not use it for `count++`.
- **Deadlock** requires all 4 Coffman conditions; break **lock ordering** to prevent it.
- **Producer-Consumer** = cooperation; use `wait()` + `notifyAll()` with a shared flag.
- **Lost-update problem** in `count++` is the **#1 cause** of race conditions in KTU exams.
- `IllegalMonitorStateException` → `wait()`/`notify()` called outside `synchronized`.
- **Java Memory Model guarantees** happen-before relationships via lock release/acquire.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suspending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Suspending # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (Java)**, **Suspending** refers to the deliberate, temporary halt of a thread's execution without terminating it, allowing the thread to be **resumed** later from the exact point where it was paused. In Java, suspension is conceptually embodied by: - The **deprecated** `Thread.suspend()` method (legacy mechanism). - The **modern** inter-thread communication mechanism: `Object.wait()`, `Object.notify()`, and `Object.notifyAll()`. - The static `Thread.sleep(long millis)` method for timed suspension. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEEIT741 typically covers **Multithreading and Concurrency**. "Suspending" is a core sub-topic under *Thread Lifecycle Management* and *Inter-Thread Communication*. A thread is said to be in a **Suspended State** when its execution is paused, releasing the CPU but **NOT** releasing the monitor locks it currently holds. This is the critical distinction between `suspend()` (deprecated) and `wait()` (recommended). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **symphony orchestra rehearsal**: - The **conductor** (the main thread / scheduler) tells the **violinist** (a worker thread) to "**pause**" mid-performance because the cellist isn't ready. - The violinist **stops playing** (thread suspended) but **keeps her bow on the violin** and **holds the music sheet tightly** (the monitor lock is retained). - Later, the conductor says "**resume**" → the violinist continues exactly from the paused note (resumption from saved state). - The bow & music sheet = **Monitor Lock**. The violinist won't let go until she's completely done = **`suspend()` problem**. > [!NOTE] > **Better Analogy for `wait()`:** The violinist not only stops playing but **places her violin carefully on the table** (releases the lock) and **waits** for the conductor's signal. Other musicians can now use the violin stand (other threads can acquire the lock). When the conductor signals, she picks it up again. ## 1.3 Standard Terminology & State Metrics | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Suspended Thread** | A thread whose `run()` method execution is temporarily halted | | **Resume** | The act of restarting a suspended thread from its paused point | | **Monitor Lock** | An intrinsic lock associated with every Java object | | **Deadlock Risk** | Probability ≈ **High** with `suspend()`; ≈ **Low** with proper `wait()/notify()` | | **Deprecated** | Marked obsolete since **JDK 1.2 (1998)** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Suspension Paradigms in Java ### Paradigm A: Deprecated `suspend()` / `resume()` ```java public final void suspend() // Deprecated since JDK 1.2 public final void resume() // Deprecated since JDK 1.2 ``` **Operational Characteristics:** - Thread enters **Suspended** state. - **Locks are NOT released** → this is the root cause of deadlocks. - Uses an internal flag-based mechanism inside the JVM. - If `resume()` is called before `suspend()`, the suspend becomes "pending" and executes later (race condition). ### Paradigm B: Recommended `wait()` / `notify()` / `notifyAll()` ```java public final void wait() throws InterruptedException public final void wait(long timeout) throws InterruptedException public final void notify() public final void notifyAll() ``` **Operational Characteristics:** - Defined in **`java.lang.Object`** (so every object can be a synchronization point). - Thread enters the **Wait Set** (a special waiting queue). - **Locks ARE released** atomically. - Must be called from a `synchronized` context (else `IllegalMonitorStateException`). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Source | |---------|----------------|--------| | suspend() release of locks? | **No** – locks retained | `Thread` (deprecated) | | wait() release of locks? | **Yes** – atomic release | `Object` | | Prerequisite for wait() | Must be inside `synchronized(obj)` | JLS §17.2 | | Prerequisite for notify() | Must be inside `synchronized(obj)` | JLS §17.2 | | Exception on misuse | `IllegalMonitorStateException` | Built-in | | Number of waiting threads woken by `notify()` | **1 (arbitrary)** | JVM-dependent | | Number of waiting threads woken by `notifyAll()` | **All** | Guaranteed | | `sleep()` release of locks? | **No** | Static method | | `yield()` release of locks? | **No** – hint to scheduler | Static method | | `join()` release of locks? | **No** – waits for thread death | Instance method | ## 2.3 Why `suspend()` Was Deprecated — The "Why" Behind the Rule The Java specification deprecates `suspend()` for three core reasons: 1. **Locks are not released** → If Thread A holds a lock and is suspended, Thread B waiting for that lock will block forever → **Deadlock**. 2. **Asynchronous nature** → A suspended thread can be resumed by any other thread that has access, violating encapsulation. 3. **Invisible side effects** → A `suspend()` call deep in a method stack can suspend the entire call chain, including the main thread, without warning. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Use:** Suspension mechanisms underpin **producer-consumer queues**, **thread pools** (ExecutorService), **GUI event dispatch threads (EDT)**, and **asynchronous I/O frameworks** like NIO. Modern Java uses `java.util.concurrent.locks` (ReentrantLock with `await()`/`signal()`) instead of raw `wait/notify`. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Code Analysis — The `suspend()` Problem ### Step 1: Source Code Setup ```java class SharedResource { synchronized void criticalMethod() { System.out.println("Lock acquired by: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); try { Thread.currentThread().suspend(); } // Legacy suspension catch (Exception e) { e.printStackTrace(); } System.out.println("Lock released by: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); } } public class SuspendDemo { public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException { SharedResource obj = new SharedResource(); Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> obj.criticalMethod(), "T1"); Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> obj.criticalMethod(), "T2"); t1.start(); Thread.sleep(100); // Ensure T1 enters synchronized block t2.start(); } } ``` ### Step 2: Execution Trace | Time (ms) | T1 State | T2 State | Lock Owner | Output | |-----------|----------|----------|------------|--------| | 0 | RUNNABLE | NEW | none | — | | 10 | RUNNABLE (inside `synchronized`) | NEW | **T1** | "Lock acquired by: T1" | | 100 | **SUSPENDED** | BLOCKED (waiting for monitor) | **T1** | — | | ∞ | SUSPENDED | BLOCKED (forever) | **T1** | **DEADLOCK** | ### Step 3: Mathematical Deduction of Deadlock Condition Let $L_i$ denote the set of locks held by thread $T_i$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At time } t = 10\text{ms:} \quad & L_{T_1} = \{\text{obj}\} \\ & L_{T_2} = \emptyset \\ \text{At time } t = 100\text{ms:} \quad & L_{T_1} = \{\text{obj}\} \quad (\text{T1 suspended, lock retained}) \\ & L_{T_2} = \emptyset \quad (\text{waiting for } \text{obj}) \\ \text{Deadlock condition:} \quad & L_{T_1} \cap \text{Want}(T_2) \neq \emptyset \quad \land \quad T_1 \notin \text{Runnable} \end{aligned} $$ Conclusion: A **permanent hold-and-wait cycle** is established, satisfying all four **Coffman deadlock conditions** (Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait). ## 3.2 Detailed Code Analysis — The Correct `wait()/notify()` Pattern ### Step 1: Producer-Consumer Model ```java class Message { private String content; private boolean available = false; public synchronized void produce(String msg) throws InterruptedException { while (available) { // Guarded wait wait(); // Releases lock, enters wait-set } this.content = msg; available = true; notifyAll(); // Wake up consumers } public synchronized String consume() throws InterruptedException { while (!available) { // Guarded wait wait(); // Releases lock atomically } available = false; notifyAll(); // Wake up producers return content; } } ``` ### Step 2: Lock State Transition Analysis $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State 0 (Initial):} \quad & L_{\text{producer}} = \emptyset,\ L_{\text{consumer}} = \emptyset,\ \text{available} = \text{false} \\ \text{State 1 (Producer acquires):} \quad & L_{\text{producer}} = \{\text{this}\},\ \text{available} = \text{false} \\ \text{State 2 (Producer writes & notifies):} \quad & L_{\text{producer}} = \{\text{this}\},\ \text{available} = \text{true} \\ \text{State 3 (Producer releases):} \quad & L_{\text{producer}} = \emptyset \\ \text{State 4 (Consumer wakes & acquires):} \quad & L_{\text{consumer}} = \{\text{this}\} \\ \text{State 5 (Consumer reads & notifies):} \quad & L_{\text{consumer}} = \{\text{this}\},\ \text{available} = \text{false} \\ \text{State 6 (Consumer releases):} \quad & L_{\text{consumer}} = \emptyset \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Why `while` Instead of `if`? The wait may be subject to **spurious wakeups** (JLS permits the JVM to wake a thread without notification). Hence, the condition check **must** be re-evaluated in a loop: $$ \text{Guarded Wait Pattern:} \quad \text{while}(\text{condition} == \text{false}) \; \text{wait()}; $$ This guarantees **Mesa semantics** (the standard monitor semantics in Java), as opposed to **Hoare semantics** which would require `if`. ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: `suspend()` vs `wait()` vs `sleep()` | Property | `suspend()` | `wait()` | `sleep()` | |----------|-------------|----------|-----------| | **Class** | `Thread` | `Object` | `Thread` | | **Releases lock?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Static method?** | ❌ Instance | ❌ Instance | ✅ Static | | **Throws InterruptedException?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Must be in synchronized?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Status in Java 17+** | Deprecated, may be removed | Active & supported | Active & supported | | **Use case** | None (legacy) | Inter-thread communication | Timed delay / polling | | **Resumed by** | `resume()` | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` | Timeout expiry | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Thread State Lifecycle with Suspension ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> New New --> Runnable : start() Runnable --> Blocked : waiting for monitor lock Blocked --> Runnable : lock acquired Runnable --> Waiting : wait() called Waiting --> Runnable : notify() or notifyAll() Runnable --> TimedWaiting : sleep(ms) / wait(ms) TimedWaiting --> Runnable : timeout expires Runnable --> Terminated : run() completes Terminated --> [*] note right of Waiting : Suspended (lock-released) state note right of Blocked : Suspended (lock-held but waiting) state ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Suspension Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph ApplicationLayer["Application Layer (Main Thread)"] A1[Caller Thread] --> A2{Choose Suspension Strategy} end A2 -->|Legacy| B1["Thread.suspend() / resume()"] A2 -->|Modern Java| B2["Object.wait() / notify()"] A2 -->|Timed Delay| B3["Thread.sleep(milliseconds)"] A2 -->|High-level Concurrency| B4["ReentrantLock.await() / signal()"] B1 --> C1["Suspended (Locks HELD) - DEADLOCK PRONE"] B2 --> C2["Wait Set (Locks RELEASED) - SAFE"] B3 --> C3["Timed Wait (Locks HELD)"] B4 --> C4["Condition Variable (Locks RELEASED) - SAFE"] C1 --> D1[Deprecated Warning] C2 --> D2[Recommended Pattern] C3 --> D3[Use for Polling Only] C4 --> D4[Production Grade] style ApplicationLayer fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style C1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style C2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style C4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style D1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style D2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ## 4.3 Suspend-Resume Sequence Diagram (Producer-Consumer) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Producer Thread participant M as Shared Monitor (Lock) participant C as Consumer Thread P->>M: synchronized acquire M-->>P: lock granted P->>M: produce(item) P->>M: notifyAll() P->>M: release lock (exit synchronized) C->>M: synchronized acquire M-->>C: lock granted C->>M: consume(item) C->>M: notifyAll() C->>M: release lock Note over C,M: Consumer suspends via wait() if no item available Note over P,M: Producer suspends via wait() if buffer full ``` ## 4.4 Deadlock Formation with `suspend()` ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["Thread T1
Holds: obj lock
State: SUSPENDED"] -->|wants to release| Wait["Cannot release - suspended!"]:::danger T2["Thread T2
Wants: obj lock
State: BLOCKED"] -->|waiting for| T1 Wait -.->|deadlock| T2 classDef danger fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between `suspend()` and `wait()` methods in Java.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `suspend()` | `wait()` | |--------|-------------|----------| | Defined in | `java.lang.Thread` | `java.lang.Object` | | Releases lock? | **No** | **Yes** | | Status | **Deprecated** | Active | | Calling context | Anywhere | Only inside `synchronized` | | Resumed by | `resume()` | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` | | Deadlock risk | **High** | **Low** (if used correctly) | `wait()` is preferred because it releases the monitor lock, allowing other threads to proceed. `suspend()` retains the lock and is prone to deadlock, hence deprecated since JDK 1.2. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **What is the purpose of the `notifyAll()` method? Why is it preferred over `notify()` in most scenarios?** **Model Answer:** `notifyAll()` wakes up **all** threads currently waiting on the object's monitor. It is preferred over `notify()` because `notify()` awakens only **one arbitrary** waiting thread, which may not be the one best suited to proceed (e.g., in a producer-consumer scenario, a producer might wake another producer, leading to starvation). `notifyAll()` guarantees that the appropriate thread will re-check the condition and proceed, avoiding missed signals and starvation. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Pattern]** **Q.A.(a)** Explain the concept of thread suspension in Java. Why is the `suspend()` method deprecated? Describe a scenario where improper suspension leads to deadlock with a code example. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Thread suspension is the temporary halting of a thread's execution, allowing it to be resumed later. The legacy `Thread.suspend()` method pauses a thread without releasing the monitor locks it holds. This is deprecated for the following reasons: 1. **Deadlock Risk:** A suspended thread holding a lock blocks all other threads waiting for that lock indefinitely. 2. **Encapsulation Violation:** Any external thread with a reference can resume a suspended thread. 3. **Stack Corruption:** If `suspend()` is invoked deep in a call chain, it freezes the entire chain unpredictably. **Code Example Demonstrating Deadlock:** ```java class Resource { synchronized void use() { System.out.println("Started: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); Thread.currentThread().suspend(); // Dangerous! System.out.println("Ended: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); } } public class DeadlockDemo { public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception { Resource r = new Resource(); Thread t1 = new Thread(r::use, "T1"); Thread t2 = new Thread(r::use, "T2"); t1.start(); Thread.sleep(200); t2.start(); // T2 will block forever } } ``` **Output:** Only "Started: T1" is printed. T2 blocks because T1 holds the lock and is suspended, satisfying the deadlock conditions (Mutual Exclusion, Hold-and-Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait). --- **Q.A.(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating the **Producer-Consumer problem** using the `wait()` and `notifyAll()` methods. Explain how `wait()` differs from `suspend()` in this context. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** ```java class Buffer { private int item; private boolean available = false; public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException { while (available) { System.out.println("Producer waiting..."); wait(); // Releases lock, enters wait-set } item = value; available = true; System.out.println("Produced: " + item); notifyAll(); // Wake up consumers } public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException { while (!available) { System.out.println("Consumer waiting..."); wait(); // Releases lock atomically } available = false; System.out.println("Consumed: " + item); notifyAll(); // Wake up producers return item; } } public class ProducerConsumer { public static void main(String[] args) { Buffer buf = new Buffer(); new Thread(() -> { try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buf.produce(i); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } }, "Producer").start(); new Thread(() -> { try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buf.consume(); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } }, "Consumer").start(); } } ``` **Explanation:** In the producer's `produce()` method, when the buffer is full (`available == true`), the producer calls `wait()`. This **atomically releases the lock** and suspends the producer. When the consumer calls `notifyAll()`, the producer re-acquires the lock and re-checks the condition in the `while` loop. Unlike `suspend()`, this approach is **deadlock-free** because locks are released during suspension. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q.B.(a)** Compare and contrast the following methods with respect to thread suspension: `sleep()`, `wait()`, `suspend()`, `yield()`, and `join()`. Provide a comparative table and explain the use cases of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Method | Class | Static? | Releases Lock? | Use Case | |--------|-------|---------|----------------|----------| | `sleep(ms)` | Thread | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Pausing execution for fixed duration (e.g., polling) | | `wait()` | Object | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Inter-thread communication; producer-consumer | | `suspend()` | Thread | ❌ No | ❌ No | **Deprecated** — historical, causes deadlocks | | `yield()` | Thread | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Hint to scheduler to give other threads a chance | | `join()` | Thread | ❌ No | ❌ No | Wait for another thread to terminate | **Use Cases:** - **`sleep()`**: Use for timed retries, animation delays, throttling loops. - **`wait()`**: Use when a thread must wait for a condition to become true (paired with `notify()`). - **`suspend()`**: **Never use** in new code. Replaced by `wait()/notify()`. - **`yield()`**: Use in CPU-intensive loops to allow cooperative multitasking. - **`join()`**: Use when a parent thread needs results from a child thread before proceeding. --- **Q.B.(b)** Discuss the **spurious wakeup problem** in Java's `wait()/notify()` mechanism. Why must the condition check be inside a `while` loop and not an `if` statement? Illustrate with a code snippet. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A **spurious wakeup** occurs when a thread is woken from `wait()` without a corresponding `notify()` call — an artifact permitted by the Java Language Specification for performance and complexity reasons in monitor implementation. If `if` is used instead of `while`, the thread will proceed assuming the condition is true, even though it may still be false, leading to **race conditions** and **data corruption**. **Correct Pattern (while loop):** ```java synchronized void waitForCondition() throws InterruptedException { while (!conditionMet) { // Re-check after every wakeup wait(); } // Proceed safely } ``` **Incorrect Pattern (if statement — vulnerable):** ```java synchronized void waitForCondition() throws InterruptedException { if (!conditionMet) { // Will not re-check on spurious wakeup wait(); } // May proceed when condition is still false! } ``` **Explanation:** The `while` loop enforces **Mesa semantics** — every wakeup must be followed by condition re-evaluation. This is the universally recommended pattern in Java concurrency, and is the **KTU 2024 marking scheme's key differentiator** for full marks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Using `if` instead of `while`** with `wait()` — **−2 marks** for violating guarded-wait pattern. > 2. **Forgetting `synchronized` keyword** around `wait()/notify()` — `IllegalMonitorStateException` — **−1 to 2 marks**. > 3. **Confusing `sleep()` and `wait()`** — `sleep()` does NOT release locks; many students incorrectly claim it does — **−2 marks**. > 4. **Not mentioning deprecation** of `suspend()` — losing the "why" component — **−1 mark**. > 5. **Missing InterruptedException declaration** in `wait()`/`sleep()`/`join()` methods — compiler error in KTU lab exams — **−1 mark**. > 6. **Confusing `notify()` vs `notifyAll()`** — failing to mention starvation risk — **−1 mark**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Suspending** in Java = temporarily halting a thread's execution. - ✅ **`suspend()` is DEPRECATED** since JDK 1.2 — do **not** use in new code. - ✅ **`suspend()` does NOT release locks** → causes **deadlock**. - ✅ **`wait()` is the correct suspension mechanism** — releases locks atomically. - ✅ **`wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` are in `java.lang.Object`**, not `Thread`. - ✅ **Must be called inside `synchronized` block** on the same object. - ✅ **Always use `while` loop** around `wait()` to handle **spurious wakeups**. - ✅ **`notify()` wakes one arbitrary thread**; **`notifyAll()` wakes all**. - ✅ **`sleep()` does NOT release locks** but does NOT require `synchronized`. - ✅ **`join()` is used to wait for another thread's termination**. - ✅ **Modern alternative:** `java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock` with `Condition.await()/signal()`. - ✅ **Thread states in Java:** New → Runnable → (Blocked / Waiting / Timed Waiting) → Terminated. - ✅ **Golden Rule:** *"If a thread waits, it must release the lock; if it holds the lock, it cannot be safely suspended."*

topics.title: Resuming and Stopping Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT741 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Resuming and Stopping Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Java Object-Oriented Programming** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Resuming and Stopping Threads** refers to the set of techniques used to control the lifecycle of a thread after it has been started via `start()`. Historically, the `java.lang.Thread` class provided three methods — `stop()`, `suspend()`, and `resume()` — to forcibly terminate, pause, and resume a thread. These methods were **deprecated in JDK 1.2** because they could leave shared objects in inconsistent (corrupt) states by releasing intrinsic locks unpredictably.

Modern KTU-accepted techniques use **cooperative mechanisms**:
- **Stopping a thread**: Using a shared boolean flag (the *cancellation flag pattern*) or the **thread interruption mechanism** (`Thread.interrupt()`, `InterruptedException`).
- **Resuming/Pausing a thread**: Using `Object.wait()`, `Object.notify()` / `notifyAll()`, `Thread.sleep()`, or a guarded boolean flag (`waitFlag`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For examinations, students must explain *why* `stop()`, `suspend()`, and `resume()` were deprecated and demonstrate the **modern, safe alternatives** with code sketches.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Remote Control and a TV

Imagine a TV (the **thread**) playing a movie (executing `run()`). The old remote had three buttons:
- **STOP (Red Button)** — ripped the power cord instantly. The TV *might* corrupt its internal memory because it had no time to save the channel settings.
- **SUSPEND (Pause Button)** — froze the screen mid-frame, but the *background tuner was still on*. When paused in the middle of writing data, the tuner remained locked on a channel, blocking other TVs.
- **RESUME** — simply unfroze the screen.

The **new "smart" remote** works *cooperatively*:
- To **stop**: it sends a polite "please finish your current scene and shut down" signal (`interrupt()` + flag).
- To **pause/resume**: it sends a "wait until I press play" signal (`wait()` / `notify()`).

This **cooperative** approach guarantees that the TV (thread) finishes writing its settings before powering off — leaving shared state **consistent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Cooperative Termination:**
> *Cooperative termination* is a thread-control paradigm where a running thread is never killed by external force. Instead, the thread periodically checks a shared flag (or `Thread.interrupted()` status) and exits its `run()` method gracefully on its own.

---

### 1.3 The Deprecated Trio — At a Glance

| Method | Purpose | Status | Reason for Deprecation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `stop()` | Forcibly kills the thread | **Deprecated** | Releases all locks → shared data corruption |
| `suspend()` | Pauses thread without releasing locks | **Deprecated** | Prone to **deadlock** |
| `resume()` | Resumes a suspended thread | **Deprecated** | Inherits risks of `suspend()` |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test the *reason* for deprecation. Memorizing the phrase **"release of intrinsic locks leads to inconsistent object state"** scores full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Lifecycle of a Thread — Where Stop / Resume Fit

A thread in Java exists in one of **six states** (defined in `Thread.State` enum):

```
NEW  →  RUNNABLE  ⇄  BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING  →  TERMINATED
```

- **`stop()` (deprecated)** forces a jump from *any state* → `TERMINATED`.
- **`suspend()` (deprecated)** keeps the thread in `RUNNABLE` but blocks it indefinitely.
- **`resume()` (deprecated)** moves the thread back to `RUNNABLE`.

### 2.2 The Three Modern Techniques (Step-by-Step Logic)

#### Technique A — The Shared Flag (Cancellation Flag Pattern)

This is the **simplest** and most commonly tested pattern.

**Step 1:** Declare a `volatile` boolean instance field inside the `Runnable` class.

**Step 2:** The worker thread's `run()` loop checks this flag on every iteration.

**Step 3:** The controlling (main) thread flips the flag to `true` to request stopping.

**Step 4:** The worker observes `true`, exits the loop, and returns from `run()` — entering `TERMINATED` naturally.

> [!NOTE]
> The keyword **`volatile`** is critical. It guarantees that writes by the main thread are **immediately visible** to the worker thread (preventing JVM-level caching optimizations). KTU exams often award marks specifically for mentioning `volatile`.

#### Technique B — The Interruption Mechanism

**Step 1:** The main thread calls `worker.interrupt()`. This sets the thread's **interrupt status flag** to `true`.

**Step 2:** If the worker is blocked in `wait()`, `sleep()`, or `join()`, an `InterruptedException` is thrown — the worker catches it and exits gracefully.

**Step 3:** If the worker is **actively running** (e.g., inside a tight `for` loop), it must call `Thread.interrupted()` (static) or `isInterrupted()` (instance) to check the flag.

**Step 4:** When the flag is `true`, the worker breaks out of its loop and terminates.

#### Technique C — `wait()` / `notify()` for Pausing & Resuming

**Step 1:** The worker thread reaches a checkpoint and calls `wait()` on a shared monitor object — it **releases the intrinsic lock** and enters the `WAITING` state.

**Step 2:** Another thread calls `notify()` (or `notifyAll()`) on the same monitor — the worker **wakes up**, reacquires the lock, and continues from where it stopped.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `wait()`/`notify()` **must** be called inside a `synchronized` block on the same monitor object. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Old `stop()` contract | Forcibly releases **all locks** held by the thread | Deprecated in **JDK 1.2** |
| Old `suspend()` contract | Holds **all locks** while paused | Causes deadlock |
| Modern stop | `volatile boolean running = false;` | Cooperatively terminates |
| Modern interrupt | `Thread.interrupt()` | Sets interrupt flag |
| Check interrupt | `Thread.interrupted()` (clears flag) vs `isInterrupted()` (does not clear) | Static vs instance |
| Pause (cooperative) | `synchronized(monitor) { monitor.wait(); }` | Releases lock |
| Resume | `synchronized(monitor) { monitor.notify(); }` | Wakes one waiter |
| Visibility guarantee | `volatile` keyword | **Prevents JVM caching** |
| Legality of `wait/notify` | Must be inside `synchronized` block on the *same* object | Compile-time rule |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These patterns are used everywhere in production Java systems:
- **Apache Tomcat** uses the volatile-flag pattern to shut down worker pools gracefully.
- **Java Concurrency's `ExecutorService.shutdownNow()`** internally uses `interrupt()` on every pooled thread.
- **Producer-Consumer queues** (used in message brokers like Kafka) rely on `wait()`/`notify()` (or modern `BlockingQueue`) to pause/resume consumers when the queue is empty/full.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Why `stop()` Is Unsafe — State Corruption Walkthrough

Consider a shared `BankAccount` object with fields `balance` and `transactionInProgress`.

```
Original state: balance = 1000, transactionInProgress = false
```

A worker thread T1 begins transferring money:

```
T1 acquires intrinsic lock on bankAccount.
T1 executes: balance = balance - 500;     // balance = 500
[ at this exact moment, T1 is preempted ]
```

If `stop()` were called here, **JDK 1.0 behavior** was:
1. T1 is force-killed.
2. T1's lock on `bankAccount` is **released**.
3. But T1 had not yet executed `transactionInProgress = false;` or `notifyAll();`.

Result: another thread T2 now sees `transactionInProgress = true` forever — a **classic inconsistent state** known as **"object resurrection problem"**.

### 3.2 Mathematical Model of the Flag-Pattern Stopping Latency

Let:

$$
T_{\text{check}} = \text{period between flag checks by worker}
$$

$$
T_{\text{flag\_set}} = \text{time when main thread sets the flag} = 0 \text{ (reference)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{exit}} = \text{time when worker actually exits run()}
$$

Then the **worst-case stopping latency** is:

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{exit}} - T_{\text{flag\_set}} \leq T_{\text{check}}
$$

**Derivation:**
- The worker thread performs $n$ checks per second, so $T_{\text{check}} = \frac{1}{n}$.
- After the flag is set, the worker continues its current iteration until reaching the next check.
- Maximum wait = exactly one full check period.

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{latency}} \;\leq\; \frac{1}{n}}
$$

Where $n$ = number of flag checks per iteration of the worker's loop.

> **Engineering interpretation:** Increasing $n$ (more frequent checks) decreases latency but adds CPU overhead. This is the classic **responsiveness vs throughput tradeoff**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Long-running compute loop | **Flag pattern** | Simplest, lowest overhead |
| Thread blocked in I/O / sleep | **Interrupt + InterruptedException** | Forces blocked calls to throw |
| Producer-Consumer coordination | **`wait()` / `notify()`** | Built-in lock release |
| ThreadPool executor shutdown | **`shutdownNow()` (calls `interrupt()`)** | Standard JDK API |
| GUI event dispatch (Swing) | **`SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` + flag** | EDT safety |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough — The Safe Stop Pattern

```java
class SafeWorker implements Runnable {
    private volatile boolean running = true;

    public void stop() {
        running = false;            // Step 1: main thread sets the flag
    }

    public void run() {
        while (running) {           // Step 2: worker checks flag every iteration
            try {
                System.out.println("Working...");
                Thread.sleep(500);  // Step 3: simulated work
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Interrupted!");
                break;              // Step 4: graceful exit on interrupt
            }
        }
        System.out.println("Worker cleanly terminated.");
    }
}
```

**Line-by-line KTU-trace:**
- `volatile boolean running` → visibility guarantee (no caching).
- `running = false` → atomic write, immediately visible.
- `while (running)` → cooperative check.
- `Thread.sleep(500)` inside `try` → if `interrupt()` is called during sleep, `InterruptedException` is thrown and caught.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thread State Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread()
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() / join()
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / wait(ms)
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : synchronized lock contention
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / notifyAll()
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout / notify()
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() returns / flag = false / InterruptedException
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

### 4.2 Deprecated vs Modern Stopping Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OLD["Deprecated JDK 1.0 API"]
        A1[Main Thread] -- stop() --> B1[Worker Thread]
        B1 -- Force Kill --> C1[TERMINATED State]
        C1 -. Releases ALL locks .-> D1[Shared Object Corruption]
    end

    subgraph NEW["Modern Cooperative API"]
        A2[Main Thread] -- interrupt() --> B2[Worker Thread]
        B2 -- checks flag --> C2{Interrupted?}
        C2 -- Yes --> D2[Clean run() exit]
        C2 -- No --> E2[Continues Loop]
        D2 --> F2[TERMINATED State]
        F2 -. Locks released NORMALLY .-> G2[Shared Object CONSISTENT]
    end

    style OLD fill:#fdd
    style NEW fill:#dfd
```

### 4.3 Pause/Resume Sequence — wait()/notify() Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Thread
    participant Worker as Worker Thread
    participant Monitor as Shared Monitor Object

    Note over Worker: Executing run()
    Worker->>Monitor: synchronized(monitor)
    Worker->>Monitor: monitor.wait()
    Note over Worker: RELEASES lock, enters WAITING
    Main->>Monitor: synchronized(monitor)
    Main->>Monitor: monitor.notify()
    Note over Worker: WAKES UP, reacquires lock
    Worker->>Worker: Resume execution
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Why were the methods `stop()`, `suspend()`, and `resume()` of the `Thread` class deprecated in Java?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The methods `stop()`, `suspend()`, and `resume()` were deprecated in **JDK 1.2** because they are inherently **unsafe**. The `stop()` method forcibly terminates a target thread and **abruptly releases all the intrinsic locks** held by that thread. If the target thread was in the middle of modifying a shared object (e.g., updating a bank balance), the object can be left in an **inconsistent or corrupted state**, leading to data corruption visible to other threads. Similarly, `suspend()` pauses a thread **without releasing its locks**, which frequently causes **deadlock** because other threads waiting for those locks are blocked indefinitely. Modern Java prefers **cooperative mechanisms** like the `volatile` flag pattern and the `Thread.interrupt()` mechanism, which allow the target thread to release resources and exit cleanly on its own.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Differentiate between `Thread.interrupted()` and `isInterrupted()` in Java.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `Thread.interrupted()` | `isInterrupted()` |
|---|---|---|
| Type | **Static** method | **Instance** method |
| Clears interrupt flag? | **Yes** — resets flag to `false` | **No** — flag remains as-is |
| Tests which thread? | The **currently executing** thread | The **specific thread** on which it is called |
| Typical use | Inside `run()` to check & clear own flag | Externally to *query* another thread's flag |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a suitable Java program how a thread can be **safely stopped** using a shared boolean flag. Why is the `volatile` keyword mandatory in this design? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **thread interruption mechanism** with an example. How does it differ from the deprecated `stop()` method? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A shared boolean flag is a `volatile boolean` instance variable that the worker thread polls inside its `run()` loop. When the controlling thread sets it to `false`, the worker exits the loop and terminates gracefully.

**Code (5 marks):**

```java
class FlagWorker implements Runnable {
    private volatile boolean running = true;   // (1) volatile for visibility

    public void run() {
        while (running) {                     // (2) cooperative check
            System.out.println("Worker running...");
            try { Thread.sleep(500); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { break; }
        }
        System.out.println("Worker cleanly stopped.");
    }

    public void shutdown() {
        running = false;                      // (3) request stop
    }
}

public class FlagDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        FlagWorker w = new FlagWorker();
        Thread t = new Thread(w);
        t.start();
        Thread.sleep(2000);
        w.shutdown();                         // (4) signal stop
        t.join();                             // (5) wait for clean exit
    }
}
```

**Why `volatile` is mandatory (1 mark):**
Without `volatile`, the JIT compiler may cache the `running` variable in a CPU register or local cache of the worker thread. Even when the main thread writes `false`, the worker may keep reading the cached `true` and never terminate. The `volatile` keyword forces a **happens-before relationship**: all writes are flushed to main memory and reads come from main memory, guaranteeing visibility across threads.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The interruption mechanism is a cooperative cancellation protocol where one thread sets the *interrupt status flag* of another by calling `targetThread.interrupt()`. The target thread must explicitly check the flag (via `Thread.interrupted()` or `isInterrupted()`) or handle an `InterruptedException` to terminate.

**Code (4 marks):**

```java
class InterruptWorker extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
                System.out.println("Step " + i);
                Thread.sleep(1000);            // blocks here
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Thread was interrupted — exiting cleanly.");
        }
    }
}

public class InterruptDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        InterruptWorker t = new InterruptWorker();
        t.start();
        try { Thread.sleep(3500); }
        catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        t.interrupt();                         // sends interrupt signal
    }
}
```

**Difference from `stop()` (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Deprecated `stop()` | Modern `interrupt()` |
|---|---|---|
| Action | **Forces** immediate termination | **Requests** cooperative termination |
| Lock release | **Abrupt** — leaves objects inconsistent | **Graceful** — only on `run()` exit |
| Safety | **Unsafe** — deprecated since JDK 1.2 | **Safe** — recommended JDK practice |
| Who decides exit? | Calling thread | The target thread itself |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` methods of the `Object` class. Explain how they are used to **pause and resume** a thread. Why must these methods be called inside a `synchronized` block? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating a **Producer-Consumer** scenario where the consumer thread is paused using `wait()` when the buffer is empty and resumed using `notify()` when the producer adds an item. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 marks:**

**The three methods (3 marks):**
- `wait()` — Causes the current thread to release the intrinsic lock of the object on which it is called and enter the `WAITING` state until another thread invokes `notify()` / `notifyAll()` on the **same object**, or `wait(long timeout)` expires.
- `notify()` — Wakes up **one** thread that is currently waiting on the monitor of this object. The choice of which thread is arbitrary.
- `notifyAll()` — Wakes up **all** threads waiting on this object's monitor. They compete for the lock; only one acquires it and continues.

**Pause/Resume usage (2 marks):**
- **Pausing:** Worker thread reaches a condition-not-met state and calls `obj.wait()` — releases its lock and sleeps.
- **Resuming:** Another thread changes the state and calls `obj.notify()` — wakes up one waiting worker, which reacquires the lock and continues.

**Why inside `synchronized` (2 marks):**
- Calling `wait()` releases the intrinsic lock — but the thread **must own** that lock first. Hence `wait()` can only be called inside a `synchronized` block on the same object; otherwise `IllegalMonitorStateException` is thrown.
- This design guarantees that the check-and-wait is **atomic**, preventing a *lost wakeup* race condition.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 marks:**

```java
class Buffer {
    private int data;
    private boolean available = false;

    public synchronized int consume() {
        while (!available) {
            try { wait(); }                       // consumer paused
            catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
        available = false;
        notify();                                  // wake producer
        return data;
    }

    public synchronized void produce(int value) {
        while (available) {
            try { wait(); }                       // producer paused
            catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
        data = value;
        available = true;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + value);
        notify();                                  // wake consumer
    }
}

class Producer extends Thread {
    Buffer b;
    Producer(Buffer b) { this.b = b; }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) b.produce(i);
    }
}

class Consumer extends Thread {
    Buffer b;
    Consumer(Buffer b) { this.b = b; }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) System.out.println("Consumed: " + b.consume());
    }
}

public class ProdConsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Buffer b = new Buffer();
        new Producer(b).start();
        new Consumer(b).start();
    }
}
```

**Trace explanation (1 mark):**
- Consumer starts first, sees `available = false`, calls `wait()` → pauses.
- Producer adds data, sets `available = true`, calls `notify()` → wakes consumer.
- Consumer resumes, reads data, signals producer again.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Forgetting `volatile` on the flag → loses **1–2 marks** for visibility reasoning.
> 2. Calling `wait()` / `notify()` *outside* a `synchronized` block → throw `IllegalMonitorStateException` → program crashes during viva.
> 3. Confusing `Thread.sleep()` (does **not** release lock) with `wait()` (does **release** lock). This is a **favourite trick question**.
> 4. Saying "interrupt() stops the thread immediately" — **wrong**. It only *requests*; the target decides when to stop.
> 5. Using `stop()` in code snippets → instant **0 marks** for that sub-question.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The methods `stop()`, `suspend()`, `resume()` are **deprecated since JDK 1.2**.
- ✅ `stop()` releases all locks → **shared object corruption**.
- ✅ `suspend()` retains all locks → **deadlock**.
- ✅ Modern stopping = **shared `volatile` flag** OR **`Thread.interrupt()`** + cooperative handling.
- ✅ Modern pause/resume = **`Object.wait()`** + **`Object.notify()` / `notifyAll()`**.
- ✅ `wait()` **releases** the intrinsic lock; `sleep()` does **not**.
- ✅ `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` **must** be called inside a `synchronized` block on the same monitor.
- ✅ `Thread.interrupted()` is static and **clears** the flag; `isInterrupted()` is instance and **preserves** it.
- ✅ `volatile` guarantees **cross-thread visibility** of shared state.
- ✅ Always prefer **cooperative termination** over forceful killing — it preserves object invariants.
